```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> Don't know enough about the B5000/B5500 itself, but I can say that its
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> not do arithmetic on items in decimal format.

My admittedly porous memory is that the B5xxx never had decimal hardware.
I participated in a benchmark at the Large Systems Plant at Paoli, Penn. in
late '73. I know that the B6700 COBOL compiler we used definitely generated
decimal/binary convert code. Chuck, do you remember when DMS was new
and the Large Systems COBOL compiler didn't yet have the DMS syntax? You
had to use COMPUTE to call the DMS functions. :-) We had to do that for the
benchmark.

A B5500 in Huntsville, Ala. was in a below ground center which was flooded.
The engineers took it completely apart in the parking lot, hosed it down well,
let it dry, put it back together, and it ran perfectly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need for packed decimal was Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T13:04:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bram4r$219h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <b3638c46.0312041401.62296f72@posting.google.com> <br8mv6$67e$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <bra8fi$1msq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211143948.871$dt@news.newsreader.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031211143948.871$dt@news.newsreader.com...
> Chuck, do you remember when DMS was new
> and the Large Systems COBOL compiler didn't yet have the DMS syntax? You
> had to use COMPUTE to call the DMS functions. :-) We had to do that for
the
> benchmark.

That sounds like DMS *as distinct from* DMSII, and that's *way* before my
time with data bases on the Burroughs Large System!  The DMSII syntax has
been around, based on internal evidence in the COBOL(68) compiler symbol,
since Mark 2.4, and that's a very long time.  I came to California to work
in languages in September 1984, and Mark 3.4 software had just gone out the
door.

I had started with Burroughs in May 1974 as a Burroughs Small System
specialist for the local branch, and held the same position at the district,
then the region, level.  During that time I did participate in a couple of
Large System benchmarks (one IBM S/360 assembler to B6700 COBOL via the
Beacon translator, because I knew something about IBM assembler, and one
Univac 1108 to B6700 Fortran, because I'd spent five years programming in
Fortran; maybe one or two more), but I've only been working exclusively on
the Large System since '84.   I think the B5700 (a rebadge of the B5500) was
still being marketed when I went to work for Burroughs in '74, but there
weren't any around where I was -- there were fewer than a dozen Large
Systems of any flavor in our region when I moved to California in '84,
though LINC and the B5900 were starting to catch on.   Earliest and smallest
Large System I worked on was a two-bulk-mod single-proc B6700, in Houston,
when we first started working on the assembler benchmark.   It was around
the time I moved here that the Large System evolved from the B5/6/7 sequence
of names to the A Series.

> A B5500 in Huntsville, Ala. was in a below ground center which was
flooded.
> The engineers took it completely apart in the parking lot, hosed it down
well,
> let it dry, put it back together, and it ran perfectly.

Then there were the standard part of the script for Large Systems
demonstrations in which the customer representatives were invited to open
any cabinet of a running machine and pull any card out of it, to prove that,
no matter what, the machine didn't quit running.  That was in the
mid-to-late 1970's, IIRC.

But neither the B1985 backplane upon which a crazed operator micturated
while it was running, nor the B7800 processor that slipped out of its sling
and was dropped down a multistory atrium while being delivered, fared nearly
so well as your B5500,  alas  ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need for packed decimal was Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T09:40:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031212104158.454$gN@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <b3638c46.0312041401.62296f72@posting.google.com> <br8mv6$67e$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <bra8fi$1msq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211143948.871$dt@news.newsreader.com> <bram4r$219h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> But neither the B1985 backplane upon which a crazed operator micturated
> while it was running, nor the B7800 processor that slipped out of its sling
> and was dropped down a multistory atrium while being delivered, fared nearly
> so well as your B5500,  alas  ...


I heard about the dropped one. Reminds me of a some goofs at my clients'
sites. The information and power cables on an old HPT EU were the same
(serious design flaw!), and when an engineer plugged them in backwards,
it fried the EU and all five drives. That same engineer reversed the non-
polarized plug (another design flaw) of the head assembly motion sensor
on a disk pack. All was fine until the massive linear motor moved the head
assembly. The sensor told the motor the head was moving backward, so the
linear motor pushed harder, etc. The head assembly accelerated violently
and crashed out of the cabinet into a nearby drive cabinet, narrowly missing
the astonished engineer. Another (excellent) engineer accidentally reversed
the (non-polarized) movement sensor connector on a tape drive with a DC
motor. Same thing, the reel rapidly accelerated, and in 5 seconds the tape
unspooled and packed the tape bay with tape so tightly that it broke the glass
bay door. Burroughs needed to be more careful with plug design. :-)

You should enjoy this one. The engineers needed to work on the power
supply of one of the last core-memory medium systems at a client site,
and they had a very long batch job running. I brought everything down but
that program, which I stopped. When MCP went into a wait loop, I copied
down all the state settings and powered off the machine. When repairs
were done, I powered up the machine, reset the state, and it ran some ten
hours until the batch job was complete, when we rebooted. Expressions
on the engineers' faces when it powered up and ran were interesting. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need for packed decimal was Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T11:21:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brd4gg$or0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <b3638c46.0312041401.62296f72@posting.google.com> <br8mv6$67e$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <bra8fi$1msq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211143948.871$dt@news.newsreader.com> <bram4r$219h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031212104158.454$gN@news.newsreader.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031212104158.454$gN@news.newsreader.com...
> Another (excellent) engineer accidentally reversed
> the (non-polarized) movement sensor connector on a tape drive with a DC
> motor. Same thing, the reel rapidly accelerated, and in 5 seconds the tape
> unspooled and packed the tape bay with tape so tightly that it broke the
glass
> bay door. Burroughs needed to be more careful with plug design. :-)

Got a similar one, but IBM this time, and not the engineer's fault.  At one
point, IBM 7080's were being installed at a government installation.  These
particular monsters, as I heard it, had the maximum of four tape channels,
each with the maximum of ten tape drives.  The tape drives were the latest
high-speed 729/VI, I think   At the time, this was a Big Deal, probably the
biggest system in this medium-sized city.  They brought the press corps in
to see this Technological Wonder.  The programmers set up a demonstration to
impress the reporters that involved reading all forty tape units down to the
end and then putting them all in a high-speed rewind simultaneously on cue.
All the visiting dignitaries oohed and aahed at the sight of these eighty
tape reels spinning at a gazillion RPM, then the press photogs fired their
Speed Graphics and all the takeup reels stopped instantly, filling all forty
729's with tape (though I don't think it broke the glass!)  ... (I would
probably never have heard this story had my first job out of college been on
a S/360 running 7080 emulation).

And of course you didn't *have* to close the 729's windows during operation,
including during high-speed rewind, so things got interesting if you hadn't
triple-checked to make sure the reel was *secure* on the hub.

We also ran 1401 emulation on our 360/40 for a while, then we got some
second-hand four-tape 16k 1401's.  The programs were originally written to
presume 729 tape drives, but the machines we got actually had 7330's.
Didn't take long before the in-column 15-inch-per-second "high-speed" rewind
of the 7330's convinced the shop that maybe some 729's would be a worthwhile
purchase ...

Then there's the IBM 2321 Data Cell Drive ... We had three in our shop, each
for critical-path ISAM applications, with the data occupying most of the
bins and the indices in another ... 'nuff said ...

> You should enjoy this one. The engineers needed to work on the power
> supply of one of the last core-memory medium systems at a client site,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> on the engineers' faces when it powered up and ran were interesting. :-)

Yeah, I'm not surprised in the general case; it does help if the state's
simple to record!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need for packed decimal was Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T17:27:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RjrCb.15076$aF2.1696951@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<brd4gg$or0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <b3638c46.0312041401.62296f72@posting.google.com> <br8mv6$67e$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <bra8fi$1msq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211143948.871$dt@news.newsreader.com> <bram4r$219h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031212104158.454$gN@news.newsreader.com> <brd4gg$or0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
> news:20031212104158.454$gN@news.newsreader.com...
> 
>>Another (excellent) engineer accidentally reversed
>>the (non-polarized) movement sensor connector on a tape drive with a DC

I had one quite a bit worse, and unfortunately it was my own (though not 
completely so).  I was working on a real time system designed to fill 
trucks with gravel. The trucks sat in a weigh scale, and I opened a gate 
above, loaded up the correct amount by opening a gate, then after a 
short delay for bounce gave the truck a final weighing, printed a bill 
of lading, forwarded that to the mainframe, and turned a traffic light 
green.  We had a BIG RED STOP button to one side that cut power to the 
gate, which would automatically stop material flow in emergencies (it 
was a hydrolic gate, and turning off power would close it).

One day I put in an upgrade, and realized the damned program was in an 
infinite loop of some sort.  I hit the switch, and discovered some 
electrician had wired arround the switch.  It did not shut off.  Knowing 
where the main fuse box was two stories below, I proceeded downstairs at 
a flat run.  The door was locked.  The crushed stone, by this time, was 
overflowing the truck box quite nicely.  I ran back up two flights, 
screeming "Where is the !@#$%^&* key to the power room?".  I got it.

Unfortunately, by the time I got back downstairs and unlocked the door 
and cut power, I had managed to put +-600,000 pound of stone into a 
truck designed for about 35,000.  The pile was approximately 45 feet 
accross at the bottom ands 25 feet deep. Once we got the front end 
loader out, we were able to find it with a bit of digging.

For weeks you could make be twitch by mentioning the word "dump" ...

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T21:54:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD00183.484845C2@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> positive number or you could not possibly do arithmetic on it? :-)

What earthly difference does it make?  You DON'T have to write a sign!

>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> kidding us, right? :-)

Assuming you're not kidding us --  presumably the people who first thought about
this decided that it was less complicated to have numeric fields implemented in the
hardware with the sign position than to have two kinds of "numeric" fields, one
with, one without a sign.  And very probably nobody since has thought it worth while
debating (yourself excluded, of course).  How can you get so worked up over 4
(EBCDIC) bits?

I don't usually agree that the majority must be always right - but this has been in
existence so long that surely by now a compelling argument for dropping the sign
position would have emerged by now if there was one.

PL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T07:38:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqq8pc$1bq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <3FD00183.484845C2@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:3FD00183.484845C2@mb.sympatico.ca...

> I don't usually agree that the majority must be always right - but this
has been in
> existence so long that surely by now a compelling argument for dropping
the sign
> position would have emerged by now if there was one.

The argument was compelling enough for a great many implementations to do
so.  A table of sixty thousand one-digit unsigned packed-decimal items
occupies thirty thousand eight-bit bytes on a Unisys MCP system, and has
since the introduction of the 8-bit representation on that architecture on
the Burroughs Large System.  Why is the counter-argument -- that the
packed-decimal representation of these items should occupy the same space as
a display representation of the same items -- compelling?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
> position would have emerged by now if there was one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T11:07:26+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd3a4e4_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <3FD00183.484845C2@mb.sympatico.ca> <20031206180347.530$E4@news.newsreader.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031206180347.530$E4@news.newsreader.com...
> "Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> > Judson McClendon wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In this case it is NOT "stupid waste".  It is a consistent usage by COBOL
compilers to simplify decimal arithmetic in generated code on IBM
mainframes.

You have become so incensed about the "stupid waste" that you have failed to
see it from the point of view of the compiler writer.

I'm wondering if you would be so worked up about people who run the tap
while they brush their teeth, or overfill the bath so the overflow has to
cope when they get in, or run their cars on empty and put 5 bucks worth of
gas in when they absolutely HAVE to, or don't recycle glass, plastic, and
paper. All examples of "stupid waste" that most of us simply accept as being
a personal choice. (I may feel strongly about it myself, but I respect the
right of other people to make their own choices.)

If you looked at a cross section of COBOL programs, on a cross section of
IBM installations you would find that less than 0.1% of the object code is
packed fields used as data storage. Of that 0.1% , given the average size of
a packed field is 4 bytes, you are looking at a "waste" of  0.0125 percent
of available storage, even discounting the cases where these fields are
negative and a sign IS required.

As the Operating System inherently "wastes" around 7% of the environment
(implementing routines that will never be used, but are there just in case),
it would seem to me that your concern over "waste" is focussed in the wrong
area.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T14:18:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031208151936.493$ru@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <3FD00183.484845C2@mb.sympatico.ca> <20031206180347.530$E4@news.newsreader.com> <3fd3a4e4_2@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> I'm wondering if you would be so worked up about people who run the tap
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> right of other people to make their own choices.)

I too respect people's choices about what they do. But my attitude is a bit
more severe toward what people choose to make me do. :-)

> If you looked at a cross section of COBOL programs, on a cross section of
> IBM installations you would find that less than 0.1% of the object code is
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> area.

I accede that this is a personal foible of mine, but I look at these things
from at least two perspectives. One is "how significant is the result?"
The other is "How 'excusable' was the decision?" They are separate and
unlinked, though looking at them as a cumulative result might also be a
valid perspective. Your argument above is from the first perspective,
and though I think your figures are much too low, I won't quibble. The
real reason for my being so 'worked up' ;-) is that I believe the decision
to require a sign on all packed BCD fields to do math was completely
wrongheaded, unjustifiable and wasteful, compounded by not fixing it
after forty years. Again a personal foible, but I abhor a very stupid
decision with little bad consequence more than a reasonably justifiable
and reasonable decision with severe consequences. This is because I
view them as disparate issues, somewhat analogous to 'guilt' and
'consequence'. One possibly cannot help the consequences of what they
do, but one *can* help their intentions, so guilt and consequence may be
only indirectly linked, if at all.

But, I respect your position. I don't think we're going to change anyone's
mind further on the issue. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Francis ANDRE" <francis.andre@easynet.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T19:40:01+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fdf5197$0$13872$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <20031204082440.078$7y@news.newsreader.com> <3FD00183.484845C2@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
> I don't usually agree that the majority must be always right - but this
has been in
> existence so long that surely by now a compelling argument for dropping
the sign
> position would have emerged by now if there was one.

There is a simple case where it is interesting to not have a niblle sign on
packed decimal
as for example, the DATE group.

01 TODAY-BASE.
    05 YEAR        PIC 9999    COMP-3.
    05 MONTH    PIC 99       COMP-3.
    05 DAY          PIC 99       COMP-3.
01 TODAY REDEFINES TODAY-BASE  PIC 9(8) COMP-3.

It is simply less error prone and more efficient to write

   IF  TODAY > ANOTHER-DAY THEN

than

   IF YEAR      OF TODAY-BASE   >   YEAR     OF ANOTHER-BASE    AND
        MONTH OF TODAY-BASE   >   MONTH OF ANOTHER-BASE    AND
        DAY       OF TODAY-BASE   >   DAY       OF  ANOTHER-BASE    THEN....

specially when you have dozen of comparisons of date in your program!!


But the main compelling argument for dropping the sign position is simply
coherence...
If PACKED DECIMAL have been introduced to "PACK" decimal datas in order to
save memory by dividing by 2 the space allocated to such data type compare
to the ZONED DECIMAL, why
should one keep the sign nibble for unsigned quantities if there is no need
for???

Packing is Packing...don't stop in the middle of the river

Regards
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: phone numbers (was Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?))

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T08:23:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqnn2i$2hbu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <mLqdnTgEDMTbmVCiU-KYlg@giganews.com> <20031203100056.678$Uv@news.newsreader.com> <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com> <bqnebo$kll$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
My family owned and ran a small hotel in Texas from 1946 to 1954.  We had
two telephones in the building, one for the hotel itself and a pay phone in
a booth for the guests.  The telephone number for the hotel was 141 (and
yes, the phone had a crank).  The telephone number for the pay phone was
9501.   All calls went through the operator.  The idea of having a prefix
was way too uptown for this place; at this point in history that was for Big
Towns like San Antonio.

Once, my aunt called my mother person-to-person from New York at the hotel.
The operator knew my mother had gone to the movies that afternoon.  The
first clue my Aunt had of this was when the person answering the phone said
"Plaza Theater" instead of "Kendall Inn".  My aunt was absolutely amazed
when my mother came to the phone less than a minute later.

When we sold the hotel in 1954, we moved to an area that actually had an
automated exchange, and all the numbers were fixed at five digits (ours was
5-3596).  It was some years later that they added the MYrtle prefix to our
number, increasing it to seven digits.

    -Chuck Stevens



<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bqnebo$kll$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <g7-dnbS9jf-HxVOiU-KYjQ@giganews.com>,
> JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >numbers, store it, then reconstruct it. Because in another location it
might
> >be "PLumbo 2000."
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I don't get it guys. IBM won't provide a signless packed data type
because they want to sell more storage. Yet they introduced packed dec
in the first place to reduce the use of storage. Huh?

Regards, Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T10:02:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com>`

```
scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote 

> Richard writes ...
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> wierd but that _look_ OK.

'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.  However
that does not mean that there are not quantitities that are invalid if
negative.

> Why are so many people upset about the sign nibble? It's a fact of life in IBM
> mainframes, it's not going away, memory is cheap today. Get over it. Move on.

1) I do not use IBM Mainframes, never have, probably never will.

2) I almost never use packed decimal so sign nibbles are irrelevant to
me.

3) The issue was whether it was _mandatory_ for a number to have a
sign.  It isn't.  And whether it can take place in arithmetic
operations without one.  It can. If it cannot on IBM mainframes, then
that is a limitation of those machines, not of 'numbers' or
'arithmetic operations'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T18:18:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com...
> scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.

Yes, it is.  QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.
You either have stock on the shelf, or you don't.  It isn't like a bank account,
where you can sign up for overdraft protection.

QOH is not the same as [Quantity-on-hand less Inventory Demand],
which can be a negative value.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qovusv8nt1e817fkg3n9jm7cjf7utlqhb7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Thu, 04 Dec 2003 18:18:23 GMT "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

:>Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com...

:>> scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote

:>> > Richard writes ...

:>> > >There are plenty of uses for numbers that can not be negative, or
:>> > >would be invalid if they were negative.  Prices, discount rates, even
:>> > >quantities.
:>> >
:>> > Quantities _should_ be signed. The human reality is, inventory in stock might
:>> > not match inventory as recorded in the computer record. At least if the value
:>> > shows up as negative you have some indication of trouble. If the field were
:>> > designed to not allow negative numbers, then you start getting results that are
:>> > wierd but that _look_ OK.

:>> 'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.

:>Yes, it is.  QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.
:>You either have stock on the shelf, or you don't.  It isn't like a bank account,
:>where you can sign up for overdraft protection.

True quantity on hand cannot go negative.

Recorded QOH however can go negative if the received goods were not properly
recorded or not recorded in a timely way.

:>QOH is not the same as [Quantity-on-hand less Inventory Demand],
:>which can be a negative value.
```

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:qovusv8nt1e817fkg3n9jm7cjf7utlqhb7@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 04 Dec 2003 18:18:23 GMT "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> recorded or not recorded in a timely way.

The whole point of having control systems is to ensure
 that transactions are accurately recorded in a timely fashion.

QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.

Any "control system" that allows material release for shipment
under the conditions you mentioned has serious logic errors.
```

```
:>> :>> 'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.

:>> :>Yes, it is.  QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.

:>> True quantity on hand cannot go negative.

:>> Recorded QOH however can go negative if the received goods were not properly

:>The whole point of having control systems is to ensure
                                                  ^^^^^^
                                                  attempt
:> that transactions are accurately recorded in a timely fashion.

:>QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.

Or a procedure error.

:>Any "control system" that allows material release for shipment
:>under the conditions you mentioned has serious logic errors.

How would that apply to a store?

Goods are delivered, placed on the shelf and purchased before being recorded
in the ICS. The sales register immediately records the sales.

I guess one could argue that the store should not place them on the shelf
until the paperwork is done, but the store is in the business of making money
and turning over the inventory ASAP.
```

```
then it is badly designed and incompetently programmed.
```

- **References:** `<217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <qovusv8nt1e817fkg3n9jm7cjf7utlqhb7@4ax.com> <ACOzb.390486$0v4.19752691@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8cevsv4dam40s7ckp9bh9ff0fikt944qh0@4ax.com> <1ZPzb.390909$0v4.19760791@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <43kvsvgoshg17vt2rj63q9d1fc7oijjvhs@4ax.com> <uNQzb.391175$0v4.19765673@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Fri, 05 Dec 2003 01:01:14 GMT "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:43kvsvgoshg17vt2rj63q9d1fc7oijjvhs@4ax.com...

:>> On Fri, 05 Dec 2003 00:05:17 GMT "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

:>> :>Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:8cevsv4dam40s7ckp9bh9ff0fikt944qh0@4ax.com...

:>> :>> On Thu, 04 Dec 2003 22:33:04 GMT "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

:>> :>> :>Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:qovusv8nt1e817fkg3n9jm7cjf7utlqhb7@4ax.com...
:>> :>> :>> On Thu, 04 Dec 2003 18:18:23 GMT "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

:>> :>> :>> :>Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com...

:>> :>> :>> :>> scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote

:>> :>> :>> :>> > Richard writes ...

:>> :>> :>> :>> > >There are plenty of uses for numbers that can not be negative, or
:>> :>> :>> :>> > >would be invalid if they were negative.  Prices, discount rates, even
:>> :>> :>> :>> > >quantities.

:>> :>> :>> :>> > Quantities _should_ be signed. The human reality is, inventory in stock might
:>> :>> :>> :>> > not match inventory as recorded in the computer record. At least if the value
:>> :>> :>> :>> > shows up as negative you have some indication of trouble. If the field were
:>> :>> :>> :>> > designed to not allow negative numbers, then you start getting results that are
:>> :>> :>> :>> > wierd but that _look_ OK.

:>> :>> :>> :>> 'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.

:>> :>> :>> :>Yes, it is.  QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.
:>> :>> :>> :>You either have stock on the shelf, or you don't.  It isn't like a bank account,
:>> :>> :>> :>where you can sign up for overdraft protection.

:>> :>> :>> True quantity on hand cannot go negative.

:>> :>> :>> Recorded QOH however can go negative if the received goods were not properly
:>> :>> :>> recorded or not recorded in a timely way.

:>> :>> :>The whole point of having control systems is to ensure
:>> :>>                                                   ^^^^^^
:>> :>>                                                   attempt
:>> :>> :> that transactions are accurately recorded in a timely fashion.

:>> :>> :>QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.

:>> :>> Or a procedure error.

:>> :>Any control system worthy of the name will ensure that daily receipts
:>> :>are posted before processing the daily sales transactions.

:>> Ah, the batch mentality.

:>Nooooo.  I said nothing about "how".  I used the words "control system".
:>That does not automatically mean "computer system" or "computer processing".

In most stores the check-out (purchase) system has a scanner and is easy to
directly interface to the ICS.

The deliveries, on the other hand, are not.

You suggested that the sales data not be recorded until the deliveries are
recorded.

Certainly sounds like batch mentality.

:>> :>> :>Any "control system" that allows material release for shipment
:>> :>> :>under the conditions you mentioned has serious logic errors.

:>> :>> How would that apply to a store?

:>> :>The environment doesn't matter.  You either have an accurate control system,
:>> :>or you do not.

:>> I fail to see a reason to stop selling just to get the paperwork in order,
:>> except period inventory checks.

:>Agreed, although I don't see where you mentioned the need for that (?).

You did.

"Any control system worthy of the name will ensure that daily receipts are
posted before processing the daily sales transactions."

:>> :>> Goods are delivered, placed on the shelf and purchased before being recorded
:>> :>> in the ICS. The sales register immediately records the sales.

:>> I wonder why you have difficulty understanding this concept.

:>The concept is basic.  No difficulty is involved, except in your mind.

Snicker.

:>> :>> I guess one could argue that the store should not place them on the shelf
:>> :>> until the paperwork is done, but the store is in the business of making money
:>> :>> and turning over the inventory ASAP.

:>> :>If the "system" decrements QOH into negative territory,
:>> :>then it is badly designed and incompetently programmed.

:>> False.

:>I (obviously) disagree.  QOH is a well-defined concept.
:>It cannot be negative.

To repeat:

True quantity on hand cannot go negative.

Recorded QOH however can go negative if the received goods were not properly
recorded or not recorded in a timely way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T21:55:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote

> > 'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.
> 
> Yes, it is.  QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.
> 
> You either have stock on the shelf, or you don't.  

The computer system doesn't work by counting the stock items on the
shelf, it works by reading the number in a computer file.

There are several reasons why the computer can record a negative for
that number, some of which are not 'logic errors'.

It may be that some systems will _enforce_ the QOH to always be
positive, and will not let, say, an order to be entered if this would
exceed the QOH.  If, however, this is only because the stock receival
procedure is held up for some trivial reason then the users will be
really pissed off at such an inflexible and authoritarian system, not
to say stupid.

The golden rule of systems (IMHO) is that regardless of what _should_
happen, it is only a tool, and it shouldn't get in the way of running
the business.

> QOH is not the same as [Quantity-on-hand less Inventory Demand],
> which can be a negative value.

That's another one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T06:34:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com...

> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> shelf,

I understand that.  That is a function of cycle count.

> it works by reading the number in a computer file.

That's a very simplistic description, but I won't argue the toss.
>
> There are several reasons why the computer can record a negative for
> that number, some of which are not 'logic errors'.

They are ALL logic errors.  You either have something, or you don't.
What does negative stock on the shelf look like?
That concept is nothing but trouble if it is allowed to occur in a "system".

> It may be that some systems will _enforce_ the QOH to always be
> positive,

No maybe.  Mine does.  Any that do not are wrong.
>
> and will not let, say, an order to be entered if this would
> exceed the QOH.

There is nothing whatsoever incorrect or improper
about allowing sales orders to be entered without
stock on hand to cover those orders.  That simply
triggers a make-or-buy decision, which in turn
triggers a shop ticket or a purchase order to a supplier.

> If, however, this is only because the stock receival
> procedure is held up for some trivial reason then the users will be
> really pissed off at such an inflexible and authoritarian system, not
> to say stupid.

The same problem exists when the computer system or network goes down.
If the system does not incorporate workaround procedures,
then it isn't a system.

> The golden rule of systems (IMHO) is that regardless of what _should_
> happen, it is only a tool, and it shouldn't get in the way of running
> the business.

What I said, only expressed differently, but we agree here.
>
> > QOH is not the same as [Quantity-on-hand less Inventory Demand],
> > which can be a negative value.
>
> That's another one.

Another what?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T11:37:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312051137.17c70160@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com> <IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote

> > There are several reasons why the computer can record a negative for
> > that number, some of which are not 'logic errors'.
> 
> They are ALL logic errors.  You either have something, or you don't.

There may be a stock on the shelf that is not in the computer system
but that is not necessarily a _LOGIC_ error.  It may be a keying
error, or a timing issue or an identification error, or any number of
other things.

The reverese of having no stock on the shelf but the computer showing
a number may also not be a _logic_ error, it may be theft of breakages
or simply put on the wrong shelf.

> No maybe.  Mine does.  Any that do not are wrong.

'Wrong' in what way ?  Given that you know nothing about those other
systems, who are you to say that they are _wrong_.

They are only numbers, they represent particular states.

> > and will not let, say, an order to be entered if this would
> > exceed the QOH.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> stock on hand to cover those orders.  

That depends entirely on the system that the business chooses to use.

> That simply
> triggers a make-or-buy decision, which in turn
> triggers a shop ticket or a purchase order to a supplier.

That may well be an excellent scheme for those businesses.  Others
wish to run their businesses differently.  For example my systems
generally warn the operator and allow the order quantity to be split
between available and back ordered, but the operator has the ability
to change the suggested quantities if the stock is _actually_
available and, for example, waiting to be processed in the receivals
area.

Triggering a PO is not a good idea, but then being at the end of the
earth here there may be lead times of months.  This means there may
already be a shipment on its way.  The business can choose to have the
whole sales order back ordered so it can be shipped at one time when
all stock available, or it can put the (free) stock into negative and
forward date it so it is not picked until the current shipment is
unpacked.

These are _different_ not wrong, and may be applicable to different
sized businesses.

> The same problem exists when the computer system or network goes down.
> If the system does not incorporate workaround procedures,
> then it isn't a system.

Exactly.  The 'workaround' is to allow the (free) stock to go
negative.

> > That's another one.
> 
> Another what?

Another quantity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T18:26:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vt28hgcriqg6a7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com> <IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:
>>There are several reasons why the computer can record a negative for
>>that number, some of which are not 'logic errors'.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> That concept is nothing but trouble if it is allowed to occur in a "system".

I hate to get into this (and I should have learned my lesson by now), 
but let's play...

I run Toys-R-Us.  We have a pre-sale on Lion King Special Edition DVDs. 
  We sell 237 copies of these before the shipment even leaves for our 
store.  When the shipment arrives, my stock people in the back unload 
the truck and pass the bill of lading on to the warehouse clerk, who 
keys a receipt of 1,000, which adds 1,000 to our QOH of this item.  Our 
QOH is now 763, some of which will be pulled from the back and put out 
on the shelf.

How did this happen?  Each of these pre-sold copies decremented our QOH 
by 1.  Before the inventory arrived in the store, we had -237.  Enter 
the inventory - we now have 763 ready to sell.

Your system wouldn't support this?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with nosign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T01:21:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<maaAb.155950$Ec1.6110352@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com> <IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vt28hgcriqg6a7@corp.supernews.com>`

```

LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message news:vt28hgcriqg6a7@corp.supernews.com...
> Hugh Candlin wrote:
> >>There are several reasons why the computer can record a negative for
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> but let's play...

I make my living making sure that Inventory, Sales Orders, Inventory Demand,
Inventory Cost, Bill of Materials, Shop Tickets, Stock Reservation. Warehouse
Locator, Warehouse Allocation and numerous other databases are in sync.
I do not in any shape, manner or form see it as play, although I do enjoy my work.
>
> I run Toys-R-Us.  We have a pre-sale on Lion King Special Edition DVDs.

A pre-sale.  I understand.

> We sell

Stop here.  Why are you treating 2 totally different procedures the same?
A pre-sale is not a sale.

You cannot ship a pre-sale, nor can you process it as a normal sales order.
There is a clue/hint there.

> 237 copies of these before the shipment even leaves for our store.

No.  You have an encumbrance against future receipts of Inventory,
which may never arrive due to any number of factors.
A huge difference in business philosophy and accounting procedure.
You are financially booking sales of product that you do not own.
People go to jail for that.

I won't even ask how you provide pre-sale reports to management.

> When the shipment arrives, my stock people in the back unload
> the truck and pass the bill of lading on to the warehouse clerk, who
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> by 1.

A terrible design.  I wish you well.  Particularly as you find yourself unable to detect
actual problems by running a scan to ensure the absence of negative QOH,
although that is a trivial matter in the larger scheme.
```

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>

- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com> <IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vt28hgcriqg6a7@corp.supernews.com> <maaAb.155950$Ec1.6110352@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <dpGdnf9jX4dd0kyiU-KYhw@comcast.com>`

```

Russell Styles <rws0202@comcast.net> wrote in message news:dpGdnf9jX4dd0kyiU-KYhw@comcast.com...
>
> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>     I don't see the problem.

Neither do I.  I was, however, asked to comment on someone elses procedure.

> Those 237 DVD's are NOT sales.

That is true.  They are open Sales Orders.  They are not Sales.
You have not yet created a shipper and/or an Invoice.

> They
> (hopefully)
> do not effect the sales totals at all until the customer buys them.

That is what is being disputed.  I stated that you cannot sell what you haven't got,
nor decrement Stock-on-Hand (QOH) that isn't on hand.  Others disagree.

> Call them
> pre-sales, call them something else.  Bottom line, these 237 customers
> are at the front of the line, should that particular product turn out to be
> hot.

Inventory Demand is what I call it, prioritized by Booking Date/ime Stamp.

>     Now what you do call them, and what you tell upper management
> is another question.

If there is a question about that, then you do not have a control system.
You have a kludge of modules purporting to be a system.

> I suppose that you could take the customer's money at time of order.

Pre-payment processing would be defined as a requirement in the FSD
and incorporated appropriately, but that isn't the issue.

> I am really not competent to comment on the legal twists of that method.
> I was assuming that no money changes hands until the merchandise changes
> hands.

Money isn't the issue either, not is money changing hands a pre-requisite
to a financial booking transaction taking place.
>
> > I won't even ask how you provide pre-sale reports to management.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > although that is a trivial matter in the larger scheme.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with nosign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>

- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com> <IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vt28hgcriqg6a7@corp.supernews.com> <maaAb.155950$Ec1.6110352@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:
> LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message news:vt28hgcriqg6a7@corp.supernews.com...
> 
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That is the siliest thing I have heard in years.  People go to jail for 
using a sign bit on an inventory control system?  Perhaps you could 
provide a reference, but I doubt it.

If you changed the above scenario to two different parameters, one 
called stock-on-hand, and one called back-orders, plus a flag to keep 
them straight, the math is exactly the same. However the code is more 
complex, and more error prone.

In my opinion, there have been far more screw ups over the years due to 
programmers demanding code based on their philosopies/egos taking 
precidence over reality than any other single factor. I'll stick to 
making the system work, thank you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with nosignnibble?)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote 

> A huge difference in business philosophy and accounting procedure.

Each has their own.

> You are financially booking sales of product that you do not own.
> People go to jail for that.

No, wrong. The 'financial' part of any sales order system is distinct
from the order processing part and will only occur when the order is
turned to an invoice upon shipment.

It is no more a jailable offence than producing a forward demand
projection.
 
> A terrible design.  I wish you well.  Particularly as you find yourself unable to detect
> actual problems by running a scan to ensure the absence of negative QOH,
> although that is a trivial matter in the larger scheme.

Sorry to bust your bubble, but having no negative QOH, is not
validation of correctness.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T18:21:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031206192231.465$HM@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com> <IFVzb.152614$Ec1.6024451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:
> Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> > There are several reasons why the computer can record a negative for
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> That concept is nothing but trouble if it is allowed to occur in a "system".

Hugh, I don't mean to offend you, but if you had spent a little time in
the consulting business dealing with actual customers, the ones writing
your check, you would never espouse such an inflexible attitude. As
Richard has alluded, it would get your butt kicked out the door fast
enough to make your head spin if you tell the owner of a company that
they CANNOT sell an item until they have entered the item received
into the computer. Just because your experience is limited in that you
have not encountered this does not mean that it doesn't work that way.
Believe me, your position is actually humerous to those of us who
have BTDT many, many times. Business is simply not as clean as we
might like to be sometimes. This is not 'logic error', it is dealing with
reality that is often messy. Remember, the tail does not wag the dog. :-)

> > It may be that some systems will _enforce_ the QOH to always be
> > positive,
>
> No maybe.  Mine does.  Any that do not are wrong.

No, you are the one who is wrong. Your system may be doing exactly
what it should be doing in your situation. But your situation does not
encompass every conceivable inventory control situation. Believe me,
it doesn't. Try to understand that.

> > and will not let, say, an order to be entered if this would
> > exceed the QOH.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> triggers a shop ticket or a purchase order to a supplier.

Right, the customer walks in, sees a $10,000 item on the shelf, says
"I want to buy that" and holds out the cash. The owner then goes to
his computer to find out that his idiot brother-in-law entered the
item incorrectly and the inventory does not show the item in stock.
Now, you are telling us you really think the owner of that store is
going to tell that customer "Well, I know that item is setting there,
but my computer does not show it in stock. You will just have to
wait until I can fire off an order to get another one you can buy."
Right. Hugh, if you think any store owner on the planet is really
going to do that, you're nuts! ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T08:41:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kf%Ab.7923$3y1.653832@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote
> 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's another one.

In my experience, even from a pure theory standpoint, designing a system 
that allows credits but not debits in *any* account causes huge 
problems.  The fact it is a "quantity-on-hand" is irrelevent.  Balancing 
the books is crucial.  If they balance, then finding the discrepancy is 
simple.  If they do not, it is a bitch. Double entry is not new technology.

If you cannot have an over drawn inventory total, then why should you 
allow have a negative number in a bank balance? Both make equal "sense".

Donald.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T09:45:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br22qa$sss$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0312042155.5f42669e@posting.google.com> <Kf%Ab.7923$3y1.653832@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
In article <Kf%Ab.7923$3y1.653832@news20.bellglobal.com>,
Donald Tees  <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>In my experience, even from a pure theory standpoint, designing a system 
>that allows credits but not debits in *any* account causes huge 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>allow have a negative number in a bank balance? Both make equal "sense".

Bingo... I was wondering where to chime in on this one and this seems to
be the entry-point.  Time I spent coding a back-office trading system for
the Deutsche Bank taught me that just about any numeric field used for
calculations (with the exception, maybe, of dates) should allow for
negatives... any trade, at any time, must allow for adjustment, reversal,
back-dating, etc.

(Note that the assumption 'within the limits of applicable laws' applies.)

What is done with a field that 'shouldn't go negative is a matter for 
system reporting design... but it would be difficult, to say the least, to 
get the desired warning from checking IF QTY-ON-HAND < REORDER-POINT if 
QTY-ON-HAND is defined as PIC 99 instead of S99.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T17:15:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031206181642.161$RH@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com> <PTKzb.149980$Ec1.5958306@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:
> Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> which can be a negative value.


Technically, you are correct. However, business systems are rarely so
clean. You must allow for human errors in the system, such as failing
to input a received shipment. If that is done, then when you ship those
items out, you could easily run a negative QOH, or lose the amount of
inventory involved altogether (if you don't keep the negative quantity
you don't know how short you really are). How 'tight' you can make
such systems depends on factors like how the organization functions
and just how strict the people who run the show want the computer to
be. Some users would insist you should never ship items if the QOH
does go negative, but others would say "Why hold up an important
shipment to a customer because we screwed up on data entry? We'll
deal with that later."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 7)_

```
On Sat, 6 Dec 2003 17:15:41 -0600 "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
wrote:

:>"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:
:>> Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

:>> > 'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.

:>> Yes, it is.  QOH cannot go below zero unless you have a logic error.
:>> You either have stock on the shelf, or you don't.  It isn't like a bank account,
:>> where you can sign up for overdraft protection.

:>> QOH is not the same as [Quantity-on-hand less Inventory Demand],
:>> which can be a negative value.

:>Technically, you are correct. However, business systems are rarely so
:>clean. You must allow for human errors in the system, such as failing
:>to input a received shipment. If that is done, then when you ship those
:>items out, you could easily run a negative QOH, or lose the amount of
:>inventory involved altogether (if you don't keep the negative quantity
:>you don't know how short you really are). How 'tight' you can make
:>such systems depends on factors like how the organization functions
:>and just how strict the people who run the show want the computer to
:>be. Some users would insist you should never ship items if the QOH
:>does go negative, but others would say "Why hold up an important
:>shipment to a customer because we screwed up on data entry? We'll
:>deal with that later."

That is the major point that some have difficulty comprehending.

The point of the business is to sell its product, not to make sure that the
inventory figures are correct at all times.

Software that refuses to allow sales because it thinks the numbers are out of
wack is bad software. 

The software should warn, but must allow an override.
```

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> The software should warn, but must allow an override.

Correct. Even in situations where it would seem the computer should
never be overridden, such as medical equipment or flight control systems,
you have to allow for machine malfunction, human error, and situations
beyond design foresight. None of us are omniscient. It's better that we
recognize this from the start than be grossly embarrassed by it later. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The quibble over nibbles (was Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T19:13:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCF84D7.BF12BD94@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0312012013.2802b2f5@posting.google.com> <20031202140101.07934.00000015@mb-m16.aol.com> <217e491a.0312041002.7a76bab6@posting.google.com>`

```


Richard wrote:

>
> 'Stock on hand' is not a quantity that is always positive.  However
> that does not mean that there are not quantitities that are invalid if
> negative.

The *joys* of inventory control in Fashions and Fashion accessories.

(1) :
Manageress : phones Central Buying in Wigmore Street, near Selfridges.
"Hello Mr. Trimble. Why do you keep sending us Blue raincoats ?".

Trimble : "Because that's your best seller".

Manageress : "No its not we are selling more of the Bright Yellow. Here are the
figures.......  The Blue isn't moving at the same rate".

Trimble : "Oh my gawd ! Here we are trying to save money, getting the Manufacturer to
ticket (Kimball Tag) the items. They've obviously mixed up the colour codes !".

(2)

New system going in to stock count wool (knitting yarns). Yours truly keeps a
watching brief as system starts. One store tells us they have 1,902 of X...., 1,903
of Y.... etc. Jeez ! They have enough stock for the whole group ! I phone.

JJG : "Are you sure about the quantities you have of 1,902, 1,903 etc....".

Lady : (Swear she had to be a 105 !). "Well dear. I've only been working here for a
month and those are the numbers shown on the labels".

JJG : (Gently). "You have the descriptions and *our* codes printed on your stock
count sheets. It's not the manufacturer's  numbers we are after but the number of
balls of wool you have on the shelves/displays, by make and colour".

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T13:13:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hCkAb.158103$Ec1.6169716@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <R5udnSJM4tc8p1aiU-KYkQ@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:R5udnSJM4tc8p1aiU-KYkQ@giganews.com...
| newsgroup sponsor wrote:
| > Received a download file from an MVS system. Have a field that
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
| Huh? How could a system record numbers for further use without a sign?

This is NOT a good idea.

Could be manipulation.
You can get one extra digit, by shifting everything right.

You take the number, multiply by 10.
Drop the trailing 0, and sign on the output.

i.e.

x'0123456F'
x'1234560F'
output:
x'123456'
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-12-01T21:49:46+00:00

- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>`

```
I came across packed decimal data where the sign nibble was dropped and 
the numerics shuffled half a byte to fill the space at Cambridgeshire 
County Council in 1982. We called it Elbradecimal after the analyst who 
came up with the idea in order to save a byte of filespace. It was not a 
standard IBM data type and we had to code through hoops to get around 
it.

In message <8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>, newsgroup sponsor 
<admin@bpm.no-ip.net> writes
>Received a download file from an MVS system. Have a field that appears to be
>packed decimal, but with no sign nibble. I understand that this is a valid
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Wasn't this format simply called BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) to
distinguish it from IBM packed-decimal?  At least that's what I
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** "Martin Jones" <martindjonesNOT@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T09:09:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QEYyb.36180$aT.34342@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <xWcJcSB6d7y$EwDF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
I have also seen this format in 1980 under IBM DOS/VSE. We called this
format 'CRAMMED' and used it extensively for compression of dates and
all sorts of numeric codes. Site standards dictated that all display numeric
fields be an even number of digits. They were then CRAMMED to half their
size for storage on disk. ASM subroutines 'CRAM' and 'UNCRAM' were
provided to convert between, say, X'F8F0F1F0F1F3' to/from X'801013'
which is how we held our dates in the pre Y2K conscious era. The subroutines
took source-field, target field and field-length as arguments.

Of course this was not a native IBM format and all programmers knew this.
(Some programmers preferred to handle conversion themselves using
packed fields and suitable redefinitions with the appropriate divide or
multiply
by 10 to allign the numerics correctly).

We also had crude record level compression on our variable length VSAM
files. All data beyond fixed length keys  was scanned and strings of up to
9 chars containing spaces or low-values would be replaced by a single byte
of the
format X'xn' where x=char to be replaced and n=2 - 9 indicating the number
of positions to be substituted. Only spaces and low-values were treated this
way, (think of the leading zeros in packed amounts). From memory there
weren't any binary numeric fields in the record layouts. The value of 'x'
above
was obviously chosen so as not to conflict with the values of high-order
nibbles used for alpha characters. We used to achieve up to 25% reduction
on some of our larger records with lots of Name and address fields etc.

All of this processing would have been put in place in the early seventies
when disk storage was genuinely expensive. (I think my current desktop has
around 20 times the storage that IBM site had in total.)


"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:xWcJcSB6d7y$EwDF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> I came across packed decimal data where the sign nibble was dropped and
> the numerics shuffled half a byte to fill the space at Cambridgeshire
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Received a download file from an MVS system. Have a field that appears to
be
> >packed decimal, but with no sign nibble. I understand that this is a
valid
> >MVS data type, but I can't figure out what COBOL data type would produce
> >this field.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>             Don't you need any IT support?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T06:44:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031202074525.298$YC@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Have you ever wondered why the original IBM 360 series was designed
to always require a sign in the first place? Perhaps to sell more memory?
There have always been unsigned (implicitly positive) numeric fields,
such as dates. I've always considered that a peculiar design 'quirk'. Other
mainframe architectures provided unsigned numeric fields.
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSpam@NoMoSpam.ORG>
- **Date:** 2003-12-01T17:04:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3_ydnU_wuI-aVFaiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>`

```
Soooo.... you mean a decimal value of +1234 looks
like x'1234' instead of x'01234C'? Micro-Focus
supports this as COMP-6 I believe, but not IBM...
at least not directly. I would be easy enough to
manipulate tho. Take a comp-3 field, multiply by 10,
redefine, truncate the last byte, move the rest to
the file in a pic x field. You'd have to do the
reverse of course to read the field.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T06:13:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCC2E5D.C91ABECA@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <3_ydnU_wuI-aVFaiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```
Ron wrote:
> 
> Soooo.... you mean a decimal value of +1234 looks
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> reverse of course to read the field.

01  BCD-WORK-AREA.
    05  IBM-COMP-3-FOUR-DIGITS      PIC 9(4)V9   COMP-3 VALUE ZERO.
    05  BCD-EXTRACT                 REDEFINES IBM-COMP-3-FOUR-DIGITS.
        10  BCD-FOUR-DIGITS         PIC X(02).
        10  FILLER                  PIC X. 

Move any four-digit integer to IBM-COMP-3-FOUR-DIGITS, its BCD
equivalent is in BCD-FOUR-DIGITS.

To convert two BCD bytes to IBM numeric format first move zero to
IBM-COMP-3-FOUR-DIGITS, then load BCD-FOUR-DIGITS.  If
IBM-COMP-3-FOUR-DIGITS is numeric (you don't want a S0C7 abend!), then
you have successfully converted it from BCD.  Move it to some integer
field. 

With a little work, this logic could be generalized to handle BCD
fields of multiple lengths, with no multiplying or dividing.
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-12-01T19:59:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0312011959.529213b8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>`

```
You say this data was downloaded from an MVS system, implying that mvs
generated this data. As far as I know a "signless" packed decimal is
not a valid data type in MVS and related systems. Is each field length
a multiple of two? Scan the recs and check for non-numeric characters
in these fields.

If so, you might have binary (COMP, COMP-4, COMP-5), not packed
(COMP-3) fields. So, if you have a field that contains leading zero
nibbles, and ends with a "1" nibble and a "5" nibble, the value might
not be "fifteen", but "twenty one" (16+5).

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
I converted programs from IBM DOS to IBM OS.   One thing we did when reading old
files was to move unsigned numbers to signed numbers and back before comparing
them to new data.    It seems that IBM changed how it stored unsigned data.   
This was over 20 years ago.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T08:03:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031204090444.778$of@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <8a2d426e.0312011959.529213b8@posting.google.com> <bql3cm$li7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> I converted programs from IBM DOS to IBM OS.   One thing we did when reading old
> files was to move unsigned numbers to signed numbers and back before comparing
> them to new data.    It seems that IBM changed how it stored unsigned data.
> This was over 20 years ago.

That's because the IBM PC used an Intel CPU. The IBM System/360 family
and it's descendents and clones are the only CPUs I know of that cannot store
a number without a sign. It may seem strange to IBM-only folks, but the rest
of the computer industry has always known that IBM has always designed
somewhat inefficient computer architectures, both hardware and software.
I'm not IBM bashing, it's the honest truth. I'm sure most of the people here
who have had extensive IBM and non-IBM experience will agree with me.
It has always been incredible to me that IBM mainframe users seem content
to jump through so many hoops that other vendors do not require. Just one
typical example: Way back in the early 70's a knowledgeable person could
walk into any Burroughs mainframe shop with no online configuration at
8AM, find a reasonably small (say 5 lines & 30 stations) configuration on
their desk, and have the system software up and running by noon, ready to
run online applications, without interrupting batch processing to do it. Do
any of you remember what it took back then to do that on 'IBM iron'? :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** arnb <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T08:53:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqkptt$235q69$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>`

```
On or about 12/1/2003 7:52 AM, it came to pass that newsgroup sponsor wrote:
> Received a download file from an MVS system. Have a field that appears to be
> packed decimal, but with no sign nibble. I understand that this is a valid
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
MVS normally won't create this but can be easily made to work with it. If your 
field is 4 bytes of packed data with no sign, do the following

   in file definition
      03  file-field     pic x(4).

  in working storage

     	.
     03 worked-packed     pic s9(8)v9 usage comp-3 value zero.
     03 worked-packedx redefines work-packed   pic x(5).
     03 the-number                             pic s9(9) comp-3.


In procedure division
to make the data useable
      move file-field to worked-packedx (1:4)
*    manipulate work-packed as needed for example to drop the sign
      move worked-packed   to the-number

if you want to generate such a field
      move 234234 to worked-packed
      move worked-packedx to file-field
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** Jim Reinhart <jreinhart1@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T15:14:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BBF4FFC4.2228%jreinhart1@cox.net>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>`

```
On 12/1/03 5:52 AM, in article 8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com,
"newsgroup sponsor" <admin@bpm.no-ip.net> wrote:

> Received a download file from an MVS system. Have a field that appears to be
> packed decimal, but with no sign nibble. I understand that this is a valid
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

MVS COBOL from ANS to zOS

05  ABC  PIC 9 COMP-3  VALUE +1. This is an unsigned packed decimal number
 the internal representation would be X'1F'
MOVE -1 TO ABC.
  the internal representation would be X'1F' regardless of positive or
negative.

If it was signed, the statement would read
05  ABC  PIC S9 COMP-3  VALUE +1.
  the internal representation would be X'1C' for positive
MOVE -1 TO ABC.
  the internal representation would be X'1D' for negative

Everything that is needed to know is in the IBM redbooks for Zone Decimal
(DISPLAY), PACKED-DECIMAL (COMP-3), BINARY (COMP) and FLOAT (COMP-1,
COMP-2).
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T09:36:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-1CD3A1.09365511122003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>`

```
In article <8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com>,
 "newsgroup sponsor" <admin@bpm.no-ip.net> wrote:

> Received a download file from an MVS system. Have a field that appears to be
> packed decimal, but with no sign nibble. I understand that this is a valid
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is a rather ugly varient sometimes called "packed-nosign" (as 
opposed to the normal packed-signed).

This is not a 'valid' type in the sense that you can use native S/390 
opcodes on such data without an exception -- but it is quite easy to 
adapt to.

From cobol, you would do something like this (assume a 3-byte nosign 
example):

   10   PN-2-PS.
      15  PN-2-PS-Signed    Pic S9(6)V9 
                            Packed-Decimal
                            Value +0.
      15  PN-2-PS-Nosign    redefines PN-2-PS-Signed
                            Pic X(3).

....

   Move my-input-pn-field to PN-2-PS-Nosign
   Move PN-2-PS-Signed    to where-you-want-your-data
      
This is a bad idea whose time has long since gone.  I thought we cleaned 
all of that up as part of the y2k mess.  Anyway, your vendor needs to 
get with the times (he said, fearful that the file might have come from 
his own shop) and use the standard notation.
```

```
Which S/390 opcodes cannot manipulate, look at, or change a packed
unsigned field?

Assuming FLDA is a packed unsigned field and FLDB is anthing as big, I
most certainly can do:

                        MVC         FLDA,FLDB

with great success.  The machine code for MVC is D5 and S/390 would
execute that instruction just fine.  There are others, but you get my
drift.

>example):
```

```
data were packed unsigned.  An UNPK will work just fine on an unsigned
packed decimal and not cause an abend.
```

```
Steve Wiegand writes ...

[snip]

>First I said MVC was a D5 op code.  It is a D2..sorry about the typo.
>Secondly, not all Packed Decimal opcodes would cause a SOC7 if the
>data were packed unsigned.  An UNPK will work just fine on an unsigned
>packed decimal and not cause an abend

Actually, PACK and UNPK are part of the general instruction set, not the
decimal instruction set, and neither will S0C7 you regardless of the data in
the operands.

And to this list we can add the new PACK ASCII (PKA), PACK UNICODE (PKU),
UNPACK ASCII (UNPKA), and UNPACK UNICODE (UNPKU); but that's a different
course, more money.


Kind regards,



-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
- Acucorp's Acucobol and Extend versions (UNIX versions, VMS versions,
  Open VMS versions 
  as well as Windows Versions)
- Liant RM COBOL (UNIX and Windows versions)
- Fujitsu COBOL (Linux version and all Windows versions)
- IBM Visual Age COBOL
- Egan Systems ICOBOL (UNIX and Windows versions)
- mbp Visual COBOL (UNIX and Windows versions)
- VAX COBOL (VAX/VMS and Open VMS)
- KOBOL (Linux and Windows versions)
```
