- **References:** `<PEmxb.341930$0v4.18881380@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FC6405E.4A3BAC78@shaw.ca> <Qp0yb.354853$0v4.19091296@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FC90460.C1397CD@shaw.ca> <Zmkyb.360935$0v4.19207337@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FCA4C7B.2E849F06@shaw.ca>`

```
On Sun, 30 Nov 2003 20:12:10 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>I should stress - I believe the Norcomm and Flexus products handle all the PC
>compilers you are likely to be interested in.
---------------
Jimmy,

You're correct, Norcom's GUI ScreenIO supports all of the 32-bit COBOL
compilers under Windows.  

We don't even need to get involved when new compilers appear because
you call our runtime interface using the standard Windows system
linkage conventions.  Therefore, any 32-bit COBOL compiler that can
call a Windows API can use GUI ScreenIO.  You just modify one little
COBOL pass-through routine (about 10 lines in entirety) and you're off
to the races.

Regards,
Kevin

Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

```
Thanks, again!

I've been busy putting out a production fire (luckily it ain't my fault,
ain't even my part of the system.)

I'll have a bit more time to look into a few more options for the Work-Flow
component.

I originally proposed changes to the current method ,Option A, but Option B
was already on the table, and I was told to proceed on Option B which
required the Work-Flow component. (Luckily, I got the Option A algorithm
incorporated into Option B.)

Using current production data I modeled (produced listings showing the
Work-Flow) the Current system, Option A, and Option B. (I wasn't supposed to
model Option A, but it's only a listing, I'm rather hard-headed, and they
humor me from time to time.)

The Option A results showed a significant improvement over the current
system, and the decision was made to implement Option A ASAP, and phase in
Option B. In the end, Option A will be used for 70% of the work, and Option
B will handle the remaining 30%.

More below.

'honeymoon'

firmly
| established they have somewhat changed their tune.

| - Micro Focus - like F/J there is 'paid support' but also there is the
chat
| group Answer Exchange. Downside - runtime royalty fees. But that again
shouldn't
| worry you from a big iron installation, and apart from which, if your
| applications are in-house then no fee is involved.

They were using M/F where I'm at, actually they were trying to use it.

I've seen this at a few places.
They want to do Mainframe development on the PC, but they don't build an
infrastructure to support it, people get frustrated, and then don't use it.

The main problem is that systems have a number of Indexed (VSAM) files,
and/or RDB Tables, and they have to be "in sync".
You usually need a test system tailored to a test plan <G>.

It's so simple.
Put all your test data on a single file.
You need a front end to select and consolidate the data, and a back end to
distribute the data.
It ain't Rocket Science.
It beats the hell out of trying to keep track of which versions of 15 files
have to be used to run a test, and it simplifies the download process.

I know, because I've done it.
I also had a compare system built into it.
Select test data, Load the test system, Run the test, put the test results
on a single file, Run a system compare.
The QA group took it over.
The compare programs were generated from the Copy Books, so they took the
compare program generator also.
The first run uncovered an unknown bug.


| Jimmy, Calgary AB
|
```

```
> Pete.

Yep, we're using Enterprise.

No, GEM won't support versioning - at least it won't incorporate the version
number in the PPJ, it won't support deltas, rollback, etc.

I believe GEM will work with NETCOBOL inasmuch as NETCOBOL is normal source
code. But if so, why use GEM when SourceSafe could do a better job?

We don't use NC because we've got too much invested in PC. If FJ ever makes
a PowerCOBOL to NETCOBOL conversion routine, then, maybe....
```

```
On 26-Nov-2003, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> "Standard" = Monopoly by he who sets the standard.

What's bad is when we have a de jure standard and a de facto standard which
don't agree with each other.   For instance Opera has chosen to follow the de
jure standard, but most people write web pages to work with IE, which does not
follow that standard.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message

> On 26-Nov-2003, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's bad is when we have a de jure standard and a de facto standard
which
> don't agree with each other.   For instance Opera has chosen to follow the
de
> jure standard, but most people write web pages to work with IE, which does
not
> follow that standard.

...or if you have an awesome product that fills a hole in a library (Logging
for example)....and then a standard comes along (Java Logging API) that
doesn't follow your particular model and...well....you have to change to be
acceptable....

or

you turn around and the standard (API) changes every time you look around
and the once backward compatible promise is no more...

JCE
```

<a id="t-1703e9f8c6"></a>
## Thread: OT 50 States was: OT: Hemlines — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: OT 50 States was: OT: Hemlines

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-01T10:15:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031201111603.341$cT@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com> <8HBxb.12665$Cv6.4489@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <20031128074105.052$5p@news.newsreader.com> <3fc7d7d4_9@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Redondo, or Long Beach, or Malibu...<G>

Yeah, but I'll bet you SAW those rocks, even if you didn't trip over 'em! :-)
I did stubb my toe at Long Beach once, and almost got a stone bruise on
another rock. Don't remember which beach that was, but in the L.A. area.

> > I'm from Alabama.
>
> Always wanted to visit Alabama; haven't yet, but hope to change that.

Most of the people from other parts of the country who have never visited
the southern states are pleasantly surprised. One of the big surprises is
how friendly most of the people are here, especially in the less urban
areas. There is an honest openness here that is not as prevalent in other
parts of the country. It's also not 'backward' as people seem to believe
who know the south only through movies and TV. If you have ever heard
of the book "A Walk Across America", there is a chapter called "TAA
(Totally Amazed by Alabama)". The author lived in Maryland, I believe,
and had never visited the south.

> > From midway south, Alabama is mostly flat, pine
> > trees abound. North of midway, the terrain varies from plains and low
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is that the same as a "boll" weevil?

Yeah, and I've picked lots of cotton too (grew up on a farm), so I should
have caught that. :-)

> >There is a lot of industry in the Birmingham area (historically
> > second only to Pittsburgh in steel production), and lots of high-tech
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Shades of "Gone with the Wind"?)

Not quite everything in the south was destroyed in the Civil War,
though the Union forces tried hard enough, during and after the war.

Ironic aside: Many people don't know that the Civil War was not really
about slavery, it was about states rights. The southern states proposed
legislation before the Civil War to abolish slavery over several years,
but the northern states voted it down. In 1860 the state of Virginia came
within one vote of abolishing slavery there. A government program of
cash incentives to release slaves, progressively increasing taxes upon
slave owners, and a deadline after which slaves would be legally freed
could have freed all the slaves within the same time frame as the Civil
War. A lot of people both black and white lost there lives in that stupid
war that could easily have been avoided. The framers of the United
States Constitution worked very hard to create a relatively weak central
government, with most of the power at the state level, much closer to
the people. The Civil War was the first major break with that, and now
we are burdened with a huge, inefficient central government that is
constantly trying to 'make one shoe fit all'. I can't help but wonder how
different the U.S. and the world would be had the abolition of slavery
been handled with wisdom and compassion instead of a hot headed
"fix it right now and damn the cost" attitude.
```

```

On  1-Dec-2003, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> Yeah, on the farm we used to struggle over how to describe people
> who hoe cotton/corn a lot. :-)

I grew up having absolutely no idea that the word "whore" was ever pronounced
differently than it was spelled.   Interesting enough

http://www.m-w.com/cgi-bin/dictionary?book=Dictionary&va=whore

doesn't give the "hoe" pronunciation as an option.

Dictionaries often are very slow at including dialects - even when those
dialects become mainstream.

Note:  I read where police in Colorado are told to spell phonetically when
transcribing what people tell them.    It ends up that they do this when someone
speaks with a black dialect but since white dialects are "normal" they don't do
so with a local white accent.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> On  1-Dec-2003, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't give the "hoe" pronunciation as an option.


I don't understand what you mean, Howard. The word 'hoe' is pronounced
'HO' (silent 'e'). A person who 'hoes' would be a 'hoer', and I would expect
that to be pronounced 'HOer', or 'HOr' (silent 'e'). The first pronounciation
given at the link above is 'HOr', and if you click the first sound button, the
pronounciation is 'HOr', precisely as I would expect.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<bql47m$pfo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com> <vsfup5croun392@corp.supernews.com> <3fc808e7_1@news.athenanews.com> <20031201112139.358$OD@news.newsreader.com> <bqg0vf$s9m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20031202070547.485$pW@news.newsreader.com>`

```
On  2-Dec-2003, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> > I grew up having absolutely no idea that the word "whore" was ever
> > pronounced
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> pronounciation is 'HOr', precisely as I would expect.

The verb is pronounced the same as the noun.   The noun is the primary word.   I
grew up hearing it pronounced "whore".   In modern movies I have heard people
pronounce it "hoe".   Silent "r".   This is not what that link gives, but it is
what is in modern movies.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT 50 States was: OT: Hemlines

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T10:21:55+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd0f735_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-A92A7D.10320228112003@corp.supernews.com> <3fc7daa7_2@news.athenanews.com> <20031201113019.107$Di@news.newsreader.com> <3fcbafe2_9@news.athenanews.com> <VgQgRBENt8z$Ew8V@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3kd0tvs70r1apok1ss1rm8oss3ebi0tfup@4ax.com>`

```

"Habitant" <berlutte@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:3kd0tvs70r1apok1ss1rm8oss3ebi0tfup@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 00:03:25 +0000, Alistair Maclean
> <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Because their jailers' deeds were even worse but whitewashed?

Yes, there is certainly truth in that. Some of the old penal colonies have
been restored, and can be toured. At least one friend in Melbourne told me
she was severely disturbed after such a tour and had nightmares for some
time. The punishments far outweighed the crimes and were harsh beyond
belief. Justice was a commodity in very short supply and was replaced by
brutality and Sadism. Control by fear was the order of the day. Despite this
there were escape attempts (a very few of which succeeded...) If you got
away you were faced by a hostile wilderness... It was not a pleasant chapter
in Australia's history and it is little wonder that less than 200 years
later, there is still sensitivity to it.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT 50 States was: OT: Hemlines

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<20031207123502.353$85@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-A92A7D.10320228112003@corp.supernews.com> <3fc7daa7_2@news.athenanews.com> <20031201113019.107$Di@news.newsreader.com> <3fcbafe2_9@news.athenanews.com> <VgQgRBENt8z$Ew8V@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3kd0tvs70r1apok1ss1rm8oss3ebi0tfup@4ax.com> <3fd0f735_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> Yes, there is certainly truth in that. Some of the old penal colonies have
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> later, there is still sensitivity to it.

Please excuse the brief OT discussion below. I don't want to start a
religious debate, only to clarify a very common misconception. What
Pete has written above makes an excellent point on what is really meant
by the expression "An eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth" in the Bible.
This is probably the most misunderstood and wrongly applied verse in
the Bible. It's easy to verify by looking them up in a concordance and
reading the context, that in every single use, this verse applies entirely
to judicial punishment, and never once does it apply to personal revenge.
In other words, don't execute a person for stealing bread, and don't slap
them on the wrist for murder; the punishment should be in proportion to
the crime itself. It is another way of saying "let the punishment meet the
crime," a foundation principle of all modern Western law. Had it been
applied, the atrocities Peter lists above would not have occurred.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT 50 States was: OT: Hemlines

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T07:18:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031208081928.144$dh@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-A92A7D.10320228112003@corp.supernews.com> <3fc7daa7_2@news.athenanews.com> <20031201113019.107$Di@news.newsreader.com> <3fcbafe2_9@news.athenanews.com> <VgQgRBENt8z$Ew8V@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3kd0tvs70r1apok1ss1rm8oss3ebi0tfup@4ax.com> <3fd0f735_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031207123502.353$85@news.newsreader.com> <3fd3a7db_9@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > Please excuse the brief OT discussion below. I don't want to start a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> And these were all (both Prisoners and Wardens) good Christian people...

They considered, or at least claimed, themselves to be that, true enough. But
neither Christ Himself nor the principles written in the Bible would support
this position. It was a time when many common people were forbidden by
their religious leaders to read the Bible for themselves. John Tyndale, who
made the first English translation of the Bible, was burned at the stake for
it. Amazing that God would prepare a book for mankind, then forbid them
from reading it, no? Many atrocities have been done by people claiming
the name of Christian, but who didn't have the foggiest idea what Christ
really taught, and lived anything but. When asked what the most important
commandment was, Jesus said there were two, loving God and loving your
neighbor "as yourself", and that on these two principles were based "all the
law and the prophets." It would be difficult indeed to find in these two
principles any justification for any atrocities. :-)  Besides, if God does
exist, and if Jesus is God's Son, and if He did come to earth to die to pay
the penalty for man's sins (as my SIG, John 3:16, says), it is inconceivable
that such a God would condone atrocities inflicted on those He loved that
much. Just doesn't make sense. :-)
```

```
> >> >World...

> >> from a person who has committed a crime is pretty low. So why are the

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

None of the ones I know, do. However, when there are no strangers present, I
am sure that some Australians do this.

> That's the way I heard it and reported as much in a different posting.
>
> >They 'start a fist fight' because they are touchy about
> >being descended from the English, and dislike New Zealanders (this
> >part of the world) making _any_ comments at all.

I disagree, but I don't think it is important enough to pursue it here.

I visit Australia regularly, have a broad cross section of friends there,
have spent many hours in Aussie pubs, drinking with and talking to the
locals. When/if you have done the same and come away with a different
viewpoint all I can say is our experience differs.

The convict heritage of Australia is a subject "best avoided" when drinking
as a "new chum" in Aussie pubs. Discarding this advice may lead to fist
fighting. That is all I have ever said about it and I stand by it.

>
> In the words of S. Clay Wilson's Checkered Demon: '... or maybe they just
> like to fight.'
>

Undoubtedly, some do. When they do, I'd rather have them on my side than be
opposing them...

Pete.
```

<a id="t-78a33df91f"></a>
## Thread: <ad>new course, COBOL and XML [mainframe] — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### <ad>new course, COBOL and XML [mainframe]

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-12-01T16:34:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031201113446.03434.00001307@mb-m29.aol.com>`

```
Just a short announcement of a new course.

Our new course, Enterprise COBOL: Unicode
and XML Support is available now.

When installations are looking to integrate
existing COBOL-based applications and new
Internet / Web-based applications, one of
the fairly new tools to do this is: COBOL.

Hard to believe. But z/OS mainframe COBOL
now supports processing XML documents, such
as those generated by web-based transactions.

In this short (1 day), intensive course, we
teach experienced COBOL programmers how to
handle data in Unicode (the encoding scheme
of HTML, XHTML, XML, Web services, and more)
and how to use the IBM high speed XML
parser.

Clear lectures and six hands on labs help
your people pick up critical skills quickly.

This new course is described in detail on
our website, at:

I hope you find this useful, interesting, 
helpful, and not too intrusive.
```

<a id="t-1afbe92053"></a>
## Thread: ALPHANUMERIC tests — 20 msgs

### ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T14:32:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
I recently encountered a problem with a program which performs edits
on data fields when an edit which tests whether the first digit of a name
is ALPHABETIC encountered a name which began with a digit. After doing
some research and not seeing an ALPHANUMERIC class test, I endeavored
to create an ALPHANUMBER class using a SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph but got
a fatal compile error, apparently because ALPHNUMERIC is a reserved word.
I can't seem to find any indication of an ALPHANUMERIC class test, but
am curious as to what's going on. 

Oh yeah, I side-stepped the problem by using 
SPECIAL-NAMES. CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS 'A' THRU '9'. 
but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.
```

#### ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T10:17:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031202111832.359$DK@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote:
>
> Oh yeah, I side-stepped the problem by using
> SPECIAL-NAMES. CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS 'A' THRU '9'.
> but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.

Though the ordinal number of  '9' is greater than 'A' in EBCDIC, this is
not true in ASCII. And even in EBCDIC there are more characters that
come between 'A' and '9' than just the letters and numbers (specifically,
hex 'CA' through 'CF', 'DA' through 'DF' and 'EA' through 'EF'). Also,
what about lower case? Some names begin with lower case letters, and
all lower case letters collate outside your range, in both EBCDIC and
ASCII.  I.E. your solution is neither complete nor general.

You could do it like this:

    SPECIAL-NAMES.
        CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS
            'a' THRU 'i'
            'j' THRU 'r'
            's' THRU 'z'
            'A' THRU 'I'
            'J' THRU 'R'
            'S' THRU 'Z'
            '0' THRU '9'.

The above is complete, and would work in both EBCDIC and ASCII
(though ASCII would not require that the alphabet be divided in this
way). But it would still not be general for every possible collating
sequence. That would require that you list every single character,
like this:

    SPECIAL-NAMES.
        CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS
            'a' 'b' 'c' ... 'y' 'z'
            'A' 'B' 'C' ... 'Y' 'Z'
            '0' '1' '2' ... '8' '9'.

The above is complete and general but, depending on how the compiler
implemented it, could potentially be VERY inefficient.

A simple complete and general solution is:

    IF (Name(1:1) IS NOT ALPHABETIC)
         AND
        (Name(1:1) IS NOT NUMERIC)
          <error>

It may not have the 'elegance' of the special names class solution, but
it has the advantage of being immediately obvious. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T05:05:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0aezb.143418$Ec1.5782513@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<20031202111832.359$DK@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com> <20031202111832.359$DK@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> (snip) 
> You could do it like this:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> it has the advantage of being immediately obvious. :-)

You might want to be careful with this.  The ALPHABETIC class includes 
spaces, while the SPECIAL-NAMES class ALPHA-NUMERIC (as defined) does 
not include spaces.

Does the program require that that first character of the field be 
alphabetic and non-blank?  Or aphabetic or numeric and non-blank?
```

```
On Tue, 2 Dec 2003 14:32:31 +0000 (UTC) weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote:

:>I recently encountered a problem with a program which performs edits
:>on data fields when an edit which tests whether the first digit of a name
:>is ALPHABETIC encountered a name which began with a digit. After doing
:>some research and not seeing an ALPHANUMERIC class test, I endeavored
:>to create an ALPHANUMBER class using a SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph but got
:>a fatal compile error, apparently because ALPHNUMERIC is a reserved word.
:>I can't seem to find any indication of an ALPHANUMERIC class test, but
:>am curious as to what's going on. 

Not aware of it being a reserved word.

What was the fatal error?

:>Oh yeah, I side-stepped the problem by using 
:>SPECIAL-NAMES. CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS 'A' THRU '9'. 
:>but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.

Don't know which character set you are using, but in ASCII '9' is before 'A'
and in EBCDIC there are symbols between 'A' and '9'.

You might wish to be more specific.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T18:45:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqimkv$1dd$1@news.utelfla.com>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com> <l2hpsv08v3ea6q2sr7gmfjut73v50vv2h0@4ax.com>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

: Not aware of it being a reserved word.
: What was the fatal error?

IGYDS1362-S "ALPHANUMERIC" was found in the "CLASS" clause. The clause was discarded.

I subsequently discovered "Alphanumeric" is listed as a COBOL reserved word in
this documentation, but was unable to find anything in the index. Weird, eh?

: :>Oh yeah, I side-stepped the problem by using 
: :>SPECIAL-NAMES. CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS 'A' THRU '9'. 
: :>but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.
:
: Don't know which character set you are using, but in ASCII '9' is before 'A'
: and in EBCDIC there are symbols between 'A' and '9'.
:
: You might wish to be more specific.

Well, I didn't want to bog things down with details, but this program reads
an ASCII input file and I use the COBOL function UPPER-CASE to get rid of
anything that might be lower-case. I always have trouble remembering if
letters go before or after letters, but it seems to work fine.

I sppse testing for NOT ALPHABETIC AND NOT NUMERIC would do the trick too. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T19:06:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vo5zb.8385$aw2.3698767@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com> <l2hpsv08v3ea6q2sr7gmfjut73v50vv2h0@4ax.com> <bqimkv$1dd$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:bqimkv$1dd$1@news.utelfla.com...
>
> I sppse testing for NOT ALPHABETIC AND NOT NUMERIC would do the trick too.
:-)

"123ABC" is NOT ALPHABETIC and NOT NUMERIC, but it is ALPHA-NUMERIC

"123?!@#$%^&*ABC" is also not alphabetic and not numeric, but is not
ALPHA-NUMERIC.

A "CLASS" definition is required to make the test requested

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T05:17:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6mezb.25471$sb4.24591@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com> <l2hpsv08v3ea6q2sr7gmfjut73v50vv2h0@4ax.com> <bqimkv$1dd$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
"Alphanumeric" is a reserved word as the INITIALIZE statement allows one to
initialize ONLY items of "class alphanumeric" to a specified value.

As the IGY message means that this was an IBM compiler, see:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/6.2.20
```

```
JerryMouse writes ...

[snip]>Why can't a name begin with a special character?
>
>@home
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A number of years ago I met an instructor from SanFrancisco who had his name
legally changed to 3. (Actually, it was probably III (Roman numeral 3) since
the story was his name was such-and-such the third and he figured the only part
of his name that was truly his was the III.) Quite a few computer systems
weren't up to the task then, not sure how many systems could handle it even
today.

Kind regards,



-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716

256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T06:55:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031203075622.378$gl@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<74ydnXKq78m0nlCiU-KYkA@giganews.com> <20031202174537.08030.00000042@mb-m16.aol.com>`

```
"S Comstock" <scomstock@aol.com> wrote:
>
> A number of years ago I met an instructor from SanFrancisco who had his name
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> today.

Any well designed ones would. I have been involved with quite a few
systems handling public records, and you see some very strange names
out there. There appear to be people who must do business with the
government (criminal justice, driver's license, etc.) who rarely deal
with companies, at least with the same names. You should see some of
the AKAs that come through a criminal justice system! And those must
also be soundexed. Many systems must accept company names, and
those can be just about anything. Systems like vehicle registration must
handle both. I usually build in a way to override normal verification
and store peculiar names exactly as entered. You usually need to store
the name in a way that sorts properly but can be decoded to print
properly. In this part of the world  you must allow for name prefixes
like Dr. and Mrs., and name suffixes like Jr. and III. Various cultures
are not always consistent in how names are structured or written, but
my experience is that this can usually be handled with standard name
techniques.

Bottom line is that, if you want to handle names correctly, you must
write name handling code very carefully with lots of thought, and test
it on lots of real data. Sadly, as you point out, this is not always done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

#### ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T16:50:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312021650.5e8a676d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote

> Oh yeah, I side-stepped the problem by using 
> SPECIAL-NAMES. CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS 'A' THRU '9'. 
> but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.

'A' THRU '9' doesn't seem to be a very good idea at all.  For ASCII it
will fail because '9' is earlier in the sequence than 'A', for EBCDIC
it allows all the odd characters that are between 'Z' and '0' and
those that are within the 'A' to 'Z' range between, ummmm, 'I' and 'J'
and 'R' and 'S'.
```

#### ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T23:32:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0312022332.5a92127c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
Hi UB,

You may want to check this out. If memory serves the THRU feature will
give you a "true" return on any char with a hex value between X'C1'
and X'F9', inclusive. Someone here s/b able to confirm or contradict
this, or you can test the premis yourself.

Regards, Jack. 



weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote in message news:<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com>...
> I recently encountered a problem with a program which performs edits
> on data fields when an edit which tests whether the first digit of a name
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T07:03:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031203080407.895$Ua@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com> <8a2d426e.0312022332.5a92127c@posting.google.com>`

```
More precisely, 'A' THRU '9' would include characters with collating
sequence between 'A' and '9' inclusive in the character set being used by
the program when it is run. There are no characters between 'A' and '9'
in the ASCII set, because 'A' (hex 41) is greater than '9' (hex 39). In
EBCDIC 'A' is less than '9', but also included are characters between
'I' and 'J', 'R' and 'S' and 'Z' and '0'.
```

#### ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T16:13:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bql235$f6i$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```

On  2-Dec-2003, weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote:

> Oh yeah, I side-stepped the problem by using
> SPECIAL-NAMES. CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS 'A' THRU '9'.
> but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.

That practice is not portable.   Check out ASCII and EBCDIC tables and look to
see what is included in both cases.   It is safer to explicitly include the
characters you wish.
```

#### ↳ Re: ALPHANUMERIC tests

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T13:35:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jWkAb.158158$Ec1.6172050@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```

"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:bqi7pu$iiv$1@news.utelfla.com...
| I recently encountered a problem with a program which performs edits
| on data fields when an edit which tests whether the first digit of a name
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
| but would hate to re-inventing the wheel if I am overlooking something.

You could use INSPECT CONVERTING.
Move the field to work.
IF WORK = SPACES you have a bad name
ELSE
INSPECT WORK CONVERTING
      'ABCE*(%$'''   (whatever YOU want)
  to '                    ' .
If WORK NOT = SPACES you have a bad name.
END-IF twice

This will work for Ascii or Ebcdic, no thru problems.
You can use any characters you want, no need to convert to UPPER.
If Space isn't valid in the first character, you need a special test.
```

---

<a id="t-3090538baa"></a>
## Thread: dynamic calling statically linked moduled in IBM cobol — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### dynamic calling statically linked moduled in IBM cobol

- **From:** drosenbe0813@yahoo.com (drosenbe0813)
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T07:39:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com>`

```
Is it possible to (using IBM Cobol VS II) to call submodules using
dynamic syntax (i.e. using a variable for the called program name),
but statically link all of the submodules, so that the calling program
does not have to be recompiled when we add a submodule.
The approach we are using right now is an evaluate statement in the
calling program, but I would like to eliminate this. We are getting
the called program name from a table. Our environment is not allowing
us to use true dynamic linking.

Any suggestions would be helpfull.

D.R.
```

#### ↳ Re: dynamic calling statically linked moduled in IBM cobol

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T18:58:58+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s3hpsvcpl7kcmh6kdnc2sehgor1bm3nenb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com>`

```
On 2 Dec 2003 07:39:20 -0800 drosenbe0813@yahoo.com (drosenbe0813) wrote:

:>Is it possible to (using IBM Cobol VS II) to call submodules using
:>dynamic syntax (i.e. using a variable for the called program name),
:>but statically link all of the submodules, so that the calling program
:>does not have to be recompiled when we add a submodule.
:>The approach we are using right now is an evaluate statement in the
:>calling program, but I would like to eliminate this. We are getting
:>the called program name from a table. Our environment is not allowing
:>us to use true dynamic linking.

You can do it by aliasing the entry point.

Of course, this will lead to trouble if more than one program calls this
routine.
```

#### ↳ Re: dynamic calling statically linked moduled in IBM cobol

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this>
- **Date:** 2003-12-02T09:45:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kc4zb.342$b77.332@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com>`

```
drosenbe0813 wrote:
> Is it possible to (using IBM Cobol VS II) to call submodules using
> dynamic syntax (i.e. using a variable for the called program name),
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> D.R.

I think either I am not understanding your question, or that you are not 
understanding static vs. dynamic calls.

If you call a program dynamically, using either the DYNAM option and 
CALL 'literal' or CALL data-name (regardless of the DYNAM option 
setting), the calling and called programs are separately link-edited.

If you have a number of dynamically called subroutines, you could link 
all of them together, but I see no advantage to doing so.  Each of the 
dynamically called subroutines can be separately stored in the load 
module library, and each subroutine can be changed as needed without 
recompiling or relinking any other programs in the run unit.

If you link all of the dynamically called subroutines together into a 
single load module, you will need an alias for each routine, in order to 
  find that routine at execution time, and if any one of the subroutines 
is changed, the entire group will have to be relinkedited (and the 
aliases all replaced).  These programs probably have no relationship to 
each other, except that they are called by the driver.  So why try to 
put them together?

If you make dynamic calls, you should not try to put the subroutines 
into the driver's load module.  (This was one possible interpretation of 
your original question.)

Final note:
You should make every effort to get off of VS COBOL II, and onto a 
modern, supported COBOL compiler.

It is interesting that some of the IBM COBOL team have said they believe 
nearly all of their customers are using only supported COBOL compilers, 
but there is daily evidence that many IBM customers are using one or 
more of the no longer supported compilers.  I am working with two 
different large companies, one of which has both OS/VS COBOL and VS 
COBOL II with a little "new COBOL", and the other is using OS/VS COBOL 
with LANGLVL(1) - 1968 ANSI COBOL syntax - and has never even licensed 
VS COBOL II.  Both have a "new COBOL" compiler, but the applications 
staff do not use the new version.  The shops both have yet to convert to 
Language Environment run time.  This means their applications (the only 
reason they have a mainframe computer and OS/390 or z/OS) are completely 
unsupported by IBM!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic calling statically linked moduled in IBM cobol

- **From:** drosenbe0813@yahoo.com (drosenbe0813)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T05:53:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6bfe645b.0312040553.2c866aa2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com> <Kc4zb.342$b77.332@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```
First...you must understand that it usually is not the applications
people's choice to be on older compilers...somehow corporate profits
get put ahead of keeping the applications people up to speed (and in
the corner office's inhabitants mind...if it ain't broke, don't fix
it!.)

But in our case, the application in question is running an IMS/DC
environment and the systems people are having a hard time getting
dynamic linking working without a HUGE performance hit. I have
architected an enhanceable system, which must fit into an existing
application, which depends upon 'dynamically' calling submodules to do
different database access calls. Because of the current environment,
we are forced to statically link our modules together. I was hoping to
gain at least some flexibility in the calling module (i.e. not having
an ever expanding EVALUATE statement), while maintaining the
performance which static linking affords.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic calling statically linked moduled in IBM cobol

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T09:59:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IBKzb.357$b77.319@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6bfe645b.0312040553.2c866aa2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com> <Kc4zb.342$b77.332@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <6bfe645b.0312040553.2c866aa2@posting.google.com>`

```
drosenbe0813 wrote:
> First...you must understand that it usually is not the applications
> people's choice to be on older compilers...somehow corporate profits
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> performance which static linking affords.

Naturally, I know that technical advantages are often lost on management 
- if they had any technical smarts, why would they have ever become 
managers? (j/k)  Actually, there are non-technical managers who are 
smart enough to learn who knows what in their world and use those 
technically savvy people to make them look good.

"New COBOL" on the IBM mainframe offers many improvements - among them, 
in the IMS on line environment (it's IMS/TM now, no longer IMS/DC, by 
the way), the ability to create reentrant 31-bit programs which can be 
preloaded and remain resident all day long, helping performance in a 
very similar environment to what you are describing.

The generated code in "new COBOL" is so good that the CA-Optimizer 
approach of "tuning" OS/VS COBOL isn't cost effective.  (Do you use the 
OS/VS COBOL Optimize option?  Do you have the CA-Optimizer and use it? 
Are you using Language Environment to support the OS/VS COBOL programs?)

You are right that static calls are faster than dynamic calls.  If 
performance of your app is approaching "marginal" (said another way, if 
your application is performing just well enough to be acceptable to your 
users), you probably need to stay with static linking to preserve the 
performance.

But you might be able to sqeeze a bit more performance out by moving to 
"new COBOL".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Oops!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T10:18:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aUKzb.358$b77.267@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<IBKzb.357$b77.319@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`
- **References:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com> <Kc4zb.342$b77.332@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <6bfe645b.0312040553.2c866aa2@posting.google.com> <IBKzb.357$b77.319@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```
I got mixed up, and was replying as if your application was OS/VS COBOL 
based, instead of VS COBOL II.

If you are using VS COBOL II, and NOT using CMPR2 (which roughly means 
you are using 1974 ANSI COBOL syntax), moving to "new COBOL" is going to 
be relatively painless.  And, even if you don't upgrade to a supported 
COBOL, VS COBOL II offers you the ability to create 31-bit, reentrant 
programs that can be preloaded to get better performance in IMS/TM.  See 
the DATA(31) and RENT options.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Oops!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T19:58:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slMzb.28365$sb4.22104@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<6bfe645b.0312020739.5e5c0140@posting.google.com> <Kc4zb.342$b77.332@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <6bfe645b.0312040553.2c866aa2@posting.google.com> <IBKzb.357$b77.319@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <aUKzb.358$b77.267@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```
Slight "clarification" (from my experience).

Moving from the VS COBOL II compiler to the (currently supported) Enterprise
COBOL is relatively easy.

HOWEVER, this assumes that you are using the LE run-time with VS COBOL II.  If
you are currently using the VS COBOL II run-time (which hasn't been supported
for years), then the migration of run-times (with NO re-compiles - just
relinking or changing to RES) will require MAJOR testing and changing of
"development tools" (such as ABEND handling and interactive debugging)
```

## Thread: Help Please- Large Precision in Cobol Set For AIX Compiler — 3 this month / 6 total

_Thread started [2003-11](2003-11.md#t-79b071c431)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help Please- Large Precision in Cobol Set For AIX Compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T05:39:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,eit.mirror.cobol-lovers,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AGezb.25507$sb4.16128@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bq300q$4m0$1@titan.btinternet.com> <lpmxb.341861$0v4.18879593@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
If you want to use up to 31-digits and are on a "current" IBM compiler using
ARITH (or any - if there were any - implementing the 2002 ISO Standard), make
certain that you read the PERFORMANCE implications of this.  For the IBM
compilers there is either "hardware" or "software" support for 31-digits in
COBOL - but even the hardware support requires SIGNIFICANT resources.

Programmer "beware" !!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help Please- Large Precision in Cobol Set For AIX Compiler

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T09:16:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,eit.mirror.cobol-lovers,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031203101746.746$5N@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<bq300q$4m0$1@titan.btinternet.com> <lpmxb.341861$0v4.18879593@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <AGezb.25507$sb4.16128@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> If you want to use up to 31-digits and are on a "current" IBM compiler using
> ARITH (or any - if there were any - implementing the 2002 ISO Standard), make
> certain that you read the PERFORMANCE implications of this.  For the IBM
> compilers there is either "hardware" or "software" support for 31-digits in
> COBOL - but even the hardware support requires SIGNIFICANT resources.

The old IBM 1401, circa late 50's, could do 'unlimited precision'. Fields
were delimited by 'word marks', there were no field lengths in the
instruction format. The Burroughs/Unisys Medium Systems hardware
could handle an integer or floating point mantissa up to 100 digits, and
a multiply could result in a 200 digit product. IOW, it could calculate
(goggle - 1)**2, or the size of the universe in cubic microns, exactly
(well, assuming the size of the universe in cubic microns is an integer,
or a real number with only a few decimal places :-).
```

```
I haven't checked yet, but I would assume that the overhead is encountered
only on the fields that require the Extended instructions. (I hope this is
the case, and I'll check next week).

From my experience, the Extended situation comes into play when there are
columns on DB2 tables that exceed the normal limit.
The other option is to return a truncated number from DB2, and hope you
don't drop anything.
```

<a id="t-c680f561b5"></a>
## Thread: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** seemanta_18@yahoo.com (seemanta dutta)
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T08:24:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com>`

```
Greetings Cobol Gurus,
I am a newbie getting started on Cobol on the mainframe.
I have a PDS on the mainframe that has about 2000 JCLs. In each JCL I
have to update the DDNAMES of all the DD statements.
That has been done and now I have to verify those DDNAMES as to
whether they exist or not.

So my question is whether there is any way through which I can
actually read each individual DD statement of the JCLs and test for
the existence of each dataset as mentioned in the DSN parameter of
that DD statement. Can be done through a cobol program??
If this is a too silly question then please forgive me.

regards,
Seemanta Dutta
```

#### ↳ Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T20:01:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kirzb.8619$aw2.3949715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com>`

```

The DDNAMEs are defined in the using PROGRAMs and changing it requires the
program be recompiled; JCL just tells the system "when a program wants this
DDNAME, use this DATASETNAME (DSN).

If you want to amend your question to ask, "can I validate that all the
DSN's in all these JCL members are valid?" then the answer is, "I doubt it."

You can read the JCL as a sequantial file; when you come to a DD statement,
parse out the DSN from the line. Then attempt in your program to OPEN that
file, and interrogate the file status. I've seen code here and I'm pretty
sure its in the manual how to create the control structure needed to open a
dynamically-named file from your program.

BUT...

1. Many JCL members will use SYMBOLICs in the DSN (e.g
DSN=&QUAL..&NODE.MY.DATASET); in this case you are in essence, "SOL" as the
SYMBOLIC expansion occurs at EXEC time.
2. JCL will contain datasets which are created by a program... meaning that
dataset will not exist, which is a Good Thing.
3. JCL will refer to datasets created in a previous job step, meaning those
datasets will not exist until run time (also a good thing).
4. DSNs will sometimes be identified with a referback (e.g,
DSN=*.stepname.ddname), which will immeasureably make your parsing task more
difficult.
5. DDs and the corresponding DSNs may not even be specified in the job step:
they may be specified as an override in the EXEC statement which actually
starts the job; or they might be specified in the job step, but overriden
anyway!

All this assumes, of course, that what you changed were the DSNs, not the
DDs. If you changed the DDs, you had to recompile anyway and presumably you
tested. (If you changed DDs but did not change the programs, please restore
from backup and start over).

Bottom line, I think you are probably going to wait on "run time JCL Error"
to discover any mistakes. If nothing else, you should keep track of each JCL
as it is executed, so at some point you will know that all that JCL has been
tested (tested by fire, that is!).

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T20:07:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aorzb.26610$sb4.25674@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com> <Kirzb.8619$aw2.3949715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I don't know if it helps the original question, but it certainly possible to
OPEN INPUT a file (via its DDName - Select/Assign statement) and check its file
status.  I doubt that this is really the type of solution desired, but it SHOULD
work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T23:18:04+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3FCE69FE.DA822227@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com> <Kirzb.8619$aw2.3949715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <aorzb.26610$sb4.25674@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I don't know if it helps the original question, but it certainly possible to
> OPEN INPUT a file (via its DDName - Select/Assign statement) and check its file
> status.  I doubt that this is really the type of solution desired, but it SHOULD
> work.
>

Can you take that one step further to help him out. What file-status will he get ?
It's a technique I used on Radio Shack with early RM/COBOL - giving me a '98' which
strictly speaking was 'Invalid or Crashed Index". Either not finding the index file
or a crashed index - at least gave a clue that all was not kosher.

In my case :-

(a) new set of files - prompt user Yes/No to open fresh set - (Yes) open them , (No)
STOP RUN.

(b) existing set of files - after a 'messagebox' I would then STOP RUN.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T19:20:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031206202141.600$bs@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com> <Kirzb.8619$aw2.3949715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <aorzb.26610$sb4.25674@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3FCE69FE.DA822227@shaw.ca> <20031204112836.323$Q5@news.newsreader.com> <3FCF6DD0.EE57A5B8@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> up in the attic).


This was the original TRS-80, not the Model II. Back in the late 70's,
unless you were running CP/M, you mostly had BASIC, Assembly or
perhaps Pascal. I wrote a couple of systems using BASIC for accounts
receivable and land surveyor's. Then I converted those to several of
the more popular computers at the time, including the TRS-80, AKA
Trash-80. :-) In the early 80's I wrote a credit union system using MS
COBOL 2, my first COBOL application on a PC (mainframe COBOL
since 68). There have been quite a few PC COBOL apps since, plus
PC applications several other languages, when it was appropriate,
and a lot of mainframe applications too, mainly in COBOL. Assembly
and C I use for writing utilities, when I really need to get down-and-
dirty, or when it must run as fast as possible.

It's surprising how many computer languages you pick up over the years.
In a post I once said that I had programmed in something like twenty
languages. When someone challenged me, I began trying to remember
them all. I was surprised at how many there were; it took a few days
for them all to percolate to the top. Counting significantly different
dialects (e.g. Apple BASIC & VB) as different languages, it is almost
forty, including seven assembly languages. Have you other old timers
counted how many languages you have used? You would probably be
surprised how many there are.
```

```
Use the "OPTIONAL" phrase in the Select/Assign clause, and you will get a "05"
file status code (and the file will NOT be created - if you use OPEN INPUT - but
will if you use OPEN I-O or OPEN OUTPUT).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T00:10:10+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0mnssvs535sth82butt8pl0arerqmbj3pg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com> <Kirzb.8619$aw2.3949715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
On Wed, 03 Dec 2003 20:01:46 GMT "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
wrote:

:>The DDNAMEs are defined in the using PROGRAMs and changing it requires the
:>program be recompiled; JCL just tells the system "when a program wants this
:>DDNAME, use this DATASETNAME (DSN).

:>If you want to amend your question to ask, "can I validate that all the
:>DSN's in all these JCL members are valid?" then the answer is, "I doubt it."

:>You can read the JCL as a sequantial file; when you come to a DD statement,
:>parse out the DSN from the line. Then attempt in your program to OPEN that
:>file, and interrogate the file status. I've seen code here and I'm pretty
:>sure its in the manual how to create the control structure needed to open a
:>dynamically-named file from your program.

:>BUT...

:>1. Many JCL members will use SYMBOLICs in the DSN (e.g
:>DSN=&QUAL..&NODE.MY.DATASET); in this case you are in essence, "SOL" as the
:>SYMBOLIC expansion occurs at EXEC time.

Conversion time.

The converter can be invoked and the internal text examined.

:>2. JCL will contain datasets which are created by a program... meaning that
:>dataset will not exist, which is a Good Thing.

DISP=NEW or DISP=(,...) can be detected.

:>3. JCL will refer to datasets created in a previous job step, meaning those
:>datasets will not exist until run time (also a good thing).

Can be tracked.

:>4. DSNs will sometimes be identified with a referback (e.g,
:>DSN=*.stepname.ddname), which will immeasureably make your parsing task more
:>difficult.

Use the internal text.

:>5. DDs and the corresponding DSNs may not even be specified in the job step:
:>they may be specified as an override in the EXEC statement which actually
:>starts the job; or they might be specified in the job step, but overriden
:>anyway!

Only for STC's.

:>All this assumes, of course, that what you changed were the DSNs, not the
:>DDs. If you changed the DDs, you had to recompile anyway and presumably you
:>tested. (If you changed DDs but did not change the programs, please restore
:>from backup and start over).

:>Bottom line, I think you are probably going to wait on "run time JCL Error"
:>to discover any mistakes. If nothing else, you should keep track of each JCL
:>as it is executed, so at some point you will know that all that JCL has been
:>tested (tested by fire, that is!).

REXX would be a lot easier, especially for the parsing.
```

```
That raises an off topic question.  I had someone ask me recently if, at 
run time, a program could determin all of the symbolics that were used 
in conversion.

I am almost certain that that information is discarded after conversion 
-- but, it is possible to determine where the original job jcl is 
stored.  Therefore, it is possible to reconvert the job, at run time, to 
gather that data.

But I have not been able to find a reference for the converter.  I've 
been searching throught he z/OS bookshelves with little success.

Would you know where I might find a reference for the converter?
```

#### ↳ Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T23:40:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wZ7Bb.168725$Ec1.6479673@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com>`

```

"seemanta dutta" <seemanta_18@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com...
| Greetings Cobol Gurus,
| I am a newbie getting started on Cobol on the mainframe.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
|

There are a few things you can try,
if this is an IBM mainframe, (MVS) OS/390 , Z/OS.

First you don't mention if you are using PROC, or JCL execution.

If you are using PROCs, you can use a TYPRUN=SCAN on a single JOB that
Executes all the PROCS.
That will give you one hell of a listing, but everything is in one listing.
If you are on SDSF, you can use the PRINT D command to create a file of the
expanded JCL.

The tricky part is finding the DSN= in the lines that say SUBSTITUTION JCL.
If you are up to it, you can get the DSNs out of the listing.

You can also do a LISTCAT (ENTRIES or LEVEL) to check the names of the files
on your system.
You'll have to test to see the return code you get if the file isn't there.

1. Get the DSNs out of the listing
2. sort the names, and eliminate the duplicates
3. write a program to use the names to generate a LISTCAT  statement, and
run them through IDCAMS.
4. Check the return Codes from the LISTCAT.

This is a bit vague, and you'll have to look at the listings to see what's
there.
This is NOT a simple method, but writing the program could be fun.
I am assuming that you are using PROCs, and they have symbolic
substitutions.
A DSN=& is something you don't want.
The other DSN= statements should give you the file name.

Discuss this with your co-workers before trying to do it.
```

#### ↳ Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T09:17:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-F6134A.09175211122003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com>,
 seemanta_18@yahoo.com (seemanta dutta) wrote:

> Greetings Cobol Gurus,
> I am a newbie getting started on Cobol on the mainframe.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Seemanta Dutta

This is quite easy in a Cobol program, create a Rexx environment (one 
call to a TSO series program) and the use the LISTDSI function of Rexx 
on each dataset name.  You will get a "DATASET NOT FOUND" for those that 
do not exist.

The TSO/E Rexx manual has details on invoing statements from Cobol and 
other programs.
```

## Thread: MicroFocus 2.2 ProCobol - Can't update a date column to a null, help? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### MicroFocus 2.2 ProCobol - Can't update a date column to a null, help?

- **From:** morant@dnr.state.wi.us (Tom Morano)
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T09:08:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<55066e.0312030908.57d8e9e9@posting.google.com>`

```
Microfocus 2.2 / Unix / Oracle 9i  

When I'm trying to update a DATE column in procobol MicroFocus2.2 to
null, it stops processing. Anyone know how to make this work?

The column may or may not contain a value. If it does not have a value
I'd like it to contain a NULL.

Prior to the update, I'm either moving spaces to a pic x(9) column, or
a date into it.
I1-DATE-VALUE will contain either spaces (which is where the problem
is)
or will contain something like:  '13-JUL-02'  

I've tried updating code, to no avail, with :      
 col =   NVL(:I1-DATE-VALUE,NULL),
 col =   NVL(:I1-DATE-VALUE,''),
 col =   NVL(:I1-DATE-VALUE,''''),
 col =       :I1-DATE-VALUE,
 col =   NVL(:I1-DATE-VALUE,""),

and moving a null indicator to the column I1-DATE-VALUE and setting db
column to that.

It worked fine before in the 8.1.7 version of true64 cobol. 


-----------------------------------------------
CODE:
    05  I1-DATE-VALUE             PIC X(09)   VALUE SPACE.

     EXEC SQL
        UPDATE LE_STR_INSTRUCTOR
           SET LAST_CHANGED_DATE        =  SYSDATE,
               HUNT_CERTIFIED_DATE      =  NVL(:I1-DATE-VALUE,''),
       WHERE   INSTR_NO            =  :INPUT-INSTR-NO
    END-EXEC. 

-----------------------------------------------
ERROR WHEN RUNNING:

(Looks to be inserting a value with spaces, and it doesn't think it's
a valid date)

**************  SQLCA VALUES AT ABNORMAL TERMINATION **********
SQLERRMC..ORA-01847: day of month must be between 1 and last day of
month

------------------------------------------------
Pre-Compiled with this:

 procob iname=n475.pco format=terminal  MAXLITERAL=32 \ 
        mode=oracle  DBMS=V8 UNSAFE_NULL=YES  comp5=yes

-------------------------------------------------
Compiled with this (in the 'make' file):

COB=cob -vx -C sourceformat"'free'" -C 'makesyn "COMP-5" == "COMP"'
PCCPLSFLAGS=format=terminal dbms=v8 unsafe_null=yes maxliteral=32
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus 2.2 ProCobol - Can't update a date column to a null, help?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T11:10:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0312041110.1186af29@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<55066e.0312030908.57d8e9e9@posting.google.com>`

```
Hmmm... the NVL() is designed to specify what value is RETURNED if a
column IS null.  To set the column to null just do so:

col = null;
```

## Thread: cobol array editing output — 87 msgs

### cobol array editing output

- **From:** ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie)
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T12:04:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

First of all, I am using Cobol Fujitsu V3 compiler.

I am trying to diplay information which I have taken in to an array
from the user.
EG: Surname: Must have 30 Chars
    Firstname: ...20 Chars.
I need Firstname SPACE Surname to be displayed one line at a time for
each record.
    
When I display these variables there is large gaps between both names.
Does anyone know of a way to edit these variables to remove the spaces
between them and display them as required?

Also, but less importantly, does anyone know how, in Fujitsu v3, to
clear the screen?  I have tried just about everything.
I tried screen section.... but the best result I can get is another
console window opens up blank.

Thanks in advance,
Ritchie.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T12:31:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kztzb.9179$zf2.1207968@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie wrote:
> Hi,
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ritchie.

	DISPLAY SPACE AT LINE 1 COLUMN 1.
	MOVE SPACE TO SCREEN-DATA.
	STRING FIRST-NAME DELIMITED BY SPACE
           " " LAST-NAME INTO SCREEN-DATA.
	DISPLAY SCREEN-DATA AT LINE 1 COLUMN 1.

The above assumes that both fields are left justified, which is normally 
the case. If that is not the case, remove the leading space with an 
unstring delimited by ALL SPACE.  The ALL will tell it to consider 
strings of space as a single instance of a space. That is not needed 
above, as you do not really care how many trailing spaces are there.

Your problem with the console/screen is that Cobol has both an 
operator's console, and the screen section has a screen window.  They 
are two different things. The DISPLAY verb can be directed to either(see 
the ON modifer) ... but the default is that a simple "DISPLAY 'Hello 
world'" would come up on the operator's console, while a "DISPLAY 'Hello 
World' AT LINE 1 COLUMN 1" would come up on the screen. All screen 
section stuff goes to the screen, not the console. The normal way of 
doing it in Cobol is to use the console for crash information ... stuff like

		DISPLAY "The sky is falling !!!!!!!"
		DISPLAY "Fatal Error - Aborting".
		STOP RUN.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T10:19:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312041019.6a74662b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <Kztzb.9179$zf2.1207968@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca> wrote

> > First of all, I am using Cobol Fujitsu V3 compiler.

> Your problem with the console/screen is that Cobol has both an 
> operator's console, and the screen section has a screen window.  They 
> are two different things. 

This isn't a feature of 'Cobol' but only of Fujitsu Cobol on Windows. 
Other Cobols and other environments seem to cope OK with mixing
console and crt displays.

> The normal way of 
> doing it in Cobol is to use the console for crash information ... stuff like
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 		STOP RUN.

It may be 'normal' for Fujitsu users, but I turn the console off and
put error messages on line 25 of the screen, or sometimes on a
messagebox.

I don't think that having console and crt as separate windows is a
good idea, I doubt that users would know to look at the console, or
even know that it is related to the program screen, to report your
error message.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T13:40:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312041340.40824f77@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <Kztzb.9179$zf2.1207968@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca> wrote

> > EG: Surname: Must have 30 Chars
> >     Firstname: ...20 Chars.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 	DISPLAY SCREEN-DATA AT LINE 1 COLUMN 1.

     01  All-Name.
         03  First-Name      PIC X(20).
         03  SurName         PIC X(30).
         03  FILLER          PIC X.
     01  Display-Name        PIC X(50).
     01  Work-Name           PIC X(50).

         MOVE 1      TO In-Pos
         MOVE 1      TO Out-Pos
         MOVE SPACES TO Display-Name
         PERFORM
             WITH TEST AFTER
             UNTIL Work-Name = SPACES

             MOVE SPACES TO Work-Name
             UNSTRING All-Name
                 DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES
                 INTO Work-Name
                 WITH POINTER In-Pos

             IF ( Out-Pos > 1 )
                 STRING " " DELIMITED BY SIZE
                     INTO Display-Name
                     WITH POINTER Out-Pos
             END-IF

             STRING Work-Name DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 INTO Display-Name
                 WITH POINTER Out-Pos
         END-PERFORM

This handles multiple word names and multiple space separators.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T12:52:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqlifc$uu9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com>`

```
This looks a lot like homework, and I don't know anything about the Fujitsu
compilers, but a couple of suggestions from "generic COBOL" presuming that
Fujitsu V3 is an '85-compliant compiler:

I'd locate the first trailing blank in each field  using the INSPECT verb to
tally *leading* spaces in the results of the intrinsic function REVERSE
using the field as argument.  You can also do this "longhand" by redefining
the field as an array of single characters, and examine each character
starting with the last looking for something other than spaces, but that's
rather more tedious.

Once you know where the trailing blanks start in each of the fields, the
bulk of the work's done.  It's probably easiest to preinitialize the
destination to spaces.  Then you can position the two names individually
using reference modification on both source and destination.  Or you could
use a single STRING whose source arguments are the first name (suitably
reference modified) delimited by size, a single space delimited by size, and
the second name (also reference modified) delimited by size.

That's the approach I'd take, anyway.  Reference modification was introduced
as a feature in 1985 standard COBOL, and the REVERSE function in the
Intrinsic Function Amendment of 1989, so unless you're using a dialect of
ANSI-74 COBOL, neither is a vendor extension.  .

    -Chuck Stevens

"ritchie" <ritchie_s01@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
```

```
[In the Procedure Division]


MOVE    SPACES                  TO  USER-NAME-01.

IF            FIRST-NAME-POS-20   >     SPACE
               MOVE    FIRST-NAME  TO  USER-NAME-01-FIRST-20
               MOVE    LAST-NAME   TO  USER-NAME-01-LAST-30
ELSE

               MOVE    FIRST-NAME  TO  USER-NAME-02-FIRST-19
```

```
(bottom-posted reply...)

Hugh Candlin wrote:

> ritchie <ritchie_s01@yahoo.com> wrote in message news:3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com...
> 
…[224 more quoted lines elided]…
>                MOVE    LAST-NAME   TO  USER-NAME-20-LAST-30.

If I were your instructor, I'd give this a big fat "D-" - does the job, 
but is a whole lot of code for little payoff.  (I'm pretty sure you 
posted it in jest...)  :)

(I'd give a solution that used "String" a "C", since it wouldn't 
properly concatenate first name of "Mario" last name of "Van Peebles", 
though it would work if neither the first nor last name had a space.  A 
solution that used a Perform Varying loop to find the first non-space 
and reference modification to concatenate them would get an "A", and an 
"A+" to a solution that used Inspect Function Reverse.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T18:09:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vsvj5uaras3jf2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bqnih1$a1c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <bqnih1$a1c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  3-Dec-2003, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> it's clearer, I believe it's slower - but it might be harder to screw up.

Because if I were teaching newbies COBOL, and they came up with that 
solution, I would know one of two things...

1)  They really researched the problem and found the Function Reverse, 
and put it together with Inspect Tallying.  That sort of research and 
intuitiveness would show a real knack for programming.

2)  They asked an experienced COBOL professional (either here or 
elsewhere) who pointed them in this direction.  I would, of course, 
cover all different solutions in class, but learning early that it's 
okay to ask for help from more experienced folks is also a good trait - 
no programmer is an island.  :)  Teamwork is a must in a corporate 
environment.

(both IMO, of course...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T14:46:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqq5no$lld$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <bqnih1$a1c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <vsvj5uaras3jf2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On  4-Dec-2003, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> > Why do you prefer the function to the reference modification?   I'm not sure
> > it's clearer, I believe it's slower - but it might be harder to screw up.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> environment.

You could say the same thing about the person who came up with the reference
modification answer.   Even more so, as the CoBOL professionals who have tested
these two solutions agree that reference modification works faster.

I prefer the solution that is clear, fast, and direct over the one that is
clever, but slower and obscure.

And I prefer the student who completes his research and tests his intuitiveness
comparing its results with other results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T09:27:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqqf5l$1gou$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <bqnih1$a1c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <vsvj5uaras3jf2@corp.supernews.com> <bqq5no$lld$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bqq5no$lld$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> Even more so, as the CoBOL professionals who have tested
> these two solutions agree that reference modification works faster.

Really?  The COBOL professional who tested these two solutions on the Unisys
MCP system certainly does *not* agree that designs based on reference
modification work faster than designs based on the REVERSE intrinsic
function!

I see two basic tasks in this process.  One is determining how many trailing
blanks there are in the field, and the other is getting the non-blank
characters into the destination.

The main topic of discussion as far as I am concerned is the first task.  In
the second, not much matters except that transferring the non-blank
information a byte at a time is likely to be more costly than transferring
the same information in larger chunks.

I believe the *most straightforward* approach is that generally represented
by Judson's RJUST-1, though I'd probably be inclined to do the INSPECT
directly against the REVERSE function itself.

In my tests, the *worst* performance was demonstrated by the example that
used reference modification and *subscripting* to step backwards through the
input field.

I concede that the backward-stepping through the input field *using
indexing* rather than subscripts proved fastest of all (by about 20%, give
or take), than the best "reverse" function results.

But the cost of subscripting as compared to indexing is rather greater, in
the MCP case, than the overhead of using the intrinsic function REVERSE, and
I believe that's the clearest approach anyway; with the single exception
noted above *all* of the reference-modification scans were more costly than
FUNCTION REVERSE.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <bqnih1$a1c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <vsvj5uaras3jf2@corp.supernews.com> <bqq5no$lld$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bqqf5l$1gou$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> FUNCTION REVERSE.

Your statements above reflect exactly what I intuitively expected before
running the benchmarks on my PC. Probably because my experience has
been mostly on the V and A series machines. You probably better than
most of us know these things are highly dependent on the compiler's
ability to generate efficient code, and the machine architecture. I'm not
nearly as familiar with the A instruction set as the V instruction set, and
they're very different. But the V had very good hardware support for
string handling. Since the A does not have an assembly compiler, most
A programmers don't look at machine code very often. Considering the
80x86 instruction set, I was quite surprised at the huge difference in
speed between FUNCTION REVERSE and any of the other methods.
Micro Focus seems to have pretty good code efficiency in general. I
was further surprised when most of the other posters claimed similar
results using other compilers and hardware.
```
