###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T09:36:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
> (I'd give a solution that used "String" a "C", since it wouldn't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "A+" to a solution that used Inspect Function Reverse.)


We did a number of benchmarks with various compilers (see program
below) a few years back here in C.L.C, and it appears that virtually all
implementations of REVERSE were significantly less efficient than
looping with a subscript/index, which was less efficient than looping
with reference modification. Based on that, and the fact that a straight
forward use of REVERSE would not correct multiple spaces within a
name (e.g. "van___Peebles" -> "van_Peebles"), I would reverse ;-)
your 'A' and 'A+'  choices.

You may find the benchmark program below interesting. Back in '99 we
had a long discussion on how best to right justify an alphanumeric field,
a problem computationally very similar to this one. The program below
benchmarks a number of solutions proposed at the time. The times at the
top were obtained using either MF COBOL 3.2 (DOS 16 bit) or NE 3.1
(win32), I don't recall. Note that the FUNCTION REVERSE times took
two orders of magnitude longer than any of the other methods. You can
compile and run the program below in your environment and with other
test values if you like. Looping each iteration inside a single perform
would show even more dramatic difference, since the same-for-all
PERFORM overhead would be eliminated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T17:26:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0312041726.549b62cf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message news:<20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com>...
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> your 'A' and 'A+'  choices.

Hmmm...
> 
> You may find the benchmark program below interesting. 

I did <G>...

I currently use REVERSED because I like the elegance of it (although
some of this was lost in the "benchmark" implementation).

I therefore decided to re-run the old benchmark you provided and see
just how "bad" it is...

Ouch! I won't be using it in future...

Here're my results...

I took Judson's string length measuring benchmark and amended it
(slightly) so it could
compile on the Fujitsu NETCOBOL v6 compiler.

It was run on a pentium 4 (2.6 GHz) Notebook, under Windows XP
Professional.

Apart from system tasks, nothing else was running; Task Manager
reported 40 active tasks...

Here are the results, conclusions, and Source code...


* Benchmark in original form

Starting Benchmark...
50,000,000 Iterations
RJUST-1 00:40:25
RJUST-2 00:57:51
RJUST-3 00:10:72
RJUST-4 00:15:33
RJUST-5 00:15:70

Running it again to ensure clock fetches are OK...

Starting Benchmark...
50,000,000 Iterations
RJUST-1 00:40:54
RJUST-2 00:57:78
RJUST-3 00:10:61
RJUST-4 00:15:23
RJUST-5 00:15:52

The total of these seconds does NOT agree with wall clock time (around
100 seconds)
so the calculations of elapsed time are suspect before we start.

However, as they ARE consistent, let's continue...

Changing the Stringing of output in RJUST-1 to be a MOVE...


Starting Benchmark...
50,000,000 Iterations
RJUST-1 00:39:90
RJUST-2 00:57:14
RJUST-3 00:10:71
RJUST-4 00:15:26
RJUST-5 00:15:62

Didn't help much...<G>

Now calculate the parameters to the MOVE as a separate step, using
efficient arithmetic...

NOTE: COUNTER was changed to comp-5, and two comp-5 fields (J and K)
were added, as parameters
to the MOVE.

Starting Benchmark...
50,000,000 Iterations
RJUST-1 00:34:34
RJUST-2 00:51:38
RJUST-3 00:10:68
RJUST-4 00:15:23
RJUST-5 00:15:43

Better. Changing COUNTER has affected all the processes...

Now change ALL the COMP fields to COMP-5...

Starting Benchmark...
50,000,000 Iterations
RJUST-1 00:34:36
RJUST-2 00:40:71
RJUST-3 00:07:24
RJUST-4 00:06:27
RJUST-5 00:06:27

Holy Efficiency, Batman! Just allowing efficient arithmetic has
changed the order of the Techniques!

Now lets try something REALLY SIMPLE...

Add code for RJUST-6, as follows:

 
 RJUST-6.
*
* Using Fujitsu-specific Function...
*
     MOVE TEST-VALUE TO INPUT-FIELD
     IF (INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES)
         move INPUT-FIELD (1: function STORED-CHAR-LENGTH (INFIELD))
              to OUTPUT-FIELD
     END-IF.


Starting Benchmark...
50,000,000 Iterations
RJUST-1 00:35:01
RJUST-2 00:41:52
RJUST-3 00:07:33
RJUST-4 00:06:42
RJUST-5 00:06:43
RJUST-6 00:06:44

CONCLUSIONS:

1. The REVERSED function, while elegant, is SIGNIFICANTLY less
efficient at measuring string length
   than alternative approaches.
2. Efficent arithmetic on different platforms can make SIGNIFICANT
differences to performance.
3. COBOL "Benchmarks" that rely on fetching the clock, without
consistently converting to
   ticks for time arithmetic, are suspect. In multi-processing
environments like Windows it
   is difficult to compare results without doing multiple runs of the
same test. Even then it is
   STILL suspect... 
4. Despite NOT being platform independent I shall continue to use the
Fujitsu intrinsic
   "STORED-CHAR-LENGTH" because it requires less source and came out
well in this benchmark.

 Finally, here is the Benchmark code with the amendments I made so it
could run in the Fujitsu
 environment and do so efficiently.

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.  RJUSTEST.
*
*    50,000,000 Iterations on PIII 550
*    RJUST-1 37:47:43 (HH:MM:SS)
*    RJUST-2 00:31:80
*    RJUST-3 00:29:66
*    RJUST-4 00:35:70
*    RJUST-5 00:35:54
*
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  INPUT-FIELD.
     03  INPUT-CHAR              PIC  X(01)  OCCURS 30 TIMES
                                             INDEXED BY ICX.
 01  INFIELD  redefines INPUT-FIELD pic x(30). *> STORED-CHAR-LENGTH
function needs
                                               *> an elementary field.
 01  OUTPUT-FIELD                PIC  X(30).
 01  CHAR                        PIC  X(01).
 01  COUNTER                     PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
 01  INPUT-PTR                   PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
 01  INPUT-SIZE                  PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
 01  OUTPUT-PTR                  PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
 01  J                           pic s9(4) comp-5.
 01  K                           pic s9(4) comp-5.

 01  TEST-VALUE                  PIC  X(30)       VALUE
                     "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ".
 01  LOOP                        PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 50000000.
 01  LOOP-DISP                   PIC  ZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9  VALUE SPACES.
 01  TIME-START                  PIC  9(06).
 01  TIME-RJUST-1                PIC  9(06).
 01  TIME-RJUST-2                PIC  9(06).
 01  TIME-RJUST-3                PIC  9(06).
 01  TIME-RJUST-4                PIC  9(06).
 01  TIME-RJUST-5                PIC  9(06).
 01  TIME-RJUST-6                PIC  9(06).

 01  TIME-WORK.
     03  TW-T1                   PIC  9(06).
     03  TW-T1-R                 REDEFINES TW-T1.
         05  TW-T1-MM                PIC  9(02).
         05  TW-T1-SS                PIC  9(02).
         05  TW-T1-TH                PIC  9(02).
     03  TW-T2                   PIC  9(06).
     03  TW-T2-R                 REDEFINES TW-T2.
         05  TW-T2-MM                PIC  9(02).
         05  TW-T2-SS                PIC  9(02).
         05  TW-T2-TH                PIC  9(02).
     03  TW-TD.
         05  TW-DESC             PIC  X(08) VALUE "RJUST-X ".
         05  TW-TD-MM            PIC  9(02) VALUE 0.
         05                      PIC  X(01) VALUE ":".
         05  TW-TD-SS            PIC  9(02) VALUE 0.
         05                      PIC  X(01) VALUE ":".
         05  TW-TD-TH            PIC  9(02) VALUE 0.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 TEST-3-START.
     display 'Starting Benchmark...' upon syserr
     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:6) TO TIME-START.
     PERFORM RJUST-1 LOOP TIMES.
     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:6) TO TIME-RJUST-1.
     PERFORM RJUST-2 LOOP TIMES.
     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:6) TO TIME-RJUST-2.
     PERFORM RJUST-3 LOOP TIMES.
     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:6) TO TIME-RJUST-3.
     PERFORM RJUST-4 LOOP TIMES.
     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:6) TO TIME-RJUST-4.
     PERFORM RJUST-5 LOOP TIMES.
     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:6) TO TIME-RJUST-5.
     PERFORM RJUST-6 LOOP TIMES.
     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:6) TO TIME-RJUST-6.

     MOVE LOOP TO LOOP-DISP.
     DISPLAY LOOP-DISP " Iterations" upon syserr.
     MOVE "RJUST-1 "   TO TW-DESC.
     MOVE TIME-START   TO TW-T1.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-1 TO TW-T2.
     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME.
     MOVE "RJUST-2 "   TO TW-DESC.
     MOVE TIME-START TO TW-T1.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-2 TO TW-T2.
     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME.
     MOVE "RJUST-3 "   TO TW-DESC.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-2 TO TW-T1.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-3 TO TW-T2.
     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME.
     MOVE "RJUST-4 "   TO TW-DESC.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-3 TO TW-T1.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-4 TO TW-T2.
     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME.
     MOVE "RJUST-5 "   TO TW-DESC.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-4 TO TW-T1.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-5 TO TW-T2.
     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME.
     MOVE "RJUST-6 "   TO TW-DESC.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-5 TO TW-T1.
     MOVE TIME-RJUST-6 TO TW-T2.
     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME.

     STOP RUN.

 DISPLAY-TIME.
     IF (TW-T1-TH > TW-T2-TH)
         IF (TW-T2-SS < 1)
             ADD 60 TO TW-T1-SS
             SUBTRACT 1 FROM TW-T2-MM
         END-IF
         COMPUTE TW-TD-TH = TW-T2-TH + 100 - TW-T1-TH
         SUBTRACT 1 FROM TW-T2-SS
     ELSE
         COMPUTE TW-TD-TH = TW-T2-TH - TW-T1-TH
     END-IF.
     IF (TW-T1-SS > TW-T2-SS)
         COMPUTE TW-TD-SS = TW-T2-SS + 60 - TW-T1-SS
         SUBTRACT 1 FROM TW-T2-MM
     ELSE
         COMPUTE TW-TD-SS = TW-T2-SS - TW-T1-SS
     END-IF.
     COMPUTE TW-TD-MM = TW-T2-MM - TW-T1-MM.
     DISPLAY TW-TD upon syserr.

 RJUST-1.
     MOVE TEST-VALUE TO INPUT-FIELD.
     MOVE 1 TO COUNTER.
     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
         MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE(INPUT-FIELD) TO OUTPUT-FIELD
         INSPECT OUTPUT-FIELD TALLYING COUNTER FOR LEADING SPACES
*         STRING INPUT-FIELD DELIMITED BY SIZE
*             INTO OUTPUT-FIELD WITH POINTER COUNTER
         compute J = counter + 1
         compute K = function LENGTH (INPUT-FIELD) - counter
         move output-field (J: K)
              to output-field
     END-IF.

 RJUST-2.
     MOVE TEST-VALUE TO INPUT-FIELD.
     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
         compute INPUT-SIZE =  FUNCTION LENGTH (INPUT-FIELD)
         move INPUT-SIZE to   INPUT-PTR 
         PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-FIELD (INPUT-PTR:1) NOT = SPACE
             SUBTRACT 1 FROM INPUT-PTR
         END-PERFORM
         SUBTRACT INPUT-PTR FROM INPUT-SIZE
         ADD 1 INPUT-SIZE GIVING OUTPUT-PTR
         MOVE INPUT-FIELD (1:INPUT-PTR)
                 TO OUTPUT-FIELD (OUTPUT-PTR:INPUT-PTR)
         IF INPUT-SIZE > 0
             MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD (1:INPUT-SIZE)
         END-IF
     END-IF.

 RJUST-3.
     MOVE TEST-VALUE TO INPUT-FIELD
     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
         compute INPUT-SIZE = FUNCTION LENGTH (INPUT-FIELD) 
         PERFORM VARYING ICX
                 FROM INPUT-SIZE BY -1
                 UNTIL INPUT-CHAR (ICX) NOT = SPACE
         END-PERFORM
         SET INPUT-PTR TO ICX
         SUBTRACT INPUT-PTR FROM INPUT-SIZE
         ADD 1 INPUT-SIZE GIVING OUTPUT-PTR
         MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD
         MOVE INPUT-FIELD (1:INPUT-PTR)
                 TO OUTPUT-FIELD (OUTPUT-PTR:INPUT-PTR)
     END-IF.

 RJUST-4.
     MOVE TEST-VALUE TO INPUT-FIELD
     MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD.
     IF (INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES)
         compute INPUT-PTR = FUNCTION LENGTH(INPUT-FIELD)
         PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-FIELD(INPUT-PTR:1) NOT = SPACE
             SUBTRACT 1 FROM INPUT-PTR
         END-PERFORM
         COMPUTE OUTPUT-PTR = FUNCTION LENGTH(OUTPUT-FIELD)
                              - INPUT-PTR + 1
         MOVE 1 TO INPUT-PTR
         PERFORM UNTIL OUTPUT-PTR > 0
             ADD 1 TO INPUT-PTR OUTPUT-PTR
         END-PERFORM
         MOVE INPUT-FIELD(INPUT-PTR:) TO OUTPUT-FIELD(OUTPUT-PTR:)
     END-IF.

 RJUST-5.
     MOVE TEST-VALUE TO INPUT-FIELD
     MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD.
     IF (INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES)
         compute INPUT-PTR =  FUNCTION LENGTH(INPUT-FIELD)
         PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-FIELD(INPUT-PTR:1) NOT = SPACE
             SUBTRACT 1 FROM INPUT-PTR
         END-PERFORM
         COMPUTE OUTPUT-PTR = FUNCTION LENGTH(OUTPUT-FIELD)
                              - INPUT-PTR + 1
         IF (OUTPUT-PTR < 1)
             COMPUTE INPUT-PTR = 2 - OUTPUT-PTR
             MOVE 1 TO OUTPUT-PTR
         ELSE
             MOVE 1 TO INPUT-PTR
         END-IF
         MOVE INPUT-FIELD(INPUT-PTR:) TO OUTPUT-FIELD(OUTPUT-PTR:)
     END-IF.
 
 RJUST-6.
*
* Using Fujitsu-specific Function...
*
     MOVE TEST-VALUE TO INPUT-FIELD
     IF (INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES)
         move INPUT-FIELD (1: function STORED-CHAR-LENGTH (INFIELD))
              to OUTPUT-FIELD
     END-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T08:24:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Using this test program on a Unisys MCP system, I get relative performance
figures for the five approaches of  9, 11, 7, 10 and 10 respectively.

From that I would conclude that the while longhand approach associated with
RJUST-3 is somewhat more efficient than the "reverse" approach of RJUST-1,
the longhand approaches associated with RJUST-2, RJUST-4 and RJUST-5 are
*less* efficient than the "reverse" approach.  Moreover, the differences are
arguably not enough to matter except in "critical paths" in user programs,
certainly not of the magnitude that you encountered.  .

In other words, the performance of INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE on Unisys MCP
systems is acceptable in comparison to the coding alternatives, and I
believe it's much clearer, which counts for a lot.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com...
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> >
…[222 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T11:44:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
I can't speak to ex-Univac systems; I don't have ready access to one.

The reason RJUST-3 performed better than the others on the
ex-Burroughs-Large-System architecture was that it used INDEXING instead of
SUBSCRIPTING, and indexing (which maintains its values in offsets that
require no further manipulation on reference, rather than as subscripts to
which array offset and stride must both be applied arithmetically) is far
more efficient than subscripting, all other things being equal (namely, the
table of PIC X(1) items is at the beginning of the record!).

    -Chuck Stevens

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com...
>
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
> news:bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> > Using this test program on a Unisys MCP system, I get relative
performance
> > figures for the five approaches of  9, 11, 7, 10 and 10 respectively.
> >
> > From that I would conclude that the while longhand approach associated
> with
> > RJUST-3 is somewhat more efficient than the "reverse" approach of
RJUST-1,
> > the longhand approaches associated with RJUST-2, RJUST-4 and RJUST-5 are
> > *less* efficient than the "reverse" approach.  Moreover, the differences
> are
> > arguably not enough to matter except in "critical paths" in user
programs,
> > certainly not of the magnitude that you encountered.  .
> >
> > In other words, the performance of INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE on Unisys
MCP
> > systems is acceptable in comparison to the coding alternatives, and I
> > believe it's much clearer, which counts for a lot.
> >
>
> I found exactly the opposite when I ran the benchmark. See posted results
in
> this thread. Is COMP the most efficient  arithmetic usage on UNIVAC?
>
> Pete.
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T06:39:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> ... indexing (which maintains its values in offsets that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> table of PIC X(1) items is at the beginning of the record!).

I mention the following because of those out there who may not understand
the tradeoffs between subscripting and indexing.

An actual reference to a table using an index should (in general) always
be at least as fast as subscripting, usually faster. However, there are some
other considerations. The COBOL standard does not specify how indexes
are stored, it only provides the framework for the compiler vendor to use
whatever efficient method is applicable to the target hardware. The vendor
could simply implement indexes as subscripts, and the two would be the
same. Most implementations I am aware of store indexes as table offsets
{i.e. (subscript - 1) * entry size}. The Burroughs/Unisys V Series COBOL
compilers actually stored both the occurrence number (subscript) and the
offset, IIRC. However,the compiler must generate code to convert between
subscript and offset when a SET or compare is done between integer and
index data items. The actual tradeoff is a complex relation involving the
number of conversions between integer and offset, the number of actual
references and the relative speed of  the conversion. But if the program
averages 100 conversions and compares between indexes and integers for
every indexed table reference, you can be sure that indexing will be slower
overall than subscripting, in that program.

Another interesting situation is when the table elements are one byte/word
(depending on the hardware's addressing schema) long. If the hardware
architecture is such that the address of the beginning of the table is greater
than zero, so that the address of FirstElement - ElementLength >= zero,
indexing and subscripting can be identical in performance. In this case, the
compiler can simply move the subscript to the index register and reference
(FirstElement - 1) + index. Zero overhead.

My point (as ever :) is that indexing will not be faster than subscripting in
every possible situation. In order to evaluate these tradeoffs, a programmer
must understand at least generally how things are done at the machine code
level. I shudder when I see programmers blithely using an exponential
operator in a bit fiddling routine, believing that because it looks elegant
and concise it therefore must also be efficient. Ouch! Give me a break! :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T13:33:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com...

> An actual reference to a table using an index should (in general) always
> be at least as fast as subscripting, usually faster. However, there are
some
> other considerations. The COBOL standard does not specify how indexes
> are stored, it only provides the framework for the compiler vendor to use
> whatever efficient method is applicable to the target hardware. The vendor
> could simply implement indexes as subscripts, and the two would be the
> same.

True.  There is also nothing in the standard to prevent the implementor from
using the *least* efficient possible format for indices, or even from
implementing them using an electromechanically-actuated abacus attached to
the side of the computer, either!

In the general case, though, I think it's clear from the restrictions on
index-names -- that they are uniquely associated with a table and the ways
in which their values can be manipulated are limited (SEARCH, PERFORM, SET,
for example) -- that most implementors *will* use a format that is no less
efficient than the most efficient possible format in which a subscript can
be specified.

> However,the compiler must generate code to convert between
> subscript and offset when a SET or compare is done between integer and
> index data items.

I think you mean integers and index-names in standard-speak.  Index data
items are something else.  The results of comparing a number (literal,
integer, data item, whatever) against an index-name are undefined.

> The actual tradeoff is a complex relation involving the
> number of conversions between integer and offset, the number of actual
> references and the relative speed of  the conversion. But if the program
> averages 100 conversions and compares between indexes and integers for
> every indexed table reference, you can be sure that indexing will be
slower
> overall than subscripting, in that program.

I have been supporting compilers too long to take the position "Nobody in
his right mind would ever do that."  But I must comment that, if a program
averaged 100 *manipulations* of the value of either a sole-purpose subscript
or of an index for every *reference* to the table that that subscript or
index was intended to reference, I would have to wonder about the
efficiency, to say nothing of the logic, of the program.

I can imagine *that* somebody would do that, but I have to admit I can't
think of a particularly productive reason to do so.

In every implementation I know of, referencing table entries using indexing
is never slower than referencing the same table entries using indexing.  In
a very limited subset of cases it may be no faster.

SET UP BY and SET DOWN BY could be more costly than similar manipulations of
subscripts, if the efficiency of the internal format associated with the
subscript is the same or better than that used for indices (e.g., "77
<subscript-name> PIC S9(11) BINARY EXTENDED", which *everybody* knows is
*always* the most efficient specification for an integer ;-)   ), but for
just about any other format the cost of accessing the containing item will
probably outweigh the cost of arithmetic manipulation of the value.

> My point (as ever :) is that indexing will not be faster than subscripting
in
> every possible situation.

No, it won't, but it's probable that it will, and it's really unlikely that
it will be slower in any practical application.

> In order to evaluate these tradeoffs, a programmer
> must understand at least generally how things are done at the machine code
> level.

Agreed!

> I shudder when I see programmers blithely using an exponential
> operator in a bit fiddling routine, believing that because it looks
elegant
> and concise it therefore must also be efficient. Ouch! Give me a break!
:-)

This, and doing a hundred manipulations of the value of an index -- or a
sole-purpose subscript -- for every reference to the table with which it's
associated ... ;-)

I'm reminded of a sequence from "Beyond the Fringe" regarding various
methods used by coal miners to extract coal from the coal face, which ran
something like 'Then there's running at it head first, known as the "bad"
method of getting coal out of the mine ... then there's scrabbling at it
with your bare hands, known as the "almost-as-bad" method of getting coal
out of the mine ..."

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> associated ... ;-)

We're in agreement, Chuck.  I agree that indexing will almost always be
more efficient, and rarely less efficient in practice. But I have seen some
examples in real code over the years of programmers using indexes in
ways that were less efficient than subscripts, occasionally significantly
so. I don't know if you see as much crummy real world code as I do, but
there are some astonishingly dumb examples out there. I still remember
an example that a friend encountered and showed me over 30 years ago.
It was a B3500 COBOL 68 program that contained literally hundreds of
these references to one character fields:

        03    FIELD-X    PIC  X(01).

        EXAMINE FIELD-X TALLYING LEADING ZEROS.
        IF TALLY < 1  ...

The mind truly boggles.  :-)
```

- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com>`

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com...

> We're in agreement, Chuck.  I agree that indexing will almost always be
> more efficient, and rarely less efficient in practice. But I have seen
some
> examples in real code over the years of programmers using indexes in
> ways that were less efficient than subscripts, occasionally significantly
> so. I don't know if you see as much crummy real world code as I do, but
> there are some astonishingly dumb examples out there.

Oh, believe me, in my twenty years supporting the Unisys MCP/AS COBOL
compilers (all three dialects), I have seen some astonishingly dumb
examples.

> I still remember
> an example that a friend encountered and showed me over 30 years ago.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

Yeah, that's pretty good ...

I remember one on the B1000.  Customer was an RPG shop, and we kept
recommending that they convert to COBOL to consolidate some of their job
streams.  They were using B-Indexing for ISAM in RPG, which COBOL didn't
directly support.  Their approach to programming it in COBOL was to do a
sequential search of the file instead of building index tables in memory
when the file was opened as RPG did behind the user's back.  When they found
that the COBOL program didn't perform nearly as well as its RPG antecedent,
they raised a big ruckus with Burroughs about the efficiency of COBOL object
code as compared to RPG object code ...

Then there was a COBOL(68) cross-reference utility, originally written as a
xref-and-flowchart utility in COBOL by what has to have been a programmer
who spent many years writing in FORTRAN, and who had recently become
enamored of the ALTER verb as a fundamentally superior approach to good prog
ram design  ... Half the flowcharting stuff had been ripped out when I saw
it, but the remainder stubbornly resisted everyone's efforts at extraction
...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T13:05:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031211140707.634$2K@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bra68e$1l4r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> I remember one on the B1000.  Customer was an RPG shop, and we kept
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> code as compared to RPG object code ...


That reminds me of one I saw in the 70's, written by a 'converted' clerk.
It was comparing a sequential file of sorted transactions against another
sequential file of sorted master records. Somebody had complained that
the program was occasionally very slow. If the program didn't get an
equal match against the master file, it kept reading to the end of the file
looking for one. When it got to EOF on the master, it closed and opened
the master file, incremented a counter and if the counter was less than 9
it went through again, looking for a match on that same transaction. Once
it passed the master file 9 times and didn't find a match it printed that as
an error and started again with the next transaction.

Circa 1970 I traveled to several USAF bases in the US installing new
B3500 application software to replace old IBM 1401 punched card
applications. When I visited Nellis AFB in Las Vegas, one programmer
showed me a couple of good ones that he had kept. One was a punched
card, and the printed letters across the top had been erased and re-typed.
The user had called to complain that their inventory report wasn't being
updated, even after they had 'updated' the data cards. The other was a
group of punched cards that had been additionally punched with  two
half-inch holes and placed on an old style circular rolodex. That user
was surprised when he couldn't get a report from those data cards.

In 1980 one of our customers was having trouble reading their 8 inch
floppy disks. They were using a very strong magnet to stick the disks
to the side of a file cabinet drawer.
```

I got a T shirt from the Shark in ComputerWorld telling about the following:

I created a dBASE application for a user, and included procedures for backing up
the data with dire warnings about what would happen if this wasn't done.    A
few months later the database got corrupted.   The user remembered those dire
warnings and took her single backup floppy and backed up the corrupted database
before calling for help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: In defence of the indefensible. WAS: Re: COBOL array editing output

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T12:36:50+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 11-Dec-2003, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> I thought I'd seen it all.

Supposing....just supposing....

...that the programmer concerned had graduated from the Burroughs B500 into
the new B3500 environment.

(This was a fairly common growth path at the time.)

The COBOL Compiler on the B500 was notorious for its inefficient generated
code, when programmers used the COBOL IF verb.

In fact, it ALWAYS generated extra instructions to support an ELSE branch,
whether these were required or not.

As Chief Programmer on a Burroughs site at the time (1969), I endorsed a
local standard requiring people to AVOID the use of IF when writing COBOL
for this machine. We used ALTER instead, as it was extremely efficient and
(unlike the programming wimps of today <G>)  we never had any trouble
maintaining it. (All "switch" type comparisons were implemented with ALTER
and we only used IF when it was a KEY (string) comparison. Arithmetic
comparisons were carried out using subtraction and sign testing... You can
tell whether A >, <, or = B by subtracting A from B and testing the sign of
the result...)

If you find that amazing, consider that it was standard practice at the time
I came on board, to do a COBOL compile, punch an object deck, then patch the
cards to remove the redundant branches from the generated IFs, before
loading and running the program. At least the above was an attempt to avoid
patching object decks.

(Bear in mind that my team was composed exclusively of ex-B500 Assembler
programmers who had no qualms at all about modifying instructions
dynamically. They were just getting used to using COBOL and the body of
standard and best practices which we take for granted today, was not in
place then...)

Anyway, "everybody knew" that IF in COBOL was crap, and avoided it.

Now let's consider our hypothetical programmer moving out of this
environment into the new B3500 environment.

He hasn't had a course on the new machine (Hey, it's COBOL, right? We bought
it because Burroughs told us we could increase productivity and it was
platform independent... And training takes time and costs money...)

So our hypothetical programmer has had it drilled into him that IF is "Best
Avoided" and by now (it is a few years later), ALTER is being "frowned on"
as well.

But wait! There's this EXAMINE verb that can check for something being zero
or not WITHOUT using an IF!

Set the fields with an initial value of zero. Then test if they have
anything in them. No worries about arithmetic exceptions and the new
powerful hardware should cream it. After all, it is only one byte we're
checking...

While it is impossible to establish whether the above is actually what
happened or not, the point I am trying to make here is that, in my
experience, programmers (for the most part) are NOT stupid people. If they
do stupid things it is usually because they are operating on false
information or assumptions.

In 2003 it is easy to snigger at "solutions" from 30 years ago. Before you
do so, consider that someone in 2033 may do the same to that beautiful piece
of code you cut yesterday, of which you are so proud...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In defence of the indefensible. WAS: Re: COBOL array editing output

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T08:36:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031212093749.258$gy@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> the new B3500 environment.

Wasn't the B500 that peculiar machine that used a 5ms HD for main memory?
I was a mainframe Senior Systems Rep with Burroughs at the time, and I was
only vaguely aware of the smaller stuff. I remember the salesmen used to joke
that you had to program one of the old mechanical 'L' machines "with a screw-
driver and a file." :-)

> The COBOL Compiler on the B500 was notorious for its inefficient generated
> code, when programmers used the COBOL IF verb.
>
> In fact, it ALWAYS generated extra instructions to support an ELSE branch,
> whether these were required or not. ...

Note that you have an IF anyway. The IF could have tested FIELD-X for a
zero, or tested TALLY for < 1, but you still have an IF. :-)

Pete, your story is an excellent example of why I advocate learning what rules
apply to programming in general, what rules apply to the language, and what
rules apply to your platform. You need to know the WHY for the rules, as well
as the WHAT. :-)
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031212093749.258$gy@news.newsreader.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> > "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> > ...that the programmer concerned had graduated from the Burroughs B500
into
> > the new B3500 environment.
>
> Wasn't the B500 that peculiar machine that used a 5ms HD for main memory?
> I was a mainframe Senior Systems Rep with Burroughs at the time, and I was
> only vaguely aware of the smaller stuff. I remember the salesmen used to
joke
> that you had to program one of the old mechanical 'L' machines "with a
screw-
> driver and a file." :-)
>
I always thought it had main memory, but now you have me wondering. It was a
long time ago and I can't be absolutely sure any more. It DID use a fixed
hard drive as primary storage.

> > The COBOL Compiler on the B500 was notorious for its inefficient
generated
> > code, when programmers used the COBOL IF verb.
> >
> > In fact, it ALWAYS generated extra instructions to support an ELSE
branch,
> > whether these were required or not. ...
>
> Note that you have an IF anyway. The IF could have tested FIELD-X for a
> zero, or tested TALLY for < 1, but you still have an IF. :-)
>
You have totally missed the point here. It was ALPHANUMERIC "IF"s that were
to be avoided.

> Pete, your story is an excellent example of why I advocate learning what
rules
> apply to programming in general, what rules apply to the language, and
what
> rules apply to your platform. You need to know the WHY for the rules, as
well
> as the WHAT. :-)

I wouldn't disagree with that, but you need to be careful that the "in
depth" knowledge you acquire has some reasonable life span during which you
can get a return on your investment. And it was not the point of my story.

The point is: Be gentle in your criticism of other people's code, and don't
assume they are idiots.

When I encounter code that looks "strange" to me, I approach it as a
possible learning experience, not as a chance to criticise. Only after
thorough examination and having considered possible WHYs, would I then
decide whether it is crap or not. Sometimes I adopt things I learned in this
way, other times I totally discard it. And there are cases where I recognise
that what I'm seeing is "better", but I probably won't adopt it because of
inertia and the fact that what I have is working for me.

I guess I am also sensitive to old time code taking stick from the new breed
who have had the benefit of computer science educations. It is easy to find
fault with stuff that was written before there were best practices and
standards and programming theory. People did the best they could with what
was there. For the most part, it worked. To see the same people who smugly
criticise the old stuff, resisting fiercely the new concepts in programming
(like OO), just adds insult to injury.

In 20 years it will be irrelevant and many of those who are so smug in their
"knowledge", will find that that "knowledge" is obsolete, just as we
constantly learned new stuff only to find there was no demand for it 5 years
later.

Enjoy your time in the sun. Clouds are gathering over the horizon....

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In defence of the indefensible. WAS: Re: COBOL array editing output

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-13T07:41:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031213084227.375$Dn@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031212093749.258$gy@news.newsreader.com> <3fda3be0_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> to be avoided.

Sorry, I did miss that.

> > Pete, your story is an excellent example of why I advocate learning what rules
> > apply to programming in general, what rules apply to the language, and what
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> can get a return on your investment.

Don't understand the 'but'. The REASON you DO need to know the scope of
your knowledge is precisely because of it's potentially limited range, in time
and scope, and if you don't know those limits, you can apply the knowledge
inappropriately, as your store illustrates.

> And it was not the point of my story.

I agree, but when in this NG do we limit comments to the specific point
of a message? ;-)

> The point is: Be gentle in your criticism of other people's code, and don't
> assume they are idiots.

I agree, don't assume they are idiots. You may safely assume, however, that
when you see a pattern of bad code, they are either ignorant, careless, or
lacking in aptitude. You can't help the latter, but the former can be helped
by proper constructive criticism and teaching. Please forgive me if I am not
being 'gentle'. There is an expression, common in the Peace Corps: "If you
give a man a fish, you feed him a meal, if you teach him to fish, you feed
him for a lifetime." To my mind, it is better, when helping a person with a
programming problem, to teach him to correct the pattern of thinking that
led him to the problem in the first place, so he may be able apply that in a
much wider context. This gets deeper into one's psyche than does simply
dealing with coding facts, perhaps. My point? Don't excuse crud, teach
the crud'or how not to write crud. Blame is not the point, getting it right is
the point. If you are about to undergo neurosurgery, what attitude on this
subject (getting it right) would you want your neurosurgeon to have? If
you were a cook, would you rather people said, after eating a meal you
had cooked, "Thanks, that was okay" or "Thanks, that was the best ... I
ever ate?" To me, it's not a matter of boosting my ego (though it used to
be), it is a matter of having the satisfaction that I have put my time and
talents to good use, that I 'did it right'.

> When I encounter code that looks "strange" to me, I approach it as a
> possible learning experience, not as a chance to criticise. Only after
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> inertia and the fact that what I have is working for me.

The point is not negative criticism, but constructive criticism. We must apply
constructive criticism to everything we evaluate, either our work or others'.
Without constructive criticism, you cannot assess whether anything has flaws.
That criticism need not be verbalized, unless you are trying to teach (my
primary motive for participating in this NG, and probably of most of the 'old
timers' here) or make a point. :-)

> I guess I am also sensitive to old time code taking stick from the new breed
> who have had the benefit of computer science educations. It is easy to find
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (like OO), just adds insult to injury.

If you are referring to me, you misrepresent my positions. :-) I do not 'hack' at
those who wrote much poorer code before structured principles were well
understood, I was one of them. :-) You're right, we did the best we could with
what we knew at the time. But you make an unwarranted assumption to think
that pointing out the flaws in it is equivalent to criticizing those who wrote it.
How would you know that structured code was better, for example, if you did
not know the relative pros and cons? A programmer who never dealt with
spaghetti code might not know just how bad it was, unless it was pointed out
to them. Knowing why we do it the way we do is a Good Thing.

The purpose of any criticism should be to help, not harm. As far as resisting
or promoting new technology, that should depend on the benefits (or lack) of
the new technology. Take a look at 'fly by wire' airplanes. A 'fly by wire'
airplane is vastly more complex than a conventional one. There are many
advantages to 'fly by wire', but one of the most important ones is that it is
MUCH EASIER to fly a FBW plane. Consider automobiles. A major design
trend is to make automobiles EASIER to drive, less easy to make errors.
When I look at some of the new technology programmers now use, I see
astonishingly steep learning curves to master tools and techniques that are
not necessarily significantly better. After structured techniques had been
widely used, there was virtually no one who would question their efficacy.
It was trivially easy to show reams and reams of statistics to prove it was
far and away better, in all respects. Our industry has invested truly vast
resources in OO. Yet, I have yet to see a single statistic to show that OO
is better across the board, in either time, cost or reliability. Better in some
cases, yes. Better always, no. Why have we invested so heavily in what,
even after 20 years, cannot be shown to be truly superior? To not question
a situation like that is naive, to say the least. The question is not Old vs.
New, but "which is best?" and "Where's my best ROI?" Those questions
are not being asked anymore. We are stampeding like lemmings into the
latest gee-whiz technology with not a thought as to if it is a good idea or not.
I am sick and tired of seeing clients pour millions of dollars into the latest
'technology du jour', and wind up with nothing to show for it. I see this every
day with my own eyes. I am not so gullible as to endorse such idiocy, just
because it is "the thing to do." Yet this seems to be the way our industry is
heading. The proof of the pudding is in the eating, but what happens when
people start ignoring that it tastes like crap and gives no nutrition? Nothing
good.

> In 20 years it will be irrelevant and many of those who are so smug in their
> "knowledge", will find that that "knowledge" is obsolete, just as we
> constantly learned new stuff only to find there was no demand for it 5 years
> later.

Precisely my point. Know the scope of your knowledge. That doesn't teach
you any new knowledge, but it helps you to know when you need it. :-)

> Enjoy your time in the sun. Clouds are gathering over the horizon....

You're not kidding. I don't know about the rest of the world, but there used to
be, here in the US, a real desire for excellence. You can see it in architecture
and craftsmanship of things built more than a few decades ago. It grieves me
to see people churning out crud and not caring that it is crud. Michelangelo
was painting the Sistine Chapel ceiling when someone observed that he was
taking as much time with the details invisible from the floor as he did on the
parts visible from the floor. They asked him "Why do you spend so much time
on those details? No one will ever see them." He replied "I see them." That
is one reason why he was such a great artist; he did not, would not, crank out
crud. He would rather not do it than do it poorly. I have always had a strong
desire for excellence, and I strive to encourage others to excellence as well.
When we are at the end of our lives, do we want to say "I have produced a
lifetime of 'get by' work" or do we want to say "I have produced a lifetime
of 'excellence?" Sometimes you get through with this message, sometimes
you don't. But I have to try.

Fortunately, my sunset is nearing. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In defence of the indefensible. WAS: Re: COBOL array editing output

_(thread depth: 15)_

```
A good response Judson, which, for the most part, I don't take issue with.

There are a few caveats which I have included below...

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031213084227.375$Dn@news.newsreader.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

hundreds of

> > > > > >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

B500 into

> > >

memory?
> > > I was a mainframe Senior Systems Rep with Burroughs at the time, and I
was
> > > only vaguely aware of the smaller stuff. I remember the salesmen used
to joke
> > > that you had to program one of the old mechanical 'L' machines "with a
screw-
> > > driver and a file." :-)
> >
> > I always thought it had main memory, but now you have me wondering. It
was a
> > long time ago and I can't be absolutely sure any more. It DID use a
fixed
> > hard drive as primary storage.
> >

When you first looked at the above code did it not cross your mind: "I
wonder why the person who did this, did it this way?"

Rather than rejecting out of hand anything that does not "gel" with one's
personal perception of "right thinking", isn't there a case for opening the
mind and striving for "understanding"?

This approach works for me. I have learned much by seeking to understand WHY
people do what they do, whether it is in writing COBOL or in other walks of
Life...

(I would stress that it isn't always successful, and sometimes I just come
away bewildered <G>. I still think it is worth the effort...)

> > > > The COBOL Compiler on the B500 was notorious for its inefficient
generated
> > > > code, when programmers used the COBOL IF verb.
> > > >
> > > > In fact, it ALWAYS generated extra instructions to support an ELSE
branch,
> > > > whether these were required or not. ...
> > >
> > > Note that you have an IF anyway. The IF could have tested FIELD-X for
a
> > > zero, or tested TALLY for < 1, but you still have an IF. :-)
> >
> > You have totally missed the point here. It was ALPHANUMERIC "IF"s that
were
> > to be avoided.
>
> Sorry, I did miss that.
>
> > > Pete, your story is an excellent example of why I advocate learning
what rules
> > > apply to programming in general, what rules apply to the language, and
what
> > > rules apply to your platform. You need to know the WHY for the rules,
as well
> > > as the WHAT. :-)
> >
> > I wouldn't disagree with that, but you need to be careful that the "in
> > depth" knowledge you acquire has some reasonable life span during which
you
> > can get a return on your investment.
>
> Don't understand the 'but'. The REASON you DO need to know the scope of
> your knowledge is precisely because of it's potentially limited range, in
time
> and scope, and if you don't know those limits, you can apply the knowledge
> inappropriately, as your store illustrates.

My story was intended to illustrate not that knowledge was applied
inappropriately, but that the knowledge available was used to the best of
the person's ability. With hindsight and greater experience, it is easy to
decide the solution was not a good one. (For all we know, the actual object
code may have been quite efficient using this approach. Obviously, the
"solution" must have worked, so it met at least one of the prime criteria
for program development...[it is nice to be exactly on topic for a
change...<G>])

>
> > And it was not the point of my story.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, the latitude here provides fun and that is no bad thing...

> > The point is: Be gentle in your criticism of other people's code, and
don't
> > assume they are idiots.
>
> I agree, don't assume they are idiots. You may safely assume, however,
that
> when you see a pattern of bad code, they are either ignorant, careless, or
> lacking in aptitude.

That is a somewhat narrow view that excludes other possibilities which may
not be palatable to you.... (Ever consider it may not be the "other" person
whose perception is less than optimal?)

It all hinges around the definition of "bad" code and that one has been
thrashed to death here many times. The bottom line is that while we can
agree on certain specific cases as being "bad" code, we can't ALWAYS do so,
and my perception of what is bad could be different from yours. (We might be
using different priorities for development, for example...).

>You can't help the latter, but the former can be helped
> by proper constructive criticism and teaching.

I have spent a large part of my career teaching and motivating others. I
find that being judgemental is just counter productive.

Some of the people I trained have gone on to hold senior posts and I am very
proud of them. I have no reason to believe that the methods I employ are
unsuccessful. Criticising code is a last resort; there are many ways to
encourage better coding practice that do not require criticism.

> Please forgive me if I am not
> being 'gentle'. There is an expression, common in the Peace Corps: "If you
> give a man a fish, you feed him a meal, if you teach him to fish, you feed
> him for a lifetime."

I am familiar with this aphorism.

>To my mind, it is better, when helping a person with a
> programming problem, to teach him to correct the pattern of thinking that
> led him to the problem in the first place, so he may be able apply that in
a
> much wider context.

Hmmm...sounds like "brain washing" to me <G>...

If we are going to teach people to "think" (and I agree that this is
possible; there are standard philosophy courses at many Universities, that
teach straight and crooked thinking), that is not the same as teaching them
how to code in a given computer programming language.

I don't see the need to reprogram someone's thought process in the name of
correcting their computer code.

It is presumptuous in the extreme to assume that there is only one "right"
way to go about solving a problem. Besides, what works for me may simply
stifle the imagination of someone else. I LIKE the fact that people approach
problems differently and I have no problem with lateral thinking, thinking
outside the square, or whatever it takes for someone to reach a solution.

As long as the solution meets the desired criteria, I have no problem with
how it was reached, or whether it "toes the Party Line" or not.


>This gets deeper into one's psyche than does simply
> dealing with coding facts, perhaps. My point? Don't excuse crud, teach
> the crud'or how not to write crud.

I have never advocated "excusing crud"; we differ in how we would deal with
crud when it is produced.

>Blame is not the point, getting it right is
> the point.

Yes, I endorse that completely. Blame is just wasted emotion (like shame and
regret; if you are ashamed of what you did, take responsibility for it and
try to the best of your ability to correct it, regretting past action is a
waste of time also; learn by it and avoid repeating the mistakes of the past
today...).

It is essential, however, that we have a clear definition of what is
"right", in the form of desired goals that everyone is agreed upon, rather
than "the Party Line", "What the Boss says", "That's the way we do things
here...", "Because I say so..." or similar Authoritarian positions.


> If you are about to undergo neurosurgery, what attitude on this
> subject (getting it right) would you want your neurosurgeon to have? If
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
When, in fact, you did it to the best of  your abilty, based on the
information you currently have available. Just like the programmer who coded
the EXAMINEs above.

> > When I encounter code that looks "strange" to me, I approach it as a
> > possible learning experience, not as a chance to criticise. Only after
> > thorough examination and having considered possible WHYs, would I then
> > decide whether it is crap or not. Sometimes I adopt things I learned in
this
> > way, other times I totally discard it. And there are cases where I
recognise
> > that what I'm seeing is "better", but I probably won't adopt it because
of
> > inertia and the fact that what I have is working for me.
>
> The point is not negative criticism, but constructive criticism. We must
apply
> constructive criticism to everything we evaluate, either our work or
others'.

Nope. Disagree here. Criticism is ALWAYS destructive. "Constructive
criticism" is simply an oxymoron.

The problem with "constructive criticism" is that it requires an "Authority"
to make it, and it is personal.

A better solution is for the whole group to review something with a view to
improving it. It should be impersonal and it should be considered "team
property". If desired goals are not being attained, then the person or
persons who have produced the dodgy stuff should have the failed results
pointed out ("The code doesn't seem to be getting the correct balance...",
"These routines seem to be taking a long time to run...") and be given a
chance to go away and review it for themselves. Only if/when they request
assistance, would I get involved, and I am not above getting expertise in
from outside to assist us if we are unable to solve something. I also have
no problem with the iterative approach where complex software "grows"
towards a solution rather than a "Big Bang" that must be "right" on day 1.

> Without constructive criticism, you cannot assess whether anything has
flaws.
> That criticism need not be verbalized, unless you are trying to teach (my
> primary motive for participating in this NG, and probably of most of the
'old
> timers' here) or make a point. :-)
>

The contributions here are all valuable in my opinion (even the "bad" ones
have entertainment value <G>). I contribute here because it is fun; when it
stops being so, I'll go away. Any benefits from my contributions are purely
incidental, and if anyone gets anything from me that they can use, well,
that's a bonus.This is not to say I don't take it seriously and I DO TRY to
help occasionally. But that is not my prime motivation for being here.

I wouldn't presume to "teach" here; I am happy to observe and comment.

> > I guess I am also sensitive to old time code taking stick from the new
breed
> > who have had the benefit of computer science educations. It is easy to
find
> > fault with stuff that was written before there were best practices and
> > standards and programming theory. People did the best they could with
what
> > was there. For the most part, it worked. To see the same people who
smugly
> > criticise the old stuff, resisting fiercely the new concepts in
programming
> > (like OO), just adds insult to injury.
>
> If you are referring to me, you misrepresent my positions. :-)

Don't be sensitive; it wasn't aimed at you personally, any more than anyone
else who criticises code without making any attempt to understand why it was
written.

> I do not 'hack' at
> those who wrote much poorer code before structured principles were well
> understood, I was one of them. :-) You're right, we did the best we could
with
> what we knew at the time. But you make an unwarranted assumption to think
> that pointing out the flaws in it is equivalent to criticizing those who
wrote it.

It depends on HOW you "point out the flaws"... snide, superior, comments
don't help anyone. (And let's not forget that the person who wrote it, isn't
here to defend themselves; that's the main reason I started this
thread...<G>)

> How would you know that structured code was better, for example, if you
did
> not know the relative pros and cons? A programmer who never dealt with
> spaghetti code might not know just how bad it was, unless it was pointed
out
> to them. Knowing why we do it the way we do is a Good Thing.
>

Yes, having a reason to do anything is a Good Thing. Closing one's mind to
the fact that there might be other (even, possibly, better...) ways to do
it, or believing that the "received wisdom" is the only way, is NOT a Good
Thing. Rather approach it on the basis: "This is the best I know how to do;
until something better comes along, I'll do it this way..."

<anecdote on the side - skip this if you are of a serious disposition and
are only interested in the debate...>

Many years ago, before my body was wrecked by a dissolute, self indulgent
lifestyle, I used to climb mountains.

(Hanging on to a rock face by your fingernails is excellent practice for
handling IT deadlines, but I didn't know that at the time <G>)

Anyway, the guy who was my climbing teacher was one of the wild breed of
Kiwis who live ONLY for mountains. He belonged to the same club as Edmund
Hilary and was of similar disposition.

On one occasion we were working our way up a ridge on Taranaki (Mt. Egmont,
8260 feet...believe me I have counted every one of them several
times...<G>), when we came to a sheer outcrop of rock called "Humphrey's
Castle".  Noticing there was a well worn path around this obstacle, and not
being entirely stupid, I started to walk around it. Bob, my teacher, pulled
me up and said: "Whered'ja think yer goin'?". I replied that there was an
established path around it and it made sense to take the low risk approach.
He looked at me with piercing blue eyes that showed just the faintest hint
of scorn, and said: "Peter, let's pretend this is the ONLY way..."  To my
surprise we scaled it, and I was rewarded with a smile from Bob for my
efforts, and a sandwich on the top of it. The view was worth the climb, and
the lesson has never been forgotten over the ensuing years.

To this day, whenever I hear the phrase: "the only way..." I think about a
summer afternoon sitting on Humphries Castle gazing down a sheer rock face
several thousand feet into green valleys below, and realise that "the only
way" is NOT the only way; it is the only way we can think of at the moment,
and sometimes, the hard way is the most rewarding.

</anecdote on the side - skip this if you are of a serious disposition and
are only interested on the debate...>

> The purpose of any criticism should be to help, not harm. As far as
resisting
> or promoting new technology, that should depend on the benefits (or lack)
of
> the new technology. Take a look at 'fly by wire' airplanes. A 'fly by
wire'
> airplane is vastly more complex than a conventional one. There are many
> advantages to 'fly by wire', but one of the most important ones is that it
is
> MUCH EASIER to fly a FBW plane.

I won't be drawn into a discussion on FBW, but you should realise that
opinion is not unanimous on this. Certainly, there is less control
pressure... <G>

> Consider automobiles. A major design
> trend is to make automobiles EASIER to drive, less easy to make errors.
> When I look at some of the new technology programmers now use, I see
> astonishingly steep learning curves to master tools and techniques that
are
> not necessarily significantly better. After structured techniques had been
> widely used, there was virtually no one who would question their efficacy.
> It was trivially easy to show reams and reams of statistics to prove it
was
> far and away better, in all respects. Our industry has invested truly vast
> resources in OO. Yet, I have yet to see a single statistic to show that OO
> is better across the board, in either time, cost or reliability. Better in
some
> cases, yes. Better always, no.

It is better... always. (Except for Batch processing.)

It has been resisted because it requires effort to master it and many of the
concepts are "new" and unfamiliar to COBOL people. There is a large vested
interest in the status quo also... many vendors, especially mainframe ones,
are not anxious to see procedural programming replaced; they have a huge
investment in COBOL compilers and infrastructure. With a shadow already
hanging over COBOL, you can't blame them for NOT rushing to implement OO.

IBM, to their credit, are offering options into networked working from the
mainframe. This will result inevitably in OO being picked up.

In the same way that you see structured as infinitely superior to
unstructured, I see OO as infinitely superior to procedural. However, the
story doesn't stop with OO Classes; it moves into component technology and
that is where the future lies.

> Why have we invested so heavily in what,
> even after 20 years, cannot be shown to be truly superior? To not question
> a situation like that is naive, to say the least. The question is not Old
vs.
> New, but "which is best?" and "Where's my best ROI?" Those questions
> are not being asked anymore. We are stampeding like lemmings into the
> latest gee-whiz technology with not a thought as to if it is a good idea
or not.

I don't think so. Certainly not on my watch.

> I am sick and tired of seeing clients pour millions of dollars into the
latest
> 'technology du jour', and wind up with nothing to show for it. I see this
every
> day with my own eyes. I am not so gullible as to endorse such idiocy, just
> because it is "the thing to do." Yet this seems to be the way our industry
is
> heading. The proof of the pudding is in the eating, but what happens when
> people start ignoring that it tastes like crap and gives no nutrition?
Nothing
> good.
>

All new technologies have their learning curve. This one is no exception.

If the emotive scenario you suggest above were really true, pretty soon,
people would be "starving" and we would see a huge swing back to procedural
programming. It hasn't happened and it isn't likely to. The landslide away
from procedural code and into networked solutions has already started and is
well established. The pebbles don't get to vote.

Can you seriously see the world giving up its networked solutions, quick
build tools, simplified user interfaces, and going back to the days when the
only communication with a computer was via the High Priests of Programming?
Get real <G>.

> > In 20 years it will be irrelevant and many of those who are so smug in
their
> > "knowledge", will find that that "knowledge" is obsolete, just as we
> > constantly learned new stuff only to find there was no demand for it 5
years
> > later.
>
> Precisely my point. Know the scope of your knowledge. That doesn't teach
> you any new knowledge, but it helps you to know when you need it. :-)
>

I'm not saying that knowledge is bad or shouldn't be acquired (quite the
reverse, actually...). It's simply that the world outside mainframe COBOL is
changing. The need for in-depth knowledge of generated code, machine
architectures, Assembler languages, is gone. Learning these things in
today's environment (unless you are employed by a mainframe manufacturer as
a Systems Specialist...and how long a lifetime do you think that job will
have? (ANSWER: As long as mainframes are a viable architecture and people
are still buying them.)), is about as useful as roller skates would be to a
steeplejack.

There is a new breed of programmer arriving. They don't have tunnel vision
and see all solutions in terms of one language.

They are better informed than we were, have a wider experience base to draw
on than we had, and haven't had their vision impaired by continual exposure
to character based screens.

The knowledge they seek is more application oriented than system. They have
tools we could only dream about. (Assemble complete data processing systems
in minutes using Drag 'n Drop and component technology), understand (I mean
REALLY understand...) the intricacies of the Relational Model and how to get
the best from it, and they would consider writing procedural code the same
way you would consider converting all your COBOL applications to Assembler.

> > Enjoy your time in the sun. Clouds are gathering over the horizon....
>
> You're not kidding. I don't know about the rest of the world, but there
used to
> be, here in the US, a real desire for excellence. You can see it in
architecture
> and craftsmanship of things built more than a few decades ago. It grieves
me
> to see people churning out crud and not caring that it is crud.
Michelangelo
> was painting the Sistine Chapel ceiling when someone observed that he was
> taking as much time with the details invisible from the floor as he did on
the
> parts visible from the floor. They asked him "Why do you spend so much
time
> on those details? No one will ever see them." He replied "I see them."
That
> is one reason why he was such a great artist; he did not, would not, crank
out
> crud. He would rather not do it than do it poorly. I have always had a
strong
> desire for excellence, and I strive to encourage others to excellence as
well.

Yes, excellence is its own reward. I agree with you that other
considerations see the desire for excellence bing eroded in many fields
these days, but I have faith in the young <G>. Many of them have the same
desire for excellence that we had and they will get it. (And before you
start, yes, there are many that don't too <G>...it is a question of whether
the bottle is half full or half empty...<G>).

> When we are at the end of our lives, do we want to say "I have produced a
> lifetime of 'get by' work" or do we want to say "I have produced a
lifetime
> of 'excellence?" Sometimes you get through with this message, sometimes
> you don't. But I have to try.
>

I want to say: "Upon my Honour, I have done my best." (And I got paid loads
of money for it and now I don't have to worry...).


> Fortunately, my sunset is nearing. :-)

Mine too <G>.

Pete.
```

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> pressure... <G>

In the mid-1970's my father (now a retired aeronautical engineer)
worked on a fly-by-wire control system for an existing fighter plane. 
The test pilots absolutely hated it.  It was like flying a plane with
the joystick from a Pong game.  They couldn't "feel" what the aircraft
was doing.  The engineers had to go back to the drawing board to build
in simulated feedback, so the pilots would feel resistance in the
stick as they pushed the control surfaces farther.  It was quite
complicated.

I saw a documentary on the History Channel that asserted the F117
Stealth Fighter could not be flown without computerized fly-by-wire. 
It's unstable in all three axes:  pitch, roll, and yaw.  The pilot
commands the plane to do something.  The computer figures out how to
move the control surfaces to give the the pilot what he wants.

I don't think FBW systems are written in COBOL...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fly by Wire

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-12-15T06:57:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0312150657.a61db86@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031212093749.258$gy@news.newsreader.com> <3fda3be0_8@news.athenanews.com> <20031213084227.375$Dn@news.newsreader.com> <3fdb9652_5@news.athenanews.com> <3FDD6100.39DD8124@worldnet.att.net>`

```
FBW is interesting.  I won't file on Airbus airplanes partially
because of FBW.  While many other airplanes use computer assist -
nearly all have a feature called Manual reversion - whereby the flight
controls can be directly manipulated by the pilot in the event of
failure.

Airbus airplanes do not have any sort of manual reversion.  In
addition, they make, in my opinion, excessive use of composite
materials - whith unproven track record as far as cycles before
failure.

Another "feature" of FBW in civilian airplanes is that the computer
will not let you exceed the DESIGN limitiations of the aircraft. 
There was a crash of an Airbus in Europe at an airshow caused in part
by this "feature".  Most aircraft have some level of capability beyond
the "design" limitations and had the pilots been able to make use of
this - the crash MIGHT not have occurred.

So - I check my equipment when flying and I schedule around Airbus
airplanes.  But that's just me.

Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message news:<3FDD6100.39DD8124@worldnet.att.net>...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> > 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't think FBW systems are written in COBOL...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fly by Wire

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T06:27:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0312170627.2fbe51ad@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031212093749.258$gy@news.newsreader.com> <3fda3be0_8@news.athenanews.com> <20031213084227.375$Dn@news.newsreader.com> <3fdb9652_5@news.athenanews.com> <3FDD6100.39DD8124@worldnet.att.net> <bfdfc3e8.0312150657.a61db86@posting.google.com> <9NT6qtBSeg3$Ew72@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
The flying term "stall" is commonly misconstrued to mean an "engine"
quit - or stalled.  It's not what it means.  It means the wing (or a
portion of the wing) has stopped flying because of loss of lift. 
There is significant controversy about this particular accident.

Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message news:<9NT6qtBSeg3$Ew72@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>...
