"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> A good response Judson, which, for the most part, I don't take issue with.
>
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> away bewildered <G>. I still think it is worth the effort...)

I completely agree. But what you're discussing is more under the heading
of 'blame'. I'm not talking about blame here, all of us do things that are
outright dumb at times or, as here, dumb only in current context. My point
is not to criticize those who did it, but simply to encourage programmers
to be more aware of the scope of their particular knowledge. I think you
would agree that's a good idea. :-)

> > > > > The COBOL Compiler on the B500 was notorious for its inefficient
> generated
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> change...<G>])

I completely agree. Much of my early code, before 'structured enlightenment'
was, while logical, much more difficult to read. In my early days, I thought
writing very clever code that was extremely efficient and tight, but perhaps
not easy to follow, was 'cool'. Oh, the follies of youth. :-) Only after I had
to support such code over time did I see how foolish that was.

> > I agree, don't assume they are idiots. You may safely assume, however, that
> > when you see a pattern of bad code, they are either ignorant, careless, or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> whose perception is less than optimal?)

Sure, it occurs to me, I'm not quite so tunnel vision as you may think. :-) But
the parameters of the tasks we do are rarely *that* complex. It is usually fairly
easy to separate 'good' code from 'bad' code, when you understand what the
code is supposed to do. I do agree that code that was written to target specific
hardware (which may have been a good thing at the time) may be 'bad' code
on other hardware. That doesn't mean the programmer who wrote it made a
mistake. But it is important to be able to understand first, that it is 'bad' code
in the context of the new platform, and second, 'why' it is 'bad' code on the
new platform. This is simply an issue of understanding how code should be
written, not a criticism of the original author of the 'now bad' code.

> It all hinges around the definition of "bad" code and that one has been
> thrashed to death here many times. The bottom line is that while we can
> agree on certain specific cases as being "bad" code, we can't ALWAYS do so,
> and my perception of what is bad could be different from yours. (We might be
> using different priorities for development, for example...).

True. Even if you and I, for example, completely agreed on what constitutes
'good' and 'bad' code, it would still be context relative. The fairly new MIS
director at one of my clients called me up a few years ago to complain that
I had written a report program in such a way as to make it very difficult to
understand and modify. I explained that his predecessor had asked me to
rewrite an old report program to achieve two goals. First, the report had to
be very flexible, and second, it should be able to easily change the report
formats. The report contained several sections, some of which could expand
across the page and/or expand down the page(s). The old program could not
handle the situation when the data expanded horiziontally wider than could
be printed across one page. After study, I proposed two approaches. I could
'hard code' the reports, resulting in a 'simple' program, but to change the
report sections might take a lot of recoding. Or, I could write it using a table
driven report module that was very complex, but should seldom if ever need
modification itself. All the needed changes in the report could be made by
modifying the data selection code (simple) and/or modifying the table (also
simple). I was instructed to use the latter method, because the program was
frequently altered. The table driven module was extremely subtle to write
and debug, but it worked flawlessly. I put "Abandon all Hope, Ye Who Enter
Here" comments around the complex module, as well as detailed comments
on how to modify the table. I put detailed comments in the report driver code
too, but it still took a pretty smart programmer to really understand it. For
several years all went well, until a certain programmer was assigned to
change the report. Ignoring my comments, she simply assumed she would have
to start modifying code. When she saw the formidible code in the report driver,
she ran screaming to the new MIS director. Once I explained why and how,
she modified the table with no problem. No matter which approach I had used
to write this program, it could have been said to be both 'good' and 'bad', from
different perspectives. One viewpoint is that I should not have written code
that the resident staff could not completely understand. I agree with this in most
situations. Another viewpoint is that the program met all design goals, is very
flexible, is easy modify the reports using the table, takes much less effort to
maintain, and the complexity has never been an issue, except that one time when
the programmer didn't bother reading my comments. I believe either approach
could be defended.

> >You can't help the latter, but the former can be helped
> > by proper constructive criticism and teaching.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> encourage better coding practice that do not require criticism.

This is where I don't quite understand where you are coming from. I certainly
agree with your comments above. I just don't understand why you think I am
trying to pin anyone to the wall? It is a certainty that you cannot understand
why a given approach is either good or bad unless you can see, or are shown,
its pros and cons. There is no way to do this without pointing out whatever
flaws, or undesirable attributes there may be. You not only have to teach what
is good, you sometimes need to point out what is bad, or not as good.

> > Please forgive me if I am not
> > being 'gentle'. There is an expression, common in the Peace Corps: "If you
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hmmm...sounds like "brain washing" to me <G>...

Teaching is a kind of mild 'brainwashing'. How could it be otherwise? :-)

> If we are going to teach people to "think" (and I agree that this is
> possible; there are standard philosophy courses at many Universities, that
> teach straight and crooked thinking), that is not the same as teaching them
> how to code in a given computer programming language.

Ah! Here we disagree. When it comes to morality or ethics, there may be
vast differences of opinion. But when it comes to purely logical subjects
such as math or programming, there are truly objective measures of what
constitutes desirable and undesirable, even in the human dimension. Not
that you can assign a 'good' or 'bad' on every issue, of course. I have long
known, and it is easily demonstrated, that knowing how human perception
works at the base level can be very advantageous in formatting reports
and source code. It has been my observation that most programmers pay
little or no heed to these issues.

> I don't see the need to reprogram someone's thought process in the name of
> correcting their computer code.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> outside the square, or whatever it takes for someone to reach a solution.

To teach one ways to use their mind is not the same as telling them they
MUST use their minds in only one way! For goodness sake, children are
tought 'how' to do arithmetic every day, and it is normally done by using
specific methods of computation. But these are not the ONLY ways this
can be done. If you examine math textbooks over 100 years old, you can
find different methods of carrying out the basic arithmetic operations. To
be sure, the answers are the same. :-)

> As long as the solution meets the desired criteria, I have no problem with
> how it was reached, or whether it "toes the Party Line" or not.

As long as 'how it was reached' is not unreliable, innefficient, etc. Are you
determined to let everyone 're-invent the wheel'? You can teach them those
methods which have been shown to work well without forcing them to
ALWAYS use that method. An expert not only knows 'the rules', they also
know when it is okay (even desirable) to break them. But teaching 'the
rules' in the first place is an integral part of teaching any subject.

> It is essential, however, that we have a clear definition of what is
> "right", in the form of desired goals that everyone is agreed upon, rather
> than "the Party Line", "What the Boss says", "That's the way we do things
> here...", "Because I say so..." or similar Authoritarian positions.

I really hope you don;t view what I have been saying in this way. :-)

> > If you are about to undergo neurosurgery, what attitude on this
> > subject (getting it right) would you want your neurosurgeon to have? If
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the EXAMINEs above.

No! The programmer who coded those EXAMINEs was careless.  I knew
him well, he was a good friend of mine. He just didn't care about the quality
of his code, and he was lazy.

> Nope. Disagree here. Criticism is ALWAYS destructive. "Constructive
> criticism" is simply an oxymoron.

I am astonished. Are you saying that you should never make anyone aware
of any mistake or error they ever make? How can you say "This is a mistake"
or "This is an error" without saying it? How would one discipline children?
Do you see criminals being brought to trial without ever being told "you did
wrong?" Do you see criminals never being brought to trial? Or do you see
'criminal' as undefined? Right and wrong, good and bad, are NOT purely
relative terms.

> The problem with "constructive criticism" is that it requires an "Authority"
> to make it, and it is personal.

So? Knowledge and wisdom are a kind of 'authority'. What do you think
the expression 'speaks with authority' means?

> A better solution is for the whole group to review something with a view to
> improving it. It should be impersonal and it should be considered "team
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> towards a solution rather than a "Big Bang" that must be "right" on day 1.

I don't disagree with that approach. But you are trying to make 'one shoe fit
all'. But you describe an idealized process that simply does not always apply
or can be applied. There are situations when it is either not practical, or even
impossible. And when someone works for you and does not listen well, it is
sometimes necessary to confront them. Anyone who has managed many people
knows this. You maintain that people are all different (true), but then insist
that they must be dealt with identically (not true).

> > Without constructive criticism, you cannot assess whether anything has
> flaws.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> I wouldn't presume to "teach" here; I am happy to observe and comment.

If someone askes what is wrong with a piece of code, and you supply that,
are you not teaching? I maintain that you are. Any time you tell someone
something they did not know and benefit from, it can be said to be teacing.
Indeed, if not that, how would you define 'teaching'? :-)

> > > I guess I am also sensitive to old time code taking stick from the new
> breed
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> written.

I'm not sensitive, I wasn't offended in any way. :-)

> > I do not 'hack' at
> > those who wrote much poorer code before structured principles were well
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> here to defend themselves; that's the main reason I started this thread...<G>)

I really hope I am not being 'snide' or 'superior'. If I am, please accept my
apologies, seriously. :-)

> > ... Yet, I have yet to see a single statistic to show that OO is better across
> > the board, in either time, cost or reliability. Better in some cases, yes.
> > Better always, no.
>
> It is better... always. (Except for Batch processing.)

I'm eagerly waiting for statistics proving that. Consistently lower cost of
development, consistently higher efficiency, or consistently better reliability
would do the trick. :-)

> It has been resisted because it requires effort to master it and many of the
> concepts are "new" and unfamiliar to COBOL people. There is a large vested
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> hanging over COBOL, you can't blame them for NOT rushing to implement OO.

I blame anyone for diverting billions of dollars and manhours toward a
goal that is anything but decidedly superior. The term for this is W-A-S-T-E.
If the benefits are worth the extra learning curve and other costs, fine. It is
about (not necessarily immediate) ROI, as all business decisions should be.

> IBM, to their credit, are offering options into networked working from the
> mainframe. This will result inevitably in OO being picked up.
>
> In the same way that you see structured as infinitely superior to
> unstructured, I see OO as infinitely superior to procedural.

Ah, but I can give you tons of absolutely unequivocal statistics to prove that.
Where is that for OO? I've been looking, and I haven't seen anything but
'anecdocal evidence'.

> However, the
> story doesn't stop with OO Classes; it moves into component technology and
> that is where the future lies.

No doubt, that is where the industry is heading. But the question should be
"Why?" History is replete with people who were stampeded into foolish
decisions because someone was telling them it was the way to go. Let me
see the proof it is consistently better, and I will jump on the bandwagon.

> > I am sick and tired of seeing clients pour millions of dollars into the
> latest
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> All new technologies have their learning curve. This one is no exception.

The learning curve is only a minor concern. It is the abysmally poor results
that concern me. I have seen far more than sufficient evidence of this.

> Can you seriously see the world giving up its networked solutions, quick
> build tools, simplified user interfaces, and going back to the days when the
> only communication with a computer was via the High Priests of Programming?
> Get real <G>.

You state a false premise: that OO is necessary for networking. At machine
code level, that OO is implemented procedurally. Neither need it be at the
source code level.

> There is a new breed of programmer arriving. They don't have tunnel vision
> and see all solutions in terms of one language.

They DO have tunnel vision! They have been tought that GUI, C/S and OO are
THE ONLY ways to approach any conceivable problem. If that isn't tunnel
vision, I am at a loss to define 'tunnel vision'. :-)

> They are better informed than we were, have a wider experience base to draw
> on than we had,

You are kidding, right? Right?

> and haven't had their vision impaired by continual exposure
> to character based screens.

No, they have had their vision impaired by continual exposure to nothing but
GUI screens. :-) They don't have the foggiest idea there is even a question of
pros vs. cons in the GUI vs. character IO issue. In fact, most of them have no
idea that the way they are going has any 'cons' at all, they've never been told
there might be. 'Tunnel vision'? Ha! :-) Have they been told that record level
database access can be orders of magnitude faster than, say SQL? Sure! :-)

Fact is, for certain applications, it can be simply and conclusively shown that
character based screens are superior in every measure, except perhaps looks.
Nothing wrong with GUI, of course. If any mechanic in the world suggested
that one tool should be used for every task, people would think he was nuts.
Yet that is exactly what you are proposing here. Just because a newer tool
comes along doesn't mean all your old tools are all immediately obsolete and
unnessary. As long as there are tasks that are better performed by the old tools,
it is foolish to completely dump them.

Please understand that I do not object to any of the new technologies per se.
What I object to is blindly running after the latest gee-whiz technology for
no good reason. Choose the tools, old and/or new that best suit the job. I
simply do not see this being done today in our industry, and we are 'paying
through the nose' for that mistake. We have a culture that is addicted to
change. Change can be good, change can be bad. In our industry, and across
the culture, the latter possibility is being ignored.
```

I go to extreme lengths to promote a "blame free" culture in the areas I
manage. That doesn't mean that there is no responsibility; it simply means
that no-one gets hung out to dry because they tried something and it didn't
work. Instead, the failures are analysed and the group as a whole benefits.
It is quite remarkable to see the effect this style of management has on
people who have previously been rigidly controlled and afraid to do anything
in the least bit risky...

>I'm not talking about blame here, all of us do things that are
> outright dumb at times or, as here, dumb only in current context. My point

An interesting example. I wonder how it would've turned out if you had been
required to work with others, rather than write it all yourself.

> > >You can't help the latter, but the former can be helped
> > > by proper constructive criticism and teaching.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Some of the people I trained have gone on to hold senior posts and I am
very
> > proud of them. I have no reason to believe that the methods I employ are
> > unsuccessful. Criticising code is a last resort; there are many ways to
> > encourage better coding practice that do not require criticism.
>
> This is where I don't quite understand where you are coming from. I
certainly
> agree with your comments above. I just don't understand why you think I am
> trying to pin anyone to the wall? It is a certainty that you cannot
understand
> why a given approach is either good or bad unless you can see, or are
shown,
> its pros and cons. There is no way to do this without pointing out
whatever
> flaws, or undesirable attributes there may be. You not only have to teach
what
> is good, you sometimes need to point out what is bad, or not as good.

Where did I say you are trying to pin anyone to the wall? Please take what I
write at face value; there is no hidden meaning or implied ANYTHING, and it
is NOT personal.

Is it better for an "Authority" to point out the flaws, or, for the people
who made the mistakes to discover them by being directed to re-inspect the
code, based on the fact that it is not achieving what is required/desired?

I like this approach because it works at a personal as well as a
professional level. No egos are bruised, the "problem" becomes a joint one
that "we" need a solution to (rather than a Damoclean sword hanging over the
head of a given individual who will simply become defensive, and have
his/her ability to "spot the mistakes" impaired.)

Only as a last resort when an impasse has been reached would there be need
to invoke "Authority". Effective "Authority" is not seen to be
"Authoritarian".

It is true that "you sometimes need to point out what is bad, or not as
good." but there are WAYS of doing this. If you are qualified by greater
experience or knowledge, then you can certainly bring that to the table and
show why you believe something is not so good. I like to participate in
workshops and brainstorming sessions with the team, where I can contribute
my experience. I can't remember ever laying down a line and insisting it
would be done that way, because I am the Boss. (I DO reserve the right to
make the final decision after the discussion is complete, if there is no
patently obvious "best" solution.). It is all part of a "Management Style"
that is designed to make the workplace pleasant for  all concerned and
ensure that the participants receive Management Support, are motivated, and
feel it is OK to speak their minds and not be afraid of failure, or
politics, or anything else. (A safe environment is necessary for the growth
of all, self included.)

> > >To my mind, it is better, when helping a person with a
> > > programming problem, to teach him to correct the pattern of thinking
that
> > > led him to the problem in the first place, so he may be able apply
that in a
> > > much wider context.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I trained as a teacher before working in computers. Good teachers do not
need to brainwash students. Simply channelling enquiring minds and providing
tools and methods of investigation is enough to ensure that learning occurs.

> > If we are going to teach people to "think" (and I agree that this is
> > possible; there are standard philosophy courses at many Universities,
that
> > teach straight and crooked thinking), that is not the same as teaching
them
> > how to code in a given computer programming language.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> constitutes desirable and undesirable, even in the human dimension.

ANYTHING that is in "the human dimension" is going to be subjective. Yes,
there are "rules" in subjects like math and logic but these are not "good"
or "bad", they just are. "1 + 1 = 2" has no moral or emotional overtone and
cannot be considered good or bad. If one of the fields is stored as binary
and the other is stored as external decimal, with the result in floating
point, THEN you may argue that this is "good" or "bad". But your perception
is subjective, based on your experience with a given hardware platform, and
whether or not efficiency is one of the prime criteria you are seeking to
achieve (again, subjective).

You could argue that it is "good" that the COBOL compiler can do the type
conversions automatically for you, or you can argue that the generated
object code is much more than would be needed if all the operands were the
same type and is therefore "bad".

It is subjective and it is debatable.

That is why there is no place for "Authority" demanding a given approach, in
my opinion. (On the several occasions when I have been required to produce
local standards for an installation I have always made it clear in the
preface that these are "suggested best practices" and, as such, should be
complied with where it makes sense to do so. However, the code produced is
ultimately the responsibilty of the programmer writing it, and if they
perceive a need to deviate from the standard, that is OK, AS LONG AS THEY
CAN JUSTIFY IT TO THE SATISFACTION OF THE REST OF THE GROUP!!).

It is the right of Management to manage, and the final decision should rest
with the person responsible for the code. In the above example, a programmer
should be able to
code the solution using either of the subjective criteria, without fear,
knowing that he/she can explain why they took the position they did, if ever
called upon to do so.

I have seen standards on certain installations that simply stifle
imagination, are restrictive in the extreme, and gear code production to the
"lowest common denominator", rather than striving for excellence. Without
exception, these have always been installations where the culture is one of
fear and blame, yet the Management congratulate themselves on "running a
tight ship".


<snip>

> > As long as the solution meets the desired criteria, I have no problem
with
> > how it was reached, or whether it "toes the Party Line" or not.
>
> As long as 'how it was reached' is not unreliable, innefficient, etc. Are
you
> determined to let everyone 're-invent the wheel'?

No, but if that is what it takes for them to understand wheels, I have no
problem with them doing it once...

>You can teach them those
> methods which have been shown to work well without forcing them to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are confusing "the rules" with "personal preference". That happens many
times in this forum when we see religious wars about coding that are
ultimately, a matter of style (personal preference...).

An example:

1. perform something varying J from 1 by 1 until J > limit.   (It obeys the
"rules" and will compile OK).

2. perform something
         varying J
             from 1
                by 1
              until J > limit      (It obeys the "rules" and will compile
OK.)

"I ALWAYS use the form in 2 above. It is clearer and easy to change. So it
is GOOD".

"I don't like it because it takes more lines of source code and that means
more space in my library and longer when I need to search source for a
string. I think it is BAD."

This is a simple example that only involves formatting of code, and most of
us would have a fairly clear idea which one we preferred and why.

But COBOL allows many ways to do things. Why should a given approach be
better than an alternative approach because someone in "Authority" says so?
It is subjective and comes down to what criteria you are seeking to achieve.
(This is where we came in...<G>)

> > It is essential, however, that we have a clear definition of what is
> > "right", in the form of desired goals that everyone is agreed upon,
rather
> > than "the Party Line", "What the Boss says", "That's the way we do
things
> > here...", "Because I say so..." or similar Authoritarian positions.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > subject (getting it right) would you want your neurosurgeon to have?
If
> > > you were a cook, would you rather people said, after eating a meal you
> > > had cooked, "Thanks, that was okay" or "Thanks, that was the best ...
I
> > > ever ate?" To me, it's not a matter of boosting my ego (though it used
to
> > > be), it is a matter of having the satisfaction that I have put my time
and
> > > talents to good use, that I 'did it right'.
> > >
> > When, in fact, you did it to the best of  your abilty, based on the
> > information you currently have available. Just like the programmer who
coded
> > the EXAMINEs above.
>
> No! The programmer who coded those EXAMINEs was careless.  I knew
> him well, he was a good friend of mine. He just didn't care about the
quality
> of his code, and he was lazy.
>

Ah, so now you produce extra information that has been excluded from the
discussion so far. Why didn't you simply respond to my original post and
say: "No, Pete, the hypothetical scenario you posited is not what happened.
The guy was a lazy sonofabitch who didn't care about code quality. I knew
him."?

> > Nope. Disagree here. Criticism is ALWAYS destructive. "Constructive
> > criticism" is simply an oxymoron.
>
> I am astonished. Are you saying that you should never make anyone aware
> of any mistake or error they ever make? How can you say "This is a
mistake"
> or "This is an error" without saying it? How would one discipline
children?

I am astonished that you invariably try to push my argument beyond where I
stated it. I have not and do not advocate that mistakes should not be
examined and learned from. I object to the concept of "constructive
criticism" because it is a contradiction in terms.

Neither have I said there is no place for criticism. I have said it should
be a "last resort".

> Do you see criminals being brought to trial without ever being told "you
did
> wrong?"

I should think that once they have handcuffs and legirons on or are behind
bars in a cell, they might kind of get the message that they certainly
pissed SOMEONE off... I don't see it as being necessary to tell criminals
they did wrong. Only fools state the blatantly obvious...

>Do you see criminals never being brought to trial? Or do you see
> 'criminal' as undefined? Right and wrong, good and bad, are NOT purely
> relative terms.
>

No, they certainly aren't in your World. My World is different from yours.

In my World these ARE relative terms. Stealing a cookie is less "bad" than
stealing a handbag from an old lady or torturing children. I accept shades
of grey because I don't have a single Authority telling me what is black and
white.

(Part of the "rules" for living in my World is that I grant you the right to
live in yours, and defend vehemently that right, on your behalf, whether or
not you afford me the same latitude...<G>)

> > The problem with "constructive criticism" is that it requires an
"Authority"
> > to make it, and it is personal.
>
> So? Knowledge and wisdom are a kind of 'authority'. What do you think
> the expression 'speaks with authority' means?
>

If the person saying it is talking about himself, it means he is a pompous
ass. If he's saying it about someone else he could just mean that the person
referred to has done a lot of research on the subject. Anyone skilled or
trained in debate and rhetoric knows that the "Argument from Authority" is
no argument at all. (It is easily demolished by any of several rhetorical
devices...)

I had a case a few days ago where I attended a lecture by an eminent PhD who
talked total drivel. When I challenged him on several points, the room went
silent (this guy is not used to people questioning, let alone disagreeing
with him) and he responded "...the overwhelming bulk of evidence from
reputable historians shows that Oswald acted alone."

"Well, I'm not overwhelmed by it. I believe there is at least a level of
doubt that bears more examination.", I responded.

He gave me a look that would have stopped a clock, and a teenager who is
present on his course said: "Well, you're not a reputable historian..."  (I
think she was hoping to get a better grade by supporting the Boss <G>). I
smiled at her (she was quite pretty <G>) and said: "Precisely. That is why I
am not overwhelmed by the "evidence", and, by the way, you don't know who I
am or what I do, so you cannot make that assertion with confidence."  The
"lecture" continued.

BOTTOM LINE: Let the clarity of your argument speak for you, not the letters
after your name.

(Please don't push this into an "anti-Acadaemia" position or suggest that I
am saying all PhDs are idiots; I'm not adopting either of those positions.)

Above, you ascribe "Authority" to Knowledge and Wisdom. Yet the people who
indisputably have these attributes seldom adopt Authoritarian positions...
You don't see Mahatma Ghandhi, the Buddha, Jesus, or the Zen Patriarchs
demanding compliance, like petulant children. The general approach was:
"Here are some simple rules that will make life better for everybody. Live
this way and you can avoid suffering. But it is really your choice..."  NOT
"Do this or else your children will die, your fingers will drop off, and
you'll be consigned to burn in Hell for ever..."  Or... "I know so much
better than you, so you better listen to what I say, if you know what's good
for you..." etc...<G>.

> > A better solution is for the whole group to review something with a view
to
> > improving it. It should be impersonal and it should be considered "team
> > property". If desired goals are not being attained, then the person or
> > persons who have produced the dodgy stuff should have the failed results
> > pointed out ("The code doesn't seem to be getting the correct
balance...",
> > "These routines seem to be taking a long time to run...") and be given a
> > chance to go away and review it for themselves. Only if/when they
request
> > assistance, would I get involved, and I am not above getting expertise
in
> > from outside to assist us if we are unable to solve something. I also
have
> > no problem with the iterative approach where complex software "grows"
> > towards a solution rather than a "Big Bang" that must be "right" on day
1.
>
> I don't disagree with that approach. But you are trying to make 'one shoe
fit
> all'. But you describe an idealized process that simply does not always
apply
> or can be applied. There are situations when it is either not practical,
or even
> impossible.

Not having tried it you can say that <G>?

The shoe analogy is totally lost on me. There is nothing idealized about the
process I describe; it is practical in the extreme and I have found it to be
most successful. While NO particular approach is likely to ALWAYS be
applicable, I was hoping to demonstrate in general terms, the kind of
alternative to an Authoritarian one. If I hadn't given an example (and a
real one at that; I use it all the time...) you would have said I was
talking about some hypothetical Utopian approach.

>And when someone works for you and does not listen well, it is
> sometimes necessary to confront them. Anyone who has managed many people
> knows this.

Are you suggestng my experience in this area is limited, Judson <G>?

It really depends what you are requiring them to listen to. Most of us
"don't hear so good..." when what we are being told is the same old bullshit
they've been forcing down our throats for years (the "Party Line"
f'r'instance...).

Yes, I have had occasion to "confront" people. (Fortunately it doesn't
happen often. For some reason I don't quite understand, most people seem to
have no problem listening to what I have to say...<G>).

When I do, it is always in private, and it is always as much a "listen" on
my part as it is a
"discipline". If the recalcitrant proves reasonable, we both have our say
and
then see if we can work out the problem. If they are not reasonable, they
get fired on the spot <G>.

(Only joking, firing people is a much more serious matter and it is never
pleasant; I have done it a handful of times
only when I was satisfied there was no hope of salvaging the person
concerned. I have also been fired on two occasions in my career (both when I
was working as a full time employee and many years ago...) so I know about
it from both sides.)

>You maintain that people are all different (true), but then insist
> that they must be dealt with identically (not true).
>

You lost me again.

> > > Without constructive criticism, you cannot assess whether anything has
> > flaws.
> > > That criticism need not be verbalized, unless you are trying to teach
(my
> > > primary motive for participating in this NG, and probably of most of
the
> > 'old
> > > timers' here) or make a point. :-)
> >
> > The contributions here are all valuable in my opinion (even the "bad"
ones
> > have entertainment value <G>). I contribute here because it is fun; when
it
> > stops being so, I'll go away. Any benefits from my contributions are
purely
> > incidental, and if anyone gets anything from me that they can use, well,
> > that's a bonus.This is not to say I don't take it seriously and I DO TRY
to
> > help occasionally. But that is not my prime motivation for being here.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> something they did not know and benefit from, it can be said to be
teacing.

Not by me, it can't...

I see it as robbing them. Only when they have failed to work it out  for
themselves (after being given the necessary clues and a direction to look
in) should you actually present information to them, and then it must be
done gently, with desire to help, rather than show how clever we are.

> Indeed, if not that, how would you define 'teaching'? :-)

Teaching is enabling, assisting, and guiding, another person to broaden
their experience base and grow as a person. (It has nothing to do with the
transfer of knowledge; that can be achieved by reading a book...).
Furthermore, "teaching" in itself is a skill, and a good teacher can teach a
subject he/she knows very little about, very effectively. A good teacher can
apply this skill to teach him/herself, also.

It can be achieved in many ways, but the one I like least is the one where
the Teacher  is the Authority and expounds at length on the "Right"
approach. Anything that questions this is dismissed as some uninformed
criticism. I see the main function of a teacher as being to direct a
student's attention to something, and ensure that the tools needed to
unravel it are available.
Teaching is a growth process for both Teacher and Student.

Telling people "interesting facts" (never mind boring ones...) is a long
way from Teaching...I refer you to the opening sketch with Peter Cook and
John Cleese in "The Secret Policeman's Ball" (1976) - it is available now on
DVD and you might be able to hire it. The sketch is called "Interesting
Facts" and it makes my case, far more eloquently than I have space or
inclination to do here...

>
> > > > I guess I am also sensitive to old time code taking stick from the
new
> > breed
> > > > who have had the benefit of computer science educations. It is easy
to
> > find
> > > > fault with stuff that was written before there were best practices
and
> > > > standards and programming theory. People did the best they could
with
> > what
> > > > was there. For the most part, it worked. To see the same people who
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Don't be sensitive; it wasn't aimed at you personally, any more than
anyone
> > else who criticises code without making any attempt to understand why it
was
> > written.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > those who wrote much poorer code before structured principles were
well
> > > understood, I was one of them. :-) You're right, we did the best we
could
> > with
> > > what we knew at the time. But you make an unwarranted assumption to
think
> > > that pointing out the flaws in it is equivalent to criticizing those
who
> > wrote it.
> >
> > It depends on HOW you "point out the flaws"... snide, superior, comments
> > don't help anyone. (And let's not forget that the person who wrote it,
isn't
> > here to defend themselves; that's the main reason I started this
thread...<G>)
>
> I really hope I am not being 'snide' or 'superior'. If I am, please accept
my
> apologies, seriously. :-)

If you think apologies are in order, and I'm not sayng they are or they
aren't, go apologise to your friend who wrote the code. I couldn't care less
what you say about it, but he probably can...<G>

If someone looked at my code and remarked "Thought I'd seen it all...the
mind boggles" I would just naturally assume they were blown away by the
fantastic lucidity and compelling elegance of it...<G>.

>
> > > ... Yet, I have yet to see a single statistic to show that OO is
better across
> > > the board, in either time, cost or reliability. Better in some cases,
yes.
> > > Better always, no.
> >
> > It is better... always. (Except for Batch processing.)
>
> I'm eagerly waiting for statistics proving that. Consistently lower cost
of
> development, consistently higher efficiency, or consistently better
reliability
> would do the trick. :-)

Yes I have all of those, at first hand (once the initial learning curve has
been rounded..). But why would I offer my competitive advantage to the World
in general <G>? I gave up some time ago trying to persuade people to try OO.
I'm not on commission for it. Whether you use it or not is of no real
concern to me; I just don't like seeing it take a bum rap from people who
have no experience of it, even less from people who resist anything that
shakes the Status Quo...

I just have good perception of what is happening in the Marketplace (I talk
to Managers at all levels as well as to the Shop Floor and I listen to what
their concerns are and what they tell me). Also, My World exists with or
without COBOL.

It DOES concern me to see many COBOL jobs disappearing because Standard
COBOL is no longer up to the task. And yes, I do like what you call
"Gee-Whiz" technology. I like anything that makes my life easier.

>
> > It has been resisted because it requires effort to master it and many of
the
> > concepts are "new" and unfamiliar to COBOL people. There is a large
vested
> > interest in the status quo also... many vendors, especially mainframe
ones,
> > are not anxious to see procedural programming replaced; they have a huge
> > investment in COBOL compilers and infrastructure. With a shadow already
> > hanging over COBOL, you can't blame them for NOT rushing to implement
OO.
>
> I blame anyone for diverting billions of dollars and manhours toward a
> goal that is anything but decidedly superior. The term for this is
W-A-S-T-E.
> If the benefits are worth the extra learning curve and other costs, fine.
It is
> about (not necessarily immediate) ROI, as all business decisions should
be.

I don't totally agree with Milton Friedmann. It isn't ALWAYS JUST about
profit. Large Corporations have a Social Responsibility as well. ROI is
important, but I take issue that it must ALWAYS be the SOLE consideration.
>
> > IBM, to their credit, are offering options into networked working from
the
> > mainframe. This will result inevitably in OO being picked up.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ah, but I can give you tons of absolutely unequivocal statistics to prove
that.

Unlike you, I am not easily persuaded by statistics, which can be made to
show almost anything.

> Where is that for OO? I've been looking, and I haven't seen anything but
> 'anecdocal evidence'.
>
> > However, the
> > story doesn't stop with OO Classes; it moves into component technology
and
> > that is where the future lies.
>
> No doubt, that is where the industry is heading. But the question should
be
> "Why?" History is replete with people who were stampeded into foolish
> decisions because someone was telling them it was the way to go. Let me
> see the proof it is consistently better, and I will jump on the bandwagon.
>

I don't think so...<G>

> > > I am sick and tired of seeing clients pour millions of dollars into
the
> > latest
> > > 'technology du jour', and wind up with nothing to show for it.

Why? It isn't your money. They have a right to spend it any way they see
fit. It keeps computer consultants in business...<G>.

I see
this
> > every
> > > day with my own eyes. I am not so gullible as to endorse such idiocy,
just
> > > because it is "the thing to do." Yet this seems to be the way our
industry
> > is
> > > heading. The proof of the pudding is in the eating, but what happens
when
> > > people start ignoring that it tastes like crap and gives no nutrition?
> > Nothing
> > > good.
> >
> > All new technologies have their learning curve. This one is no
exception.
>
> The learning curve is only a minor concern. It is the abysmally poor
results
> that concern me. I have seen far more than sufficient evidence of this.
>
> > Can you seriously see the world giving up its networked solutions, quick
> > build tools, simplified user interfaces, and going back to the days when
the
> > only communication with a computer was via the High Priests of
Programming?
> > Get real <G>.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And at the machine code level COBOL is Assembler... Been there, done that,
what does it prove?

The premise is not false. Components (which expose Object interfaces) are
becoming an ESSENTIAL part of modern software and the emerging generation of
networks (including the Internet) will rely on this technology. While you
are "waiting to be convinced" other people are developing Network solutions
using OO and component technology, and cleaning up...

> > There is a new breed of programmer arriving. They don't have tunnel
vision
> > and see all solutions in terms of one language.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > They are better informed than we were, have a wider experience base to
draw
> > on than we had,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> No, they have had their vision impaired by continual exposure to nothing
but
> GUI screens. :-) They don't have the foggiest idea there is even a
question of
> pros vs. cons in the GUI vs. character IO issue. In fact, most of them
have no
> idea that the way they are going has any 'cons' at all, they've never been
told
> there might be. 'Tunnel vision'? Ha! :-) Have they been told that record
level
> database access can be orders of magnitude faster than, say SQL? Sure! :-)
>
> Fact is, for certain applications, it can be simply and conclusively shown
that
> character based screens are superior in every measure, except perhaps
looks.
> Nothing wrong with GUI, of course. If any mechanic in the world suggested
> that one tool should be used for every task, people would think he was
nuts.
> Yet that is exactly what you are proposing here. Just because a newer tool
> comes along doesn't mean all your old tools are all immediately obsolete
and
> unnessary. As long as there are tasks that are better performed by the old
tools,
> it is foolish to completely dump them.
>
> Please understand that I do not object to any of the new technologies per
se.
> What I object to is blindly running after the latest gee-whiz technology
for
> no good reason. Choose the tools, old and/or new that best suit the job. I
> simply do not see this being done today in our industry, and we are
'paying
> through the nose' for that mistake. We have a culture that is addicted to
> change. Change can be good, change can be bad. In our industry, and across
> the culture, the latter possibility is being ignored.

Ask yourself "Why?" it is "being ignored". Then ask yourself: "What if the
rest of the World is NOT wrong?".

I don't think we are going to achieve much here Judson and I am frankly,
tired of debating OO and New Technology with people who don't want to know.
I respect your professionalism and your right to a viewpoint, but I think
you are just plain wrong.

If you have a World that works for you, good.

I have one too.

Guess we just have to accept that they are different.

Regards,

Pete.
```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> ...

Whew! We've gotten into quite a discussion. :-) Just a few final
observations and I'll let the matter drop.

We do see a number of issues differently (see last paragraph), and I
suppose neither of us will ever convince the other. :-)

You really do misapprehend my position as a defense of 'authoritarian
control' . I really do not feel that way, and do not mean to give that
impression. If I have done so, I apologize. My personality is such that
I tend to be blunt, and that comes over poorly sometimes, and gives a
wrong impression of where I'm coming from. :-)

When it comes to a true defense of a given logical position, you are
correct that 'authority' based on knowledge and experience is no
real argument. The 'real argument' is the facts of the issue, as you
point out. Where such 'authority' can be useful is for those who may
not be knowledgeable enough to evaluate the facts directly, such as in
a court setting, or perhaps in a news form where there is a wide range
of experience represented. ;-) It is anything but an ideal solution, true.

One final point that is widely understood and well demonstrated in the
psychological community, which applies to the discipline of children,
and that has only come into clear focus in the psychological community
within the last several years. If I understand your position, you appear
to espouse what would be called a 'permissive' environment. Today,
most psychologists will tell you that there are three broad categories of
environments (omitting 'pathological ones, such as outright neglect and
abuse) for the discipline of children: permissive, balanced authority
and love, and authoritarian. Of these, the worst (and most destructive)
is permissive, followed by authoritarian. Only balanced authority and
love is most beneficial to the child. It is destructive to the human psyche
not to be given a clear set of rules of conduct that are enforced in a firm
but balanced way. :-)
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031217070626.497$Yw@news.newsreader.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> > ...
>
<snip>
>
> One final point that is widely understood and well demonstrated in the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to espouse what would be called a 'permissive' environment.

Nope. I can't ascribe to what is termed the "permissive" approach, where
kids are allowed to run wild without respect for the rights and property of
others.

But you are right, I am closer to that than to Authoritarian.

I stopped a kid (4 year old) who was running round our table in a restaurant
the other night. Explained to him (gently) that people were trying to have
dinner and it was not a playground. He was OK with that and went back to his
parents who looked at me as if I was the Devil Incarnate, as they obviously
believed it was OK for their precious son to do whatever he liked wherever
he liked... I just smiled at them and rejoined our interrupted discussion.

>Today,
> most psychologists will tell you that there are three broad categories of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> love is most beneficial to the child. It is destructive to the human
psyche
> not to be given a clear set of rules of conduct that are enforced in a
firm
> but balanced way. :-)
> -- 

Yes I agree with that 100%. The keywords are "balanced" and "fair". Kids
have as much right to justice as anybody else. And small children do need
the security of clear rules and routines.

Although I have none of my own, I enjoy spending time with children and they
seem to enjoy spending time with me.

(It is nice to be able to "give them back" when I'm exhausted by them,
though...<G>).

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In defence of the indefensible. WAS: Re: COBOL array editing output

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brn5mc$1ar$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031212093749.258$gy@news.newsreader.com> <3fda3be0_8@news.athenanews.com> <20031213084227.375$Dn@news.newsreader.com> <3fdb9652_5@news.athenanews.com> <20031215103123.434$Ci@news.newsreader.com>`

```
I'm sure we all have had arguments about whether a procedure/method should
continue to be used.

One that I was strongly against is the designed use of size overflow.   e.g.   a
table subscript with PIC 99 that overflows to zero after 99 to make the program
work correctly.    I couldn't persuade another programmer that this was a
dangerous practice.
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-15T16:35:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brknt1$bnl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031212093749.258$gy@news.newsreader.com> <3fda3be0_8@news.athenanews.com> <20031213084227.375$Dn@news.newsreader.com> <3fdb9652_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
There's the old story about the bride who cooks a roast.   She cuts off the end
of the meat and puts the rest in the roaster.   Her husband asked her why she
did this, and she said that she didn't know - that's how her mother cooked
roasts.   A call to the mother solved the problem - her mother's roasting pan
was too small to fit a whole roast.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: In defence of the indefensible. WAS: Re: COBOL array editing output

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-14T11:43:00+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fdb9657_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031211080124.752$Nx@news.newsreader.com> <bram6j$fll$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3fd8ffe3_4@news.athenanews.com> <vtn41kim5va191@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vtn41kim5va191@corp.supernews.com...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
> > As Chief Programmer on a Burroughs site at the time (1969), I endorsed a
> > local standard requiring people to AVOID the use of IF when writing
COBOL
> > for this machine. We used ALTER instead, as it was extremely efficient
and
> > (unlike the programming wimps of today <G>)  we never had any trouble
> > maintaining it. (All "switch" type comparisons were implemented with
ALTER
> > and we only used IF when it was a KEY (string) comparison. Arithmetic
> > comparisons were carried out using subtraction and sign testing... You
can
> > tell whether A >, <, or = B by subtracting A from B and testing the sign
of
> > the result...)
>
> I'm not trying to be smart...  But how did you test the sign without an
IF?
>

It's a fair question, Daniel <G>

The COBOL CLASS test generated DIFFERENT code from a straight Alphanumeric
IF.

So if you said: IF POSITIVE (or NEGATIVE or ZERO) it did a bit test on the
sign and did not generate the redundant branches  for ELSE into the IF
construct.)

The compiler was not written by Burroughs. It had been done on contract.
There was no support from Burroughs for it.
I disassembled large parts of it and managed to patch it so it could save
object code to a Tape library or the hard disk and accept input from a tape
or the hard disk. My Boss (who was really an inspiration) wrote an Operating
System for the machine which he called POPSY (The Primitive Operating System
<G>) and we ended up not having to compile COBOL every time we wanted to run
it, or maintain huge cabinets full of card decks. Martin (my Boss at the
time) wrote POPSY in two weeks, in an Assembler he had never seen, and
working outside our normal production hours. None of us believed he could
possibly do it, but he did, and it never failed. (He came from ICL in
Putney, London)

I believe to this day that his effort in doing this was one of the most
amazing feats I have ever seen in IT. Most people would not even have
attempted it. The lesson we all learned was: "If you can't get support, do
it yourself." (A lesson I had to call on very recently in Melbourne...).

Pete.
```

the compiler did... (I kept it for years but have lost it now.) Being of a
generous disposition, I was happy to tell them...<G>

scan (parsing), and was a revelation to me as I'd never sen anything like it
before. It was VERY fast....

- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On 10-Dec-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> In every implementation I know of, referencing table entries using indexing
> is never slower than referencing the same table entries using indexing.  In
> a very limited subset of cases it may be no faster.

Typo check.

> SET UP BY and SET DOWN BY could be more costly than similar manipulations of
> subscripts, if the efficiency of the internal format associated with the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> probably outweigh the cost of arithmetic manipulation of the value.

One thing that has always irritated me is when you have the following logic:
SET TP-MAX TO 1.
SET TP-MAX DOWN BY 1.

I suppose the optimizer sets it directly to zero, but why can't I do that to
begin with?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T08:45:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bra6up$1lj9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bra1fq$3pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bra1fq$3pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 10-Dec-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > In every implementation I know of, referencing table entries using
indexing
> > is never slower than referencing the same table entries using indexing.
In
> > a very limited subset of cases it may be no faster.
>
> Typo check.

Thanks.  Second line should have ended "... entries using subscripting.  In"

>
> > SET UP BY and SET DOWN BY could be more costly than similar
manipulations of
> > subscripts, if the efficiency of the internal format associated with the
> > subscript is the same or better than that used for indices (e.g., "77
> > <subscript-name> PIC S9(11) BINARY EXTENDED", which *everybody* knows is
> > *always* the most efficient specification for an integer ;-)   ), but
for
> > just about any other format the cost of accessing the containing item
will
> > probably outweigh the cost of arithmetic manipulation of the value.
>
> One thing that has always irritated me is when you have the following
logic:
> SET TP-MAX TO 1.
> SET TP-MAX DOWN BY 1.

Some optimizers avoid INTER-statement optimizations.

But presuming that TP-MAX is an index-name, its contents may or may not be
zero; that's an implementation detail.  For example, for

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000300 DATA DIVISION.
000400 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000500 01  A-TABLE.
000600     03  FILLER PIC X(3).
000700     03  A-FIELD PIC X(5) OCCURS 100 INDEXED BY AN-INDEX.
000800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000900 MAIN-PARAGRAPH.
001000     SET AN-INDEX TO 1.
001100     SET AN-INDEX DOWN BY 1.
001200     CALL SYSTEM DUMP.
001300     STOP RUN.

the contents of AN-INDEX are -5 in COBOL85 and -2 in COBOL74.  Range
checking doesn't occur unless and until an attempt is made to reference the
table to which it points (if then).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T13:14:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031211141531.095$sA@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bra1fq$3pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> One thing that has always irritated me is when you have the following logic:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> begin with?

That irritates me, too. I suppose it's because zero is an invalid occurrence
number in COBOL, but I don't think that's sufficient justification when the
index is simply being set. I could understand flagging a negative value, but
an index pointing to 'the highest entry added' in an initially empty table is
legitimately zero, a very common situation. Zero through table size + 1
should not be flagged, IMHO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T19:58:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o%3Cb.10295$rP6.316@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bra1fq$3pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20031211141531.095$sA@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Page 830 of the 2002 ISO Standard (substantive change NOT affecting existing
programs)

"121) SET index-name. The result of setting an index can be outside of the range
of the associated table. Also, the setting of an index in the TO phrase does not
have to be within the range of the associated table."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T08:38:35-06:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T09:01:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brcs91$ipm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vssshcj9c0utde@corp.supernews.com> <20031204103714.391$XB@news.newsreader.com> <bqqbg9$1e2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd0ec0b_6@news.athenanews.com> <br2kaf$2826$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <20031210074027.673$63@news.newsreader.com> <br83fc$4dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bra1fq$3pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20031211141531.095$sA@news.newsreader.com> <o%3Cb.10295$rP6.316@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20031212093943.352$nN@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Previous standards prohibited SET index-name TO 0.  It's the 2002 standard
that relaxes this restriction.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031212093943.352$nN@news.newsreader.com...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> >
> > Page 830 of the 2002 ISO Standard (substantive change NOT affecting
existing
> > programs)
> >
> > "121) SET index-name. The result of setting an index can be outside of
the range
> > of the associated table. Also, the setting of an index in the TO phrase
does not
> > have to be within the range of the associated table."
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie)
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T01:46:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bee6ba6.0312050146.54e23b7e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hi,

Thanks for the reply's.

I am trying to exit from the middle of a PERFORM statement upon a
certain condition.
EG: if the user enters 0 then the program should exit that perform.
-----------------
main-para.
	PERFORM para1 UNTIL counter = 10 OR var1 = ZERO.
	
	PERFORM para2.
	.
	.
	STOP-RUN.
		
para1.
	DISPLAY "Enter var1."
	ACCEPT var1.
*THIS is where I am trying to exit back to main-para if var1 is zero
but instead  it keeps executing
*code past the accept even if var1 is ZERO.
	.
	.	
-----------------

Does anyone know how I can exit back main-para if ZERO is entered and
not continue executing the rest of para1?

Thanks,
Ritchie
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T14:54:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqq677$o32$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3bee6ba6.0312050146.54e23b7e@posting.google.com>`

```

On  5-Dec-2003, ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie) wrote:

> I am trying to exit from the middle of a PERFORM statement upon a
> certain condition.

Old Spagetti code way:

    perform 100-loop thru 100-exit.
100-loop.
    ...
    If condition-met go to 100-exit.
    ...
    go to 100-loop.
100-exit.
    exit.

Structured alternative:

    Perform 100-loop until condition-met.
100-loop.
     ...
     if condition-met
           continue
     else
           perform 150-rest-of-loop
     end-if

New version of CoBOL (which I don't have).

100-loop.
     ...
     if condition-met
	exit paragraph
     end-if
     ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T15:17:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie) wrote 

> main-para.
> 	PERFORM para1 UNTIL counter = 10 OR var1 = ZERO.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> but instead  it keeps executing

         IF ( var1 NOT = ZERO )
>            *code past the accept even if var1 is ZERO      
> 	     ...     < do not have any full stops here ^ remove
> 	     ...	
         END-IF
         .

You may also want to put 'WITH TEST AFTER' on the PERFORM para1 unless
you are setting var1 to non-zero before the perform, and ensuring
counter is not 10.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T18:30:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vt28pgc6420a84@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3bee6ba6.0312050146.54e23b7e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3mszb.146161$Ec1.5849324@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3bee6ba6.0312050146.54e23b7e@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie wrote:

> Hi,
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> not continue executing the rest of para1?

Look at how var1 is defined, and what you're comparing it to.  Also, 
you're not dealing with an event-driven language (like VB) - the 
condition on the Perform is not tested until para1 has executed 
completely.  If you've got optional code in para1, you'll have to place 
it under an If statement to conditionally execute the remaining code 
before the test of the Perform breaks you out of the loop.
```

ritchie <ritchie_s01@yahoo.com> wrote in message news:3bee6ba6.0312050146.54e23b7e@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> -----------------

> Does anyone know how I can exit back main-para if ZERO is entered and
> not continue executing the rest of para1?
>
> Thanks,
> Ritchie

main-para.

  PERFORM para1 UNTIL counter = 10 OR var1 = ZERO.

  PERFORM para2.

  STOP-RUN.

para1.

  DISPLAY "Enter var1."

  ACCEPT var1.

  IF var1 = ZERO
       PERFORM var1-zero
  ELSE
       PERFORM para1-part2.

var1-zero.

   DISPLAY "VAR1 VALUE ENTERED = ZERO".

   etc etc etc

para1-part2.

   COMPUTE COUNTER = COUNTER + 1.

   etc etc etc

para2.

    etc etc etc......
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T12:40:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yHtzb.9188$zf2.1210052@news20.bellglobal.com>`

- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <xbmdnQSMQ7_gy1OiU-KYjw@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> ritchie wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Since that goes to the console, which scrolls and wraps, it is actually 
a double blank ... in effect will clear a fifty line console.
DISPLAY " " in the loop (or making the variable X(1)) will clear a 25 
line console. "DISPLAY SPACE AT LINE 1 COLUMN 1" will clear a screen.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T00:11:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCE7609.FE3F30A9@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com>`

```


ritchie wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ritchie.

You didn't say specifically whether you are using the 'simple' ANSI
DISPLAY or the F/J Screen Section. Chuck has given you one solution. The
following is  less elegant and not rocket science :-

SurName = 30
FirstName = 20

perform varying n from 30 by -1 until SurName(n:1) <> space
*> the above is a 'play safe' but could be written as
*> perform varying n from 30 by -1
  if Surname(n:1) not = space
     move n to Length1
     EXIT PERFORM
 End-if
End-perform

-- same for FirstName giving Length2

intiliaze ws-Text51
string Surname (1:Length1)     delimited by size
         x'20'                             delimited by size
         FirstName (1:Length2)  delimited by size
         into ws-Text51
End-string

Some might deprecate the following technique, but it's one I use. It ties
back to your initial design. I have many sets of files which have
repetitive descriptions, some 40 characters used over and over again -
sheer waste of disk space. So I 'build' the descriptions from hard coded
lists of descriptions which terminate with a Tilde (~) and STRING the
parts together using the Tilde as the delimiter.

Using it with your Name fields, the file contents would read :-

pic x(31) = Smith~
pic x(21) = Adam~

As I said my lists are hard coded, but if you were using data in a file
then you could adopt the following approach :-

Input LastName pic x(30) - file - pic x(31) allowing for maximum name plus
Tilde
Input FirstName pic x(21) - file - pic x(21) etc.....

You accept the names, do a perform FORWARDS ( using a positive 'n')
checking for the first space and put the Tilde in that position (Reference
Modification) in your file record. Only relevant if you are *constantly*
stringing names/descriptions together. There will be times where you want
to display fields independently - not showing the Tilde - INSPECT MyField
REPLACING ALL "`" by space.

The latter comments are not your immediate concern - but I've included
them as something for you to bear in mind in your future IT career.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T01:25:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCE8552.DA97B8F0@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3FCE7609.FE3F30A9@shaw.ca>`

```


"James J. Gavan" wrote:

>
> SurName = 30
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> etc....

Ritchie,

Quick update on what I wrote having read Donald's message  :-

LastName = 30 = Zeta Jones
FirstName = 20 = Catherine

My approach will work - sorry DT - yours will fail unless you do a two-space
delimiter. Those damned 'Anglos' with their snotty double-barrelled names. I
know, I know, 'Jones the Milk", 'Jones the Butcher', why couldn't she be
satisfied with 'Jones the Gorgeous'. Doesn't seem to me that 'Robert Jones
from Gloucester' has a hang-up about his singular name.

But again you have all the "van" 's in Dutch and "von" 's in German prior to
the family name; in France "de la Salle" etc......

Ritchie - Give Chuck's REVERSE function a try.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T20:35:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCF97F5.859B4319@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312031204.7c020104@posting.google.com> <3FCE7609.FE3F30A9@shaw.ca> <3FCE8552.DA97B8F0@shaw.ca> <UbGzb.12024$yd.1680077@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

>
> Jimmy, you will notice the delimiter is only used on the first name, not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald.  (PS-notice the Linux.  Having some fun here)

Yes Donald I did take note of your use of just one delimiter. I've kind of got set
into delimited by "x" and as I've seen from several STRING messages, sometimes
that can be superfluous.

I did tell Ritchie that the following could be abbreviated :-

from : -  perform varying n from 30 by -1 until n = 1
to :-       perform varying n from 30 by -1

Not so. The compiler rejects it as missing the UNTIL ........

Just to check all options and use of delimiters, the following. The commented
lines give what we want. The uncommented line gives "Zeta Catherine".
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Program-id.  StringIt.
Working-storage section.
 01 n           pic x(4) comp-5.
 01 LastName    pic x(30) value "Zeta Jones".
 01 FirstName   pic x(20) value "Catherine".
 01 Two-Spaces  pic x(02) value spaces.
 01 ws-Length   pic x(4) comp-5.
 01 ws-Text51   pic x(51).

 Procedure Division.
 perform varying n from 30 by -1 until n = 1
    if LastName(n:1) <> space
       move n to ws-Length
       EXIT PERFORM
    End-if
 End-perform
 initialize ws-Text51
*> string LastName(1:ws-Length)
*> string LastName delimited by two-spaces
string LastName delimited by space
        x"20"
        Firstname
        into ws-Text51
 end-string
 display ws-Text51
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Seeing Judson's tests on the options - I guess I will stick with the above.

Jimmy
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T19:29:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jQqzb.26586$sb4.6304@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0311220543.740ad43d@posting.google.com> <7aOdnRSY4vrxKCKiU-KYjA@giganews.com> <3bee6ba6.0311221408.7ee3d93c@posting.google.com> <vs05e3b65n6u79@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0311232240.78921bcb@posting.google.com> <3FC24C5C.3F41F74F@shaw.ca> <3FC28858.FA8506B7@shaw.ca>`

```
<G> Bill was away for 10 days.  I don't know if anyone answered this here (I
think I saw an answer elsewhere)

The optional "YYYYMMDD" and "YYYYDDD" (keywords) were not a part of the '85
Standard or the '89 or '91 amendments to it.  They are a part of the 2002
Standard.

Furthermore, they were "frozen" early in the development of the 2002 Standard
(when it was still - gasp - expected to be official by '97 or '98).  J4 and WG4
"indicated" to vendors that they INTENDED them to remain "stable" from their
early development to the approval of the "next" revision (i.e. the 2002
Standard) so MANY (but certainly NOT ALL) vendors provided support for them WELL
before 2000.

FYI - any "conforming" '85 (or '89) compiler that supports them MUST flag them
as extensions when ANSI/ISO/NIST flagging is turned on.  Did anyone who
"wondered" about their being standard actually TRY to compile with flagging
turned on?  Does any vendor "support" this syntax in an '85 compiler withOUT
flagging them???
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-03T20:03:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bkrzb.26607$sb4.23581@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<147ccfbd.0311250855.473d10b9@posting.google.com> <20031125134308.566$Fp@news.newsreader.com> <bfdfc3e8.0311270716.383e1853@posting.google.com> <20031128074852.265$sv@news.newsreader.com> <9UHxb.6113$aw2.2647823@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <20031128114911.631$t2@news.newsreader.com>`

```
For "portable" numeric fields define them as

   Usage Display (default)
   Sign is leading separate

Otherwise, one "may" find the sign indicator at the beginning, end (or
theoretically - middle) of the field.

Using "leading separate" ALSO makes "conversion" (e.g. ASCII/EBCDIC) a WHOLE lot
easier.  Obviously, it DOES take more storage and is probably NOT a great choice
for "arithmetic performance" numeric items.
```

There are several reasons:
    reduce cost for code maintenance in the long run: Java is easy to learn
and use along with its open source,
    goto OO for the same reason,
    replace native calls for across platform deployment because mainframe is
expensive,
    for better Web app using TCP/IP,
    access database using SQL in a standard like ODBC or JDBC independent of
database product,
    etc...

After all, it is a solution to save money and get the latest technology at
the same time. One stone two birds.
Cobol is working, but it becomes to complex to maintain both in language
standard itself (How many pages for  Cobol standard?) and Cobol
applications. Bugs because of No type checking at all. Character based....

Sometime, I think it is because Java is easier rather than Cobol is out of
date. Why Microsoft "designed" C# in .NET?


Thomas
