###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T20:03:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312082003.6194749e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vHszb.38122$Y4R1.5356@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3fd102ff$0$6968$7a628cd7@news.club-internet.fr> <Lx6Bb.13360$3Eb1.4830@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote

> There are several reasons:
>     reduce cost for code maintenance in the long run: Java is easy to learn
> and use along with its open source,
>     goto OO for the same reason,

Some years ago there was a Datamation report that surveyed how
developers spent there time and what languages they used.  The results
showed something like 70% of Cobol programmers time was spent on
maintenance and only 20% of C programmers time.

Some language converter company took these and concluded that if Cobol
programs were converted to C there would be a huge savings in cost due
to reduction in maintenence.

Can you see why this claim is nonsense ?

Two reasons.  The first is that converters do not convert programs
from, say, Cobol into being C or Java programs.  They convert them
into Cobol programs written in the C or Java language.  This means
that neither Cobol nor C/Java programmers cam, nor will want to, work
on them.

In fact I have seen some reccommend that the source be kept and worked
on and converted for recompiling.

The 2nd is that tha _applications_ that are traditionally written in
Cobol tend to have changes, called maintenance, that have nothing to
do with making the code work but are entirely the result of changes in
specification.  The owners want it to do something different.  C and
Java programs tend to be for applications that do not have arbitrary
changes and tend to have new versions that count as development, not
maintenance.

This means that costs are more to rise and there is _no_ saving in
what Cobol systems call 'maintenance'.

>     replace native calls for across platform deployment because mainframe is
> expensive,

<shrug>, that can be done in Cobol.

>     for better Web app using TCP/IP,

<shrug> that can be done in Cobol.  What bits do you do 'better' in
Java that I can't do in Cobol ?

>     access database using SQL in a standard like ODBC or JDBC independent of
> database product,

<shrug> can do that in Cobol.

> After all, it is a solution to save money and get the latest technology at
> the same time. One stone two birds.

Is it ?  Show the figures.

> Cobol is working, but it becomes to complex to maintain both in language
> standard itself (How many pages for  Cobol standard?) 

The Cobol standard has the advantage that it is (mostly) upwards
compatible.  I can take some 30 year old code and make it work easily
with the latest compiler.  I expect to be able to do the same in 30
years time with old and current code.

> and Cobol applications. 

Cobol applications change due to changes in business needs.  Cobol has
demonstrated that large complex systems do not become complex to
maintain.

> Bugs because of No type checking at all. 

Are there really ?  Why is that ?  What examples can you show.

> Character based....

It is no more nor less so than, say, C.  It can have GUIs as required
with a choice of several.

> Sometime, I think it is because Java is easier rather than Cobol is out of
> date. 

Is Java 'easier' ?

> Why Microsoft "designed" C# in .NET?

As a spade to beat Java with.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T15:01:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NslBb.15351$3Eb1.12673@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Different person may have different view of the same thing from a different
perspective.
Anyway, a computer language is a tool to get program done, use the best one
you have mastered in the best choice. But for a manager, there are some
other factors: wage, availability, productivty, quality, etc. general for a
group.

I learned Java forced by my boss 7 years ago. We worked together in a C/C++
program for a whole week only to find a void pointer bug. With Java, we
don't worry about deployment difficulty acroos platforms and killed C/C++
directive nightmare. Check :) for specific point of view.

It is an automatic tool to conver source code in one format into another
like Word, PDF,TEXT and other format. At least it offers another choice. I
can't afford Cobol tools,
But I can translate Cobol into Java, and then use free Java tools for UML
and IDE, and then modify code in a big picture. It is like gold mining from
old code. It is also a way to keep Cobol  programming if you like it.


Thomas


> There are several reasons:
>     reduce cost for code maintenance in the long run: Java is easy to
learn
> and use along with its open source,
>     goto OO for the same reason,

Some years ago there was a Datamation report that surveyed how
developers spent there time and what languages they used.  The results
showed something like 70% of Cobol programmers time was spent on
maintenance and only 20% of C programmers time.

Some language converter company took these and concluded that if Cobol
programs were converted to C there would be a huge savings in cost due
to reduction in maintenence.


Can you see why this claim is nonsense ?
:)Singapore government did. Fujisu Did. Canadian government is rewriting
manually in part.
etc.

Two reasons.  The first is that converters do not convert programs
from, say, Cobol into being C or Java programs.  They convert them
into Cobol programs written in the C or Java language.  This means
that neither Cobol nor C/Java programmers cam, nor will want to, work
on them.

In fact I have seen some reccommend that the source be kept and worked
on and converted for recompiling.
:) I think readability for programmer is of first priority.

The 2nd is that tha _applications_ that are traditionally written in
Cobol tend to have changes, called maintenance, that have nothing to
do with making the code work but are entirely the result of changes in
specification.  The owners want it to do something different.  C and
Java programs tend to be for applications that do not have arbitrary
changes and tend to have new versions that count as development, not
maintenance.

This means that costs are more to rise and there is _no_ saving in
what Cobol systems call 'maintenance'.

>     replace native calls for across platform deployment because mainframe
is
> expensive,

<shrug>, that can be done in Cobol.

>     for better Web app using TCP/IP,

<shrug> that can be done in Cobol.  What bits do you do 'better' in
Java that I can't do in Cobol ?

>     access database using SQL in a standard like ODBC or JDBC independent
of
> database product,

<shrug> can do that in Cobol.

> After all, it is a solution to save money and get the latest technology at
> the same time. One stone two birds.

Is it ?  Show the figures.

> Cobol is working, but it becomes to complex to maintain both in language
> standard itself (How many pages for  Cobol standard?)

The Cobol standard has the advantage that it is (mostly) upwards
compatible.  I can take some 30 year old code and make it work easily
with the latest compiler.  I expect to be able to do the same in 30
years time with old and current code.

:) Everything has its life span, what it is for Cobol language anf its
applications?
After I have written a Cobol parser, I dislike Cobol standard now. It's
lengthy and not strict.
For example, PERFORM may or may not closes with END-PERFORM. NESTED PERFORMs
are ambiguous.

> and Cobol applications.

Cobol applications change due to changes in business needs.  Cobol has
demonstrated that large complex systems do not become complex to
maintain.

:) Today's business is Web oriented, Cobol is short in this. TCP/IP API is
not a standard part of Cobol.
It is difficult to move Cobol to Web in comparison with Java. Java is
designed for this. OO is the best means to tackle complexity so far. OO
Cobol is not as good as C++, need less to say Java or C#.

> Bugs because of No type checking at all.

Are there really ?  Why is that ?  What examples can you show.

:) Move numeric and alphanumeric back and forth may cause problem.
Yesterday I fixed a program with literal 0 and "0" mixed.
What's it means to move 1 into an alphanumeric?

> Character based....

It is no more nor less so than, say, C.  It can have GUIs as required
with a choice of several.
:)C is word based in implementation. An int may be 16, 32 or even 64 bytes.
I have never seen 3 bytes int.

> Sometime, I think it is because Java is easier rather than Cobol is out of
> date.

Is Java 'easier' ?
:) Java syntax can be printed in 6 pages and Cobol in 20 using same fount
and size. Easier is only for beginner. For professionals, they are the same
tool to get a job done.

> Why Microsoft "designed" C# in .NET?

As a spade to beat Java with.
:)If Java is a nonsense, why Microsoft want to beat Java? There are
something behind.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T12:41:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312091241.336299a0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vHszb.38122$Y4R1.5356@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3fd102ff$0$6968$7a628cd7@news.club-internet.fr> <Lx6Bb.13360$3Eb1.4830@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <217e491a.0312082003.6194749e@posting.google.com> <NslBb.15351$3Eb1.12673@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote 

> I learned Java forced by my boss 7 years ago. We worked together in a C/C++
> program for a whole week only to find a void pointer bug. With Java, we
> don't worry about deployment difficulty acroos platforms and killed C/C++
> directive nightmare. Check :) for specific point of view.

If you were suggesting to use a C/C++ to Java tool that I may agree
that it would be a useful option to do that.  Of course translating a
buggy C++ into Java does not remove the bugs, but the two languages
are close enough in general structure so that only the idioms are
mismatched.  Again though, if the C++ was badly structured and this
was the cause of the difficulties with it then it will not be any
better because of being translated to a different language.
 
> It is an automatic tool to conver source code in one format into another
> like Word, PDF,TEXT and other format. 

Exactly.  Cobol programs in the Java syntax.  Just like Word -> PDF
the bits that won't work get fudged.

> But I can translate Cobol into Java, and then use free Java tools for UML
> and IDE, 

There are free (or cheap) IDEs that can be used for Cobol if that's
what you want.  Generally I don't, I haven't found an _IDE_ that I
like, preferring to use several separate sessions each with specific
tools of _my_ choice.

In my view, IDEs were developed to overcome the problem of single
tasking OSes (such as CP/M and MS-DOS) which didn't even have command
line editing and so they required continual retyping of cammands to
get things done.  Never done that.  Always (since 1980) used
multi-tasking, command line recall/editing, make, etc.

In any case how much is the converter ?  You get it 'free', but it may
be a waste to buy it just to use free tools.

> and then modify code in a big picture. It is like gold mining from
> old code. It is also a way to keep Cobol  programming if you like it.

In what way does this 'keep Cobol programming' ?  Are you suggesting
that the code be maintained in Cobol and retranslated each time for
recompiling ?

How would one then 'benefit' from Java facilities that are not in
Cobol ?

> :)Singapore government did. Fujisu Did. Canadian government is rewriting
> manually in part.

They may have done the conversion, but have they _actually_ benefitted
from cost savings at all ?

>> In fact I have seen some reccommend that the source be kept and
worked
>> on and converted for recompiling.

> :) I think readability for programmer is of first priority.

So what does that mean ?  Does a Cobol converted to Java become _more_
readable ?  Does it change all the variable names into what a Java
programmer would be more used to (and thus make it incomprehensible to
Cobolers) or what.

Show us some code where the Java is 'more readable'.  Or does this
require 'several years' first ?

Don't take my comments as entirely negative, it is an opportunity to
prove your case, to educate us to the benefits, rather than just
marketing spiel.

> The Cobol standard has the advantage that it is (mostly) upwards
> compatible.  I can take some 30 year old code and make it work easily
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> applications?

If everything has a life span and Cobol has already demonstrated a 40
odd year one with an excellent track record, then what other languages
can show that theirs will last.

Algol ? Fortran ? CPL ? PL/1 ?  C ? Pascal ? Modula2 ? Ada ? C++ ? 
Java ?  C# ?

All these were supposed to become the language that took over. Many
succeeded for particular areas, and for particular times.  Most are
still in use.  But how are we to know that Java will be the one in 10,
20 years time and not strangled by C# or 'the next best thing'.

Personally I use Python because I can write stuff that would take 4
times as much code in Java, and it is just as portable as Java (I can
use Jython which is a Python run time for any JVM).

This is not to say that I _won't_ use Java, just that it is another
option.

> After I have written a Cobol parser, I dislike Cobol standard now. It's
> lengthy and not strict.
> For example, PERFORM may or may not closes with END-PERFORM. NESTED PERFORMs
> are ambiguous.

Have you written a Java parser yet ?

Cobol is a difficult language to parse, but that is a result of
writing the language for _programmers_ and not for compiler writers. 
Pascal, for example is easy to parse, but is not effective enough as a
language (IMHO).


> Cobol applications change due to changes in business needs.  Cobol has
> demonstrated that large complex systems do not become complex to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> not a standard part of Cobol.

I have Web Applications in Cobol since 1995.  Initially written in MS
Cobol 4.5 running on OS/2 IBM WebServer.  The same code further
developed run on Windows, Unix and Linux with various servers.

I could also run applications accross the web using, say, SP/2 Thin
Client.

There are businesses that are 'web orientated' and businesses that are
web based, but most businesses just do business while their IT
departments want to play with new toys.

> It is difficult to move Cobol to Web in comparison with Java. Java is
> designed for this. OO is the best means to tackle complexity so far. OO
> Cobol is not as good as C++, need less to say Java or C#.

OO is excellent for some things.  Most of these things are unrelated
to what business applications want to do.

In the case of C++ many of the OO features are designed to overcome
difficulties in the C language that Cobol never had.

For example C is limited by its built in types.  If you want to handle
user defined types (strucs) with a library then it is necessary to
have a set of procedures that are specific to that type. A slightly
different struc and another set of routines were needed.  C++ fixed
that.  But Cobol was never a problem.  MOVE worked on everything,
Arithmetic worked on every numeric type you could create, no need for
overloading.

This is not to say that OO isn't useful, and especially as a design
tool.

> 
> > Bugs because of No type checking at all.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> :) Move numeric and alphanumeric back and forth may cause problem.

Example please, and why would you do that ?

> Yesterday I fixed a program with literal 0 and "0" mixed.

Languages never fixed bad programming.  But specifically why was this
a bug in the Cobol code ?  Show the code.

> What's it means to move 1 into an alphanumeric?

It is clearly defined by the standard, what are you trying to imply ?

>> Character based.

> It is no more nor less so than, say, C.  It can have GUIs as required
> with a choice of several.

> :)C is word based in implementation. An int may be 16, 32 or even 64 bytes.
> I have never seen 3 bytes int.

You seem to have a different idea what 'character based means'.  I
took it to mean non-graphics.

But what is your point about '3 byte int' ?  What would be wrong with
having one ?  Is it a criticism of C that it is 'word based' or a
benefit ?  How does this relate to Java ?

I think that you took some comments from a brochure without
understanding what they mean.

> :) Java syntax can be printed in 6 pages and Cobol in 20 using same fount
> and size. Easier is only for beginner. For professionals, they are the same
> tool to get a job done.

<choke> The _syntax_ may be simpler, but to get anything done you need
a wall full of library references.


> :)If Java is a nonsense, why Microsoft want to beat Java? There are
> something behind.

Who said 'Java is a nonsense' ?  It may well be the best language for
a large variety of things.  Just as Fortran has been the best language
for many computer applications for many years (and may still be), but
I would not suggest converting Cobol programs to that.

You were claiming things for conversions of Cobol systems to Java.

That _may_ be a nonsence.  Marketing hype does not demonstrate that it
is not.
 
Just because several thousand people send money to Nigeria does not
mean that I should too.
```

Check :) for specific point of view.

If you were suggesting to use a C/C++ to Java tool that I may agree
that it would be a useful option to do that.  Of course translating a
buggy C++ into Java does not remove the bugs, but the two languages
are close enough in general structure so that only the idioms are
mismatched.  Again though, if the C++ was badly structured and this
was the cause of the difficulties with it then it will not be any
better because of being translated to a different language.

:) With C++, it is possible to write good OO code. We do Cobol just because
Cobol is far from OO. Java is chosen as time being. C# will be chosen if it
is used widely.

With OO, I can design abstract class in C++ or interface in Java, which
includes only methods/procedure . For example a Printer with a method
print(Page page) before I really know how many printers and page formats I
need to support. I just need to implement a simple printer and a simple page
format and have my prototype/design done. It is up to programmer to add as
many printers and page format as need at any time. Is it possible to do it
in Cobol in an easy to understand way? In Cobol, when we move data, the
length is checked instead of its type. Type is more meaningful than length.

> It is an automatic tool to conver source code in one format into another
> like Word, PDF,TEXT and other format.

Exactly.  Cobol programs in the Java syntax.  Just like Word -> PDF
the bits that won't work get fudged.
:) It is warned if it won't work. That is the point to be improved. LIke
GOTO, we only support limited GOTO usage.

get things done.  Never done that.  Always (since 1980) used
multi-tasking, command line recall/editing, make, etc.

:) With a good IDE, we can view the structure of a program and manage

In any case how much is the converter ?  You get it 'free', but it may
be a waste to buy it just to use free tools.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T13:44:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd7be40_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vHszb.38122$Y4R1.5356@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3fd102ff$0$6968$7a628cd7@news.club-internet.fr> <Lx6Bb.13360$3Eb1.4830@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <217e491a.0312082003.6194749e@posting.google.com> <NslBb.15351$3Eb1.12673@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <217e491a.0312091241.336299a0@posting.google.com> <qzIBb.35560$r%u1.29217@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Thomas, Richard, PMFJI but there are such good points being made by both
sides here and it is such an excellent exchange I really want to add my 2
cents...<G>

First off, the main question that has to be answered is WHY anyone would
want to convert COBOL to Java.

Thomas says his customers request it, (I agree that market forces are the
prime factor in what happens with software), Richard says the two languages
are so disparate that all you'll end up with is "COBOL" written in Java. (I
agree with that too.)

For preference, I would not want to see conversion from COBOL to Java
because it is a "poor" solution that people who don't understand the
intricacies of technology may believe is bringing them into the modern (and
or OO) World, when, in fact, it isn't.

But if someone offered me $10,000 for a tool that did this, I'd build one
<G>.

I've marked this one even <G>.

Don't blame Thomas for doing it, and wish him every success with it...

Some specific responses and observations below...


"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote in message
news:qzIBb.35560$r%u1.29217@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com...
> Check :) for specific point of view.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> :) With C++, it is possible to write good OO code. We do Cobol just
because
> Cobol is far from OO. Java is chosen as time being. C# will be chosen if
it
> is used widely.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> need to support. I just need to implement a simple printer and a simple
page
> format and have my prototype/design done. It is up to programmer to add as
> many printers and page format as need at any time. Is it possible to do it
> in Cobol in an easy to understand way?
Yes, Fujitsu NETCOBOL (which has supported OO since version 4 and is now on
version 7) supports prototyping of Classes exactly as you describe. For OO
systems that use early binding (I avoid this) it also provides the necessary
repository to catalog prototyped Classes,Methods, Interfaces.

Thomas, the OO implementation of Fujitsu COBOL is really excellent. The
problem with it has not been lack of facilities in the software, it has been
lack of understanding (and consequent resistance) by the COBOL User base.

(If the COBOL user base had embraced OO several years ago, it is unlikely
that there would be any market for Thomas's tool today... watching COBOL
jobs go to Java programmers is an inevitable consequence of the "ostrich"
position adopted by many COBOL sites. I know this is unpalatable, but I said
so at the time and no-one took any notice. Whether COBOL people can see it
or not, the future is in OO; ignore it at your peril.)

> In Cobol, when we move data, the
> length is checked instead of its type. Type is more meaningful than
length.

That isn't strictly true. Type conversion occurs automatically on numeric
moves. On alphanumeric moves length is checked and space fill provided when
necessary (or not, depending on what COBOL facilities you
use...STRING/MOVE).
>
> > It is an automatic tool to conver source code in one format into another
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Obviously if it simply CAN'T be converted there must be diagnostics and
manual intervention.  Richard is right when he says "COBOL programs in the
Java syntax".

I wrote a tool a few years ago that converts ISAM files to a Relational
Database in third normal form. While this is very useful, at the end of the
day you get a system that is really an ISAM database wrapped as Relational.
From a purist point of view, this is bad, but from a practical point of view
it means that all your previously "closed" (accessible only by COBOL) files
are now open for business and can be accessed with standard tools. It is
also possible to apply the wealth of RDB performance enhancing tools and
methodologies.  Expansion and growth of the system is facilitated. There
have been positive spin offs from these conversions that I never anticipated
when I wrote the tool.

It is very possible that Thomas's coverter may provide similar unexpected
benefits. (At least the COBOL can now be leveraged in to the Classes
available in Java, for a start...)

The point is that sometimes an imperfect solution is a starting point for a
better solution, and most times is better than no solution at all.

<snipped very even exchange...>
>
> They may have done the conversion, but have they _actually_ benefitted
> from cost savings at all ?
> :)Maintenence cost 80% of a project. It likes starting a business. In
roder
> to save/earn, you need to spend/invest. Nothing comes free. Free IDE costs
> time when error occurs. After several years, it will show.
>

This is not an answer, Thomas. The fact is that you simply don't know...yet.
You HOPE it will ease maintenance costs, but Richard's points are valid;
COBOL is an excellent language for maintaining computer systems.

Like him, I am not persuaded that maintenance costs on Java systems are any
less than those on COBOL systems.

The benefits are elsewhere...

> >> In fact I have seen some reccommend that the source be kept and
> worked
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> :)For example GEORGE-BUSH will be GeorgeBush.

Hahaha! You know as well as we do that that is no more "readable"... It is
just a question of what is more familiar. (Personally, I don't like Upper
Case names in any language, but I don't find them less "readable"...)

> The true thing behind is to replace file operations with database. You
know
> Cobol is equipped with index file and used for large scale data storage.
It
> is not easy to access it by other tools. Therefore some customer wants to
> replace it with a database.
>
Yes, this has been going on for years.

> For database, because Cobol is a pioner to use it, native calls are used
> instead of ODBC/JDBC standard. This makes Cobol applications stuck to a
> database product/vendor.

Don't understand this. I use RDB exclusively for all Master file storage and
ALL of it is via ODBC, in COBOL. It is part of the n-tier model.

>
> Don't take my comments as entirely negative, it is an opportunity to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hey, I'd really like to see that <G>! (Maybe when Hell freezes over?)

> > The Cobol standard has the advantage that it is (mostly) upwards
> > compatible.  I can take some 30 year old code and make it work easily
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are right. Though I derive no pleasure from saying so. COBOL is dying,
and the fact that it could have been saved is just
vinegar in the hemlock.

> If everything has a life span and Cobol has already demonstrated a 40
> odd year one with an excellent track record, then what other languages
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Past performance is no guarantee of future performance. Consult your
Adviser."

> All these were supposed to become the language that took over. Many
> succeeded for particular areas, and for particular times.  Most are
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> VM instruction file format.

The point being missed here is that no ONE language will carry us into the
future. (Not Java, not C++, not C#, and certainly not COBOL.) The days when
it could all be done in ONE language (COBOL, FORTRAN, or possibly ALGOL) are
long gone. "Languages" themselves will acquire the significance that 8086
Assembler has today, and for the same reasons. (Yes, I know 8086 Assembler
is alive and well in some quarters, where even Windows applications are
being developed with it, but how many commercial installations do you know
that employ  8086 Assembler programmers,  as a matter of course?)

>
> Personally I use Python because I can write stuff that would take 4
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Exactly. It is simplified if you only know one language <G>.

<snip>
> >
> > :) Today's business is Web oriented, Cobol is short in this. TCP/IP API
is
> > not a standard part of Cobol.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree, Richard, and have done similar (though not as extensively as you
have). But we have to recognise that the MAJORITY of COBOL sites simply
haven't embraced COBOL as a Web tool. Thomas is right.#

> There are businesses that are 'web orientated' and businesses that are
> web based, but most businesses just do business while their IT
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Windows for three years because I think it is a toy copied. Today is
copied
> everywhere.
>

Excellent, Thomas. I like that phrase ".. a new toy may become a weapon."
It is often difficult to persuade "Management" that "play" is an essential
part of the learning curve for most Humans. You have expressed it succinctly
and powerfully. I shall steal your words with no shame...<G>

> > It is difficult to move Cobol to Web in comparison with Java. Java is
> > designed for this. OO is the best means to tackle complexity so far. OO
> > Cobol is not as good as C++, need less to say Java or C#.
>

This simply demonstrates your lack of experience with OO COBOL. It is every
bit as good as other OO languages, BUT, it is more verbose and it suffers
from being added to the language, rather than designed into it from day one,
as Java had OO designed into it. (Nevertheless, the implementation of OO
into COBOL is a major and impressive feat of software engineering and I take
my hat off to the people who did it...)

> OO is excellent for some things.  Most of these things are unrelated
> to what business applications want to do.
> :) I don't think so. OO is kind of  philosophy or a way to view real world
> in a structural way. Java, C++ or C# are olny implementation of OO.
>
As you might expect, I agree completely with Thomas here.

> In the case of C++ many of the OO features are designed to overcome
> difficulties in the C language that Cobol never had.
> :) I don't think so. C++ is slower than C. FOr critical real time
> application, I use C or assembly.
>
Not sure on this. Richard may have a point.

> For example C is limited by its built in types.  If you want to handle
> user defined types (strucs) with a library then it is necessary to
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
All I know is that, having used OO for some years now, I would not go back
to procedural COBOL, any more than I would go back to using 8086
Assembler... I find OO techniques very applicable to what Business wants to
do, and have built a number of Business components and tools using it. For
Network applications (including the Web) I find it indispensible.

<snip>

> :)  Just another choice. There is someone who needs it. I got two requests
> for trial every week. Cobol applications are not mine. It's up to the
owner
> to decide.
>

Yes, it is. There are many "Managers" who will opt for this course out of
ignorance, thinking that if they "Go Java" they have more chance of
justifying their position to their superiors... "Hey, I converted all that
old COBOL crap to the latest language...don't blame me if it is giving us
problems."  Some will really understand that it is a "less than perfect"
solution but see little alternative, within the confines of allocated
budgets. (Proper conversion and mining of COBOL Legacy is an expensive
process and everybody likes a "Quick Fix"...)

At the end of the day, it is the Corporation's money. If Managers have the
authority to spend it and get it wrong, that is part of what Business is
about.

They have a right to do what they want (even if it is technically flawed),
and Thomas is providing an option that they obviously DO want.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T10:32:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312111032.4ebe692@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vHszb.38122$Y4R1.5356@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3fd102ff$0$6968$7a628cd7@news.club-internet.fr> <Lx6Bb.13360$3Eb1.4830@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <217e491a.0312082003.6194749e@posting.google.com> <NslBb.15351$3Eb1.12673@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <217e491a.0312091241.336299a0@posting.google.com> <qzIBb.35560$r%u1.29217@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3fd7be40_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> They have a right to do what they want (even if it is technically flawed),
> and Thomas is providing an option that they obviously DO want.

You are quite right, except that what they _want_ is for their systems
to be Java programs or C++ programs or C# programs.  This would allow
them to benefit from all the hype they read and from all the newbies
coming out of school having been taught enough syntax to get past an
interview.

The question that I ask is whether Thomas is actually providing what
they _want_, or something that sounds like what they want but will
give them all the disadvantages of the existing code (reliance on
Cobol programmers, archaic structures) plus all the disadvantages of
the new languages.

It would be interesting to see some of the actual code translation. 
At the lowest level it could be merely encapulating the Cobol
statements into called routines that implement the Cobol
functionality:

            MOVE X  TO Y   ->   JCobolMove(&X, &Y, 32, 40);

I have seen Cobol to C translators do this and it is not pretty.  To
be fair I have seen Cobol compilers do this too, eg Kobol, but they
are not selling the resulting C++ source as the end product.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T17:38:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312101738.7ea77ef9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vHszb.38122$Y4R1.5356@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3fd102ff$0$6968$7a628cd7@news.club-internet.fr> <Lx6Bb.13360$3Eb1.4830@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <217e491a.0312082003.6194749e@posting.google.com> <NslBb.15351$3Eb1.12673@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <217e491a.0312091241.336299a0@posting.google.com> <qzIBb.35560$r%u1.29217@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote

> Check :) for specific point of view.

Can't you do _standard_ marking for quoted material ?

> Java is chosen as time being. C# will be chosen if it
> is used widely.

That pretty well shoots you in the foot.  You started by saying 'how
long will Cobol last' as if implying that Java is the future, and now
you say you will change to whatever turns out to be the next trendy
thing.

So how long do you think Java will last ?

I suppose this does make good business sense, for the developer of
language conversion tools.  Keep them churning from one language to
the next and profit.

Good business practice for Cobol sites may well be to keep Cobol.

>> Exactly.  Cobol programs in the Java syntax.  Just like Word -> PDF
>> the bits that won't work get fudged.

> :) With a good IDE, we can view the structure of a program and manage
> method/procedure more easily from top.

Class browser doen't have to be part of an IDE.

> :)Changed with widely accepted convention. For example GEORGE-BUSH will be
> GeorgeBush.

I am using proper case in Cobol now, I don't know any compiler that
_requires_ all upper case.  It may be that changing it makes it more
readable, but that does not require going to Java to do that.

> Show us some code where the Java is 'more readable'.  Or does this
> require 'several years' first ?
> 
> :)For example GEORGE-BUSH will be GeorgeBush.

Wheteher it is 'more readable' or not depends entirely on what the
reader is used to. (Peter: add that to my scorecard will you).

> The true thing behind is to replace file operations with database. You know
> Cobol is equipped with index file and used for large scale data storage. It
> is not easy to access it by other tools. Therefore some customer wants to
> replace it with a database.

And does you Cobol -> Java automatically convert all the SELECT
ASSIGNs, FDs and READs and WRITEs into database access ?
 
> For database, because Cobol is a pioner to use it, native calls are used
> instead of ODBC/JDBC standard. This makes Cobol applications stuck to a
> database product/vendor.

Excuse me, but where were you over the last few years ?  I am using
ODBC for my access from Cobol.
 
> :)I like your comments. It is a kind of democracy. If you have enough to
> defeat me, I will close my company or do something else.

I am not trying to 'defeat' you, I am trying to get you to support
your claims rather that just using marketing hype.
 
> :)accroding to my experience, only C, C++, Java and C# are left while
> FORTRAN has the same importance as Cobol.

FORTRAN is still used in scientific computing.

> :)If Java or C# dead, it would be easier to bury them. There are two ways:
> from source code and binary code. Both Java and C# has binary standard for
> VM instruction file format.

But both are _different_.
 
> :)Why not for both? If a compiler is small enough, it can be embeded into
> small applications.

Well, yes, I can embedded Python into my Cobol programs.

>> > :) Move numeric and alphanumeric back and forth may cause
problem.
> 
>> Example please, and why would you do that ?
> :) group them together and we get the freedom.

What sort of answer was that ?

> > What's it means to move 1 into an alphanumeric?
> 
> It is clearly defined by the standard, what are you trying to imply ?

> :)  I think it is not natural. Move 1 makes alphanumeric into 1, my daughter
> would infer move 1/3 will leave 1/3 not 0.333333333333333

In what way could you in Cobol 'MOVE 1/3 TO somewhere' ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T16:12:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GeidnZ7DDZD_B0qiRVn-sQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<vHszb.38122$Y4R1.5356@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <SrudnY44HeWypkuiRVn-vw@giganews.com> <WCIBb.35581$r%u1.14469@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Thomas A. Li wrote:
> For Cobol applications to move in the future,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> better.

Most of the time it depends on the application. Just as you would not use a
can opener to investigate wool yields on nesting sheep, you shouldn't use
COBOL there either.

For business applications (payroll, banking, general ledger, inventory
control, record keeping, insurance, etc.), COBOL is at the top of the list.

For finding oil, engineering, scientific calculations, FORTRAN and its
relatives should be the first choice.

For flames, pop-ups, programming toasters, things that wiggle and go "BOO!",
multi-media, games, keeping track of your cat picture collection, web
diversions, and the like, Java is indicated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol2Java: a Cobol to Java source code converter is available for evaluation

- **From:** "RH" <nospam@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T02:17:40+00:00

This looks like a very interesting project.  And must have been fun to
design.

However, I have to say -- after looking at your code examples -- that the
results are "not Java".  Yes, they may compile but I would not want to have
my clients maintaining that code.

I supposed it could be refactored, but that looks like a lot of work in
itself.

news:vHszb.38122$Y4R1.5356@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com...

and

> are supported without learning curves. Fast, no random error and low cost.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<a id="t-2a3f4eafb9"></a>
## Thread: Getting File Name from a ksh script — 8 msgs

### Getting File Name from a ksh script

- **From:** "Dan" <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T00:33:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vstl8o6jaibl11@corp.supernews.com>`

```
We're in the process of converting from Compaq Cobol to MF Cobol.

In Compaq Cobol I could assign a generic filename then override it in a ksh
script. I MF Cobol it doesn't like that and tries to use the file in the
Select/Assign.

Here's what I am doing:

FILE-CONTROL
        SELECT TRANFILE ASSIGN TO 'TRANFILE'
                       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
        SELECT TRANSRPT ASSIGN TO 'TRANSRPT'.


Here's the script

TRANFILE=tranfile.dat; export TRANFILE
TRANSRPT=transrpt.lis; export TRANSRPT
pgmname.exe

Is there a way to do the same thing in MF? We have some programs that are
used multiple times with various inputs/outputs plus it is easier to track
the files in the scripts.

Thanks,
Dan
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting File Name from a ksh script

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T02:55:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IdGzb.12025$yd.1680464@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<vstl8o6jaibl11@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstl8o6jaibl11@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Dan wrote:
> We're in the process of converting from Compaq Cobol to MF Cobol.
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Use a data name instead of a quoted variable, and then set the value 
appropriately before open/close.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting File Name from a ksh script

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.MicroFocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T11:02:34
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqn549$tiu$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<vstl8o6jaibl11@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Dan.

Rather than use

> TRANFILE=tranfile.dat; export TRANFILE
> TRANSRPT=transrpt.lis; export TRANSRPT

Micro Focus COBOL supports Filename Mapping thus :

dd_TRANFILE=tranfile.dat ; export dd_TRANFILE
dd_TRANSRPT=transrpt.lis ; export dd_TRANSRPT

This will map the 'TRANFILE' and 'TRANSRPT' definitions in your program to
the appropriate filename.

See the Server Express File Handling manual, chapter 3, in the section
"Filename Mapping" for more details.

SimonT.
```

- **Date:** 2003-12-04T09:03:38-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<vsuj69263jna54@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstl8o6jaibl11@corp.supernews.com> <bqn549$tiu$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```

#### ↳ Re: Getting File Name from a ksh script

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T11:42:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCF1B15.F6821427@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<vstl8o6jaibl11@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Dan wrote:

> We're in the process of converting from Compaq Cobol to MF Cobol.
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dan

I have no idea what a ksh script file is, plus I'm curious what the reference
is to the pgmname.exe in that file. I've been doing the following since Day One
using RM/COBOL, Microsoft COBOL and M/F DOS COBOL and Net Express. It must be
very close to what you want to do - see if it's adaptable.

Firstly I have two sets of ISAMs (very little use of Sequentials). :-

(a) General - data applicable to all the following (b) sets
(b) Files specific to a geographical location.

The application has a Default file - one record - so uses ws-RelativeKey = 1

01 Relative-Record.
   05 Client-Facility.
      10 ClientNumber pic x(05). *> e.g. "AM500'
      10 FacilitiyNumber pic 9(02) *> e.g. "23"
   05 ClientName pic x(30). *> e.g. Amoco Canada Ltd
   05 FacilityName pc x(30) *> e.g. Waterton Ethylene Plant
   05 other stuff..........

Application starts with a Master Menu and reads the Default file to see what is
the current Client/Facility setting. Uses Client/Facility with
CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXISTS. Even just using the old limit of 8 plus 3 for suffix
each file in a subset (b) can be identified :-

AM50023I.dat - "I" = Items
AM50023R.dat - "R" = Readings etc.....

If the FILE_EXISTS fails then there's a problem. If OK then you have the
filenames to pass to called programs :-

FILE-CONTROL.

     Select               Data-File
     assign               Data-filename
     organization         indexed
     access               dynamic
     record key           Data-PrimeKey
     file status          ws-FileStatus.

Data-Filename can be passed to the called program or each called program could
read the Default file to establish what it wants.

The Master Menu also allows :-

1 - Create new set of files - prompts for Client/Facility and then repeats
above opening a fresh set of files and putting the new ID in the Default File..

2. User might have finished with current set after two hours. Selects from Menu
to change current  ID. (Obviously close existing set). Prompt for new
Client/Facility ID which is put into the Default record and as above,
"CHECK_EXISTS" or prompt 'Do you want a new set ?'.

Even given a multi user situation the (Relative) Default File could have a
record per user - for ten users, records 1 through 10. Don't do something daft
like allocating a 3-digit user ID otherwise you finish up with unnecessary
empty space in your Relative file.

Any resemblance to what you want to do - if not come back with specifics ?

"We have some programs that are used multiple times with various inputs/outputs
plus it is easier to track"

Not quite clear when you say programs used multiple times. Assuming you *are*
using Net Express - do you want to live dangerously and cut your teeth on OO
:-). I can give you source to show that 4 classes (programs) can handle ISAM,
Relative, Sequential, LineSequential,  each file with a different record
length. E-mail me if interested.

I think my approach is simpler, but for another example of using OO with files,
go to cobolreport.com and check out the OO Examples from  Gene Web. He used Net
Express or its immediate predecessor VISOC. His file example is wrapped in a
tool called Rational Rose, so not knowing the tool it's a little difficult to
follow. But if you check through his code, I think you'll find it is
comprehensive and probably handles all you would want to do with COBOL files.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

OO

ISAM,
> Relative, Sequential, LineSequential,  each file with a different record

>

> go to cobolreport.com and check out the OO Examples from  Gene Web. He
used Net
> Express or its immediate predecessor VISOC. His file example is wrapped in
a
> tool called Rational Rose, so not knowing the tool it's a little difficult
to
> follow. But if you check through his code, I think you'll find it is
> comprehensive and probably handles all you would want to do with COBOL
files.
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>

ksh refers to using the Korn shell in Unix. The script is comparable to JCL
in MVS or DCL on a Vax. pgmname.exe is the name of the program I am running.
We append the .exe to make the file readily identifiable as a program. The
format is equivialant to EXEC PGM=PGMNAME in MVS JCL.

We do not run any interactive programs (other than some SAS). These programs
are used in a batch environment. MF Cobol allows you to hard code a file
name in the program, it should also allow you get the file name from the
script/JCL. /this makes it easier to track files as they move through the
system and allows a program to be used multiple times. For example, I have
an invoicing program that has creates standard invoices, but uses different
cover letters depending on what file I am using for the invoice.

The files exist, it's just not getting them from the script. I don't need to
check for existance since the OPEN will error if the file is not found.

Thanks
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Getting File Name from a ksh script

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCF6975.F40EC38B@shaw.ca>`

Dan wrote:

>
> ksh refers to using the Korn shell in Unix. The script is comparable to JCL
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

BUT, PLEASE, PLEASE don't call it MF COBOL ! "MF" covers a group of compilers,
and at the current time there are three that even use the word 'Express".

Another example of ambiguity. Yours truly, an ex-Brit bumps into a Brit tourist
here in the summer. By accent can sort of place them in south-west, London and
south-east, Midlands, Wales, west coast (Lancashire), east coast (Yorkshire), or
up in bonny Scotland.
.
Conversation goes as follows :-

Q: "Hello. Where are you from ?"
A: "England"
-    (I already knew that !)
Q: 'Which part of England ?"
A: "London".
-    (I knew that too !)
Q: "Which part of London ?".
A: "South"
-  (Oh my gawd !)
Q: 'Where abouts in London ?"
A: "On the Thames"
-   (&%$$@! Again !)
Q: "Where on the Thames ?"
A: "At a place called Walton. Have you heard of it ?".

No question - just my final answer

"Yes I know Walton on Thames. That's where Julie Andrews comes from. And my wife
lived up the road at Sunbury on Thames".

Capice ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Internal Sorts — 97 msgs

### Internal Sorts

- **From:** "Dan" <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T00:47:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I have some programs with internal sorts, they work fine under Compaq Cobol
but error when using MF Cobol.  I get a clean compile, but abend when
executing the program when using the same files to test with.

here is the error:
 I/O error : file '/projects/fr657/prog/SORTFILE'
error code: 9/018 (ANS74), pc=0, call=1, seg=0
 18     Read part record error: EOF before EOR or file open in wrong
mode

It can't be a partial record since this works under Compaq. And it can't be
opening wrong since the system opens the SD.

Any ideas?

Thanks,
Dan
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T10:17:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031204111821.506$2Y@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Dan" <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote:
> I have some programs with internal sorts, they work fine under Compaq Cobol
> but error when using MF Cobol.  I get a clean compile, but abend when
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any ideas?


You don't show us the format of your SORT command, so we don't know
if it's USING/GIVING or INPUT PROCEDURE/OUTPUT PROCEDURE.
However, some compiler/OS permit you to SORT USING a file that is
currently open and some do not. Check to see if you have closed all input
sort files prior to executing the SORT.
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T17:45:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FCF703A.4C138B00@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Dan wrote:

> I have some programs with internal sorts, they work fine under Compaq Cobol
> but error when using MF Cobol.  I get a clean compile, but abend when
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dan

As Judson said, have to provide more info. At least I now know you are using
Server Express ? I have very little requirement for sorting, but you can't get
simpler than this :-

*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "sortProbes".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
FILE-CONTROL.

     Select               Data-File
     assign               Data-filename
     organization         LINE SEQUENTIAL.

     Select               Sort-File
     assign               Sort-Filename.

FILE SECTION.

FD  Data-File.
copy "rmprobeA.cpy" replacing ==(tag1)== by ==01 Data-record==,
                               ==(tag)==  by ==Data==.
-------------------rmprobeA.cpy -------------------------------

    01 Data-record.

   10 Data-CliFacID              PIC X(07).
   10 FILLER                      Pic x value ",".
   10 Data-INSPECTIONDate        PIC 9(06).
   10 FILLER                      Pic x value ",".
   10 Data-PROBENUMBER           PIC 9(02).
   10 FILLER                      PIC x value ",".
   10 Data-MAKE                  PIC X(13).
   10 FILLER                      PIC x value ",".
   10 Data-FREQUENCY             PIC 9(01).9(02).
   10 FILLER                      Pic x value ",".
   10 Data-DIAMETER              PIC 9(01).9(03).
   10 FILLER                      PIC x value ",".
   10 Data-ELEMENT               PIC X(01).
   10 FILLER                      PIC x value ",".
   10 Data-TESTBLOCK             PIC X(10).
   10 FILLER                      PIC x value ",".
   10 Data-CALIBRATION           PIC X(10).
   10 FILLER                      PIC x value ",".

---------------------------------------------------------------
SD Sort-File.
copy "rmprobeA.cpy" replacing ==(tag1)== by ==01 Sort-record==,
                               ==(tag)==  by ==Sort==.
Procedure Division.
  Sort Sort-File on ascending Sort-Record
       using     Data-File
       giving    Data-File

End Method "sortProbes".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

As you are doing the switch to Micro Focus - are you aware of Answer Exchange ?

Go to microfocus.com, join Answer Exchange and post your queries under Server
Express.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** Jim Reinhart <jreinhart1@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T15:20:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On 12/3/03 11:47 PM, in article vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com, "Dan"
<deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote:

> I have some programs with internal sorts, they work fine under Compaq Cobol
> but error when using MF Cobol.  I get a clean compile, but abend when
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
If possible, use an external sort from Syncsort.  It runs much faster and
would probably make the program much easier to understand.  Generally, good
shops do not use internal sorts.  Syncsort makes their product for just
about every platform.  There is a lot less hassle with file IO when changing
products as each has their own quirks.

Regards,
Jim Reinhart
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T00:41:12+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net>`

```
On Thu, 04 Dec 2003 15:20:41 -0700 Jim Reinhart <jreinhart1@cox.net> wrote:

:>If possible, use an external sort from Syncsort.  It runs much faster and
:>would probably make the program much easier to understand. 

Depending on how one is manipulating the data.

Obviously a COBOL SORT USING/GIVING will be slower than an external sort.

Why do you find the SORT verb that hard to understand?

:>                                                            Generally, good
:>shops do not use internal sorts.  

They certainly do.

:>                                  Syncsort makes their product for just
:>about every platform.  

And one would equally expect that they would be invoked via the COBOL SORT
verb.

On MVS it is certainly the case.

:>                       There is a lot less hassle with file IO when changing
:>products as each has their own quirks.

Only to those who choose to limit their programming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T15:26:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com>`

```

On  5-Dec-2003, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> :>> Obviously a COBOL SORT USING/GIVING will be slower than an external
> :>> sort.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> How could it not be true?

1.   I am assuming you are using USING/GIVING to mean that it is using or giving
a file - otherwise why bother with CoBOL.
2.   Now you're comparing apples and oranges.   CoBOL's way of applying the
business rules used in determining the sort may be more efficient.   It IS
designed to handle business rules.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 15:26:12 GMT "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

:>On  5-Dec-2003, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

:>> :>> Obviously a COBOL SORT USING/GIVING will be slower than an external

:>> Extra program code loading.

:>> Extra overhead.

:>a file - otherwise why bother with CoBOL.

USING and GIVING.

And, yes, there is no reason to use COBOL in such a case.

:>2.   Now you're comparing apples and oranges.   CoBOL's way of applying the
:>business rules used in determining the sort may be more efficient.   It IS
:>designed to handle business rules.

Which is irrelevant when USING/GIVING - as no logic is done.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T12:11:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
>
>>> 2.   Now you're comparing apples and oranges.   CoBOL's way of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Which is irrelevant when USING/GIVING - as no logic is done.

Only if you don't count the program stack as part of "logic flow."
USING/GIVING is an alternative to exiting a program, invoking a standalone
sort, then starting another program.

Further, it's easy to envision a scenario where the same data have to be
sorted multiple times within a job (like an employee directory:
name/room/division/telephone/employee number/blood-type etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T10:39:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com...

> Such a program should be using INPUT and/or OUTPUT PROCEDURES.

No, not necessarily.  All other things being equal, I would contend a
"minimal" INPUT/OUTPUT procedure sort (that is, one that did nothing
substantive more than READ/RELEASE and RETURN/WRITE in the respective
procedures) would be less straightforward in coding, and would cause the
execution of MORE object code in both the program and the sort procedures in
the system software (and thus entail more overhead) than the corresponding
USING/GIVING sort.  I would vote for USING/GIVING in terms of performance
and overhead.   So, yes, you *could* write obscure, slow code, but I would
disagree with your contention that you *should*.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2oba$2b2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iqh9tv8177k43a91atbdvfj2b4dn799s71@4ax.com>`
