```
"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:iqh9tv8177k43a91atbdvfj2b4dn799s71@4ax.com...

> My point is that if no processing is done the SORT utility should be
executed
> directly.

And my point is twofold:

1)  That may be true on *some* machines, but it is by no means a universal
truth, and it is by no means true on Unisys MCP mainframes.

2)  A USING/GIVING sort involves processing -- if nothing more than the
comparison of the keys to accomplish the ordering of the records.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T15:12:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On  8-Dec-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> > Such a program should be using INPUT and/or OUTPUT PROCEDURES.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> disagree with your contention that you *should*.

The only reason I have come up for having a CoBOL with both using and giving is
so that the program can pick the sort order.   Typically internal sorts are done
because they have processing that gets us the output better.

e.g.  Create a sorted file of all vendors who have each part needed for my
widget - sorted by average distance from our factory.

One way to make a sort efficient is to exclude unneeded records early.   Input
procedures sometimes do this more easily and clearly than sort parms - and
sometimes do this using criteria that cannot be done by sort parms without
multiple passes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T08:45:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On our system, among the motives for using a USING...GIVING sort within a
program could be to avoid having an extra two job steps.

So far as I've been able to tell, if our users have the need to sort
information as part of a process they are more likely to write a single
program that happens to include the source than they are to write separate
preprocess, sort and postprocess programs.  And I don't think the sort
language compiler (the A Series equivalent of a "stand-alone" sort utility,
while provided as part of system software, is not used all that much; when
people want to write "stand-alone" sorts, they typically write them in
either ALGOL or one of the COBOLs depending on their predilections.

Many, and perhaps most, of our largest users have organized their data in
DMSII data bases, and if they've set up the right sets and subsets pointing
to their data sets in the first place (or added them later), they already
can access the data in any sequence they want, even excluding data
automatically, without the need for sorting.    I think there's some
sentiment in the A Series world that sorts, whether embedded or standalone,
are moving toward "quaint", evoking images of 80-80 list utilities and
reel-to-reel tapes.   Still useful, but not used that much in the biggest,
most complex and most current applications.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  8-Dec-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > execution of MORE object code in both the program and the sort
procedures in
> > the system software (and thus entail more overhead) than the
corresponding
> > USING/GIVING sort.  I would vote for USING/GIVING in terms of
performance
> > and overhead.   So, yes, you *could* write obscure, slow code, but I
would
> > disagree with your contention that you *should*.
>
> The only reason I have come up for having a CoBOL with both using and
giving is
> so that the program can pick the sort order.   Typically internal sorts
are done
> because they have processing that gets us the output better.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> One way to make a sort efficient is to exclude unneeded records early.
Input
> procedures sometimes do this more easily and clearly than sort parms - and
> sometimes do this using criteria that cannot be done by sort parms without
> multiple passes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T18:27:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br545r$ia8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On  9-Dec-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> On our system, among the motives for using a USING...GIVING sort within a
> program could be to avoid having an extra two job steps.

If you are using both a USING and a GIVING, what are two extra job steps doing?

Also - if your decision is based upon preferring one complicated job step
instead of several simple job steps - why?


> So far as I've been able to tell, if our users have the need to sort
> information as part of a process they are more likely to write a single
> program that happens to include the source than they are to write separate
> preprocess, sort and postprocess programs.

When there are business rules to include, why not have sort input and output
procedures?

> And I don't think the sort
> language compiler (the A Series equivalent of a "stand-alone" sort utility,
> while provided as part of system software, is not used all that much; when
> people want to write "stand-alone" sorts, they typically write them in
> either ALGOL or one of the COBOLs depending on their predilections.

I've never seen a shop do this when they have the native sort utility available.
  I did see a shop that didn't have one, copy files to indexed files and back to
sort.    Why compile a CoBOL program to call the sort program when you could
have called it directly quicker and easier?


> Many, and perhaps most, of our largest users have organized their data in
> DMSII data bases, and if they've set up the right sets and subsets pointing
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> most complex and most current applications.

Of course.   But I do the same thing with CoBOL sorts to allow the user to order
the report by ID or name (for instance).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T12:13:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br5adq$157n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br545r$ia8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:br545r$ia8$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  9-Dec-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > On our system, among the motives for using a USING...GIVING sort within
a
> > program could be to avoid having an extra two job steps.
>
> If you are using both a USING and a GIVING, what are two extra job steps
doing?

If a USING ... GIVING sort is unconditionally a Bad Idea, and INPUT/OUTPUT
isn't feasible or appropriate to the particular application,  the remaining
choice seems to me to be some sort of standalone sort as the middle step of
three program initiations.

> Also - if your decision is based upon preferring one complicated job step
> instead of several simple job steps - why?

Task initiation is relatively expensive on A Series systems.   There is
quite a bit less overhead in having WFL run a single object code file
encompassing the three processes than WFL run a separate program for each
process.

Perhaps a better example:  all other things being equal, a design that has a
COBOL85 program calling three *nested* programs implementing each of these
three process steps (particularly with a liberal use of GLOBAL data) will be
considerably more efficient than having basically the same three programs
compiled as independent standalone programs and executed separately.

> When there are business rules to include, why not have sort input and
output
> procedures?

Nothing wrong with that, but if for whatever reason the business rules call
for preservation of the raw unsorted and the raw sorted data the
USING...GIVING sort is probably more efficient.   Using the procedures *may*
not be as clear as delineating the process steps in the program.

> I've never seen a shop do this when they have the native sort utility
available.

Well, haven't been around A Series shops much, have you!

>   I did see a shop that didn't have one, copy files to indexed files and
back to
> sort.

Seen that done too.  Depending on the implementation of indexed files and
the size of the files, might even be an effective alternative!  See also
below.

>Why compile a CoBOL program to call the sort program when you could
> have called it directly quicker and easier?

The SORT verb in COBOL is directly compiled by the COBOL compiler and the
generated object code directly calls the sort routines in the system
software.  The COBOL program does not call a sort *program*, it calls the
same sort *mechanism* that a sort *program* would call.  I would contend
that a sort *program* (one that does nothing but sort -- e.g., the
equivalent of SORT ... USING ... GIVING) written *in COBOL85*  is likely to
be more efficient than one written *in the SORT language* (or in ALGOL, for
that matter).

The A Series did not even have a sort *utility* for a very long time; anyone
who wanted to write a utility sort most commonly wrote a tiny program in
ALGOL that used the SORT statement in that language, or (less commonly)
wrote it in COBOL.

As part of the process of encouraging our B1000 users to migrate to the A
Series a number of years back, we made a bunch of changes to the system
software to assist in those migrations.  On the B1000, many shops had
sequences of batch RPG programs with interspersed utility SORTs.

At the time, the A Series did not even have a sort *utility*.  Most
commonly, anyone who wanted to write a utility sort would dash it off in
ALGOL, and that would be the standard "Burroughs Large System" expectation
of the time.  Many of the shops that we were encouraging to move to A
eries  -- frequently diehard RPG shops -- were *most* reluctant to force
their programmers to use ALGOL.  The SORT *language* (the closest thing to a
utility sort mechanism on A Series), as I remember it, was implemented
*primarily* to assist in migrating those customers to A Series.   Anyone's
welcome to use it.  But not many do.  There have been a couple of
corrections over the last few years based on listing format requirements
changes, but other than that the last user problem report I see that
required a software change was submitted seven years ago.

> Of course.   But I do the same thing with CoBOL sorts to allow the user to
order
> the report by ID or name (for instance).

I agree, but if the selection is something that's done frequently, it *may*
be more efficient to have the necessary keys set up as sets or subsets (or,
for that matter, as alternate record keys) and built automatically.    To
cite your example of copying a "flat file" as an ISAM file, and then copying
it back in key order, it is *possible* that in some circumstances it would
be better to go ahead and make the file indexed, with alternate record keys,
in the first place.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T12:25:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br5b3k$15nv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br545r$ia8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Format problems and some unclear wording on my first try, now cancelled.
This may make more sense:

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:br545r$ia8$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  9-Dec-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > On our system, among the motives for using a USING...GIVING sort within
a
> > program could be to avoid having an extra two job steps.
>
> If you are using both a USING and a GIVING, what are two extra job steps
doing?

If a USING ... GIVING sort is unconditionally a Bad Idea, and INPUT/OUTPUT
isn't feasible or appropriate to the particular application,  the remaining
choice seems to me to be some sort of standalone sort as the middle step of
three program initiations.

> Also - if your decision is based upon preferring one complicated job step
> instead of several simple job steps - why?

Task initiation is relatively expensive on A Series systems.   There is
quite a bit less overhead in having WFL run a single object code file
encompassing the three processes than WFL run a separate program for each
process.

Perhaps a better example:  all other things being equal, a design that has a
COBOL85 program calling three *nested* programs implementing each of these
three process steps (particularly with a liberal use of GLOBAL data) will be
considerably more efficient than having basically the same three programs
compiled as independent standalone programs and executed separately.

> When there are business rules to include, why not have sort input and
> output procedures?

Nothing wrong with that, but if for whatever reason the business rules call
for preservation of the raw unsorted and the raw sorted data the
USING...GIVING sort is probably more efficient.   Using the procedures *may*
not be as clear as delineating the process steps in the program.

> I've never seen a shop do this when they have the native sort utility
available.

Well, haven't been around A Series shops much, have you!

>   I did see a shop that didn't have one, copy files to indexed files and
> back to sort.

Seen that done too.  Depending on the implementation of indexed files and
the size of the files, might even be an effective alternative!  See also
below.

>Why compile a CoBOL program to call the sort program when you could
> have called it directly quicker and easier?

The SORT verb in COBOL is directly compiled by the COBOL compiler and the
generated object code directly calls the sort routines in the system
software.  The COBOL program does not call a sort *program*, it calls the
same sort *mechanism* that a sort *program* would call.  I would contend
that a sort *program* (one that does nothing but sort -- e.g., the
equivalent of SORT ... USING ... GIVING) written *in COBOL85*  is likely to
be more efficient than one written *in the SORT language* (or in ALGOL, for
that matter).

As part of the process of encouraging our B1000 users to migrate to the A
Series a number of years back, we made a bunch of changes to the system
software to assist in those migrations.  On the B1000, many shops had
sequences of batch RPG programs with interspersed utility SORTs.

At the time, the A Series did not even *have* a sort utility.  Most
commonly, anyone who wanted to write a utility sort would dash off the file
declarations, the compare procedure, and the SORT statement in ALGOL, and
I'd say that approach would be the standard "Burroughs Large System"
expectation of the time.  Many of the shops that we were encouraging to move
to A series  -- frequently diehard RPG shops -- were *most* reluctant to
force their programmers to use ALGOL.

The SORT *language* (the closest thing to a utility sort mechanism on A
Series), as I remember it, was implemented *primarily* to assist in
migrating those customers to A Series.   Anyone's welcome to use it.  But
not many do.  Over the past decade I see indications of no more than a
double handful of customer-submitted problems against the SORT language
compiler, and more than one of these were cosmetic.

> Of course.   But I do the same thing with CoBOL sorts to allow the user to
> order the report by ID or name (for instance).

I agree, but if the selection is something that's done frequently, it *may*
be more efficient to have the necessary keys set up as sets or subsets (or,
for that matter, as alternate record keys) and built automatically.    To
cite your example of copying a "flat file" as an ISAM file, and then copying
it back in key order, it is *possible* that in some circumstances it would
be better to go ahead and make the file indexed, with alternate record keys,
in the first place.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T13:26:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd6689d_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...

>  I think there's some
> sentiment in the A Series world that sorts, whether embedded or
standalone,
> are moving toward "quaint", evoking images of 80-80 list utilities and
> reel-to-reel tapes.   Still useful, but not used that much in the biggest,
> most complex and most current applications.
>
I have read this thread with much interest.

I consider the contributors all to be people worth listening to and it has
simply amazed me that there is such intensity being generated over a
technique which I personally haven't used for more than 15 years.

Your statement above, Chuck, gives me hope.

"SORT" became redundant as soon as efficient disk storage was invented, and
people learned how to use it.

I wrote somewhere else about how we all went wild with the advent of random
access storage and strove to completely eliminate SORT as quickly as
possible. (Admittedly, with varying degrees of success <G>)
("On a Clear Disk, you can Seek Forever..."
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/archives/v3/v30606.asp)

Batch purists will protest that sorting is required in order to do batch
updates.

My response would be: "If your on-line system is efficient you won't need to
do batch updates."

"What if I require reports in different sequences?"

 My Response: "That's why ORDER BY is in SQL..."

"We don't use Relational Database, we have Indexed and sequential files
because they're more efficient and that's what COBOL has ALWAYS used..."

My Response: "So don't complain about distributing your data around the
Network, your jobs going to the Java guys, COBOL getting a bad rep as being
associated with an archaic closed file system that nobody else can read, and
not being able to use standard tools to exchange information between
departments. You've only had 20 years to make the transition. Couldn't cope
with it? Serves you right..."

If intelligent and enlightened people can passionately argue the relative
merits of SORT technology in the year 2003, that says more about the state
of the installations they work on, than it does about programming
technology.

Chuck is right; SORTing SHOULD be regarded as "quaint"...

Pleased to see that, at least as far as Unisys installations are concerned,
people are starting to creep into the 21st Century.

Pete.
```

```
> Chuck is right; SORTing SHOULD be regarded as "quaint"...

First, I would like very much to have a table sort command.   Maybe we will get
a new CoBOL someday, but I doubt it.

And even with the ability to have my input sorted from database commands,
sometimes the hoops one has to jump through to order data the way the user wants
still make sorts much better.    When my query pulls out data that need to be
massaged before I know how to sort detail lines for a customer, that massaging
is often best done within the CoBOL and the sorting done after the massaging.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:br7eot$s0e$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  9-Dec-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> First, I would like very much to have a table sort command.   Maybe we
will get
> a new CoBOL someday, but I doubt it.

Table sort is in 2002 COBOL.   Talk to your compiler vendor.

Although writing a "table sort" in previous versions of COBOL isn't quite as
neat as using the "native syntax" in 2002 COBOL, it shouldn't be at all
difficult to do so using input and output procedures.  SORT and table
handling were both specified as modules in ANSI '68 COBOL (MERGE came in in
'74).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> First, I would like very much to have a table sort command.   Maybe we will get
> a new CoBOL someday, but I doubt it.

You can SORT a table.  Use an input procedure which RELEASEs each
array item and increments the index, and an OUTPUT PROCEDURE which
RETURNs then into the table and increments an index.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T12:12:36+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd7a8b1_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd6689d_2@news.athenanews.com> <br7eot$s0e$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:br7eot$s0e$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  9-Dec-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> First, I would like very much to have a table sort command.   Maybe we
will get
> a new CoBOL someday, but I doubt it.
>

I'm not sure on this but I have a "feeling" that some existing COBOLs allow
the SORT verb to be applied to "in memory" tables. (maybe it's in the
projected standard...)

It's academic for me anyway as I would never use it.

Load "in-memory" tables sequentially using one of the numerous techniques to
do so. (I posted my favourite one here some months ago.) Sorting of memory
tables is then never required.

> And even with the ability to have my input sorted from database commands,
> sometimes the hoops one has to jump through to order data the way the user
wants
> still make sorts much better.    When my query pulls out data that need to
be
> massaged before I know how to sort detail lines for a customer, that
massaging
> is often best done within the CoBOL and the sorting done after the
massaging.

With all due respect, Howard (and I have much of it for you), this indicates
a limitation in your SQL understanding rather than a shortcoming in
Relational Technology itself.  The youngsters today who have grown up with
tools like SQL Server can make it do anything required in terms of
reporting, provided the data is actually there. (I thought I had a pretty
good grasp of SQL, until I worked with people who use it as their primary
tool...).

I understand your point that if calculations need to be applied to data
extracted from a RDB, you might then write a file which can be sorted.

There is no need to. As the results are calculated they can be thrown
randomly into a temporary table and retrieved in the required sequence or
sequences as often as needed.
Temporary tables are often resident in memory (or efficiently paged by the
RDBMS) so doing this will often outperform extracting and sorting by a
factor of many times.
Alternatively, the calculations can be included in the original SQL for the
extract, or embedded in a Database Procedure. There are many options besides
extracting and sorting.

We tend to use the solutions we understand the best and we also look at
problems in terms of those solutions. While this is reasonable, it doesn't
encourage progress...<G>.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T15:23:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bra260$6p5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On 10-Dec-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> There is no need to. As the results are calculated they can be thrown
> randomly into a temporary table and retrieved in the required sequence or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> extracting and sorting.

Yep.   There are always alternative ways of doing stuff.


> We tend to use the solutions we understand the best and we also look at
> problems in terms of those solutions.

As you just illustrated.

> While this is reasonable, it doesn't encourage progress...<G>.

What is unclear is whether your description above is "better" or "worse" than an
internal sort to do the same thing.    I hesitate to associate "newer" with
"better" or even with "progress".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T00:01:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2tOBb.9397$rP6.770@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <r42dnW3uX7Mohk-iU-KYiw@giganews.com> <i8c4tvgkt61tcitep5u6t38bd9q6t5v5um@4ax.com> <br2ghu$251a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4opc$38b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4u7p$s0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd6689d_2@news.athenanews.com> <br7eot$s0e$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <vtfag65nv1uv38@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Yes, Howard's compiler supports Input/Output procedures.  Although (as I recall)
he is not on the "latest" compiler, it even has an example of how to do this.
See:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.12.10.2

HOWEVER,
  If you think that is as "clear" and easy to maintain as a program with the
2002 ISO COBOL feature of a "native" Table SORT, I have an APL compiler that I
could sell you <G>.

P.S.  Some compilers also do NOT "perform as well" with Input/Output procedures
as they do with "simple" SORTs (and Howard's is one of them).  I don't know how
comparisons will work in the future when/if they provide a Table SORT, but this
is a consideration.

P.P.S.  For Howard's environment, IBM was *just* sent a "requirement" to provide
the ISO 2002 Standard "Table SORT feature" (even if they don't provide a full
2002 Standard compiler) and this received a fairly high user priority.
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:

have implemented it in some of our programs (on the Unisys 2200 
platform) and seen significant performance improvements.  Besides, if 
it's well-commented, it should be clear -enough- to the next guy.

> the ISO 2002 Standard "Table SORT feature" (even if they don't provide a full
> 2002 Standard compiler) and this received a fairly high user priority.

Cool - I'm going through appendix F as a starting place to identify 
features I'm going to suggest to Unisys.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T01:30:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MovAb.2255$_r6.262@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com>`

```
"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 15:26:12 GMT "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
> :>1.   I am assuming you are using USING/GIVING to mean that it is using or
giving
> :>a file - otherwise why bother with CoBOL.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel

Only true (IMHO) if the SORT statement is the ONLY part of the procedure
division.  If there is "logic" performed before and after the SORT statement
(even if it does use ONLY USING/GIVING), then the program could be "replaced" by
  - Initial COBOL program
 - External SORT
 - Final COBOL program

and in that case, you are replacing "one" program load (of a COBOL program with
an internal SORT - with FASTSRT allowing SORT to do all I/O for the SORT
portion) with 3 programs.  In what way would that be an "improvement"?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T10:00:47+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l9n5tvktka8oqc9095ilq5tq7u7u38hb7j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <MovAb.2255$_r6.262@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
On Sun, 07 Dec 2003 01:30:20 GMT "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
:>news:7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com...
:>> On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 15:26:12 GMT "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

:>> :>1.   I am assuming you are using USING/GIVING to mean that it is using or
:>giving
:>> :>a file - otherwise why bother with CoBOL.

:>> USING and GIVING.

:>> And, yes, there is no reason to use COBOL in such a case.

:>> :>2.   Now you're comparing apples and oranges.   CoBOL's way of applying the
:>> :>business rules used in determining the sort may be more efficient.   It IS
:>> :>designed to handle business rules.

:>> Which is irrelevant when USING/GIVING - as no logic is done.

:>Only true (IMHO) if the SORT statement is the ONLY part of the procedure
:>division.  If there is "logic" performed before and after the SORT statement
:>(even if it does use ONLY USING/GIVING), then the program could be "replaced" by
:>  - Initial COBOL program
:> - External SORT
:> - Final COBOL program

:>and in that case, you are replacing "one" program load (of a COBOL program with
:>an internal SORT - with FASTSRT allowing SORT to do all I/O for the SORT
:>portion) with 3 programs.  In what way would that be an "improvement"?

In the MVS environment this SHOULD be split into three programs to allow
easier recovery.

If there is a failure in part #3 there should not be a need to rerun part #1 &
#2, especially with large amounts of data.
```

```
"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:l9n5tvktka8oqc9095ilq5tq7u7u38hb7j@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 07 Dec 2003 01:30:20 GMT "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> :>> :>1.   I am assuming you are using USING/GIVING to mean that it is using
or
> :>giving
> :>> :>a file - otherwise why bother with CoBOL.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> :>> :>2.   Now you're comparing apples and oranges.   CoBOL's way of applying
the
> :>> :>business rules used in determining the sort may be more efficient.   It
IS
> :>> :>designed to handle business rules.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> :>(even if it does use ONLY USING/GIVING), then the program could be
"replaced" by
> :>  - Initial COBOL program
> :> - External SORT
> :> - Final COBOL program
>
> :>and in that case, you are replacing "one" program load (of a COBOL program
with
> :>an internal SORT - with FASTSRT allowing SORT to do all I/O for the SORT
> :>portion) with 3 programs.  In what way would that be an "improvement"?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

Huh ???
  Why not use Checkpoint/Restart with the COBOL program to avoid such a problem.
This is possible both in the SORT section of the COBOL program - see:

   http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.12.16

and in the non-SORT sections, see:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/6.1

I certainly agree that you shouldn't have to "go back to the beginning" - in the
case of a problem, but that is the entire reason why COBOL fully supports
Checkpoint/restart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T10:46:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2gtl$25dr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <MovAb.2255$_r6.262@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <l9n5tvktka8oqc9095ilq5tq7u7u38hb7j@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:l9n5tvktka8oqc9095ilq5tq7u7u38hb7j@4ax.com...

> In the MVS environment this SHOULD be split into three programs to allow
> easier recovery.
>
> If there is a failure in part #3 there should not be a need to rerun part
#1 &
> #2, especially with large amounts of data.

"In the MVS environment" is not "always", nor does its validity *in that
environment* make it "obviously" true elsewhere.  What seems simple logic to
you, based on your exposure to a particular application development
environment, does not a Universal Truth make.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T15:14:51+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Riddle

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T08:36:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031211093721.658$XW@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <l9n5tvktka8oqc9095ilq5tq7u7u38hb7j@4ax.com> <br2gtl$25dr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4otb$3lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4qin$caf$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
>
> (Similar to: 'Never believe anything you read on the UseNet, including
> this statement.')

Riddle (no trick):

You are inside a room with no windows and only two doors. One door
leads to life, the other to death, and you don't know which is which.
There are two people in the room with you, a 'truth teller' and a 'liar',
and you do not know which is which. The 'truth teller' will always tell
the truth, the 'liar' will always tell a lie. They both know which door is
which and about each other. You can ask only one question, directed
to either one of the others. What do you do? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Riddle

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T18:18:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hx2Cb.435854$0v4.20609842@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <l9n5tvktka8oqc9095ilq5tq7u7u38hb7j@4ax.com> <br2gtl$25dr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4otb$3lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4qin$caf$1@panix1.panix.com> <20031211093721.658$XW@news.newsreader.com>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message news:20031211093721.658$XW@news.newsreader.com...
> <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> to either one of the others. What do you do? :-)

Ask #1 this question:

Will #2 tell me that Door #1 leads to life?

If #1 is the truthful one, and Door #1 leads to life,
he knows that #2 will lie and say No,
so #1 will tell the truth and answer No.
[In this case Door #1 leads to life.]

If #1 is the liar, and Door #1 leads to life,
he knows that #2 will tell the truth and say Yes,
so #1 will lie and answer No.
[In this case also, Door #1 leads to life.]

So a No answer to the life-door oriented question
in either case means that Door #1 leads to life.

[Checking, the logic]
Ask #1 this question:

Will #2 tell me that Door #1 leads to death?

If #1 is the truthful one, and Door #1 leads to death,
he knows that #2 will lie and say No,
so #1 will tell the truth and answer No.
[In this case Door #1 leads to death.]

If #1 is the liar, and Door #1 leads to death,
he knows that #2 will tell the truth and say Yes,
so #1 will lie and answer No.
[In this case also, Door #1 leads to death.]

So a No answer to the death-door oriented question
in either case means that Door #1 leads to death.
```

```
Bat Guano <bat.guano@talk21dotcom> wrote in message news:<vthiranjeeobf5@corp.supernews.com>...
> Hugh Candlin wrote:
> 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your theory has a MAJOR logic error


Really - share!  Let's look at the other possibility - that Door 2
leads to life.  In this case:

Asking #1 if #2 will say door 1 leads to life....

If #1 = Truth, #2 will say YES - so a YES answer means door 2 leads to
life.
If #2 = Lie, #2 will say NO, but #1 lies so will say YES - which means
door 2 leads to life.

I find no flaw in the logic.
```

```
I have to agree. There is no logic error in the solution - even though we
are missing the third case where life and death might be considered by some
equal as in the case of Terri Schiavo. The seriousness of the situation
makes this a question of life and death importance...and not life or death.

However, the inclusion of this state and questioning the nature of the life
and death would only serve to obscure the logic of the simple answer as
requested by the original poster.

Of course if someone chose wrong and "died" then obviously Peter Dashwood
would be in charge, and he could no longer make statements like "When your
mental age gets above the level of thinking bat shit is a cool handle, we
can possibly have a discussion."

JCE
```

```
<snipped impeccable exposition of the logic and ironic comment from Bat
Guano>

> I have to agree. There is no logic error in the solution - even though we
> are missing the third case where life and death might be considered by
some
> equal as in the case of Terri Schiavo. The seriousness of the situation
> makes this a question of life and death importance...and not life or
death.

Of course we could have a "Schrodinger's Cat" situation here, where the
reality does not occur until the question is actually asked and the wave
function collapses...

However, Boolean Logic and Quantum Mechanics don't really mix (at least,
they don't at this level...)

So we can reasonably assume there are only TWO possibilities: Life or Death,
and these are discrete and NOT different views on the same underlying state.

>
> However, the inclusion of this state and questioning the nature of the
life
> and death would only serve to obscure the logic of the simple answer as
> requested by the original poster.
>

No it wouldn't. It is simply irrelevant.

> Of course if someone chose wrong and "died" then obviously Peter Dashwood
> would be in charge, and he could no longer make statements like "When your
> mental age gets above the level of thinking bat shit is a cool handle, we
> can possibly have a discussion."

First off, I don't want to be "in charge"...
Second Off, there is no "obvious" reason why I should or would be...
Third off, I will make whatever statements I like, whether I am "in charge"
or not...
Fourth off, I claim that "Bat Guano" and JCE are one and the same person and
claim my �500...<G>
Fifth off, the above paragraph is a total non sequitur, an unwarranted and
unprovoked attack on me, and a feeble attempt to wind up Mr. Bat Guano. Stop
stirring, and confine yourself to things that ARE your business. If there
are any further such allegations, action will be taken against the
alligators <G>.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Riddle

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T09:09:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031212101104.535$5x@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <l9n5tvktka8oqc9095ilq5tq7u7u38hb7j@4ax.com> <br2gtl$25dr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <br4otb$3lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <br4qin$caf$1@panix1.panix.com> <20031211093721.658$XW@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Great answers. My (equivalent) solution was this "Ask either person
'Which door would the other person tell me was the door to death?'
then take that door." This situation is logically equivalent to the math
question "How do I get a positive (true) result from a positive (true)
number and a negative (false) number, without knowing which is
which?" The answer is of necessity the product of both values, so
you must ask the question in a way that involves the product of both
unknowns, then reverse the sign (truth). By including both unknowns,
you know there is an element of truth, and also that there is a lie too.
I.e. - the truth about a lie is equivalent to a lie about a truth, in a
binary solution set.
```

```
wouldn't trust the liar to follow these rules.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T09:42:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2d5j$22r0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com...

>
> USING and GIVING.
>
> And, yes, there is no reason to use COBOL in such a case.

Are you truly asserting that for an application that
    1)  does some processing that produces a file
    2)  sorts that file
    3)  reads the sorted file to accomplish some end
there is NO reason EVER to do step 2 as a COBOL SORT ... USING ... GIVING?
That the overhead of initiating three separate processes is ALWAYS
negligible compared to the overhead associated with an internal COBOL SORT?

All other things being equal, I would contend that the assertion is flat
FALSE on Unisys MCP systems using any flavor of COBOL on that system.

Why on earth would you want to do three BOT/EOT sequences, with the
associated stack-building and blockexit code, to say nothing of the need to
catalog the file between these steps to ensure its visibility to the other
processes, if you didn't absolutely have to?

    -Chuck Stevens

> Which is irrelevant when USING/GIVING - as no logic is done.

If the business rules call for a reordering of the data, the availability of
the SORT verb is indeed relevant to the business rules.  The act of
reordering the data is indeed logic done by the sort, whether *additional*
business logic is needed or not.

It *might* be arguable that the first and third steps above could be
incorporated as INPUT and OUTPUT procedures, but if the business rules call
for retention of the unsorted input and the sorted output for still other
purposes, it is rather more straightforward to use USING ... GIVING, and in
any case I would argue that in general the choice between INPUT ... OUTPUT
... and USING ... GIVING ..., in cases where either would work acceptably,
would be a style choice.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T19:56:10+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qge9tvo0ls00s21svehjp0kigdkqe7u4kp@4ax.com>`

```

wrote:

:>> And, yes, there is no reason to use COBOL in such a case.

:>Are you truly asserting that for an application that
:>    1)  does some processing that produces a file
:>    2)  sorts that file
:>    3)  reads the sorted file to accomplish some end
:>there is NO reason EVER to do step 2 as a COBOL SORT ... USING ... GIVING?
:>That the overhead of initiating three separate processes is ALWAYS
:>negligible compared to the overhead associated with an internal COBOL SORT?

Exactly.

The SORT should be with an INPUT and OUTPUT procedure.

:>All other things being equal, I would contend that the assertion is flat
:>FALSE on Unisys MCP systems using any flavor of COBOL on that system.

Could be.

Might be a weird architecture.

:>Why on earth would you want to do three BOT/EOT sequences, with the
:>associated stack-building and blockexit code, to say nothing of the need to
:>catalog the file between these steps to ensure its visibility to the other
:>processes, if you didn't absolutely have to?

WHy would you want to make two passes thru the data when it could be done in
one pass with INPUT and OUTPUT procedures?

:>> Which is irrelevant when USING/GIVING - as no logic is done.

:>If the business rules call for a reordering of the data, the availability of
:>the SORT verb is indeed relevant to the business rules.  The act of
:>reordering the data is indeed logic done by the sort, whether *additional*
:>business logic is needed or not.

Not via a COBOL program.

:>It *might* be arguable that the first and third steps above could be
:>incorporated as INPUT and OUTPUT procedures, but if the business rules call
:>for retention of the unsorted input and the sorted output for still other
:>purposes, it is rather more straightforward to use USING ... GIVING, and in
:>any case I would argue that in general the choice between INPUT ... OUTPUT
:>... and USING ... GIVING ..., in cases where either would work acceptably,
:>would be a style choice.

Then the sort should be done standalone.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T13:18:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2pr8$2bvj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <br2d5j$22r0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qge9tvo0ls00s21svehjp0kigdkqe7u4kp@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:qge9tvo0ls00s21svehjp0kigdkqe7u4kp@4ax.com...

> :>All other things being equal, I would contend that the assertion is flat
> :>FALSE on Unisys MCP systems using any flavor of COBOL on that system.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Might be a weird architecture.

The customer base for this mainframe architecture is loyal, and continues to
grow.  "Contempt prior to investigation", perhaps?

> :>Why on earth would you want to do three BOT/EOT sequences, with the
> :>associated stack-building and blockexit code, to say nothing of the need
to
> :>catalog the file between these steps to ensure its visibility to the
other
> :>processes, if you didn't absolutely have to?
>
> WHy would you want to make two passes thru the data when it could be done
in
> one pass with INPUT and OUTPUT procedures?

Because it's not clear that the data movements and control transfers
generated by the compiler for the INPUT and OUTPUT procedures are more
efficient than those performed within the operating system for the
USING/GIVING case.

> :>If the business rules call for a reordering of the data, the
availability of
> :>the SORT verb is indeed relevant to the business rules.  The act of
> :>reordering the data is indeed logic done by the sort, whether
*additional*
> :>business logic is needed or not.
>
> Not via a COBOL program.

 What?   Unless the compiler is able to treat the keys as a single string
and thereby avoid the generation of a comparison procedure that is executed
by the sort procedure in the operating system, each comparison is done in
the program itself.  That's true of the utility sort as well as for the SORT
verb in other languages.  One of the ways in which our COBOL85 compiler is
able to produce SORT ... USING ... GIVING verbs that outperform all the
other alternatives is by going to greater lengths to do a single comparison
and thereby avoid this "conversation" between the program and the operating
system.  It is precisely the key stuff that *is* part of the business logic,
and that's the heart of the SORT verb itself.

> Then the sort should be done standalone.

You've asserted that this is a universal truth.  I don't deny that it might
well be true on IBM platforms, but I contend that it is not true on systems
(like Unisys MCP-based systems) that have in-line COBOL sorts that produces
results at least as efficient as standalone utility sorts.

Please demonstrate this as everywhere and always true.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T18:34:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gu3Bb.4146$_r6.4124@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <br2d5j$22r0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,
  I think that "from context" (in the thread) that it was clear that Binyamin
was talking about IBM mainframe environments.  In that environment, I "sort-of"
agree with him.  There is rarely (bordering on never) a REQUIREMENT to code a
COBOL program with a SORT statement that uses both USING and GIVING.  For the
way that BATCH works on IBM mainframes, if your application logic really IS
divided into

  - everything before the sort
  - a SORT with no Input *or* Output procedure
  - everything after the SORT

then breaking the program into 2 COBOL programs and an external SORT is *almost
always* a reasonable option. As I stated in one of my earlier replies to this
thread, this is a (relatively) controversial topic within the IBM mainframe
shops that I have known.  I really do think that if your application logic comes
down to such a "structure" the determining factor is what is "your" shop
standard and what are your application programmers most familiar with (and would
have the greatest ease in maintaining).

Any (potential) overhead in having a "3 step job" would be balanced by the
benefit of "encapsulating" the various parts of application logic.  It would,
also, make "restart/recovery" EASIER in many cases (where you have HUGE -
multi-tape - input/output files).

Having said all of this, I hope it is clear that I am not saying that doing an
internal SORT with both USING/GIVING (and FASTSORT compiler option) is a BAD
idea in an IBM mainframe, just that there are few reasons (that I can think of)
to make this the "only" good design for an application.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T19:55:05+00:00

```

On  8-Dec-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> Having said all of this, I hope it is clear that I am not saying that doing an
> internal SORT with both USING/GIVING (and FASTSORT compiler option) is a BAD
> idea in an IBM mainframe, just that there are few reasons (that I can think
> of) to make this the "only" good design for an application.

The implication in your argument is that there are people who think otherwise.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T20:19:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D15Bb.5912$rP6.4871@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <br2d5j$22r0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Gu3Bb.4146$_r6.4124@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <br2kup$g73$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
It is my experience that there are definitely people (and shops) - in the IBM
mainframe world - who think:

A) Always use an external SORT rather than an internal SORT that only uses
USING/GIVING

B) Always use an internal SORT, never an external SORT (regardless of whether
Input/Output Procedures are used)

C) Always use an External SORT with "control cards" rather than internal SORTs
with Input/Output Procedures, unless you absolutely cannot (e.g. input or output
is via a non-supported access method)

   ***

This is why I was trying to say that I *personally* don't see compelling
*technical* reasons for any of these approaches - only "maintenance" and
"knowledge" reasons for making a decision (in an IBM mainframe environment -
others definitely have different considerations)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T13:06:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2p55$2bgc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq82j$3vs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7mv3tv824vl3vuthtiskbqfemvkrvdd3g7@4ax.com> <br2d5j$22r0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Gu3Bb.4146$_r6.4124@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:Gu3Bb.4146$_r6.4124@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net...
> Chuck,
>   I think that "from context" (in the thread) that it was clear that
Binyamin
> was talking about IBM mainframe environments.

I disagree.  He specifically indicated this as a platform-independent truth,
and has insisted that it has to be true for the Unisys MCP architecture as
well precisely because of its universality.  Thus far, the only concession
he has offered is "Could be.  Might be a weird architecture."

I don't deny the possibility that three task initiations are better than one
on MVS systems.  That was never my point.  It is the presumption that this
is the *only* reasonable way to accomplish that task on *any* platform in
*any* implementation, IBM or otherwise.

> ... breaking the program into 2 COBOL programs and an external SORT is
*almost
> always* a reasonable option.

I haven't ever stated it wasn't a *reasonable option*.  What I stated was
that it is *not always* the *best* option on *all* platforms.

> Having said all of this, I hope it is clear that I am not saying that
doing an
> internal SORT with both USING/GIVING (and FASTSORT compiler option) is a
BAD
> idea in an IBM mainframe, just that there are few reasons (that I can
think of)
> to make this the "only" good design for an application.

The assertion was that there is *never* a case in which USING/GIVING is a
reasonable design, that either INPUT/OUTPUT procedures or a separate
execution step is *always* better.  While I don't deny that there may be
some environments -- and some applications -- where this is *sometimes*
true, I stand by my assertion that the choice is not always obvious, and
that choosing USING/GIVING will unconditionally provide poorer performance.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T07:49:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqq9et$1cg2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 06:50:33 -0600 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> :>Not obvious at all. And doubtful whether true.

> Extra program code loading.

Can you demonstrate that on a Unisys MCP system?  I am not convinced there
is more program code associated with a SORT USING/GIVING than with a
separately-executed sort utility; in fact, I would contend very much the
reverse.

> Extra overhead.

On MCP systems, task initiation and termination is expensive, file
open/close is expensive, but object code loading is cheap and largely
automatic.  What extra overhead are you talking about?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T18:27:21+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eov3tv4gdtl0ofunepl1q42r7cb46dqbmh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq9et$1cg2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 07:49:47 -0800 "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
wrote:

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
:>news:pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com...
:>> On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 06:50:33 -0600 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

:>> :>Binyamin Dissen wrote:

:>> :>> Obviously a COBOL SORT USING/GIVING will be slower than an external
:>> :>> sort.

:>> :>Not obvious at all. And doubtful whether true.

:>> Extra program code loading.

:>Can you demonstrate that on a Unisys MCP system?  I am not convinced there
:>is more program code associated with a SORT USING/GIVING than with a
:>separately-executed sort utility; in fact, I would contend very much the
:>reverse.

Seems simple logic.

System loads program, program loads sort

vs

System loads sort

How would it be different in a Unisys MCP system?

:>> Extra overhead.

:>On MCP systems, task initiation and termination is expensive, file
:>open/close is expensive, but object code loading is cheap and largely
:>automatic.  What extra overhead are you talking about?

Not saying that it is a lot - just that it exists.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T10:27:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br2fr9$24il$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <bqq9et$1cg2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eov3tv4gdtl0ofunepl1q42r7cb46dqbmh@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:eov3tv4gdtl0ofunepl1q42r7cb46dqbmh@4ax.com...
> Seems simple logic.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> System loads sort

Wrong.   The sort PROCESS is handled by a module of already-loaded system
software,  not a separate task separately loaded, or a utility *task* that a
COBOL program calls.  The mechanism, and thus the overhead, of the SORT verb
differs only insignificantly from that associated with file OPEN, CLOSE, and
even the very declaration of a data memory space as a part of program
initiation.

The system loads program, the program *calls* the SORT process, which
*returns* control to the program (and interacts with it, in the case of
input and output procedures).  It does not "load" a separate task or utility
to perform the sort; the presence or absence of the code is handled by the
basic virtual memory schemes of the system.  The "owning tasks" are already
there.  There's nothing to "initiate".  It's a procedure call.

> How would it be different in a Unisys MCP system?

The system loads program, the program *calls* the SORT module in the MCP,
which *returns* control to the program (and interacts with it, in the case
of input and output procedures).

> :>On MCP systems, task initiation and termination is expensive, file
> :>open/close is expensive, but object code loading is cheap and largely
> :>automatic.  What extra overhead are you talking about?
>
> Not saying that it is a lot - just that it exists.

No, I contend that it does not.  On MCP systems, the same sort routine is
called whether you are using a standalone batch sort utility, a COBOL sort
verb, the interactive sort utility, or the ALGOL sort statement.  The
overhead of initiating the sort *itself* is the same in all cases; it's a
procedure call to an operating system routine, not the initiation of a
separate job step.

Stated a different way, you claim it is always cheaper to:
    Run Job 1, a COBOL program to create a file and catalog it on disk.
    Run a utility program that calls MCP.SORT to sort the file.
    Run Job 3, a COBOL program that processes the sorted file.
than it is to:
    Run a COBOL program that:
         creates a file (it need not be permanent)
         calls MCP.SORT to sort the file
         processes the sorted file.

Nope.  The assertion is simply false in the general case.

Moreover, let's examine that utility program.  Such a program could be
written in the SORT language; it could be an ALGOL program that executes a
SORT statement; it could even be a COBOL program that does nothing but a
SORT ... USING ... GIVING.  I would expect NO  measurable penalty entailed
with writing this utility in COBOL than for any of the other alternatives.

In fact, for our COBOL85 compiler, for obscure internal reasons, it's very
likely that the COBOL SORT verb would outperform all of the other
alternatives, *as a standalone sort*, particularly if the key structure is
complicated.  Thus, even if the application designer wanted separate
execution steps for these, it is highly likely that the most efficient
approach on MCP systems would be to write the "utility sort" as a standalone
COBOL program whose procedure division essentially consisted of a SORT ...
USING ... GIVING statement!!!

So, tell me again why an application designed as:
    COBOL85 program to preprocess the file
    (COBOL85?) program to sort the file
    COBOL85 program to postprocess the file
ALWAYS performs better and entails LESS overhead than an application
designed as:
    COBOL program to process the file

I don't see it, and my experience leads me to believe it doesn't.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T17:12:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IfCdncRfsdhujUyiRVn-tA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 06:50:33 -0600 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Extra program code loading.

Uh, the sort routine may load at execution time and may be invoked many
times. External sorts have to be loaded for every instance. But even if a
COBOL program had to load the sort routine, an external sort has to load the
sort routine - seems like that's a push.

Even if there is overhead, the time required to load an executable is
miniscule when compared to the time to invoke any kind of sort.

I was thinking more about a logic flow. Suppose I want to list all my
customers in a specific locality in alphabetic order by name (they exist on
the master ordered by customer number).

With an internal sort:
Loop 1
  Read record.
  If in area of interest, release sort
  go to Loop 1
(Sort)
Loop 2
  Return from sort
  Print record
  Go to Loop 2

In this case, the master file is read once.

With an external sort:
(note to students - skip this example, it's really BAD)
Procedure A.
[External Sort by location/name]
Procedure B
Loop 1
   Read sorted record
   If in area of interest, print
   go to loop 1

Obviously, I have to read the entire database an average of 1.5 times as
well as sorting the entire master file (which may not be physically
possible).

Procedure 2
Read entire database, extracting records of interest to scratch file
[Invoke External Sort]
Read scratch file and print

In procedure 2 we have the complication of a three step job stream.


>
> Extra overhead.
>
> How could it not be true?

There is no extra overhead. No matter who does it, sorting code has to be
loaded sometime.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T18:27:22+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asv3tvoutn2pmgem3g1rho36ji4oqkk6ul@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net> <opdvsvgkce8ldn6cp72vheae1ptrcs60dk@4ax.com> <7sadnffnweGF4k2iRVn-jA@giganews.com> <pl11tvk76f7fbvuu4v56ui0kp51qegf91v@4ax.com> <IfCdncRfsdhujUyiRVn-tA@giganews.com>`

```
On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 17:12:52 -0600 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen wrote:
:>> On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 06:50:33 -0600 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
:>> wrote:

:>>>> Binyamin Dissen wrote:

:>>>>> Obviously a COBOL SORT USING/GIVING will be slower than an external
:>>>>> sort.

:>>>> Not obvious at all. And doubtful whether true.

:>> Extra program code loading.

:>Uh, the sort routine may load at execution time and may be invoked many
:>times. External sorts have to be loaded for every instance. But even if a
:>COBOL program had to load the sort routine, an external sort has to load the
:>sort routine - seems like that's a push.

:>Even if there is overhead, the time required to load an executable is
:>miniscule when compared to the time to invoke any kind of sort.

:>I was thinking more about a logic flow. Suppose I want to list all my
:>customers in a specific locality in alphabetic order by name (they exist on
:>the master ordered by customer number).

:>With an internal sort:
:>Loop 1
:>  Read record.
:>  If in area of interest, release sort
:>  go to Loop 1
:>(Sort)
:>Loop 2
:>  Return from sort
:>  Print record
:>  Go to Loop 2

:>In this case, the master file is read once.

And it isn't a USING/GIVING sort.

It has an INPUT PROCEDURE.

:>With an external sort:
:>(note to students - skip this example, it's really BAD)
:>Procedure A.
:>[External Sort by location/name]
:>Procedure B
:>Loop 1
:>   Read sorted record
:>   If in area of interest, print
:>   go to loop 1

:>Obviously, I have to read the entire database an average of 1.5 times as
:>well as sorting the entire master file (which may not be physically
:>possible).

:>Procedure 2
:>Read entire database, extracting records of interest to scratch file
:>[Invoke External Sort]
:>Read scratch file and print

:>In procedure 2 we have the complication of a three step job stream.

:>> Extra overhead.

:>> How could it not be true?

:>There is no extra overhead. No matter who does it, sorting code has to be
:>loaded sometime.

But the COBOL program does not.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T16:21:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqoj23$4do$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net>`

```

"Jim Reinhart" <jreinhart1@cox.net> wrote in message
news:BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net...
> On 12/3/03 11:47 PM, in article vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com, "Dan"
> <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote:
>
> If possible, use an external sort from Syncsort.  It runs much faster and
> would probably make the program much easier to understand.  Generally,
good
> shops do not use internal sorts.  Syncsort makes their product for just
> about every platform.  There is a lot less hassle with file IO when
changing
> products as each has their own quirks.

While I have seen Unisys MCP systems that use utility sorts interspersed
among processing programs, I have never seen such a system that used any
sort mechanism -- for embedded sorts or standalone ones -- but the one
provided by the operating system as invoked by the compilers and the various
utilities.

How much faster is Syncsort's offering for Unisys MCP systems  than the sort
mechanism provided by the operating system?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T01:16:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0Rzb.28975$n56.19085@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net>`

```

"Jim Reinhart" <jreinhart1@cox.net> wrote in message
news:BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net...
> On 12/3/03 11:47 PM, in article vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com, "Dan"
> <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote:
>
<snip> >
> If possible, use an external sort from Syncsort.  It runs much faster and
> would probably make the program much easier to understand.  Generally, good
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jim,
  Am I correct that you come from an IBM mainframe (probably MVS, OS/390, z/OS)
environment?  Remember that the original question gave a Micro Focus run-time
error.  Although I know that SyncSort has ported to SOME non-IBM environments,
it is USUALLY a person from an IBM mainframe who "assumes" that SyncSort (or
even an external DFSort) SORT is a "better" solution than an internal SORT
solution.

On IBM mainframes, it is actually quite CONTROVERSIAL as to when to use:
  - External Sort
  - Internal Sort
  - External Sort with E15/E35 (COBOL or Assembler) exits

My experience is that most IBM mainframe shops have a "standard" based on
research (usually done about 25 years ago) as to which performs best and which
is easiest to "maintain".  In general (with lots of possible reasons for
exceptions), I agree that at a LARGE shop, it usually best to stick to "one" (no
matter which) solution - so that any/most of YOUR shop's programmers can
maintain/enhances such applications.

NOTE:
  For IBM mainframe shops, I do strongly suggest that you ALWAYS use the FASTSRT
compiler option (available with VS COBOL II and later - z/OS, VSE, or any
mainframe environment that I know of).  This does cause "non-ANSI" behavior in
the VERY rare case that you have an I-O declarative coded for one of your SORT
(or MERGE) input/output files - but otherwise, is almost always a better
default - even when a single Input/Output procedure prevents it from being in
effect for PART of the SORT processing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T14:58:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqq6eo$p5m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <BBF50149.222A%jreinhart1@cox.net>`

```

On  4-Dec-2003, Jim Reinhart <jreinhart1@cox.net> wrote:

> If possible, use an external sort from Syncsort.  It runs much faster and
> would probably make the program much easier to understand.  Generally, good
> shops do not use internal sorts.  Syncsort makes their product for just
> about every platform.  There is a lot less hassle with file IO when changing
> products as each has their own quirks.

Generally, good shops use whichever sort works best for an application.  
Proscribing internal sorts doesn't allow this.

Sometimes internal sorts are the best solution.   IMHO, the default action
should be to design with external sorts - only using internal sorts with reason.
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T01:19:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X2Rzb.28985$n56.28583@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I notice that you have selected ANS74 file status conditions.  Is there a reason
for this?  (You can control this via compiler directives).

As far as the actual error message, what are your explicit AND implicit
definitions of the files?  I have seen that particular message when there was a
"wrong" specification for Line versus Record Sequential files.

You might want to try simply READING thru the entire input file(s) to see if you
can reach "end-of-file" without any error - with the Select/Assign, FD, and
compiler options that are in effect for your SORT program.  If not, try and find
the solution to THAT problem and it will almost certainly solve your SORT
problem.
```

#### ↳ TIMING TEST Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T15:07:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lcGdne-YxKM8pEui4p2dnA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Tiring of subjective "purple is better than seven" vs. "no it's not," I
decided to settle this has empirically.

On a pretty peppy PC,

Scenario 1 - Internal Sort
Release each of 100k, 80 byte records, sort, return and write to new file.
Total time: 2 minutes, 16 seconds.

Scenario 2 - External Sort
Read same input file and write scratch file. Exit program.
Figure out how to run FJ's stand-alone super sort and, after much
trial-and-error, do it successfully.
Read resulting sorted file and copy to new file.

Total time: 1 hr, 14 minutes, 11 seconds.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: TIMING TEST Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T00:55:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2auBb.5800$_r6.1078@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <lcGdne-YxKM8pEui4p2dnA@giganews.com>`

```
I love it !!!

Just think what that means for creating one's *first* SQL program to replace an
ISAM file.  Add about 5 days to the resulting time to account for "learning
curve".

And they said that using multiple alternate record keys might not perform as
well as using a SQL database!!!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: TIMING TEST Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T21:04:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0312092104.436cf99d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <lcGdne-YxKM8pEui4p2dnA@giganews.com>`

```
Properly installed PowerBSort is supposed to be used for any internal
COBOL sorts as well.  Dunno why it wasn't in your case.

I fould PowerBSORT to be the absolute fastest sort I could find -
tried a LOT OF them.  Second place - by only fractions of a second -
and much less expensive:  Sort Solution.

http://www.mwlabs.de/psortsol.htm

Jerry - give it a try now (it's much updated since I last compared
speed) on your peppy PC and see what you think.


"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<lcGdne-YxKM8pEui4p2dnA@giganews.com>...
> Tiring of subjective "purple is better than seven" vs. "no it's not," I

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: TIMING TEST Re: Internal Sorts

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T07:32:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_OmdnWQl5aTuvUqiRVn-vA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<vstm48i1f12b5a@corp.supernews.com> <lcGdne-YxKM8pEui4p2dnA@giganews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0312092104.436cf99d@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> Properly installed PowerBSort is supposed to be used for any internal
> COBOL sorts as well.  Dunno why it wasn't in your case.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yeah, we use Sort Solution too. I believe it was you who told me that FJ's
lickety-split sort was invoked on a development machine but when one created
a distribution for deployment, FJ substituted their slower rickshaw-speed
sort.

So, what we do, is use internal sorts (yielding the slower method) for
piddly things - like 500 records - and invoke SortSolution Active-X control
when we know the sorting may be of the large denomination.

Our users seem to be happy with the results, and the $69 one-time price sure
beats the per-distribution fee of FJ.
```
