```
An excellent example of component technology at work...<G>.

It amazes me that some people resist using it, suspicious of anything that
is "not invented here".

In one lifetime, I neither want nor expect to write EVERY piece of
application software that will ever be required by me.

Besides, others have expertise I could never hope to attain without spending
years in that particular field.

ActiveX and COM (not to mention Java Beans and CORBA on non-MS platforms)
are an excellent solution to the problem of acquiring reliable, encapsulated
functionality.

Pete.
>
```

```
In article <lcGdne-YxKM8pEui4p2dnA@giganews.com>,
 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> Tiring of subjective "purple is better than seven" vs. "no it's not," I
> decided to settle this has empirically.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

1.25 hours to sort 100,000 80-byte records?

Was that peppy PC the 4.77mhz or the 'turbo' 8mhz type?

I think even the plain vanila DOS command line sort would make very 
short work of such a small file...


BTW -- you have several errors here -- both

   "read same input file and write scratch file"

   and

   "read resulting sorted file and copy to new file"

Are logically pointless.  Why not use the original file as read-only and 
accept the resulting sorted file as your result -- as your internal sort 
scenario does.

Unless the FJ super-sort utility is very poorly built, it should be able 
to do exactly that.
```

## Thread: can cobol scale the corporate ladder? — 38 msgs

### can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T10:56:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

when designing a Cobol application to function as an information system --
mimics the behaviour and functionality of RDBMS (oracle & sql server), besides
using ISAM files layout, sequential files, detail report printing layouts and
screen section, what is the design and planning methods to accommodate for
future Scalability requirements?

thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

```
Hello Sunshine - you're at it again. <G>. As our buddy Warren would say, "Kellie
is into using $10 words - Scalability !".

Lots of stuff on the Web about R/DBMS. Go check the on-line manual covering
DataBase - it's pretty good. If you want to dabble and have Microsoft Access -
play with the ESQL Assistant which is a drop-down from the IDE Master Menu - Tools
----> OpenESQL Assistant

Here's an interesting link - the author is a movie freak :-

http://www.fluffycat.com/sql/

and you could store his data in M/S Access and use ESQL Assistant to generate the
copyfiles and your queries.

Jimmy (je ne suis pas un Quebecois <G>)
```

```
no@spam.com wrote:

> > PS: So the rest know what's going on. The young lady wrote to me suggesting I had to be
> > French or a Quebecois, because I had written, "I write cheques...."; she of course "writes
> > checks...."..
>
> Cheques is the usual UK usage, while checks is the normal US usage.

Isn't it more specific than that, i.e. :-

"Cheques" in UK, ( also in Ireland, Australia, NZ, S. Africa, Malaysia,  India, Hong Kong  etc.
?),  while it is "Checks" in US - no 'usual' or 'normal' involved.

I'm not gong to commit myself regarding Canada but I'd guess officially it is 'cheques' - but
with US influence via textbooks, 'checks' is also probably acceptable. - I can answer my own
query- just remembered I had Winston Canadian Dictionary, "cheque" official, optional "check"
(Same logic used in Canada for 'plough' and 'plow' the soil).

Quiz time Kellie - do you know what an Imperial gallon is. How many fluid ounces in a US pint
and an Imperial pint. ???

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Now the 'pints' bit rings a bell. Back when an OO chat group was active, and observing some of the
crazy EU regulations, (like the very recent nonsense a kilt was classified as a skirt), I asked an
Irish participant if he could still order a pint or did he have to ask for a litre ? (What sparked my
query was reading an English greengrocer was being fined for displaying his pricing as per Lb,
instead of per Kg. In Canuckia we compromise - showing both).

"Not to worry", he said. "I just go into the pub, nod at the landlord and say 'Give me a Guinness'".
He always got the desired nectar. BTW - the 'kilt' bit - the Scots told the EU to 'Go take a hike !'
See the Celts truly are adaptive.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T23:56:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD26C55.33598CB9@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <3FCF8948.D7942267@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312050804.2baa9d15@posting.google.com> <3FD0B544.7B38DDF9@shaw.ca> <HF3Ab.154593$Ec1.6075411@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FD0DBF4.AB5351B9@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312060303.72952ba8@posting.google.com>`

```


KELLIE FITTON wrote:

> Quiz time Kellie - do you know what an Imperial gallon is. How many
> fluid ounces in a US pint and an Imperial pint. ???
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is equals to 20 fluid ounces.

Well done lassie. They can't call you McDummy <G>. You've found a good use for searching
the Web. I hope others in your age group are inquisitive enough to latch on.

BTW - Tupperware - caused no end of confusion up here in Canada because the cup sizes are
US fluid ounces. Sometimes people refer to an old English recipe in Lbs and parts of pints
- using the US cup doesn't cut it.

Back to searching the Web and knowing young people are impressionable, I was in my
generation, so are you any different. You have to differentiate between the 'factual' as
per measurements above and 'opinion'.

Factual :

This is an interesting one - HMS Victory - Horatio Nelson's flagship at the Battle of
Trafalgar, 1805 Oct 21 - defeated combined French and Spanish fleets. Nelson was mortally
wounded by a French sniper.

Back when my son was about five, I was assembling one of those plastic model kits; I had
the hull built. "Mr. Curious" reached up, dropped it on the floor and broke it.
Over here he had a pang of conscience about the age of 12-15 and bought me a new Airfix kit
- haven't built it yet. Perhaps one day......

Go googling and come up with about a dozen sites dedicated just to HMS Victory. You'll
appreciate the model kits are a bit limited on describing colour schemes. Well I find a
site which gives me the whole shebang on the colour scheme. Primarily black, and what I
knew to be Yellow or Gold is referred to as Yellow Ochre. But get this - it then lists
parts of the model which weren't kosher in 1805. (Don't ask me what the terminology is) :-

Mizzen Royal Stay and Mizzen Royal Backstay - to be omitted: not introduced until 1810.
Mizzen Preventer Stay - To be omitted : not introduced until circa 1815.

My home town was Portsmouth so I've been over the Victory some 3 or 4 times. Magnificent.
Without checking, built about 1791 and was still floating until 1922.
Now dry docked because the massive oak timbers were being gnawed to death by the Death
Watch Beetle.  They still commemorate Nelson's  death on October 21 each year on board the
Victory. It also serves as  the flagship of the C-in-C Portsmouth.

Opinion :

Really got to watch for this one. After all, anybody can post an 'essay' on a Website - so
you have to use your judgement. Example, a while back I mentioned Operation Sea Lion which
was a German plan during WW2 to invade UK about 1940. Richard (in NZ) refuted that it could
happen. His comment was backed up by an 'essay' on the Web.

The Web authors main contention was that the Royal Navy would not have let it happen - i.e.
the Germans making it the 21 miles from Calais to Dover. The RN would have sent in
battleships, and God knows what else from Plymouth and Portsmouth. Plausible. But prior to
this time the RN had already lost some capital ships to bombing or U-boats.

Now let's move on some two years later to Dieppe - Wednesday 19 August 1942. A one day raid
by Canadians, supported by two British commando units on the flanks and a handful of US
Rangers. (Plus the air battle overhead was the biggest since the Battle of Britain).

The Canadians had more casualties, (dead, wounded and POWs) during the period 06:00 to
18:00 that one day than they had in  their slog up through Italy along with British, Poles,
Indians and Mark Clark's US divisions.

Dieppe is a sleepy little seaside town - big pebbles on the beach, (which proved disastrous
for the Calgary Tank Regiment), plus either side, headlands which had artillery
emplacements.

Now back to the RN using battleships in 1940 - Lord Louis Mountbatten (cousin to George
VI), and head of Combined Operations, approached the First Sea Lord asking for battleship
support. The reply he got, "You must be mad Dicky !". I don't need to spell it out -
exactly the same reason that they wouldn't have used them in 1940.

All sorts of things went wrong, but without doubt, had a battleship's guns crushed those
artillery pieces on the headlands, it would have been a slightly different outcome. As it
was they had to rely on RN support with 4-inch guns from destroyers - just like using pea
shooters.

OK you can debate the issue with "what if....". The whole venture didn't happen because the
RAF won the Battle of Britain. There are certainly photographs of the Wermacht training on
French beaches, plus with Germanic thoroughness there was a meticulous plan.  And part of
that plan, not available until documentation was captured at the end of WW2 - was a plan to
take yours truly and other kids between about the ages of 5-12, (providing we looked
'Aryan'), back to Germany to be inculcated into Nazism.

You wouldn't now being asking if I was from Quebec. In JFK style I could reply "Ich bin ein
Berliner".

Caveat emptor. Let the buyer beware.

Websites - Let the reader beware ! Make your own judgements.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T05:13:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD7FB53.EA449F7D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <3FCF8948.D7942267@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312050804.2baa9d15@posting.google.com> <3FD0B544.7B38DDF9@shaw.ca> <HF3Ab.154593$Ec1.6075411@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FD0DBF4.AB5351B9@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312060303.72952ba8@posting.google.com> <3FD26C55.33598CB9@shaw.ca> <5Vd7MKCmj40$EwCg@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```


Alistair Maclean wrote:

> >My home town was Portsmouth so I've been over the Victory some 3 or 4
> >times. Magnificent.
> >Without checking, built about 1791 and was still floating until 1922.

Got that wrong. Keel laid in 1759. She was launched in 1765, but commissioned
about 1771. Floating until 1921.

>
> >Now dry docked because the massive oak timbers were being gnawed to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Cough ! Cough ! Death Watch Beetle (and that was from memory only).

Official History  ( www.hms-victory.com ) :-

"During the Second World War the ship was closed to visitors and she was
stripped of her upper masts and rigging. She narrowly escaped destruction on the
night of 10/11 March 1941 when a 500lb high  explosive bomb fell into the dry
dock and burst just under her port bow, blowing a hole 8ft x 15ft in the  ships
side. After the war her restoration continued and she was reopened to the
public.

After fighting off many enemies, her most formidable foe now was the Death Watch

beetle, but between the years 1954 and 1956 she was fumigated with such
excellent
results that the beetles were brought under control."

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T20:45:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312072045.68542b5d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <3FCF8948.D7942267@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312050804.2baa9d15@posting.google.com> <3FD0B544.7B38DDF9@shaw.ca> <HF3Ab.154593$Ec1.6075411@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FD0DBF4.AB5351B9@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312060303.72952ba8@posting.google.com> <3FD26C55.33598CB9@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> Really got to watch for this one. After all, anybody can post an 'essay' on a Website - so
> you have to use your judgement. Example, a while back I mentioned Operation Sea Lion which
> was a German plan during WW2 to invade UK about 1940. Richard (in NZ) refuted that it could
> happen. His comment was backed up by an 'essay' on the Web.

That is rather a simplification.  My argument was based on reading
many books on the subject such as 'Invasion' by Peter Flemming (Ian's
brother) who was the commander of the Auxilliary Units in Kent at the
time and possibly still the best commentary on whether it could have
happened.

Your suggestion was that Germany should have invaded just after
Dunkirk, when they had no mechanism at all to do so.  There were no
seaworthy barges (river barges would be no use), no landing craft, the
Army had never done more than a few river crossings which were based
on establishing pontoon bridges.  What were you suggesting ?  That the
tanks should just do a right turn and _drive_ to Dover ?

As for "backed up by an 'essay' on the web", you may like to indicate
where this idea of yours came from, there certainly isn't any such
thing in my response which is still on Google and I just checked. 
There may well be dubious and fact-free essays, but no more so than
your speculations that the invasion should have happened and
could/would have succeeded.

What is more nothing in my response required that the RN get involved
with any more than their existing coastal defences, your associating
the main battle fleet with repelling the invasion was not my making.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T20:52:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312062052.74b98959@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <3FCF8948.D7942267@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312050804.2baa9d15@posting.google.com> <3FD0B544.7B38DDF9@shaw.ca> <HF3Ab.154593$Ec1.6075411@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FD0DBF4.AB5351B9@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312060303.72952ba8@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote

> according to google web search:
> a British imperial equals to 4 quarts or 4.545 liters.

The weight of an Imperial Gallon of water (at some set of conditions)
is 10 pounds, that of a US gallon is 8 pounds.

> An Imperial Pint is used to measure beer throughout the old British
> Empire.
> It is equals to 20 fluid ounces.

While a pint of milk is 20 fluid ounces, when measuring beer a
'reputed' quart is 26 oz making a 'reputed' pint 13 oz.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T11:53:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312081153.413f1408@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <3FCF8948.D7942267@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312050804.2baa9d15@posting.google.com> <3FD0B544.7B38DDF9@shaw.ca> <HF3Ab.154593$Ec1.6075411@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FD0DBF4.AB5351B9@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312060303.72952ba8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0312062052.74b98959@posting.google.com> <0VA4chCSk40$Ewjr@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Tk%Ab.7941$3y1.657008@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca> wrote 

> Funny, "quart" comes from "quarter".  But in Texas, it is called a 
> fifth.  The texans are apparently more rigorous when it comes to liquor.

A 'fifth' (which is primarily a US term) is actually 1/5 of a US
Gallon.  The US Gallon is 128 fl.oz so diviving by 5 gives the
(approx) 26 fl.oz (roughly 750ml) of a 'standard' bottle.

This 26 fl.oz is often called a (reputed) quart.  Check out what you
call a quart of beer, wine or liquor.  It should be 32 fl.oz for an
actual US Quart or 40oz for an imperial Quart.
```

- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <3FCF8948.D7942267@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312050804.2baa9d15@posting.google.com> <3FD0B544.7B38DDF9@shaw.ca> <HF3Ab.154593$Ec1.6075411@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3FD0DBF4.AB5351B9@shaw.ca> <41758a6b.0312060303.72952ba8@posting.google.com>`

```

On  6-Dec-2003, KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote:

> Quiz time Kellie - do you know what an Imperial gallon is. How many
> fluid ounces in a US pint and an Imperial pint. ???

In the U.S. we can buy liter, quart, or fifth.    Or any size fluid container
the vendor wishes to supply.    In fact there are products (tuna cans), that
regularly go to smaller sizes instead of raising prices - figuring we would
rather have broken recipes than pay an extra 2 cents per can.

I don't understand why people fight using other systems though.   Keeping track
of mileage is a pain when I buy gas in Canada or Mexico, but not many people
track their mileage.    If I see a speed limit sign that says 80Kph, I look at
my speedometer and go 80Kph.   I don't do any conversions.    I never need to
convert fifths and liters.

It was confusing when France went metric and people forgot how tall Napoleon's
5'2" (French) was in English (about 5'6" - which was about average for his
time).
```

#### ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T15:16:00-06:00

```
KELLIE FITTON wrote:
> Hello everyone,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.

Basic rules:
1. A large system that works is invariably found to have evolved from a
small system that worked.
2. A large system designed from scratch will never work and cannot be made
to work, although malfunction may not be discovered for a long time, if
ever.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T08:49:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UomdnaVaM6D_cUyiRVn-hQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <oLidnXCCJ9aSOVKiRVn-gw@giganews.com> <bqr4tb$25moiq$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>>>>> JerryMouse<nospam@bisusa.com> 12/4/2003 2:16:00 PM >>>
>> KELLIE FITTON wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> think I'll print it out and put it on my wall!

Actually #2 above was from memory. The actual rule is:
"A complex system designed from scratch never works and cannot patched up to
make it work. You have to start over, beginning with a working simple
system." And the other half: "In complex systems, malfunction and even total
nonfunction may not be detectable for long periods, if ever."

But there's a competing rule: "A large system produced by expanding the
dimensions of a smaller system does not behave like the smaller system."

There's more: Get a copy of "SystemAntics" by John Gall*. To whet your
appetite:

Systems tend to expand to fill the known universe.
Positive feedback is dangerous.
The system itself does not do what it says it is doing.
Programs never run the first time. Complex programs never run.
Systems develop goals of their own the instant they come into being.
Intrasystem goals come first.
Crucial variables are discovered by accident.
When a fail-safe system fails, it fails by failing to fail safe.
Efficient systems are dangerous to themselves and others.

----------
*Out of print. You'll have to scrounge the used-book online community.
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

```
keep referring to - Edmund Arranga and Frank Coyle "Object-Oriented COBOL"
published '96. Again out of print. (Ed is editor of cobolreport.com).

But for those curious about OO COBOL, both amazon.com and abebooks.com have
roughly 20 used copies for re-sale (if the Net Express people haven't already
bought them).

name and the book title.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T01:42:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD284B3.75617053@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com> <oLidnXCCJ9aSOVKiRVn-gw@giganews.com> <bqr4tb$25moiq$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <UomdnaVaM6D_cUyiRVn-hQ@giganews.com> <3FD23F2D.F1A7CF07@shaw.ca> <KoSdnQENDupN50-iRVn-gg@giganews.com>`

```


JerryMouse wrote:

> James J. Gavan wrote:
> > JerryMouse wrote:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> If so, there is much truth to your observation.

Actually No, Jerry <G> I was picking up on the 'out of print' bit. But like
Frank (I think), I agree with you 100 percent. And in the same vein as your
observation two examples.

Unigate - dairy company. They bring in consultants who look at the WHOLE
organization. They present us early systems people with a road map. Looks just
like a drawing of the NYC Subway or London Underground systems.

They focused on the West and East of the diagram, milk intake and products sold,
respectively. That's exactly what was first computerized. The latter bit, as
I've said before, we did ONLY invoicing; it didn't incorporate updating the
Sales(A/R) Ledgers. I left shortly after invoicing went 'live' - so I don't know
what occurred later. But it was a successful start.

Compare that to following - roughly about 10 years back, can't be sure, but I
think it was the London Stock Exchange. I believe they already had some computer
application but were going through a revamp.

In comes their new manager, probably several university designations after his
name, smooth and a marketing motor mouth. (What's the betting he had also been
to the 'right school' ?). Impresses the Board greatly.

Languages, hardware configuration unknown, but with this guy they go for the big
bang approach. He recruits something like 300 people at various levels. Forget
time span, but time, (years) drift by, "Well, we are almost there.......".

Chairman or CEO gets frustrated and calls in consultants. They report - "Good
news is it's cost you 5 million and it's coming along. Bad news is it will cost
you another 5 million and can happen in 18 to 24 months".

The CEO fires everybody, but EVERYBODY !

What you implied from my first message does also apply to OO - perhaps even more
so than procedural. You have got to plan the grand design - albeit in sketch
form - then concentrate on a module, say A/R or A/P. If you are going to get the
best bang for your buck from those modules then you must PRE-PLAN - what
hierachial structure do you need to achieve reuse of code. It's worth bearing in
mind that the hierachial/reuse aspect thought out in say A/P can be equally
applied to A/R - particularly as they have common features like GUI-ing and
lists/collections.

I think people get fazed by 'Object' or 'Object Reference' - it's another way of
saying procedure pointer. There's no point in switching to OO for the hell of
it. Used properly it is intended to help the development cycle.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T21:48:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FYNzb.147474$Fv8.95333@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com>`

```
It is impractical for long time investment.

This is why database replaces file system. Compete with Oracle?
It is also why legacy Cobol is difficult to maintain. Why OO is used?
The success of RDBMS is its standard SQL. Why Microsoft SQL server and
Oracle coexist?
The same is Java/C# for web application with standard TCP/IP. Why Microsoft
design C#?

But for a special situation, a complex file maybe designed for internal use
for best speed at the expense of cost of maintenance.

Thomas Li

"KELLIE FITTON" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com...
> Hello everyone,
>
> when designing a Cobol application to function as an information system --
> mimics the behaviour and functionality of RDBMS (oracle & sql server),
besides
> using ISAM files layout, sequential files, detail report printing layouts
and
> screen section, what is the design and planning methods to accommodate for
> future Scalability requirements?
>
> thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-04T18:23:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vsvk02di0hf6c2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIE FITTON wrote:

> Hello everyone,
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> future Scalability requirements?

The only thing that comes to mind is making sure each module / class / 
etc. plays nice with others.  Don't hold or lock a file that you don't 
have to hold or lock.  Don't limit yourself in numbers of files and 
such, so users can "create more tables".  Make sure that whatever 
metadata you collect about the information stores is backed up, and not 
limited in size either.

Just some things that come to mind....
```

#### ↳ Re: can cobol scale the corporate ladder?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T07:48:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0312060748.682b94d9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com>`

```
Reply below (imagine that!)

KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote in message news:<41758a6b.0312041056.19fb9ef4@posting.google.com>...
> Hello everyone,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.

I wouldn't.  COBOL is fully capable of playing in the RDBMS arena -
it's not a REPLACEMENT for these systems, nor are these systems a
replacement for COBOL.

The data is the fuel used by the engine - whether the engine be a
Train or a car.

Now if you want to talk generally about the scalability between RDBMS
and Indexed files that's something else.  At the heart of any RDBMS is
a series of Indexed files!  ISAM is the heart of all RDBMS systems. 
Funny eh?  You don't get into RDBMS because you need scalability -
that's what mainframes are for.  You get into RDBMS for "open" access
to the data and ad hoc queries.  "Open" access to the data has up
sides and down sides.  That's yet another debate.

If the requirements are to utilize RDBMS - the COBOL can do that.  No
big deal.
```

## Thread: Debate re: Internal or free standing sort — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Debate re: Internal or free standing sort

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T19:43:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ad5Ab.12104$FG2.2187062@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
Here, again, a little history is helpful.
Prior to computers as we know them NOW,
there were unit record machines that processed
information one 80 or 90 column card. Perhaps
even 5 chanel paper tape.

As a consequence, such processing required lots
of matching, collating, and sorting. Please do not
drop the box!

Early computers interfaced THAT world. DASD
or Mass storage, etc. were yet rare. Tape drives
were limited in capacity AS WELL.

INTERNAL first and last pass SORTING fit that
world well. A Univac I "tape time" was six minutes.
Most applications ran in that time as programs were
and still may be I/O bound.

Doing a colation, or selection, etc. at the same time
the data was handed to the sort program saved as
least on tape time. Tapes on that system held 1000
blocks of 60 characters each. An input file could
easily be many boxes of cards converted to tape
requiring multi-reel files with their control problems.

The guy who upgraded Flowmatic to integrate
the early handling with the sort, was as still is
in my book a hero.

These days, your camera can have more storage
and it is the size of a stick of gum or perhaps
a small CD.

Such a technique is in my opinion applicable
in todays world. Are we storing pictures? or
other forms of data or NOT.

That guy I mentioned worked for Univac at
Ontario Hydro at the time, and he fixed
Flowmatic to allow this.

Certainly, in todays world there are needs
to accomidate different conditions and the
pico seconds saved are Not that significant.

Oh, perhaps they are if you are trying to
hit Mars.

Warren Simmons
wsimmons5@optonline.net

P.S. If I don't get to use a spell checker on
this there may be several laughs for some of
you, and I do not mind at all.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

>
> Loved the whole thing and your common sense approach. Wasn't aware there
> was a 90 column card. I'm assuming it must have been fairly short lived
>

UNIVAC had a 90-column card - actually a standard 80-column-sized card with
two rows of 45 columns each.  It must have been around for a while.  I know
that when I worked for UNIVAC in 1969 we had to do a 90-to-80 column
conversion - using an 80-column reader!  An extremely obnoxious but very
talented programmer mnaged to do it by OR and AND'ing two or three columns
together - I forget the details but it was 99% effective.

Peter

>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Debate re: Internal or free standing sort

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T10:42:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031207114342.158$us@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<Ad5Ab.12104$FG2.2187062@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3FD0E046.98339B84@shaw.ca> <3FD142E9.A28D8BCC@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
> UNIVAC had a 90-column card - actually a standard 80-column-sized card with
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> together - I forget the details but it was 99% effective.

This reminds me of an old early 1970s Varian machine relabeled and sold
as the DC1100 by Burroughs. As one of the few Burroughs programmers
in the Southeast 'smart enough' (read 'dumb enough' :) to tackle assembly
language on the thing, it was much like what we would label microcode
today. At the end of the two week course, the final goal was to ring the
bell on the TTY console. That's it, no message, just ring the bell. I had
several years heavy assembly background, so my group was the only one
of four to complete the project. It took a routine roughly 1,000 (that's right,
'one thousand') lines long just to read a card. You had to read each column
in two I/Os, first to read the uppermost 6 punches (yes, punches = bits)
and the second to read the lowermost 6 punches. Then you had to translate
the 2 bytes of punches using the encoding scheme of the card. One small
advantage was that the sucker could read or punch ANYTHING, even 80
columns with all 12 punches out in each column. It made an interesting
sound when you punched that, reminiscent of a dump truck filled with
gravel striking a wall. Though the computer was much larger (spinet
piano size) the front panel was similar to the first 8-bit microcomputers,
like the Altair or IMSAI, with lights and switches to read and set
addresses and data in binary. There was no OS, and debugging was fun,
fun, fun, especially at 2AM. :-)
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Sorry!! I was wrong about the # of rows, columns, etc.    :-(      My error
..... I trusted faded memory instead of checking references.  Here's a brief
description copy and pasted from the webpage at URL
http://homepages.cwi.nl/~dik/english/codes/punched.html  -----

Finally by the end of the sixties IBM came with the much smaller 96 column
card. The definition has 26 rows of 32 columns with punch positions for
small roundish holes. The 26 rows were divided in 6 upper rows, 2 rows that
were always blank and 18 lower rows. The first 32 columns were defined by
the 2 topmost rows of the upper part plus the 6 topmost rows of the lower
part, giving 8 punch positions for each symbol. The remainder was defined in
a similar fashion as shown in the table below. Within such a group the rows
were designated from top to bottom as D, C, B, A, 8, 4, 2 and 1. The reason
for this curious arrangement was the possible interpretation of the symbols
on the top of the card. A symbol was only interpreted when the D and C
punches were both absent, in that case on that place the symbol was printed
(this allowed interpretation for the most commonly used 64 symbols). In rows
7 and 8 32 further symbols could be printed, but they would not have
associated punches.



> I recall also seeing them used at one time as "receipts" printed by bank
> ATMs (Automatic Teller Machines) instead of the slips of paper now in
common
> use.
>
>
```

```
I always thought that the System 3 cards were 3 rows of 32 characters.
 Each character was represented like paper tape with 8 punches
positions.  This gave a card that was 32 x 24 punch positions.  10 per
inch or so gave 3.5" x 2.7" or so.
```

```
Warren is referring to the 96 column card, I believe. They were about
4" square with the upper left corner clipped. The data were encoded in
three rows, each of 32 columns, IIRC. Don't remember the encoding
scheme. They were a stopgap and enjoyed only brief use before cards
bit the dust entirely.
```

#### ↳ Re: Debate re: Internal or free standing sort

- **From:** "Vince Coen" <Vince_Coen@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.org>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T15:42:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Hello Warren!

05 Dec 03 19:43, Warren Simmons wrote to All:

 WS> Here, again, a little history is helpful. Prior to computers as we
 WS> know them NOW, there were unit record machines that processed
 WS> information one 80 or 90 column card. Perhaps even 5 chanel paper
 WS> tape.


There is/was two types of punched card 80 column and 96 column and this later 
one was used by the Debit Card services such as Amex and Diners and some 
large banks. The 80 col was the standard used at most commercial DP sites.



Vince
```

#### ↳ Re: Debate re: Internal or free standing sort

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T15:08:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br245b$3ko$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<Ad5Ab.12104$FG2.2187062@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
There aren't many situations where I pick which sort is the most efficient to
run.   I usually pick which is the most efficient to maintain.

With this in mind (and with knowledge of run-time efficiency in the back of my
mind) - I default to external sorts - using internal sorts where the data
indicate.

Sometimes the internal sort can winnow the amount of data down more effectively
than an external sort does.

Sometimes the internal business logic necessary to create the sort key works
well in CoBOL - and there is no good reason to write out the file with the new
key simply to make the sort external.

Sometimes the sort order is determined by business rules.   Sure, I can write
out a SORTIN file, but why?
```

## Thread: OT: Spam Checkers — 25 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### OT: Spam Checkers

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T20:07:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca>`

```
Just curious if anybody has had any success with the Spam Checkers
currently being touted.

As is, I can build in my own manual checks with Netscape. For a while it
seemed effective, sending much of the junk to Trash. However the
*bastards* are getting clever, e.g., comes in as say *p*e*n*i*s so you
can't trap it. So my trash mail is on the up and up.

A Spam checker surely can only be an automated version of what I'm doing
- and just as I could manually, there is the possibility that a Spam
checker *will* Trash a message I REALLY DO want to receive ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Spam Checkers

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T21:33:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD0FA7B.37874E5F@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Just curious if anybody has had any success with the Spam Checkers
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

K9 is a small, freeware Spam checker that uses Baysian (sp?)
statistical analysis.  While I haven't used it myself, it is regularly
discussed on Steve Gibson's grc.spam newsgroup at news.grc.com.

http://keir.net/k9.html

The author updates it often, and he takes suggestions from the users. 
It appears to be fairly popular and achieves 90-98% identification of
Spam messages based on your criteria.  It doesn't delete anything, it
just previews your email and adds something "SPAM" in the header.  I
believe it also offers blacklists and whitelists for email addresses. 
It's only a 69K zip file (105K with the installer).  

Good luck!
```

```
1. The new Outlook has spam-checking built in and it catches a few that
sneak through #2
2. There are several add-ons that filter your mail (We use SpamEater Pro).
What you want is one that can access external, dynamic, blocking lists in
addition to its own rules. The external lists we use are (numbers in
brackets [x] are quantity blocked so far this month):

*SpamCop (bl.spamcop.net) [21430]
*Distributed Server Boycott List (list.dsbl.org)[6048]
*Distributed Server Multi-Hop List (multihop.dsbl.org)[14]
*RFC Ignorant list (ipwhois.rfc-ignorant.org)[925]
*Many countries (Argentina, Brazil, China, Korea, HongKong,Japan, Malaysia,
Nigeria, Russia, Singapore, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey) all availabl at
www.blackholes.us [~3000]
*Several spam-friendly ISPs (Verio, Burst,XO) [~200]
*SPEWS (spews.org) [167]**

3. The problem with Baysian filters is that they let the first one through -
you have to train the filter.

4. Lurk in news.admin.net-abuse.email newsgroup for a few months. You'll be
amazed. I'll get you started on the lingo:
[C&C] - Cat & Coke = Warning: the following post is so funny, you should set
aside any cats or Cokes nearby because uncontrolled laughter will otherwise
result in scratches or liquid-in-the-nose.
[CARTOONEY] - Legal threat, usually involving interference with "Frea
Speach"
LART = Luser Attidude Readjustment Tool - A complaint to an ISP about a
spammer.
ROKSO = Registry of Known Spam Organizations.
SPAM = SPiced hAM - A trademark of Hormel
FOAD = Fuck Off And Die - Usual retort to spammers.
"Innocent bystander" = Someone caught in an IP-range block that's not a
spammer himself (anti-spammers call these people "human shields"). The usual
response to someone whineing about being so trapped is: "Sometimes the
innocent must suffer, often in great numbers, so that no spammer prevails."
-----------

**SPEWS (Spam Prevention Early Warning System) lists offending IP addresses.
If the responsible ISP does not evict the spammer, SPEWS escalates the
listing to eventually encompass the entire IP space of the spam-friendly
ISP. Spews has no contact name, telephone number, email address, or physical
location. Their servers are located in Irktusk, Siberia. They do not show
pity, negotiate, or defend their actions. As such, they are immune to
lawsuits.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Spam Checkers

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T20:49:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD143A5.99041576@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Just curious if anybody has had any success with the Spam Checkers
> currently being touted.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I have found it necessary to log onto my e-mail area on the (ISP) server and
manually delete all the junk before it's downloaded to me.  I can do a mass
mark-for-delete then unflag the ones I want.  Takes about five minutes for
the 200-250 messages per day I get.   My ISP is Manitoba Telephone System.
I assume that other ISP's offer the same utility.

Because there is NO WAY any automated spam checker can make every decision,
pro or con, properly.

pl
```

```
Peter Lacey wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> pl

It's amazing where the Canucks show up - why the modesty <G>

Like you I can go into Shaw and delete as you suggest. As is, I let the messages
flow in and using Netscape preferences I automatically knock-out the obvious
ones. (Not going to list them - one of the swine will be reading this no doubt
!) Then I look for legit messages and move them to my "Real IN folder'. Then I
do a block delete for the remainder sending them off to Trashville..

Hence my original query, because as you say, how can you automatically get rid
of the 'bad' and keep the 'good'.

OK - one trick is to have in your e-mail address "remove this before sending".
Why the hell should I ?. Never mind all the huffing and puffing about 'our
rights', 'no government intervention'. All Western governments should make a
concerted effort to make their intrusion into our lives a criminal offence -
punished by (1) Unequivocally removing/destroying their computer installations,
(2) fines starting at $10K, (3) Imprisonment starting at five years. Unless
seriously tracked and tackled by governments, these intruders will destroy the
whole concept of the Web.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Spam Checkers

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T09:07:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031207100815.848$vT@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Just curious if anybody has had any success with the Spam Checkers
> currently being touted.

I find that obfuscating your return address as below does the trick. In
cases where it gets bad, then changing your email address every several
years fixes it, though it's aggravating to lose an old email address. I
have learned that when dealing with an ISP and not your own domain,
you should *never* use the primary name as your email address. Always
create and use aliases, which are easy to change. I made that mistake
(using the primary name) with my old 'judmc at bellsouth' address, and
I had to cancel the whole account to get rid of it. Now I can't even check
that address or use it for six months. Grrr!

They are working on the problem here in the US. What we really need
is a law that forbids any business to send an email unless specifically
permitted by the recipient. A law to that effect for telephone calls went
into effect Nov. 1 in the U.S. The exceptions for that are charities, true
polls (can't offer to sell you anything) and companies you have done
business with in the last 90 days. I could live with that for SPAM.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
> > Just curious if anybody has had any success with the Spam Checkers
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> business with in the last 90 days. I could live with that for SPAM.

I don't follow it step-by-step - although we are involved up here as well. But
aren't the tele-marketers challenging that law based on 'ones civil rights'.

This is the best I've  heard to deal with tele-marketers, or door-to-door for
that matter.

T: "How are you today ?"
O: "I'm not"
T: (not to be daunted)
    "Well we're offering some very interesting....".
O: "Not interested".
T: "But you must have seen in the newspapers...."
O: "Don't read the papers".
T: "Well how about in  books ?"
O: "Don't read books".
T: "Why not ?"
O: "Because we can't read"
T: "But if you were writing something about......"
O: "None of us knows how to write".
T: (Very last effort).
    "But if you were sat on your sofa watching TV....".
O: "Don't have a sofa, or furniture. We aint got no TV neither !"

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> This is the best I've  heard to deal with tele-marketers, or door-to-door for
> that matter.

IMHO there are _much_ better ways to annoy them: feign great interest
and then tell them they should talk to 'my father' and you will go get
him, put phone down on desk or close door, and ignore them completely
until they eventually go away.

Sometimes, after wasting 10 minutes, they ring back and say you were
getting him, so just see if they can hit the waiting record.
```

```
>>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>at the door. :-)

I have a device that is plugged into an unused phone jack.  Whenever I
detect a telemarketer (which is rare since I have an unlisted number),
I press the star button on my phone and hang up.  The telemarketer
hears a very polite voice of what might be a butler saying, "Pardon
me, this is the Phone Butler, and I have been directed to inform you
that this household must respectfully decline your inquiry. Kindly
place this number on your do-not-call list. Good Day." and the line is
disconnected.  Works very nicely.  It is called the PhoneButler.  Read
about it at:  http://www.telemarketer-terminator.com/what.html

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Who lived an old maid
But died an old Mann.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Spam Checkers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <steve_nospam_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T18:48:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1a40294d95b05d509896a2@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca> <20031207100815.848$vT@news.newsreader.com> <3FD3822F.FFBF24A8@shaw.ca> <20031208145335.878$XW@news.newsreader.com> <qhobtvsqg880o8jj99rl83f409nbl7q3hl@4ax.com>`

```
In article <qhobtvsqg880o8jj99rl83f409nbl7q3hl@4ax.com>, 
swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com says...
<snip>
> I have a device that is plugged into an unused phone jack.  Whenever I
> detect a telemarketer (which is rare since I have an unlisted number),
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> about it at:  http://www.telemarketer-terminator.com/what.html
<snip>

I have a new method: 
1 Pick up the phone
2 If no immediate answer or a series of clicks:
2.1 Put the phone down and wait for the off the hook warble
2.2 If immediate answer (and they are a telemarketer), state 
"Take us off your list and do not call back", put the phone down 
and wait for off the hook warble

Why wait for the off hook warble? Because they have to pay for 
the connect time and it ties up one of their outgoing lines for 
a while. Note that it sometimes takes their equipment a LONG 
time to detect you ain't there and disconnect.

When telemarketing becomes more expensive than what it is worth, 
it will cease.

Same is true of spam. 

BTW - For those of you in the USofA (whether you can vote or 
not), make sure your congresscritters' opponents know that your 
congresscritter(s) voted to make spam legal and via eminent 
domain have taken possession of your inbasket (don't believe me, 
read the [You] CAN SPAM act).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Spam Checkers

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T20:05:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UcidnUP6RqH54kui4p2dnA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca> <20031207100815.848$vT@news.newsreader.com> <3FD3822F.FFBF24A8@shaw.ca> <20031208145335.878$XW@news.newsreader.com> <qhobtvsqg880o8jj99rl83f409nbl7q3hl@4ax.com> <MPG.1a40294d95b05d509896a2@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:

> 2.2 If immediate answer (and they are a telemarketer), state
> "Take us off your list and do not call back", put the phone down
> and wait for off the hook warble
>

There is a subtle, yet important, difference between "take us off your list"
and "add us to your do-not-call list."

The first is advisory, the second carries legal a sanction. The law actually
reads something like "... where the customer has specifically requested to
be placed on the 'do not call' list..."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Spam Checkers

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T17:30:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VMadncqeE8iUJU6iRVn-ig@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca> <20031207100815.848$vT@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> They are working on the problem here in the US. What we really need
> is a law that forbids any business to send an email unless
> specifically permitted by the recipient.

Even more specifically, we need an "Opt-In" law. The new U.S. federal law
that goes into effect 1 Jan works like this:

As a waiter approaches your table, you say: "I'll have a bowl of today's
soup, please."

The waiter pulls a hammer from beneath his apron and hits you on the head.

"Ouch!" you say: "WTF's wrong with you?"

The waiter says: "New policy, sir. We are to hit every customer on the head
until he tells us to stop." He hits you again.

"Goddamn it! Are you insane?"

Whack!

You stagger to your feet: "I'm never coming back to this restaurant!"

"No problem, sir," replies the waiter. "I have your address. I'll be over
later to hit you some more. And, by the way, if you tell me to stop, it'll
have no effect on the other waiters, either here or at any other restaurant
in town."

He manages to get in one more thump before you get through the door.

The above technique is called "Opt-out," and is what the new law authorizes.
```

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

```
Pat Hall wrote:
> I'm using Mailwasher.  Seems to work pretty good and I like that it
> sends a reply back to the spammer that looks like the Mailer Daemon
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> James J. Gavan wrote:

TURN IT OFF! ** RIGHT NOW! ** TURN IT ALL THE WAY OFF!

About 20%, and rising, of spam fakes the FROM address. You are inadvertently
spamming (with a bounce) some poor schumck whose address has been used by
the spammer. I get upwards of 20+ bounces a day (mostly from the AOL
servers).

Further, you are confirming that there is a sentinent computer at the
address. This tells the spammer that he might get through with a more clever
form of obfuscation.

Google groups for "mailwasher+bounce" for more information.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Spam Checkers

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T09:13:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BC96C6.09133711122003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3FD0E2D5.EC6F221@shaw.ca>,
 "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Just curious if anybody has had any success with the Spam Checkers
> currently being touted.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

One approach that will work, but requires some code would be a scrabble 
algorithm.

After doing the obvious, like excluding people in your address book, 
verifying the format of the return address, etc.

Sort the letters of the title string into lexical order, in the 
*P*E*N*I*S* example this would be:

  EINPS*****

Do a char-by-char compare that against a list of common spam words 
sorted in the same style, e.g. penis, refinance, enlarge, debt, make 
money fast, hot teenage poodles, whatever.

The idea being to count how many spam-words one can make from the 
subject line and score the mail as such.

You will notice that by tweaking your spam word list and studying the 
mails that fall through that pretty soon you can consistently score a 
spam higher than a non-spam.

Use that score to file it in a junk folder that you check once every 
month or so for accidental ids.
```

#### ↳ Re: help and direction

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T11:27:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd1077c_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<e65abccf.0312051319.420c0ab2@posting.google.com>`

```

"Ran" <mallman_il@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:e65abccf.0312051319.420c0ab2@posting.google.com...
> Hello all
> I am a Cobol programmer on IBM MF, with all the going on I would like
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks

I sympathize with your dilemma, having been there myself.

The following is arguable and represents opinion. It is not necessarily fact
and many here will disagree. However, it is what I honestly believe...

The Situation:

1. There is little place for  traditional COBOL in the "New Technology".
2. There was a chance for COBOL to claim some of this territory by the
provision of OO COBOL. This was late arriving, due largely to a monolithic
Standards process, was rejected by the user base, who didn't really
understand the Network anyway and weren't prepared to learn about it,  and
the result is that the ship has sailed and COBOL is still on the quay.
(Despite the glee in some quarters over the .COM collapse, many businesses
are now starting to realise that the baby was almost thrown out with the
bath water, and that the Internet is not only here to stay, it is an
ESSENTIAL part of doing business. I predict an upsurge in Web Based business
and increased use of the Internet by nonWeb Based businesses. While COBOL
COULD play a part in this, it is unlikely to do so to any great degree.)
3. The future of COBOL is in batch processing. CICS will keep online legacy
systems running until they are converted, and there will be some limited
development in the future, but its once huge market share will be
continuously eroded by faster, cheaper, transaction processing alternatives
based on OO languages, and packages.
4. There is a strong market demand for less complexity and less need for
procedural programming. This is being met by a number of technologies,
including component based design, and it is likely we will see fundamental
changes in the way that corporations develop computer systems (if they
continue to do so at all...outsourcing is a very attractive alternative to
many businesses), with NO COBOL involved at all.
5. There is a new breed of IT professionals coming out of Acadaemia. They
are NOT "one language wonders" but have a broad understanding of the New
Technology and the tools and languageS available. They do not see solutions
in terms of "How would I program that in COBOL?". These will be the Managers
of tomorrow.
6. Business users and the World in general are becoming more "computer
literate" and capable of developing their own local business solutions with
standard software (spreadsheets, databases, etc.). The traditional role of
"computer programmer" is becoming like the role of "chauffeur"; once the
interface was simplified to the point where "anyone can do it", people opted
(for the most part) to drive themselves.
7. Emerging technologies within the next 15 years will place undreamed of
power in the hands of business users. It is likely that people will interact
iteratively with "smart" software, in their own natural language, to define
business processes.

A bleak picture for COBOL? Maybe. I use it (OO COBOL) to build business
components and find it excellent for that, but I accept that even this use
has a limited lifetime (about as long as my own...<G>).

What to do about it:

1. Expand your skill set and your IT horizons. You are, and will be, as
employable as the skills you have. Learn about standard software, be
familiar with spreadsheets, Relational databases, and Word Processors.

Make sure the following are in your "armoury":

1. At least one OO Language. (I recommend Java, but C++ is probably OK.  You
can teach yourself Java in 4 weeks easily if you apply yourself a couple of
hours a day...what is 4 weeks against the remainder of your career?)
2. HTML, XML, and knowledge about the Web and how to use it.(Again, easily
learned at home.)
3. Develop at least one area of "specialist expertise"; maybe an area of the
business...be the "General Ledger/Inventory
Control/SalesAnalysis/whatever..." Guru. This is saleable.
4. Optionally, study part time for an Accounting or Legal (Corporate Law)
qualification.

Look on your COBOL knowledge as valuable experience that got you into
commercial data processing and gave you insight into the use of computers in
business. You didn't waste your time studying it; it has given you
understanding of many fundamental concepts, but realise that there are also
other possibilities, and the world is changing. Don't expect every new
solution to be like COBOL, and try not to measure appples against oranges.
(Keep your mind open and realise there is more than ONE solution).

2. Consider moving from Technology into Applications. Business Process (Re)
Engineering is an interesting and useful area. So is Auditing (ISO9000
compliance is particularly lucrative...). "Computer programming" is not
everything... and it is becoming less and less...

3.  Do not fear the future. You learned COBOL; you can learn other stuff.
Never lose the "yearning for learning" and your future will take care of
itself.

Good Luck!

Pete.
```

### Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-05T16:43:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031205174458.964$v0@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <xWcJcSB6d7y$EwDF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <QEYyb.36180$aT.34342@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <20031202074525.298$YC@news.newsreader.com> <3fccadee_2@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> point, or external decimal (which was converted to binary for calculations).

Peter, I am looking at a book titled "Logical Programming with System/360"
by Don H. Stabley, copyright 1970, John Wiley publisher. On page 60, is a
paragraph with the heading "2. Packed Decimal Data", and on the opposite
page (61) is a chart showing how various packed decimal values would be
stored, dutifully showing the (IBM required) sign nibble. How can you say
that the System/360 did not require a sign on packed decimal fields? We are
discussing packed decimal fields, no? Any packed decimal field on an IBM
System/360 or descendant requires a sign nibble. That's what I said, and it's
true.

> > Perhaps to sell more memory?
>
> Nope. Not at all. Memory (being hand made) was certainly expensive but one
> of the reasons for implementing packed in the first place was to SAVE
> memory.

Saving memory with packed decimal, yes. Saving memory by adding a
redundant mandatory sign to 'unsigned' fields, no. :-)

Don't reject that idea so casually. When it comes to using every possible
angle to sell more hardware, IBM was second to none in those days. Bill
Gates probably learned much of what he knows about that from watching
IBM. Gates did say he wanted to be "the IBM of software," remember?
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-06T11:41:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nc14tv0c1g1r9u8cq7aa991gr002np2ve8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <xWcJcSB6d7y$EwDF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <QEYyb.36180$aT.34342@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <20031202074525.298$YC@news.newsreader.com> <3fccadee_2@news.athenanews.com> <20031205174458.964$v0@news.newsreader.com>`

```
On Fri, 5 Dec 2003 16:43:58 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> enlightened us:

>"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Let's clarify here.  IBM required the sign nibble on a Packed Decimal
field if you wanted to perform arithmetic operations on it or move it
to an unpacked field.  However, you could certainly store packed
decimal numbers on disk or tape without the sign nibble.  I wrote many
an Assembler conversion program for some of the old banking systems
(including IBM's own PAL package) that stored data in this way.  When
converting the data, we had to shift the data a half byte and insert a
sign so that the data could be used in Cobol in a Comp-3 data field.


>> > Perhaps to sell more memory?
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>IBM. Gates did say he wanted to be "the IBM of software," remember?

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


Real Tombstone Epitaphs:

In a Thurmont, Maryland, cemetery:
Here lies an Atheist
All dressed up And no place to go.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
I read this after I formulated my response to Judson, but it endorses my own
position on this.

I think that because this is a COBOL forum, many people (in particular,
Judson) just aren't aware that Packed can be used in this way.

It is ironic that he has convinced himself  that  the sign is a ploy by IBM
to sell more memory <G>.

Pete.

> >> point, or external decimal (which was converted to binary for
calculations).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T06:27:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xLzAb.3363$rP6.2479@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <xWcJcSB6d7y$EwDF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <QEYyb.36180$aT.34342@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <20031202074525.298$YC@news.newsreader.com> <3fccadee_2@news.athenanews.com> <20031205174458.964$v0@news.newsreader.com> <nc14tv0c1g1r9u8cq7aa991gr002np2ve8@4ax.com> <3fd26fb1_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Pete and Steve, (Judson and others)
   I guess I am confused about exactly whether this is an argument about
terminology or history or what.

There SEEMS to be agreement that on S/360 and subsequent IBM "mainframe"
machines, one must have a "valid" sign-nibble in a packed-decimal field in order
to do arithmetic on it.

Furthermore, I think we all agree that all such machines support UNSIGNED
"zoned-decimal" numeric items (that one can do arithmetic on).

The question (seems to me) to come down to whether you can "pack" an unsigned
numeric value into a "valid" packed field that you will NOT be doing arithmetic
on.

Here I think the "terminology" problem arises.  Yes, you certainly can put the
"bit-patterns" representing the digits from 0-9 in each nibble of a field and
that is a "valid" storage of something in S/360 and following.  HOWEVER, in
IBM's terminology this is NOT (and as far as I know, never has been) a "valid
packed field".  For the most current definition, see:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dz9zr002/8.1.2

which is the definition of "Packed Format" and which says (in part),

"  In  the  packed  format, each byte contains two decimal digits (D), except
for the rightmost byte, which contains a sign to the right  of  a  decimal
digit."

which (seems to me) to indicate that if the last nibble isn't a sign, it isn't
in "packed format".

Concerning the "question" of whether you can "pack" data that does NOT include a
sign nibble in the penultimate nibble of a zoned decimal sending item,

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dz9zr002/7.5.98

implies (or I infer) that you may - but are "minimally" supported - as it says,

"The second operand is treated as having the zoned format. The numeric bits of
each byte are treated as a digit. The zone bits are ignored, except the zone
bits in the rightmost byte, which are treated as a sign."

It even addresses what I think started this conversation when it says,

 "3. To remove the zone bits of all bytes of a field, including the rightmost
byte, both operands should be extended on the right with a dummy byte, which
subsequently should be ignored in the result field."

So you probably (and I suspect some mainframe assembler programmers DO) "pack"
fields where the sending field's sign-nibble is not A-F.  As Pete and Steve
indicated, you will be in "trouble" if you then try to use this in arithmetic -
furthermore, you can, however, even use the UNPACK instruction - as
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dz9zr002/7.5.141

says,

 "The sign and digits are not checked for valid codes."

So if your sending field were X"ABCDE1" - when you unpack it you will get (I
think)
    X"FAFBFCFD1E"

which must be useful <G> for something.

For further information on
  "Decimal-Operand Data Exception"
(known and loved by IBM mainframe COBOL programmers as S0C7), see:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dz9zr002/8.2.5

    ***

Does this put us all back talking about the same things????
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T11:16:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031207121721.593$2d@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8YudnQLu9tKopFaiRVn-uw@comcast.com> <xWcJcSB6d7y$EwDF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <QEYyb.36180$aT.34342@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <20031202074525.298$YC@news.newsreader.com> <3fccadee_2@news.athenanews.com> <20031205174458.964$v0@news.newsreader.com> <nc14tv0c1g1r9u8cq7aa991gr002np2ve8@4ax.com> <3fd26fb1_8@news.athenanews.com> <xLzAb.3363$rP6.2479@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Bill, thanks for the post. What you say below is precisely as I have known
it to be all along. What I have been trying to say can be expressed succinctly
this way:

"On most non-IBM hardware, you can create a field PIC 9999 (with some
COMP-? usage) that will be stored as packed decimal into two bytes, with
all four half-bytes representing decimal values, and no sign. This field can
then be used in any arithmetic operation as any other numeric field, and it
will always be considered positive for this purpose. IBM mainframe
hardware (System/360 & descendents) does not support this use."

I'm surprised anyone thought I was trying to say that you could not stuff
four decimal digits into two bytes with no sign using assembly language
or even cleverly written COBOL. As an old time assembly programmer
I considered this a no-brainer non-issue. Wow, I must come across as
pretty dumb. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ How do they do it? (was" Re: MVS Packed Decimal with no sign nibble?)

```
[snip]

>"On most non-IBM hardware, you can create a field PIC 9999 (with some
>COMP-? usage) that will be stored as packed decimal into two bytes, with
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just out of curiosity, as a lifelong IBM mainframe bigot, can you tell me how
such numbers convey sign when necessary? It seems they can't. So when you need
a sign, you have to waste a whole byte on it? Is that how they do it on non-IBM
iron?
```
