I know all of the system-specific tricks you mention. Been doing that for
years. Its the protability requirement for the vendors in question that I
needed to satisfy. Most (all?) of the non-standard COBOL they produce is
widely implemented as extensions, just not in UCOB.

DG

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vt7mnvo61g3h91@corp.supernews.com...
> Douglas Gallant wrote:
>
> > Using only COBOL 85 syntax, what is a portable method of representing
ASCII
> > control character values in a PIC X(1) field?
> >
> > The problem that I have encountered is that two separate vendors,
claiming
> > to produce COBOL 85  Standard syntax, both end up with a field like
> > LINE-FEED PIC X(1) VALUE X'0A' in their code. To the best of my
knowledge
> > hex constants are non-standard (though widely implemented) and my
compiler
> > (Unisys 2200 series UCOB) will not accept that. For my specific compiler
I
> > know how to do this rather simply but that is based on either vendor
> > extensions or knowledge of USAGE BINARY allocation rules which are also
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > collating sequence were being used. I'm sure there is a common solution
to
> > this that I have overlooked so any help would be appreciated.
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard way to implement ASCII control chars

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T08:24:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MpCdnSmQJa6iRkiiRVn-vg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<havAb.190447$ZC4.113271@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Douglas Gallant wrote:
> Using only COBOL 85 syntax, what is a portable method of representing
> ASCII control character values in a PIC X(1) field?
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Douglas Gallant

01  A1  PIC X(10).
01  A1-9 REDEFINES A1 PIC 9(7) BINARY.
01  LF  PIC X.

MOVE 10 TO A1-9.
PERFORM VARYING Indx FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL Indx > 10
   IF A1(Indx:1) NOT = LOW-VALUES
      MOVE A1(Indx) TO LF
      END-IF
END-PERFORM.

This works if the compiler knows about binary.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standard way to implement ASCII control chars

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T14:58:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eN-dnb2c1rjzqkuiRVn-hQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<havAb.190447$ZC4.113271@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <MpCdnSmQJa6iRkiiRVn-vg@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

>
> 01  A1  PIC X(10).
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> This works if the compiler knows about binary.

Revised code (nothing ever works right the first time)

MOVE 10 TO A1-9
PERFORM VARYING Indx FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL Indx > 10
  IF A1(Indx:1) NOT = LOW-VALUES
    MOVE A1(Indx:1) TO LF
  END-IF
END-PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard way to implement ASCII control chars

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T02:05:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cvBb.87$JW3.11@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
A clever bit. I'll keep this one in mind. The use of SYMBOLIC CHARACTERS
from a couple of other posters looks like a winner if my compiler likes it.

DG

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standard way to implement ASCII control chars

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T19:37:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TvpBb.173$rf2.72364133@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<havAb.190447$ZC4.113271@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3FD5ECE9.2F08B657@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
"Karl Kiesel" <Karl.Kiesel@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote in message
news:3FD5ECE9.2F08B657@fujitsu-siemens.com...
> we would have proposed as a portable solution the definition of a
> 'constant' in special-names paragraph as
>   SYMBOLIC CHARACTERS  line-feed  IS  11
> (without an IN alphabet clause).
> Anything wrong with this solution, since nobody else did offer it ??

Karl,

It is the correct solution, though it is normally overlooked.  To expand a
bit:

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
CONFIGURATION SECTION.
SPECIAL-NAMES.
    SYMBOLIC line-feed IS 11.
...
...
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  LF-CHARACTER PIC X VALUE line-feed.
...

The SYMBOLIC mechanism defines a symbolic (aka figurative) constant which
may be used in the manner of other predefined figurative constants (such as
SPACE).

I will attempt to answer preemptively a follow-on question, "Why 11?"  The
integer that is specified is the ordinal position of the character within
the alphabet.  In this case the alphabet is NATIVE (ASCII), and the line
feed character, the value of which is X'0A', is the eleventh charater in the
'ASCII alphabet.'

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard way to implement ASCII control chars

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T21:23:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n3rBb.5594$_r6.4337@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<havAb.190447$ZC4.113271@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3FD5ECE9.2F08B657@fujitsu-siemens.com> <TvpBb.173$rf2.72364133@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Re:

"In this case the alphabet is NATIVE (ASCII), and the line feed character, the
value of which is X'0A', is the eleventh character in the 'ASCII alphabet.'

It should be noted that X"0A" is the 11th "character" in ANY computer character
set that supports all bit combinations from X"00" thru X"0A" - regardless of
what those bits MEAN.  This is why using "11" in either the CHAR intrinsic
function or the SYMBOLIC CHARACTER phrase of the Special-Names paragraph will
allow a programmer to produce an X"0A" - even if that bit-pattern does NOT mean
"line-feed" on that computer.  This is why either solution (where available)
will allow a program to create/use an ASCII "line feed" character - even in a
NON-ASCII environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard way to implement ASCII control chars

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T15:49:31+00:00

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:n3rBb.5594$_r6.4337@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net...

the

character
> set that supports all bit combinations from X"00" thru X"0A" - regardless
of
> what those bits MEAN.  [snip]

Dang it, Bill, you are right again.  All these character sets floating
around cause me to have fevers, I guess!

My apologies to the group...  [blush]

Tom Morrison
```

## Thread: LOTR — 4 msgs

### Re: LOTR

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T07:50:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031207085139.545$gc@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3fcc8929_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Yesterday we had the World Premiere here of "Lord of the Rings:
> The Return of the King".

Peter, I've been meaning to mention how impressed I have been with the
stunning NZ scenery in LOTR. IIRC NZ was where they filmed much of
Willow too, also stunningly beautiful. Do you folks spend all your free
time looking at the scenery and pushing sheep out of the way? ;-)

I am a LOTR fan from 'way back and have probably read The Hobbit
and LOTR more than ten times. Naturally I saw both previous movies
and own the standard and extended DVDs of each, and plan on seeing
Return of the King as soon as released here. I know it's too much to ask
after he has spent so much of his life on LOTR, but wouldn't it be nice
if Peter Jackson made The Hobbit also? After the original crummy
productions of these movies, I feared we would never see a good
LOTR movie. Thanks, Peter Jackson, et al!

Just one question to any of you who have seen Return of the King:
does it cover the hobbits returning home and cleaning out the Shire?
I've been hoping they would, but since it's almost an epilogue, have
been concerned they might not. Was glad to see they included the
Ents' part at Helm's Deep in the extended edition of Two Towers.
```

#### ↳ Re: LOTR

- **From:** jeff.dot.lanam@compaq.com.not.dot.net (Jeff Lanam)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T19:38:40+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3fd4d31e.609835026@news.compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<3fcc8929_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031207085139.545$gc@news.newsreader.com>`

```
On Sun, 7 Dec 2003 07:50:39 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:


>I am a LOTR fan from 'way back and have probably read The Hobbit
>and LOTR more than ten times. Naturally I saw both previous movies
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

http://story.news.yahoo.com/news?tmpl=story&cid=638&e=1&u=/nm/20031208/en_nm/arts_rings_director_dc

#### ↳ Re: LOTR

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T11:24:41+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd4fa74_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3fcc8929_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031207085139.545$gc@news.newsreader.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031207085139.545$gc@news.newsreader.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> > Yesterday we had the World Premiere here of "Lord of the Rings:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

For those of us who grew up here we do tend to take it for granted.

Returning after many years abroad (apart from the regular short visits) it
is like discovering it all again.

There are still occasions when I gasp at how beautiful it is. You come over
a hill or round a corner and have to stop the car...

Most of us are very aware that it is "special" and there is high
environmental awareness here, even (and especially) amongst the rising
generation.

Although LOTR is filmed in both islands (the Shire is 30 minutes from where
I live, in a fertile river valley where NZ's longest river, the Waikato
winds its way from Lake Taupo (in the middle of the North Island) to the
Tasman Sea, emerging just South of Auckland), the most spectacular scenery
is in the South Island. The mountain scenes were shot in the Southern Alps
and the city of Rohan was assembled and completely removed after shooting,
in one of the Southern National Parks. (It was a condition of the permit to
shoot that everything must be left exactly as they found it, and the
contract was honoured. Jackson (being a Kiwi) realised how important this
was, where another Director might not have.The logistics of building and
removing a city (even in set form) in an inaccessible part of the South
Island must have been quite formidable.

Mordor and Mount Doom are in the middle of the North Island (Mount Doom is
based around an actual active volcano) in an area of desert where I did my
military service 35 years ago...the army like this place because it is miles
from habitation and you can fire off high explosive without hurting
anything...<G>)

As for the sheep... we are no longer as reliant on sheep as we were.
Originally, we were simply an outpost of the British Empire, designed to
provide Mother England with ovine products. By the turn of the 20th Century
we were well geared up for this and were shipping frozen lamb (on sailing
ships in the 1880s!), wool, and, later, butter to the Old country. This
worked out well for all concerned, and we simply lived off the wool cheques
up until the 1960s, when Ted Heath shafted us in the name of EuroGreed.
(Personally, I don't blame him for entering Europe; I DO blame him for not
protecting the interests of his traditional allies and standing up to the
French when they demanded an end to imports of NZ lamb and butter. Thatcher
would have done it differently and we would have had time to re-gear our
economy.)

Our standard of living fell from 4th highest in the World to 38th. (today we
are somewhere around 16th, so we're coming back...<G>).

It took many years to recover from this and there is still much bitterness
over the way it was handled, but, in the long run, it was good for us. We
had toget off our arses and find other markets and other products. (Movie
making is just one of these areas of diversity which is starting to pay
off.)

Today we run around 35,000,000 sheep. If that sounds like a lot, remember
that in 1960 the figure was nearer 70,000,000.
(Most of it goes to the Middle East and we have slaughterhouses here that
are Halal and face East, specifically for this market...)

> I am a LOTR fan from 'way back and have probably read The Hobbit
> and LOTR more than ten times. Naturally I saw both previous movies
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, he certainly did an amazing job on the trilogy. The "word"  is (I have
not consulted the official web sites but I do have a few friends who are in
the industry) that he has said he would like to make "The Hobbit" and is
waiting to be asked. At the moment he is gearing up for the re-make of King
Kong, which he has wanted to do for many years apparently.

> Just one question to any of you who have seen Return of the King:
> does it cover the hobbits returning home and cleaning out the Shire?
> I've been hoping they would, but since it's almost an epilogue, have
> been concerned they might not. Was glad to see they included the
> Ents' part at Helm's Deep in the extended edition of Two Towers.

We keep getting "teased" here with occasional clips and previews. Some of
these I have deliberately avoided because I want to see the film without
watching for the clips I have previewed. All-in-all there are now about 25
minutes of it which have been "pre-released". I don't know whether the
Hobbits go back and clean up the Shire in the movie, but Jackson is so
dedicated to the story that it might well be on the extended edition, when
it is finally released.

The critics here were not allowed to publish reviews until the American
press had seen it (after the Premiere) and the first one I saw was
yesterday. The guy reckoned it was not as good as "The Two Towers" and was
fairly luke warm on it. This augurs well. I find that almost invariably when
critics dislike a movie, I enjoy it immensely, and vice versa. They raved
about "The English Patient" and it bored me rigid. They slagged off
"Terminator 3", but it made me laugh and I enjoyed it...(there is definitely
a place for mindless mayhem in the world of comedy...)

Pete.
```

documentary somewhere).

"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:s1K+07A4a40$EwiS@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> Surely that is counter clockwise in the northern hemisphere and
> clockwise in the southern hemisphere? Apparently, water generally
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Saw your post after responding to Judson, Alistair.

As I'm not sure whether it should be clockwise or counterclockwise, I
decided to do some experiments with the kitchen sink. It is a standard
stainless steel sink, with a waste disposal outlet situated more or less in
the middle of it, and supplied by a "swingable" mixer tap. ("faucet" for our
North American friends.)

I found some "interesting" results.

If the faucet is swung to the side of the sink so that water flows IN
anticlockwise, when the plug is pulled (always carefully and directly
upwards so as not to impart any spin), the water flows OUT anticlockwise.

If the faucet is swung to the side of the sink so that water flows IN
clockwise, when the plug is pulled (always carefully and directly upwards so
as not to impart any spin), the water flows OUT clockwise.

I quickly realised that the slightest inclination towards a particular
direction affected the outcome. So I repeated the trials (4 in each
direction) and ensured that the body of water in the sink (always about the
same amount; half an inch over the plug) was completely at rest before
removing the plug. (I used tiny bubbles to detect any movement in the body
of water and was satisfied that within minutes of filling, it was at rest.)

I got exactly the same results! It was as if the water somehow remembered
the direction it HAD BEEN flowing in... (Coincidence? I guess it has to
be...)

So then I tried doing it with the water flowing in directly over the plug,
so there was no pre-disposition to swirl in either direction.

In this case it went anticlockwise 3 times and clockwise once.

Draw your own conclusions.

(My conclusion is that this is an indeterminate experiment and proves
nothing. It MAY show a preferment to wards anti-clockwise, but this is
impossible to confirm.)

Pete.



> In message <20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com>, Judson McClendon
> <judmc@sunvaley0.com> writes

Pete,
Thanks for the post. I'm too lazy to get off my fat *rs* and check this 
out so cheers for conducting the experiment.

Some additional info:
1. Scientists found that it is necessary to leave the water standing for 
four days before conducting each plug pull. They thought that there was 
some movement of the water, undetectable by sight, still occuring for 
some time after filling.

2. Coriolli's force is usually ascribed to having a greater influence on 
moving bodies than it really does. It is apparently rather weak; hence 
the idea that sink/bath design has a greater influence.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Hemlines

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T19:42:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312071942.2436c996@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2grohjARG9v$Ewp$@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <bq16tb$4fj$1@panix1.panix.com> <p5o9sv0p8shhrt1j7nr0hmk3bfk4o8e2br@4ax.com> <3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <bq5827$j46$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fc665be_8@news.athenanews.com> <asghsvkrnfj2pp2aiknag69fqkqui9aqtk@4ax.com> <3fc9464c_8@news.athenanews.com> <ybUyb.35803$Cv6.21802@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3fcc7a98_1@news.athenanews.com> <20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com> <s1K+07A4a40$EwiS@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote 

> Surely that is counter clockwise in the northern hemisphere and 
> clockwise in the southern hemisphere? Apparently, water generally 
> follows Coriollis but with some clever sink/bath design you can get the 
> water to flow in the other direction (I think I saw that on a tv 
> documentary somewhere).

'documentary', you must mean 'mockumentary'.

The Coriollis force is a result of the different speeds at the surface
of the Earth at different lattitudes.  At the equator it is
approximately 1000 mph.  At the poles it is zero.  Here is it roughly
700 mph.  This means that if I walk to the equator I will have to
speed up by 300 mph.  Or to be exact I will have to be sped up by the
surface.  It will take me approx 1000 hours to walk at 3 mph without
stopping, thus the surface will accelerate me at 0.3 m.p.h. per hour,
or approx 0.0001 ft per sec per sec.  This is 1/300,000 the force of
gravity.

When air or water moves north or south it will tend to lag or lead
slightly based on those figures.  The result is that for a statically
sized flow there is a tendancy to reult in a circulation. The final
result will be a rotation with a rate of up to once per day.  Rates
faster than this are the result of the rotation being reduced in size
by other forces or effects and the conserving of angular momentum
(just as a spinning skater can speed up by bring the arms in to the
body).

If you were to carry the sinkfull of water with you when walking to
the equator, and given no other forces are applied then eventually it
will be swirling away at some fraction of one rotation per day.  If
the plug is then pulled then it will speed up as it drains due to the
reduction in radius lower down the sink.

While the sink is fixed in your house there is _no_ coriollis force
acting on the water at all, this is entirely the result of moving
north or south.  Any residual tiny rotation in either direction is
amplified by the conservation of angular momentum as the water reduces
in radius.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Hemlines

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T08:44:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031208094550.842$Mf@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<2grohjARG9v$Ewp$@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <bq16tb$4fj$1@panix1.panix.com> <p5o9sv0p8shhrt1j7nr0hmk3bfk4o8e2br@4ax.com> <3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <bq5827$j46$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fc665be_8@news.athenanews.com> <asghsvkrnfj2pp2aiknag69fqkqui9aqtk@4ax.com> <3fc9464c_8@news.athenanews.com> <ybUyb.35803$Cv6.21802@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3fcc7a98_1@news.athenanews.com> <20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com> <s1K+07A4a40$EwiS@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <217e491a.0312071942.2436c996@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> The Coriollis force is a result of the different speeds at the surface
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> reduces in radius.

Good explanation, Richard. It is the conservation of angular momentum
as mass moves either closer or farther away from the axis of rotation
(ie. the raidus of rotation changes) that results in the Corolis effect.
The same angular momentum results in  faster or slower rotation as the
radius of rotation decreases or increases, respectively. The radius of
rotation (distance from the axis) for any latitude is proportional to
cosine(latitude). Since the radius of the earth is relatively uniform,
we can omit that for discussion. Since it is the change in distance that
causes the Coriolis effect, you can get the rate of change as the first
derivative of cosine(latitude), which is (conveniently) sine(latitude).
When you calculate the difference in rotational velocity between the
opposite sides of a hurricane hundreds miles across, it is enormous.
As the heated air at the center rises, causing the hurricane to contract,
the effect is overwhelming. Both the contraction of the hurricane as
it rotates about its own center, and the contraction and rotation of the
hurricane as the earth rotates, work together to increase rotation. But
the difference between the sides of a whirlpool a few inches across is
infinitesimal. Almost any movement in the water could be greater than
Corolis at that level. However, once rotation begins, and the movement
is toward the center, the change in radius of rotation as the water moves
inward toward the drain becomes very significant. Like an ice skater
who spins, then pulls in their arms to rotate very rapidly. Whatever
rotation is present at that point will swamp any effect from the earth's
rotation. The water would need to be very still in a symmetrical bowl to
get a good test. In those conditions you should see counterclockwise
(northern hemisphere) and clockwise (southern hemisphere) rotation,
pretty consistently. Because of the small Corolis effect at this scale,
the system is somewhat chaotic, so you may not see absolutely uniform
results. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Hemlines

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T12:14:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312081214.760aeb05@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2grohjARG9v$Ewp$@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <bq5827$j46$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fc665be_8@news.athenanews.com> <asghsvkrnfj2pp2aiknag69fqkqui9aqtk@4ax.com> <3fc9464c_8@news.athenanews.com> <ybUyb.35803$Cv6.21802@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3fcc7a98_1@news.athenanews.com> <20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com> <s1K+07A4a40$EwiS@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <217e491a.0312071942.2436c996@posting.google.com> <20031208094550.842$Mf@news.newsreader.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote 

> The water would need to be very still in a symmetrical bowl to
> get a good test. In those conditions you should see counterclockwise
> (northern hemisphere) and clockwise (southern hemisphere) rotation,
> pretty consistently. 

No.  In a very still symmetrical bowl with no irregularities the water
will simply drop straight down the outlet.  The amount of residual
angular momentum is so infintesimal at those sizes that amplifying it
by a couple of orders of magnitude due to reduction in radius still
results in rotation that is not measurable in the time taken to empty
the sink (for any rational concepts of 'sink').

Consider that the water will be rotating once per day if stationary. 
Amplify that by 100 (which is _way_ to much) in a sink that will take
5 seconds to drain and the total resulting rotation of the last drops
will 'swirl' through 2 degrees.

> Because of the small Corolis effect at this scale,
> the system is somewhat chaotic, so you may not see absolutely uniform
> results. :-)

You will most likely always see 'uniform' results, especially when
someone is demonstrating that is _really_ does happen.

There are shysters in the tropics that will 'prove' that they are on
the actual equator by having a tub of water drain.  They put it in one
place and it goes straight down, move it a few feet north and it
rotates one way, a few feet south and it rotates the other.  Some are
entirely fooled by this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Hemlines

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T09:59:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031209112125.411$L4@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<2grohjARG9v$Ewp$@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <bq5827$j46$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fc665be_8@news.athenanews.com> <asghsvkrnfj2pp2aiknag69fqkqui9aqtk@4ax.com> <3fc9464c_8@news.athenanews.com> <ybUyb.35803$Cv6.21802@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3fcc7a98_1@news.athenanews.com> <20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com> <s1K+07A4a40$EwiS@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <217e491a.0312071942.2436c996@posting.google.com> <20031208094550.842$Mf@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0312081214.760aeb05@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the sink (for any rational concepts of 'sink').


Based only on Corolis from the earth's rotation, I agree. But when you
figure in the many-orders-of-magnitude-greater effect of the conservation
of the angular momentum of the (very slowly) rotating water as it moves
toward the center, the rotational velocity rapidly accelerates. In stasis it
is balanced on a knife-edge, ready to begin rapid rotation at the slightest
movement in either direction. Corolis provides just barely enough force
to tip the balance much of the time. The earth's Corolis effect would never
rotate any small whirlpool enough to notice, all by itself. Have you ever
seen a sink, bathtub, pool, or anything else, completely drain with no
rotation? I haven't. :-)  I live at about 34* N latitude (Well, 33* 45' 10"
N, 86* 40' 00" W to be precise - my big telescope has a GPS but I have
to know to set the smaller one :), and still water in a sink or tub will
drain CCW a significant majority of the time (around 75-80% IIRC from
my last experiments). Near the equator where the surface tangent is almost
parallel to the equator, it probably would be around 50% CW vs. CCW.
Near the poles consistent rotation should be close to 100%. I haven't been
there to try it, though. :-)

Another interesting and somewhat related OT subject would be tidal forces
and Roche's Limit, particularly near massive bodies. That was what broke
up comet Shumaker-Levy some months prior to impact. :-)
```

That is what I am saying. It takes only minute movement to begin with to

can be just barely enough to start it, some of the time, enough to show a
statistical bias toward CW/CCW rotation. But not enough to ensure it.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Hemlines

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T17:49:28+00:00

- **References:** `<2grohjARG9v$Ewp$@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <bq16tb$4fj$1@panix1.panix.com> <p5o9sv0p8shhrt1j7nr0hmk3bfk4o8e2br@4ax.com> <3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <bq5827$j46$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fc665be_8@news.athenanews.com> <asghsvkrnfj2pp2aiknag69fqkqui9aqtk@4ax.com> <3fc9464c_8@news.athenanews.com> <ybUyb.35803$Cv6.21802@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3fcc7a98_1@news.athenanews.com> <20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com> <s1K+07A4a40$EwiS@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <217e491a.0312071942.2436c996@posting.google.com>`

```
In message <217e491a.0312071942.2436c996@posting.google.com>, Richard 
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> writes
>When air or water moves north or south it will tend to lag or lead
>slightly based on those figures.  The result is that for a statically
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>in radius.

Cheers for the explanation, however, despite a degree in Marine Biology 
I find the detail excessive. One tiny point. It isn't the sink that is 
affected by the force but the water within the sink. In moving 30cm, the 
water is going to be affected by the force but sink design will be of 
greater importance. If you are PD then the angle of swivel is of greater 
importance.

However, if you use the Pacific Ocean, or any other as your sink, then 
Coriolli's force will apply to moving bodies of water, forming gyres, 
which obey the right in the northern hemisphere rule (left in the 
southern). I can think of no example where sink design, on such a large 
scale, affects the direction of rotation. However, I expect that people 
could provide examples of whirlpools which defy Coriolli's.
```

- **References:** `<2grohjARG9v$Ewp$@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <bq16tb$4fj$1@panix1.panix.com> <p5o9sv0p8shhrt1j7nr0hmk3bfk4o8e2br@4ax.com> <3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <bq5827$j46$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fc665be_8@news.athenanews.com> <asghsvkrnfj2pp2aiknag69fqkqui9aqtk@4ax.com> <3fc9464c_8@news.athenanews.com> <ybUyb.35803$Cv6.21802@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3fcc7a98_1@news.athenanews.com> <20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com>`

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031207094540.525$AQ@news.newsreader.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> all that? :-)

Yes, they do.

However, there are so many factors that can affect it (irregularities in the
surface of the sink, asymetry in the design, etc.) that it is not always
consistent. I believe that under laboratory conditions it is clearly
demonstrable and Coriolus DOES behave as expected.

The most "interesting" effect I have personally observed between Hemispheres
is watching the great constellation of Orion slowly stand on its head.

From my home in Tauranga it has a particular configuration. (You can argue
whether this is "up" or "down" depending on your point of view and
upbringing. I have been into Astronomy since the age of 6 when my father
first showed me the craters on the moon and the dancing moons of Jupiter. I
became "used" to seeing Orion a certain way and his dog, Sirius following
him.).

Go to Singapore (which is almost on the Equator) and the giant huntsman
appears to be laying down.

Arrive in London and now he is standing on his head with the dog running
ahead of him...<G>

I was quite amazed by this. (Yes, you can understand something
"intellectually", but when you experience it at first hand, it often has an
unexpected reality that is exciting.)

Finally, here's a piece I wrote 6 years ago about the Coriolus effect, which
is the major cause of turbulence when crossing the Equator by air.
===============================================================
The Hutchinson encyclopaedia describes it thus....

Coriolis effect

The effect of the Earth's rotation on the atmosphere and on all
objects on the Earth's surface. In the northern hemisphere it
causes moving objects and currents to be deflected to the right;
in the southern hemisphere it causes deflection to the left. The
effect is named after its discoverer, French mathematician
Gaspard Coriolis (1792-1843).

(c) Copyright Helicon Publishing Ltd 1996

Of course, at the Equator it plays merry Hell with the atmosphere, the
Southern air masses
being deflected against the Northern air masses and the result being huge
lumps of
displaced air, running round the Equator sulking because they don't know
which way to
turn... If you happen to be in an aircraft, the lack of air stability causes
turbulence. I have seen
an extreme example of this when a Jumbo jet on which I was travelling
literally dropped out
of the sky. It was probably only a couple of hundred feet (in the context of
36,000 feet this
is negligible) but it seemed like an eternity, with people screaming (not
just the rookies...)
luggage falling out of the lockers, and people in the aisles falling over
everybody else.

When they tell you that "seasoned travellers" always keep their seat belts
loosely fastened
while sitting down, believe it.

After an experience like that, it really gives you complete faith in the
engineers at Boeing and
the usual vibrations which rattle your coffee cups and shake the plane a bit
are no cause for
alarm.
=================================================================

Pete.
```

## Thread: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: to check for existence of dataset from within a cobol program on mainframe

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T20:40:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD38FFC.C9DF0D5C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<fd383985.0312030824.64fade70@posting.google.com> <Kirzb.8619$aw2.3949715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <aorzb.26610$sb4.25674@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3FCE69FE.DA822227@shaw.ca> <20031204112836.323$Q5@news.newsreader.com> <3FCF6DD0.EE57A5B8@shaw.ca> <20031206202141.600$bs@news.newsreader.com> <0vf6tv4nk7a06qi0ick9gb0a4g12oem1gl@4ax.com>`

```


Volker Bandke wrote:

> On Sat, 6 Dec 2003 19:20:41 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
> <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 48K of RAM ....

Question for Volker at the end of this.

I'm not sure of sequencing, but one of the old timers can probably put it into
correct timeline  - I think Richard is probably wised up on this.

Before the PC took off, all that there was here in Calgary in the late 70's was one
small hobby shop in a back street. Enthusiasts used to go in and buy bits and
pieces to assemble their own computers. I think the Altair was the next thing to
happen.

Late 70's again,  Datapoint came out with their computer series - but this wasn't a
Mickey Mouse approach. A PC unit (computer, screen and disks - old style platters).
Metal casing and a heavy bugger to lift. I worked on their 32K Model using their
own language DATABUS - something like half way between Assembler and COBOL and
integrated with their own Operating System, which allowed you to do some neat
tricks. (At the time their support guy here in Calgary, who was a classical music
buff - could go to concerts and then phone the office to trigger the machine to get
software updates automatically from back East). They also had a 64K (which had a
COBOL - don't know who wrote it). I subsequently upgraded my application to their
Model ??? which used a true hard disk unit.

Radio Shack (aka Tandy), introduced their Model I - from his description, seems to
me like Volker had a souped up version. Out of sequence they then introduced the
Model III, (32K) one unit (screen, computer and *diskettes* - I think). Then they
introduced the Model II, (64K) again one unit - but with platters - on which I used
RM/COBOL (ANSI 74). As DD is always saying, 'my memory is porous' - one platter
(disk) size around 8 or 10 inches held 0.25 Million bytes. I was into it big time,
I had an auxiliary disk unit which took three more platters. These machines were
all, I think, what I refer to as battleship grey.

The Shack also around this time introduced their Color computer. Had a very loyal
following which hung on, way after the machine went out of fashion. Meanwhile I got
involved with a small fertilizer company that wanted to go multi-user - four
clerks.
I switched to their Model 16 (can't remember memory) but it was a nice White and
the OS was MS Xenix - their version of Unix. Now I'm using the RM/COBOL multi user
version. The pig was I had to develop programs at home on my Model II, then at
weekends when the office was empty, transfer from my Model II platters to the Model
16 platters and re-compile and test on the Model 16. The 16's platters, to the best
of my memory, were 1 MILLION bytes - Wow !

I think somewhere in all this the first PCs were becoming available. But at the
same time there was the Commodore 64 (coloured I think), smartly followed by the
Amiga and very popular. There is still an Amiga Club going strong in Calgary.

Oops. Don't forget the Apple series which had an extremely loyal following, dented
somewhat by the introduction of the "PC".

All the above becomes like Arthurian history with the arrival of the PCs - and we
know where it went from there.

Question for Volker - your COBOL on the Model I - who wrote the compiler ?

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-c0c42d215d"></a>
## Thread: How does Cobol handle garbage collection? — 15 msgs

### How does Cobol handle garbage collection?

- **From:** barackes@aol.com (student)
- **Date:** 2003-12-07T18:26:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<525e8815.0312071826.137018e8@posting.google.com>`

```
I am teaching myself Cobol for a project and have a few questions that
I have had trouble finding answers to in the books I have used. thanks
to all;

Did Cobol have any form of encapsulation before it had the object
oriented feature, if so what was it?

Was security measures are available by the Cobol language?

Does Cobol only use pass-by-value ?

Is Cobol now considered a cross between an imperative and
object-oriented language?

How is Cobol scoped and typed? 

thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: How does Cobol handle garbage collection?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T04:23:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031207232306.03883.00000414@mb-m07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<525e8815.0312071826.137018e8@posting.google.com>`

```
barackes writes ...

>I am teaching myself Cobol for a project and have a few questions that
>I have had trouble finding answers to in the books I have used. thanks
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not as you would recognize it.

>Was security measures are available by the Cobol language?

None in the language. It relies on the operating system. We focus on the
application here, not on doing the work of the operating system.

Which also relates to the subject of your post, which is not included in the
body of your post.

>
>Does Cobol only use pass-by-value ?

Not at all. Originally only passing by reference, then added pass by content;
only recently adding pass by value.
A much richer collection of choices than those mere C++ and Java languages.

>
>Is Cobol now considered a cross between an imperative and
>object-oriented language?

COBOL is considered to be essentially a procedural language by most
practitioners.

>
>How is Cobol scoped and typed? 

Hah! why do you think such terms even have meaning in the COBOL world-view?
COBOL is free of the bondage of scoping and typing.


>
>thank you
>

You're welcome.

Kind regards,


-Steve Comstock

Denver, CO 80224
USA

? - I infer that to mean "yes".  How so - I mean at a language level ?

> >Does Cobol only use pass-by-value ?

content;
> only recently adding pass by value.

? - How "much" richer?  You can achieve reference/value in C++ ....the

see this making it "richer".

world-view?
> COBOL is free of the bondage of scoping and typing.
? - It's not typed, but it is statically scoped.

JCE

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How does Cobol handle garbage collection?

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T06:36:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vt8s2kq5q76d96@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<20031207232306.03883.00000414@mb-m07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<525e8815.0312071826.137018e8@posting.google.com> <20031207232306.03883.00000414@mb-m07.aol.com>`

```
S Comstock wrote:
>>Does Cobol only use pass-by-value ?
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> A much richer collection of choices than those mere C++ and Java languages.

What's the difference between passing by content and passing by value? 
Aren't those the same thing?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How does Cobol handle garbage collection?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T13:55:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031208085529.26331.00000389@mb-m15.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<vt8s2kq5q76d96@corp.supernews.com>`

```
 LX-i lxi0007 writes ...

>What's the difference between passing by content and passing by value? 
>Aren't those the same thing?
>

Essentially COBOL passes arguments in a list in one of three ways:

1. By reference; the list contains the address of the actual data item
2. By content; the list contains the address of a copy of the data item
3. By value; the list contains a copy of the data item

From the caller's perspective,

* by content allows one to include literals in the argument list of the call

* by content allows the passed item to be "read only" in the sense that
       any changes made to the argument in the called subroutine
       are not reflected back to the caller

* by value is there to support COBOL/C interoperability better; not all
  data items can be passed by value; you can pass and receive
  arguments by value between two COBOL programs, though as
  one poster noted, by value doesn't really enhance COBOL to
  COBOL communication in any signficant way that I can see



From the subroutine's perspective,

* by content and by reference appear to be the same: an
  address of the data item; the subroutine cannot distinguish
  between these two

* an argument's being passed by value needs to be known in
  advance and the using phrase on the procedure division 
  header needs to include a "by value" modifier

Hope this helps.

Kind regards,


-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

No one answered the question in the title - that is how does COBOL
handle garbage collection.  The simple answer - from the 2002 Standard
is - It handles it.

It's all implementor defined.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How does Cobol handle garbage collection?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T08:48:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031212094938.564$gq@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<525e8815.0312071826.137018e8@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0312111622.3f4a5058@posting.google.com> <LJqdnaoqad5OjUSi4p2dnA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> > No one answered the question in the title - that is how does COBOL
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> What garbage?


Temporarily allocated memory, such as intermediate work areas that
may be used for COMPUTE, STRING, UNSTRING, OCCURS ...
DEPENDING ON, CALLs to functions and procedures (e.g. pass
by content), etc. This is a serious issue in languages like BASIC and
FORTRAN that permit implicit variable storage allocation on the fly,
but much less of one in COBOL, since virtually all working fields are
not only explicitly defined, but most are mapped as well (i.e. Records).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How does Cobol handle garbage collection?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-12-15T09:57:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0312150957.281b8d79@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<525e8815.0312071826.137018e8@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0312111622.3f4a5058@posting.google.com> <LJqdnaoqad5OjUSi4p2dnA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<LJqdnaoqad5OjUSi4p2dnA@giganews.com>...
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> > No one answered the question in the title - that is how does COBOL
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> What garbage?

I'm assuming this is a serious question.  Since you use PowerCOBOL it
certainly matters to you.  Garbage is objects and resources that "can
no longer participate in the continued execution of the run unit". 
These are basically objects that are no longer reachable.  Fujitsu
COBOL does have garbage collection - but I have no clue how it's
implemented.
```

### Re: LOTR

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T06:16:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031208071726.484$9G@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3fcc8929_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031207085139.545$gc@news.newsreader.com> <e9s6tvsrf3pbt33092hmmj17llgkc3m0aa@4ax.com>`

"Volker Bandke" <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> found out that this sequence was cut from the final version.

Thanks, Volker, that's my answer. The only significant part Saruman
has in Return of the King is in the Shire at the end. Well, since they
'cut' his scenes, it means they filmed them and they should be included
in the extended edition DVD. :-)  The extended editions are not 'patch
ups', but what the movies would have been to start with, theater time
restrictions permitting.
```

#### ↳ Re: LOTR [Possible spoiler alert]

- **From:** rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T12:45:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DbM1/oHpvWlZ092yn@visi.com>`
- **References:** `<3fcc8929_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031207085139.545$gc@news.newsreader.com> <e9s6tvsrf3pbt33092hmmj17llgkc3m0aa@4ax.com> <20031208071726.484$9G@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Here in comp.lang.cobol,
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> spake unto us, saying:

>"Volker Bandke" <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the extended edition DVD. :-)

Some clarification is in order, methinks.  :-)

If you read sites like theonering.net at all, you would be aware that
the "scourge of the shire" is something that Peter Jackson apparently
had no interest in filming.  From all indications, the scene was never
shot, and thus will not appear on the DVD in either format.

The scene that was hoped for by many people was the Two Towers scene
entitled "The Voice of Saruman" at the tower of Orthanc where Gandalf
confronts and then reprimands Saruman and breaks his staff -- that
scene was dropped from ROTK (or never shot) as well, apparently.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: LOTR [Possible spoiler alert]

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T13:36:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031208143712.902$EH@news.newsreader.com>`

```
"Richard Steiner" <rsteiner@visi.com> wrote:
>
> If you read sites like theonering.net at all, you would be aware that
> the "scourge of the shire" is something that Peter Jackson apparently
> had no interest in filming.  From all indications, the scene was never
> shot, and thus will not appear on the DVD in either format.

Ouch! Just me perhaps, but that was one of my favorite parts of the book,
showing both how the Hobbits in the Shire had suffered too, and how all
the Hobbits had grown in maturity and strength through their trials. It also
explained how Shire 'pipeweed' got to Isengard, and marked 'paid' on
Saruman's and Wormtongue's accounts.

> The scene that was hoped for by many people was the Two Towers scene
> entitled "The Voice of Saruman" at the tower of Orthanc where Gandalf
> confronts and then reprimands Saruman and breaks his staff -- that
> scene was dropped from ROTK (or never shot) as well, apparently.

Ouch again!

Thanks, Richard, even if it is bad news. :-)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: LOTR [Possible spoiler alert]

- **From:** rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner)
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T21:03:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rtT1/oHpvSKU092yn@visi.com>`
- **References:** `<3fcc8929_4@news.athenanews.com> <20031207085139.545$gc@news.newsreader.com> <e9s6tvsrf3pbt33092hmmj17llgkc3m0aa@4ax.com> <20031208071726.484$9G@news.newsreader.com> <DbM1/oHpvWlZ092yn@visi.com> <20031208143712.902$EH@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Here in comp.lang.cobol,
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> spake unto us, saying:

>"Richard Steiner" <rsteiner@visi.com> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>the Hobbits had grown in maturity and strength through their trials.

I agree.  I can somewhat understand where P.J. is coming from, though,
since he is approaching LOTR more as an adventure epic, and since he
also has a finite amount of screen time to play with in each movie.

>> The scene that was hoped for by many people was the Two Towers scene
>> entitled "The Voice of Saruman" at the tower of Orthanc where Gandalf
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ouch again!

Yeah.  :-(

>Thanks, Richard, even if it is bad news. :-)

From a purist perspective, I don't like it, but then again I'd prefer
to see LOTR as a 20-hour miniseries, and that might be required to do
a complete/faithful interpretation of the story in video.  :-)

All in all, he's done a better job with the first two movies (especially
the first) than I was expecting, so I guess I shouldn't be surprised at
the changes he is making.

I agree that it's disappointing, though.
```

```
Check the COBOL FAQ - but off the top of my head, you can check out:

 - Kobol
 - Micro Focus (educational version)
 - Fujitsu (either free V3 or educational current versions)
 - RM (I *think* they have a low cost introductory version)
 - AcuCOBOL (same as RM)
 - PerCOBOL
 - IBM VisualAge COBOL (I don't know if they have ANY inexpensive version)
 - CA-Realia (same as IBM)
```

The original message specified use of the compiler under windows XP.I have
Fujitsu version 4 as well as numerous copies of version 3 from books.I have
just purchased a new XP machine.I was told that version 4 will not run.The
documentation for Kobol is terrible and I cannot find any help on Google,but
I bought it because it's very cheap and will only let you use the trial
version for a very short time.
I am working my way thru a blindfold learning curve.It works, and is , I
think, simpler to use (with the IDE) than the Fujitsu compiler.I don't know
about telnet, and there is certainly no GUI.I think however that the Cobol
is pretty much '85 plus functions and enhanced accept/display.

John Sampson

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

> > I was told that version
> > 4 will not run.
> 
> Huh? Who told you that and under what conditions would FJ V4 not run?

From Fujitsu web site:

"""Windows 2000 and Windows XP Application Compatibility Tips for
Version 3 and 4

If you are using Windows 2000 and Windows XP and have problems making
Version 3 or 4 work, we suggest you check out the following link. We
can't guarantee that they will work for you, but it should give you
the best chance of making things work.

Go to http://msdn.microsoft.com/compatibility/ where you should be
able to find the "Windows Application Compatibility Toolkit".

Download the Toolkit and go from there. The file at time of writing is
named act.exe. After installing it, it will likely enable you to run
several
programs that may not otherwise be compatible with Windows 2000 and
Windows XP.

If this does not work, you will have to purchase Version 6.1. If you
are a
student, there are academic licenses available."""

Note that even with the MS patch it may still not work - according to
Fujitsu.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Inexpensive COBOL Compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "John & Marie Sampson" <jamsam@it.net.au>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T15:49:10+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br3upm$2hj$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au>`
- **References:** `<58322937.0312081011.e298a3e@posting.google.com> <Vk3Bb.4134$_r6.795@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <br2ug8$hd9$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <UsidnatABJDM1UiiRVn-uw@giganews.com>`

```
 I got the following from the Fujitsu site:

Support for Previous Versions
In general, it is not Fujitsu Software Corporation's policy to support
previously released versions of our software. If you are using Version 5 or
6 of Fujitsu COBOL (NetCOBOL for Windows) and are still have a current
maintenance agreement, we recommend that you take advantage of the free
upgrades and upgrade to Version 7. As our run-time system supports code
compiled with these earlier releases, upgrading does not force you to
recompile all your applications. If you are still current on maintenance you
are still able to use our support systems as described on our main support
Web page.

If you are not on maintenance and need support for these versions (something
we expect is unlikely as you've probably sorted out your questions by now)
then your options are either to purchase a maintenance agreement or use some
of the other resources listed on our main support page.


Support for Versions 3 and 4
Fujitsu COBOL versions 3 and 4 are included in many COBOL books and Version
3 is available for students to download for FREE. It is provided primarily
for educational use. We do not provide support for these products. The
original support pages, however, are still available linked from the
navigation bar at the top left of this page.

Fujitsu COBOL V3, V4 and Windows 2000 / Windows XP
Fujitsu COBOL V3 and V4 will not install on the Microsoft Windows 2000 and
Windows XP operating systems. If you attempt to do this you will receive the
following message:

"NT Service Pack 3 must be installed before Fujitsu can be installed"

Your best course of action is to read the Windows 2000 and Windows XP
Application Compatibility Tips which should provide a solution.

The reference is to a MS site which contains a 23mb download and again no
guarantees and no indication of what collateral damage might occur.During my
journey to the site I collected a worm.



John Sampson

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:UsidnatABJDM1UiiRVn-uw@giganews.com...
> John & Marie Sampson wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Inexpensive COBOL Compiler

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T07:19:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0312090719.2d5eda2a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<58322937.0312081011.e298a3e@posting.google.com> <Vk3Bb.4134$_r6.795@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <br2ug8$hd9$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <UsidnatABJDM1UiiRVn-uw@giganews.com> <br3upm$2hj$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au>`

```
Interesting --- John, there is a typo on the Fujitsu page - or an
error.

I know v3 will install on XP.  There were some inssues with 2000 as
well, but it does install if you first install the "Application
compatibility toolkit" from MS.

For XP and v3 - set 98 compatibility for the install program and
things seem to work just fine.

Since v3 is included in my Book - Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24

## Thread: Only 24-hours to go (to vote on COBOL & LE requirements) — 1 msgs

### Only 24-hours to go (to vote on COBOL & LE requirements)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-08T22:30:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,ibm.software.cobol,ibm.software.pli
- **Message-ID:** `<KX6Bb.4373$_r6.750@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
For SHARE (IBM mainframe user group) members:

As previously indicated, I plan on "closing" the voting on the currently "open"
COBOL & LE (so far no PL/I) requirements at the "end of business" tomorrow,
Tues, Dec 9.

If ANYONE who receives this email (or sees it in a newsgroup) still plans on
voting, but isn't certain that they can get their votes in by that time, please
let me know (with an estimate on when you WILL get your votes in).

So far, we have 6 to 12 votes per requirement which is better than some products
get, but certainly NOT as "representative" as I would have hoped for.

FYI - the following is the current "order" (of priorities - from most desirable
to least) that the requirements are in (although this may change in the next 24
hours):

    SSLNGC0313583 Increase Individual Item and Table Compiler Limits
    SSLNGC0313594 2002 ISO COBOL - Enhanced INITIALIZE (To VALUE & FILLER)
    SSLNGC0313598 2002 ISO COBOL - Table SORT
    SSLNGC0313593 2002 ISO COBOL - SET TO FALSE and WHEN SET TO FALSE
    SSLNGC0313597 2002 ISO COBOL - STOP With STATUS
    SSLNGC0313580 Port CEE5DLY functionality to LE for z/OS and for z/VM
    SSLNGC0313599 2002 ISO COBOL - TEST-xxxx Intrinsic Functions
    SSLNGC03004 COBOL compiler option to generate CEEUOPT run-time options
    SSLNGC03003 Compiler option to make BLOCK CONTAINS clause SMS sensitive
    SSLNGC03001 Provide COBOL compiler options in CEEnDUMP
    SSLNGC0313578 Implement the new 2002 COBOL standard in a phased manner
    SSLNGC0313586 Enhance CEE3PRM Callable Service
    SSLNGC0313579 IBM support effort to provide Native COBOL XML support
    SSLNGC0313596 2002 ISO COBOL - READ PREVIOUS & START LESS THAN
    SSLNGC03002 Allow underscore in COBOL User-Defined Words
    SSLNGC0313588 2002 ISO COBOL - 1-byte and Other "True" Binary Enhancements
    SSLNGC0313590 2002 ISO COBOL - COPY & REPLACE Partial Word Replacement
    SSLNGC0313587 New LE Callable Service to get (various) Program Names
    SSLNGC0313592 CeeDump Enhancement
    SSLNGC0313585 2002 ISO COBOL - EXIT and PERFORM Enhancements
    SSLNGC0313582 Assembler DSECTS to COBOL utility
    SSLNGC0313589 2002 ISO COBOL - CALL prototypes, Compile-time Arg Checking
    SSLNGC0313591 2002 ISO COBOL - Compiler Directives
    SSLNGC0313595 2002 ISO COBOL - Expand use of Numeric & Integer Functions
    SSLNGC0313600 2002 ISO COBOL - Floating Indicators

***

Remember - your installation's vote CAN make a difference in where IBM places
its resources.
```

#### ↳ Re: Abt cobol data types

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T19:16:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br571n$1sa$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1c6174f7.0312090252.36833868@posting.google.com>`

```

On  9-Dec-2003, vadiraj_br@yahoo.com (vadi) wrote:

> Can anybody give me list of data types in cobol and their internal
> representation in MF-COBOL and ANSI cobol.

Someone (not me) can give the internal representation in MF-COBOL (as long as
this is on only one platform), but not for ANSI COBOL.

The reason is, that internal representation is up to the implementer - it isn't
part of the COBOL standard.   So what someone sees on an IBM mainframe, Univac
mainframe, PC, or MacIntosh can be completely different.
```

#### ↳ Re: Abt cobol data types

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T08:10:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031211091148.505$y2@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<1c6174f7.0312090252.36833868@posting.google.com>`

```
"vadi" <vadiraj_br@yahoo.com> wrote:
>
> Can anybody give me list of data types in cobol and their internal
> representation in MF-COBOL and ANSI cobol.

Homework, eh? :-)

Off the top of my head, there are these types:

Alphanumeric                    PIC X
Alphanumeric Edited         PIC XBX
Alphabetic                         PIC A
Numeric                             PIC S99v99
Numeric Edited                  PIC $$$.99-
Index                                  OCCURS ...  INDEXED BY ...
Index Data Name               USAGE IS INDEX

Numeric fields can also be USAGE DISPLAY (decimal, full byte per
digit) or USAGE COMPUTATIONAL[-?] which is implementation
dependent, but usually includes binary and packed BCD. Numeric
fields may contain up to 18 digits plus sign. COBOL is probably more
flexible in directly supported data types than almost any language.
Micro Focus COBOL even supports C type null delimited strings.
Some versions of COBOL support procedure and/or data pointers.
Standard COBOL also supports various literal types, and most
compilers support others, such as hex or binary.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Abt cobol data types

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-11T08:19:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bra5dm$1kfk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1c6174f7.0312090252.36833868@posting.google.com> <20031211091148.505$y2@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Both USAGE BINARY and USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL are specified by ANSI X3.23-1985.
The format of each is implementation dependent, as is that of COMPUTATIONAL.

However, that standard does require of PACKED-DECIMAL that "a radix of 10 is
used to represent a numeric item in the storage of the computer.
Furthermore, this clause specifies that each digit position must occupy the
minimum possible configuration in computer storage ...".  Thus, it doesn't
say in so many words that a packed-decimal digit *must* occupy exactly four
bits, but three bits is too few and five bits is more than the minimum
required ...

    -Chuck Stevens

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031211091148.505$y2@news.newsreader.com...
> "vadi" <vadiraj_br@yahoo.com> wrote:
> >
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-54eeab3958"></a>
## Thread: Client Server with MF? — 1 msgs

### Client Server with MF?

- **From:** "PeterSamson NewsGroups" <pesa@xs4all.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-12-09T23:15:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fd64a89$0$125$e4fe514c@dreader13.news.xs4all.nl>`

```
Old state---
MF Cobol RTS32 running in POSIX environment with programs build with
'callable' I-O modules, 'callable' businessfunctions and 'callable'
frequently used general functions. Data Management with Informix SE
Structures via middleware communication between RTS and ISAM environment.
New state---
MF Cobol RTS32 running in POSIX environment with programs build with
'callable' I-O modules, 'callable' businessfunctions and 'callable'
frequently used general functions. Data Management on a separate database
server (Oracle) via middleware communication(TCP) between RTS and Oracle
server.

Problem description.

Middleware communicates via TCP the FileControlData as well as buffer
content for each proces-id from the client towards the database server and
vice versa.

114     Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11)
Occurs in the following situation:

Main program doesn't do anything with a needed table. The OPEN of this table
is done by a called I-O program (io_tablename.int).
Traces show a succesfull operation.
A business function is called from the main program that calls in turn the
forementioned I-O program with specific functions like START / READ NEXT.

As soon as a buffer is retransmitted (READ or READ NEXT) from this, as a
third level, called I-O module to RTS the above Error occurs.
When done the same I-O action directly from the main program, as a second
level, everything functions as planned.

Summary:
1. All programs, main and the called ones, have the same proces-id
2. The I-O modules work with tables(Files) NOT declared in the main program,
but only declared in the called I-O programs.
3. When calling a I-O module with buffer data exchange from main no problem
arise. When calling from a called business function RTS complains with the
forementioned error message and signal preceded by the program name of the
main program that isn't active at that moment!
4. Called programs aren't CANCELled.
5. RTS seems to interpret FCD information from a, in a second level, called
program to connect to the main program and obvious these address information
is in a total different part of memory and so beyond bounds of MAIN program
memory.
6. Working in a non Client/Server structure didn't result in errors.

Is there anyone that have some thoughts, ideas or theories that might
explain the different results when doing ALMOST? the same within the one
environment?

Kind regards,

Peter Samson
```

<a id="t-9eb13fc9d8"></a>
## Thread: pass tables of data in Cobol to any other format — 4 msgs

### pass tables of data in Cobol to any other format

- **From:** "Elite" <alvaro.herbecon@terra.es>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T11:27:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AqDBb.1792962$uj6.4327531@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
Hello!
 It would please me to know as I can pass tables of data in Cobol to any
other format (DBase, Paradox, Acces, etc..)
  I thank for your aid.
 A greeting.
```

#### ↳ Re: pass tables of data in Cobol to any other format

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T12:46:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HAEBb.10692$aw2.5424475@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<AqDBb.1792962$uj6.4327531@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
"Elite" <alvaro.herbecon@terra.es> wrote in message
news:AqDBb.1792962$uj6.4327531@telenews.teleline.es...
> Hello!
>  It would please me to know as I can pass tables of data in Cobol to any
> other format (DBase, Paradox, Acces, etc..)
>   I thank for your aid.

If you are talking specifically about dBase, Paradox(r), Access(r) and other
RDBMS products, you'd probably want to look at using embedded SQL. Consult
your compiler manual for implementation details for your brand and version
of the compiler.

If you are speaking generically, the COBOL CALL verb can pass the address of
the start of a COBOL table, which surely can be interpreted by the CALLed
program or module. Details and mechanics vary by operating system and in
some cases by compiler.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: pass tables of data in Cobol to any other format

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T19:35:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br7shi$2fg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<AqDBb.1792962$uj6.4327531@telenews.teleline.es>`

```

On 10-Dec-2003, "Elite" <alvaro.herbecon@terra.es> wrote:

> Hello!
>  It would please me to know as I can pass tables of data in Cobol to any
> other format (DBase, Paradox, Acces, etc..)
>   I thank for your aid.
>  A greeting.

CoBOL doesn't have a native format.   To pass data so that some other program
can access it, what you need to do is learn exactly what your other program
needs.

The easiest way to move data so that a spreadsheet can read it is to write it
out so that the spreadsheet can import it.   Sometimes this will be in tab
delimited files with numbers the way you see it in the spreadsheet.    I have
written programs that create files with tab delimited fields, making sure to
write all numbers with signs.

The following is the beginning of a record format I used on my EBCDIC mainframe
to produce a file that was FTP'd to a PC and imported into an Excel spreadsheet:

 01  SPREAD-REC.
     05  FILLER              PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.
     05  SPREAD-BUCKET       PIC X(06)   VALUE 'BUCKET'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.

     05  FILLER-DELIMITER    PIC X(01)   VALUE X'05'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.
     05  SPREAD-ID           PIC X(09)   VALUE 'ID'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.

     05  FILLER-DELIMITER    PIC X(01)   VALUE X'05'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.
     05  SPREAD-NAME         PIC X(32)   VALUE 'NAME'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.

     05  FILLER-DELIMITER    PIC X(01)   VALUE X'05'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.
     05  SPREAD-EXT          PIC X(03)   VALUE 'EXT'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.


     05  FILLER-DELIMITER    PIC X(01)   VALUE X'05'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.
     05  FILLER-TRANS-AMT                VALUE 'TRANS-AMT'.
         10  SPREAD-TRANS-AMT PIC -(7).99.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.

     05  FILLER-DELIMITER    PIC X(01)   VALUE X'05'.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.
     05  FILLER-PAID-AMT                 VALUE 'PAID-AMT'.
         10  SPREAD-PAID-AMT PIC -(7).99.
     05  FILLER-QUOTES       PIC X(01)   VALUE '"'.


Some people will recommend SIGN SEPARATE, but this worked for me.    Binary and
packed do not work.
```

#### ↳ Re: pass tables of data in Cobol to any other format

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T19:23:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EAoCb.1693$NNW1.45@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<AqDBb.1792962$uj6.4327531@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
There are two ways:
    programmatically, using SQL
    No programming, dump your table out as text file delimited and import it
into DB

<a id="t-3068ee7a8a"></a>
## Thread: Level Number 88 vs. Piano KeyBoard? — 1 msgs

### Level Number 88 vs. Piano KeyBoard?

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T15:29:14+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br770v$lcq$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
2003-12-10 14:28:56 MET

The other day we noticed that a Standard Piano
KeyBoard has 88 Keys - and there is the
Level-Number 88 for conditions. Coincidence?

After all, one could have chosen the present
Level Numbers 66, 77 and 88 into the range
of 97 .. 99, and extend the legal data
Levels from 49 to 96.

Joke aside - if I were to encounter a
data-structure with all 49 Level numbers in
use, I would refuse even to attempt to
understand it.

Herwig
```

## Thread: cobol array editing output — 11 msgs

### Re: cobol array editing output

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T14:00:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br852g$5id$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

I mistyped.  To Judson McClendon's .

> > However,the compiler must generate code to convert between
> > subscript and offset when a SET or compare is done between integer and
> > index data items.

I responded

> I think you mean integers and index-names in standard-speak.  ...
> The results of comparing a number ... against an index-name
> are undefined.

That should have been

> I think you mean integers and index-names in standard-speak. ...
> The results of comparing a number ... against an index data item
> are undefined.

Index data items -- i.e., items declared USAGE IS INDEX -- are of limited
utility, IMHO.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```

> Index data items -- i.e., items declared USAGE IS INDEX -- are of limited
> utility, IMHO.

Yes, and even dangerous. Much better to declare additional indexes to
the same table.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T08:56:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brcs0c$id8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<br852g$5id$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd8f0a8_8@news.athenanews.com> <brav27$27td$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd99ea9_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3fd99ea9_1@news.athenanews.com...
>
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'd probably be inclined to SET a numeric data item to the index-name in the
caller, and pass that to the called subprogram, where I SET the local
index-name to that numeric data item.  .

Since range checks have to be performed on the SET that would occur in the
called subprogram anyway, and presuming that the implementor has provided a
USAGE that corresponds in format to that which would be used for an index,
it seems to me unlikely that the SET of an index-name to an index data item
would differ *significantly* in performance from the SET of an index-name to
a numeric data item.   I see maybe a couple of  math ops difference.  Yes,
there is a difference, but I'm not convinced that difference is likely to be
significant compared to everything else the called program would be expected
to do.  And if all the called subprogram does is a single reference to the
table (or anything else trivial enough to make the couple of math ops
measurable, to say nothing of significant), getting rid of the called
subprogram altogether and doing the work "in situ" would improve performance
even more.

When an index-name is set to the contents of an index data item, and both
the table and the index data item are passed in, there's a presumption that
the index data item *was* properly set to the table to begin with.  No
conversion takes place in this case.  I'm not sure I'm comfortable with that
presumption and its consequences, particularly when the user has turned off
range checking on indices.  I'd prefer to pass in a *subscript* and cause
both the conversion and the range check to occur during the SET that the
called subprogram executes.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

the

a

item
> would differ *significantly* in performance from the SET of an index-name
to
> a numeric data item.   I see maybe a couple of  math ops difference.  Yes,
> there is a difference, but I'm not convinced that difference is likely to
be
> significant compared to everything else the called program would be
expected
> to do.  And if all the called subprogram does is a single reference to the

> measurable, to say nothing of significant), getting rid of the called

performance
> even more.

> When an index-name is set to the contents of an index data item, and both
> the table and the index data item are passed in, there's a presumption
that

> conversion takes place in this case.  I'm not sure I'm comfortable with
that
> presumption and its consequences, particularly when the user has turned
off
> range checking on indices.  I'd prefer to pass in a *subscript* and cause
> both the conversion and the range check to occur during the SET that the
> called subprogram executes.
>
Thank you. A very revealing response.

Not being an adherent of the paranoid programming school, I would (and do)
use an index data item. It is set correctly by the calling program and can
be used with confidence by the called program. That's what they're for.

Pete.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Set numeric value to INDEX was Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-12T19:08:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<broiv2$rh5$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<brcs0c$id8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<br852g$5id$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd8f0a8_8@news.athenanews.com> <brav27$27td$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3fd99ea9_1@news.athenanews.com> <brcs0c$id8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
> news:3fd99ea9_1@news.athenanews.com...
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
The set numeric item to INDEX involves a divide thus would be somewhat
expensive.  Using an USAGE INDEX item means that the set can generate 
simple move or load store combinations rather than using multiply and 
divide.  Of course the penalty on modern systems is far less in the 
overall scheme of things than it was 10 - 15 years ago but it doe add an 
inefficiency in what may be a compute intensive operation depending on 
what the sub-routine is doing (or the caller).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set numeric value to INDEX was Re: cobol array editing output

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T06:10:54-06:00

##### ↳ ↳ Mainframe not a good architecture for interactive was Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-10T22:10:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FD7D224.90906@istar.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<2941363.1054317456@dbforums.com>`
