#### ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T14:44:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312161444.53727fe@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> First question: is this so, that a value set in the WORKING-STORAGE 
> SECTION of a staticaly-called subroutine persists in subsequent calls by 
> the same program?

Yes.
 
> Second question: will A's call to the subroutine demonstrate the 
> persistence of value in C which B has set?

Yes.  There is just one copy of C and it neither knows nor cares what
other programs CALL it, as long as valid linkage is passed.  It can't
tell that this time it is A that is calling it.
 
Of course if B CANCELled C then the effect may be different.

> I think not, based on the behavior I have seen and a fix which allows for 
> the 'self-documenting syntax' of:
> 
> IF FIRST-BATCH AND NOT LAST-REC

If a call is made that had both first-batch and last-rec true then the
file won't be opened and won't be closed.
 
> ... but I'd be interested in hearing what others might have experienced 
> along these lines.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that had been written by being passed from B.)

Why would hitting the OPEN EXTEND wipe out anything ?  If the file was
already open then the OPEN should just give a file status error code,
or did it abend ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T21:54:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<broghm$920$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <217e491a.0312161444.53727fe@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0312161444.53727fe@posting.google.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yes.

This is what my experience shows.

> 
>> Second question: will A's call to the subroutine demonstrate the 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>tell that this time it is A that is calling it.

This is not what my experience shows.

> 
>Of course if B CANCELled C then the effect may be different.

There was no CANCEL in B.

>
>> I think not, based on the behavior I have seen and a fix which allows for 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>file won't be opened and won't be closed.

This does not follow my understanding of the logic-flow of the code I have 
given... since the code was removed from your follow-up I'll re-post the 
relevant part of the PROCEDURE DIVISION:

--begin quoted text:

   0000-WRITE-TA-REJECT-RECORD.
       IF FIRST-BATCH AND NOT LAST-REC
           OPEN EXTEND TA-REJECT-FILE
           MOVE 'O' TO OUTPUT-SW.
       IF LAST-REC
           IF REJECT-FILE-OPEN
               CLOSE TA-REJECT-FILE
               MOVE 'C' TO OUTPUT-SW
           ELSE
               NEXT SENTENCE
       ELSE
           WRITE TAREJECT-RECORD FROM TA-REJECT-RECORD.
   0000-END-OF-WRITE.
       EXIT PROGRAM.

--end quoted text

Now what might not have been clear is that the first statement was *not*, 
originally, IF FIRST-BATCH AND NOT LAST-REC... it was merely IF 
FIRST-BATCH.

FIRST-BATCH is dependent on the WORKING-STORAGE field OUTPUT-SW.  
LAST-REC is dependent on a LINKAGE SECTION field.

> 
>> ... but I'd be interested in hearing what others might have experienced 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>or did it abend ?

It did not abend, it did not give a file status error code (as no file 
status was declared)... it just wiped out everything in the output file.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T09:54:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312170954.767066a1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <217e491a.0312161444.53727fe@posting.google.com> <broghm$920$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> >Yes.  There is just one copy of C and it neither knows nor cares what
> >other programs CALL it, as long as valid linkage is passed.  It can't
> >tell that this time it is A that is calling it.
> 
> This is not what my experience shows.

Perhaps, as someone else has suggested, you have two copies of C in
the runtime.  That does not occur in any environment that I care
about, but might in yours.

> >If a call is made that had both first-batch and last-rec true then the
> >file won't be opened and won't be closed.
> 
> This does not follow my understanding of the logic-flow of the code I have 
> given... 

In what way ?   recoding slightly for clarity:
 
>    0000-WRITE-TA-REJECT-RECORD.
>   *    IF FIRST-BATCH AND NOT LAST-REC
         IF First-Batch
             IF Last-Rec
    *            the file is not opened if Last-Rec is true
                 CONTINUE
             ELSE
                 OPEN EXTEND TA-REJECT-FILE
                 MOVE 'O' TO OUTPUT-SW
             END-IF
         END-IF
>        IF LAST-REC
>            IF REJECT-FILE-OPEN
    *            only if file is open will it be closed
>                CLOSE TA-REJECT-FILE
>                MOVE 'C' TO OUTPUT-SW
>            END-IF
>        ELSE
>            WRITE TAREJECT-RECORD FROM TA-REJECT-RECORD
         END-IF
         .
>    0000-END-OF-WRITE.
>        EXIT PROGRAM.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> FIRST-BATCH.

This change stopped it opening and then closing the file if there were
no records to be written.  ie if the first call was from A at the end
of run.

> FIRST-BATCH is dependent on the WORKING-STORAGE field OUTPUT-SW.  
> LAST-REC is dependent on a LINKAGE SECTION field.

Yes, I understood that.  

> >Why would hitting the OPEN EXTEND wipe out anything ?  If the file was
> >already open then the OPEN should just give a file status error code,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> status was declared)... it just wiped out everything in the output file.

OPEN EXTEND should never wipe out a file.  Perhaps it was never
created.  Either because it is not OPTIONAL (I can't recall) or
because there were no CALLs from B with records to write.  You may
note that when the file is CLOSEd it doesn't reset the file-open
indicator back to its original value, this means that it won't be
reOPENed later.

Perhaps there are _other_ programs in the run-unit which CALLed C and
left it in a state where First-Batch was not true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

>created.  Either because it is not OPTIONAL (I can't recall) or
>because there were no CALLs from B with records to write.

Neither of these was the case.

[snip]

>Perhaps there are _other_ programs in the run-unit which CALLed C and
>left it in a state where First-Batch was not true.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T19:10:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vtvb84cdhg310f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> So... the first time B calls C the WORKING-STORAGE field OUTPUT-SW 
> contains SPACES; condition FIRST-BATCH is met so the file is OPENed and an 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the same program?

Yes, unless you say
program-id.  c IS INITIAL
                ^^^^^^^^^^

This tells it to refresh the initial values of working-storage on each 
entry.

> Anyhow... A gets done processing and as part of 9999-EOJ checks another 
> flag; based on this flag A will call C directly (remember, static 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> persistence of value in C which B has set?

On the UNISYS 2200, assuming A, B, and C were statically linked, I would 
say yes.  On IBM, I have no experience.  If you look at your link, you 
may be able to tell if you're linking in 1 copy of C (in which case one 
might assume it would be persistent) or 2 copies (one for each external 
reference, in which case you would see the behavior you're seeing now). 
  In the other case, if B is linked with C (making what I'll call BC), 
and then you link A with BC, you might also see this behavior, as you've 
got the same module linked in two places, and C in A isn't calling the 
same C that B does.

This is one case where my experience with subprograms is an asset, but 
my lack of experience on IBM is a liability...

> (Hint: the error-symptom was that even though C had written output it hit 
> the OPEN EXTEND statement when called from A... thus wiping out everything 
> that had been written by being passed from B.)

Isn't OPEN EXTEND supposed to leave what's there, and extend the file 
from there?  Can you do a second open without doing a close first, and 
not generate an error?  That's interesting...  I'll be watching this 
thread too.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T03:17:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vVPDb.7862$0s2.3672@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <vtvb84cdhg310f@corp.supernews.com> <brognu$9lo$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:brognu$9lo$1@panix1.panix.com...
<snip>
> >
> >Isn't OPEN EXTEND supposed to leave what's there, and extend the file
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Does the file have DISP=MOD?  If not, you might try that.  (Or is it "new" in
the first program - so it has DISP=NEW?)  My "suggestions" would be to use 2
different DDNames (assuming the file isn't EXTERNAL) and allocate it as DISP=NEW
for the DDName of the file that creates it and DISP=MOD for the one that is
intended to "extend" it.

Is this possible with your application design?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T07:46:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brpj73$1fs$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <vtvb84cdhg310f@corp.supernews.com> <brognu$9lo$1@panix1.panix.com> <vVPDb.7862$0s2.3672@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
In article <vVPDb.7862$0s2.3672@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:brognu$9lo$1@panix1.panix.com...
><snip>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>the first program - so it has DISP=NEW?)

DISP=(,CATLG,CATLG)

>My "suggestions" would be to use 2
>different DDNames (assuming the file isn't EXTERNAL) and allocate it as DISP=NEW
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is this possible with your application design?

In a word... nope.  The file is addressed *only* by subroutine C... it 
seems that during 9999-EOJ in A a 'belt-and-suspenders' check is made that 
B set a flag used to signal C to close the file; if that flag is not set 
then A sets it and calls C... and C will then OPEN EXTEND and then CLOSE 
the file (which wipes out what B had written previously).

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T07:45:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FE00A58.359FF13C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <vtvb84cdhg310f@corp.supernews.com> <brognu$9lo$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Hey, Doc!

I don't know if this applies to you, but I once saw a nasty bug where
Program A called subprogram B (static) and B called C (static), but A
also called C (static).

Of course, this was back in the days of OS/VS COBOL.  Program B had
one version of subprogram C linked statically to it, and program A got
a DIFFERENT version of subprogram C statically linked.

As I recall, part of our problem was that compiles on MVS always
searched the target loadlib for subprograms to be linked statically,
and all compiles were linked to the loadlib.

Is there any possibility something like that happened to you?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <vtvb84cdhg310f@corp.supernews.com> <brognu$9lo$1@panix1.panix.com> <3FE00A58.359FF13C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <3FE00A58.359FF13C@worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>Hey, Doc!

Good day, Mr Trembley... things go well with you, I trust?

>
>I don't know if this applies to you, but I once saw a nasty bug where
>Program A called subprogram B (static) and B called C (static), but A
>also called C (static).

This seems to mirror my present circumstances, aye.

>
>Of course, this was back in the days of OS/VS COBOL.  Program B had
>one version of subprogram C linked statically to it, and program A got
>a DIFFERENT version of subprogram C statically linked.

I've seen this also... as a Wise Philosopher once said, 'Fooey!'.

>
>As I recall, part of our problem was that compiles on MVS always
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is there any possibility something like that happened to you?

I believe that I've precluded that possibility by diligently and rigidly 
recompiling all three programs with three separate-but-identical (except 
for module names) jobstreams in the order of:

recompile C
recompile B
recompile A

... but the world has, at times, run roughshod on my beliefs.  Just for
laffs I gave each program an unique identifying-literal as the first
statement in the PROCEDURE DIVISION; C had 'DISPLAY 'QZQ1'', B had
'DISPLAY 'QZQ2'' and A had 'DISPLAY 'QZQ3''.  I then recompiled the bunch 
of them and did a browse on the resulting load modules.

(An applications-jockey looking at load modules usually indicates either 
Deep Trouble or Deep Interest.)

What I found clarified... nothing.  In C I found 'QZQ1', in B I found 
'QZQ2' and QZQ1'... and in A I found 'QZQ3' and that was it.

Oh well... at least I am getting the output I expect now.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T03:26:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B1QDb.7874$0s2.2468@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,
  Are you on a "semi-current" compiler yet?  From:

    http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/4.1.3.1

"In the static CALL statement, the COBOL program and all called programs are
part of the same load module. When control is transferred, the called program
already resides in storage, and a branch to it takes place. Subsequent
executions of the CALL statement make the called program available in its
last-used state, unless the called program has the INITIAL attribute. In that
case, the called program and each program directly or indirectly contained
within it are placed into its initial state every time the called program is
called within a run unit.
If you specify alternate entry points, a static CALL statement can use any
alternate entry point to enter the called subprogram."



However, (in case it is relevant) from:

     http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/4.1.3.4

"When a dynamic CALL statement and a static CALL statement to the same
subprogram are issued within one program, a second copy of the subprogram is
loaded into storage. Because this arrangement does not guarantee that the
subprogram will be left in its last-used state, results can be unpredictable."



NOTE to non-IBM people, from

    "2.4.1 Option settings for COBOL 85 Standard conformance"

at

     http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.4.1

the "DYNAM" compiler option *must* be set (in the IBM mainframe compiler) to get
"ANSI/ISO conforming" behavior.  When NODYNAM (required for static linking) is
set, there are KNOWN cases where "initial" and "last-used" state are NOT correct
according to the ANSI/ISO rules.  (They are for DYNAM compiled programs.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T08:10:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brpkke$622$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <B1QDb.7874$0s2.2468@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
In article <B1QDb.7874$0s2.2468@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>Doc,
>  Are you on a "semi-current" compiler yet?

The listing shows 'IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and OS/390 3.2.0'

>  From:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>last-used state, unless the called program has the INITIAL attribute.

Aye, there's the rub.  This is what happens when B calls C, certainly, and 
(as mentioned in another posting) literals coded in the source of B and C 
can be found in the load modules of both B and C.  The load module of A, 
though, shows only the literal coded in A... yargh.

There is no use, however, of INITIAL or ENTRY.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T00:47:37+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fe042b2_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
It looks like A and B each have their own copies of C...

This can happen with static linkage. (It can't with dynamic...)

It's a long time since I worked in your environment and it may be that
things have changed so considerably that some of the considerations we had
no longer apply. I'll have a bash at this anyway, because what I'm saying
might strike a spark, even if it is no longer entirely relevant...

I take it this is all one "run unit" Doc? If both A and B are considered to
be "Main" programs, they may have been LINKed as such, with C being LINKed
as a CALLed Suprogram. Given that they are statically linked, I would then
expect both A and B to contain their own copy of C. In this case it will
behave as you are experiencing.

If A is a "Main" program and the other 2 are "SubPrograms", it should behave
as youwould expect. (As it isn't, this is, hopefully, not true...<G>)

Have another look at HOW and in what order these programs were LINKed. (If
that is still important...<G>)

I think you can fix it by changing:  "IF FIRST-BATCH AND NOT LAST-REC" in C,
to be:  "IF FIRST-BATCH AND NOT LAST-REC AND NOT REJECT FILE OPEN"

Then it won't matter which copy of C is being executed...<G>

Others, notably Dan Summers and Steve Comstock, have covered answers to your
specific questions which I agree with, so I won't repeat what they have
said.

The "odd" behaviour of the OPEN EXTEND *could* be accounted for by the
system considering two "programs" to be connected to different logical
files, which are mapped to the same physical file through JCL. Bill's advice
on DISP=NEW and DISP=MOD is probably very sound...

Good luck, old fruit...<G>

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T10:47:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brptpo$hit$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fe042b2_2@news.athenanews.com>`

```
In article <3fe042b2_2@news.athenanews.com>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>It looks like A and B each have their own copies of C...

As pointed out in the PROBLEM SOLVED posting... they did; the source-code 
for A contained a compiler directive for dynamic linking instead of the 
compile jobstream's static.

[snip]

>Good luck, old fruit...<G>

What's this, an ultimatum?  No... more of an old tomato.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T14:56:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brsf7l$987$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Boy it is much easier when there is no good reason to have any static calls.  
If everything is dynamic, then everything is the same version.

I work at an environment where Endevor compiles all programs before they go into
production - and overrides the parms that are put in the source even.   It does
mean that I can't optimize the sort when told that I can.   But it also means
that we don't accidentally have a static call.

If the shop compiler requires static calls, we are in a bit more danger - not
only because different programs may be compiled with different versions of the
program, but because programmers can force dynamic calls by calling variable
names.

I have seen this variable name calling as shop standards in many locations.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T10:04:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brsfl8$cb2$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<brngki$74c$1@panix1.panix.com> <brsf7l$987$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <brsf7l$987$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>Boy it is much easier when there is no good reason to have any static calls.  
>If everything is dynamic, then everything is the same version.

Well, it turns out that this - 'everything is to be dynamic' - was the 
intention of the shop and folks started putting in the compiler directives 
card in the source code to Just Make Sure that it worked that way...

... but nobody's touched programs B and C in an eon so nobody added the 
card to their source.

DD
```

```
Hi

Wanted to ask if anyone knew anything about this??

I a trying to create a program where I open up an Indexed sequental
file and write to it but when I look in the file it is filled with
junk.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
000290	INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000300	FILE-CONTROL.
000310		SELECT credit-ac ASSIGN TO "Credit-Details.dat"
000311			ORGANIZATION IS Indexed
000312		        ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
000313			RECORD KEY IS credit-ac-no
000314			ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS credit-ac-last-name 
000315			WITH DUPLICATES.	
.
.
.001530		OPEN I-O credit-ac.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Each time I run the program it get's filled with even more junk plus
what I'm writing to it, and after a couple of runs, when I run the
program I get an error.
Has anybody had this problem before? 

Any help aprechiated,
Thanks,
Ritchie
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Sequental file - junk when created...

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T13:57:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031216145849.266$Pm@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312161036.28edd6f8@posting.google.com>`

```
More, more! :-)  You need to tell us more, such as what compiler you
are using, and what the FD and 01 record(s) look like, and the actual
procedure division code that creates the file. You don't tell us how you
viewed the data to determine it is junk. Did you read it with another
COBOL program, or did you use a file viewer utility?

Also please note that an ISAM file, by definition, contains one or more
indexes. Some implementations place those indexes in the data file with
the data (some use separate files for the index). Those index tables very
well could look like 'junk'. :-)
```

```
"ritchie" <ritchie_s01@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:3bee6ba6.0312161036.28edd6f8@posting.google.com...

>
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Sequental file - junk when created...

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T16:44:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<leadnSRP7a4oF0KiRVn-gQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312161036.28edd6f8@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie wrote:
> Hi
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> junk.

One man's junk is another man's treasure.

ISAM files are highly optimized, contain much stuff other than data (such as
positioning information, overflow-areas, pointers, etc.), and the data are
often compacted.

You cannot read an ISAM file with anything other than an ISAM-knowledgeable
program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Sequental file - junk when created...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T14:54:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312161454.5746a0df@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312161036.28edd6f8@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie) wrote

> Wanted to ask if anyone knew anything about this??
> 
> I a trying to create a program where I open up an Indexed sequental
> file and write to it but when I look in the file it is filled with
> junk.

That may be quite normal, depending on the compiler and run-time being
used.  ISAM files are usually _NOT_ just a bunch of records.

For examnple the system may create a file of a certain size ready to
write data records to it, but there are no valid records yet so the
actual content is irrelevant.  If you were to READ it the system would
report no records (AT END).

Doing this saves the system having to extend the file for every record
and thus results in better performance.

Alternately, if it is Fujitsu, eg, then there will be index data,
record size headers and all manner of other stuff required by the
system _and_ the data may be compressed.

It isn't 'junk', it is just that you don't know what it is.

> Each time I run the program it get's filled with even more junk plus
> what I'm writing to it, 

It isn't 'junk', it is things that the system requires.  You want the
file to be 'indexed', that is probably the index.

> and after a couple of runs, when I run the
> program I get an error.
> Has anybody had this problem before? 

Yes, I've had an error before.  At least two, possibly three.  If I
recall they were actually my faulty code that caused them.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Sequental file - junk when created...

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-16T19:11:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FDFAD4E.1040505@netscape.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3bee6ba6.0312161036.28edd6f8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312161036.28edd6f8@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie wrote:

> Hi
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> junk.

It's not junk, it's your indexes...  If you want a human readable file, 
look at "sequential".
```

```
: junk.
```

<a id="t-a04bac70fd"></a>
## Thread: Panvalet Files — 5 msgs

### Panvalet Files

- **From:** user@email.address.here (User)
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T00:26:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fdf9d01.304271203@netnews.att.net>`

```
I am working with a bunch of binary data files that were generated
(and still are generated) by programs written by people who left my
company a long time ago.  Nobody left around here knows much about the
computer programs or how they do what they do.  They merely know what
program they have to run for a specific task.

Scary.

I can't track down any information on the binary data file formats
that are used, but the name "Panvalet" keeps coming up.  Through much
reverse-engineering, a couple of us have written Matlab routines that
read and write these binary files, but as we dig deeper, it almost
looks like we're using a perversion of this file format.

It appears that the file format is designed to store a bunch of real
scalars (*REALS), integer scalars (*INTEGRS), alphanumeric scalars
(*ALPHAS), real vectors (*REALV), integer vectors (*INTEGRV),
alphanumeric vectors (*ALPHAV) and '*COMARY' (??? common array???),
which we're using to store a 2D array as a vector (with the i,k
dimensions stored as scalars elsewhere in the file).  A bunch of the
integer scalars are used to store the lengths of arrays, etc., but
these numbers are repeated deep inside the binary format where you'd
expect, them so storing them explicitly is probably a waste.  Some
strings are stored as single strings, and others are stored in pieces
as arrays of 4-byte strings.  (Some numbers are stored as 32-bit
reals, other numbers are stored as strings containing digits and
decimal points.  Our computer programs interpret these things
correctly, but I suspect that's merely one patch slapped on top of
another patch on top of another patch to make things work, and it make
adding/updating virtually impossible.  And to top it all off, most of
this stuff is written in Fortran 4.  (I'm only posting to a Cobol
newsgroup because Cobol came up a lot when I Googled "panvalet".)

We're trying to update things, and we're looking for binary file
format specifications for these files.  Does anybody know where I
might be able to find such a thing for "Panvalet" binary files?   Or
is this a cruel joke left behind by a bunch of retirees?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Panvalet Files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T01:30:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2lODb.7384$Pg1.473@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3fdf9d01.304271203@netnews.att.net>`

```
Panvalet (originally from Pansophic - but from Computer Associates for MANY
years now) is a system for storing files (often source code - but potentially
"anything") in a condensed format with automatic versioning / back-ups.

I doubt it will help much, but you could check out the current production
information - starting at:

 http://www3.ca.com/Solutions/Product.asp?ID=1431
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0312191330.48596c80@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3fdf9d01.304271203@netnews.att.net>`


<a id="t-7be815843f"></a>
## Thread: PROBLEM SOLVED - State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines — 29 msgs

### PROBLEM SOLVED - State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-17T09:22:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brporn$n45$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

Thanks to all who offered assistance... especially Messrs Trembley and 
Klein.  The problem was one of 'sneaking in something technically more 
sophisticated than the rest of the code.'

Mr Klein posted some documentation about mixing static and dynamic calls;  
I knew this was not the case since the same CLIST generated compile JCL
for all programs and the output showed the compiler invocation had
PARM='SIZE(MAX),LIB,NUMPROC(MIG),OFF,MAP,XREF,OPT,FLAG(I,I), 
NOSEQ,TEST(NONE,SYM),NOADV' and thi would cause the compiler to default to 
the NODYNAM option.

Mr Trembley posted a problem from the Oldene Dayse about mixing static and 
dynamic calls... and again I knew this was not the case, same CLIST for 
all, compiled in deepest-call-to-driver sequence... and just to be sure I 
put literals in each program and browsed the load modules.  Program C had 
its literal, program B had literals for B and C... and program A had its 
own literal only.

This confused me... and a mistake showed me what was going on.  I brought 
up A in the ISPF editor and wanted to position myself at the PROCEDURE 
DIVISION; I do this by typing 'f proced 8'.  The screen showed no 
change... I had typo'd 'proced' to 'priced' and there was an error-message 
in the upper right-hand corner telling me 'No CHARS 'PRICED' found'.

That wasn't the helpful part... what was helpful was having to stare at 
the top of the code for an extra few seconds.  Instead of first line being 
the expected IDENTIFICATION DIVISION I saw, for the first time, that it 
was:

000100  CBL DYNAM,TEST(NONE),MAP,FASTSRT,LIST

For those who don't get the 'joke'... these are directives which will
override the PARM which was specified at the invocation of the compiler.  
The DYNAM is the fly in the ointment... even though the JCL showed that 
there was no overriding of the compiler's default of NODYNAM (calls 
are static) the compiler would read the line hown above... and set the 
option to DYNAM (calls are dynamic).

This is why I did not see the literals from B and C in the load module for 
A... and why A, when it called C directly, got an initialised version and 
not one which reflected having been called by B.

Compiler directives in the code... that's 'whodunnit'.

Thanks to all who e'er-so-generously donated their time, effort and 
expertise.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PROBLEM SOLVED - State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T07:07:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vu39koig3kt9b5@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<RYfEb.13125$aw2.7094642@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<brporn$n45$1@panix1.panix.com> <vu1p1sslpbqc7c@corp.supernews.com> <RYfEb.13125$aw2.7094642@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:vu1p1sslpbqc7c@corp.supernews.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> and obtuse method.

When I first got here, I knew little about COBOL or most any other 
programming language (most of my experience was with Commodore 64 
BASIC).  We were releasing a new version of the database for Y2K, and 
there was a help file for the currently fielded version.  However, the 
developer that did that was gone.  I piped up and said I would do it - 
it would be something to get my hands on...

Anyway, the database is laid out like COBOL records (01, 05, 10, etc.). 
  I went through the entire database, making static HTML pages that used 
tables and cells to align all the 05-levels together, all the 10-levels 
at another level of indention, etc., and all the picture clauses aside 
from that.  Took me around 2 months to complete.  Straight HTML - no CSS.

Once I had finished this task, I was looking through a list of HTML tags 
and found the <PRE> tag.  This tag (for those who don't know) tells the 
page you have preformatted text - it preserves the whitespace, carriage 
returns, etc.  So, what took 2 months, I could have done 
<PRE><Ctrl-V></PRE> - probably finished the whole thing in a day.  :)

Now, I have all the items in a database, and I generate XML from the 
database.  I then format these using XSLT.  It's great - if I want an 
up-to-date copy, I can just regenerate every page with one click, and it 
pulls the most recent data definitions.  :)

> Then I utter an expletive.

I think I was a little past that when I found that <PRE> tag.  The 
ironic thing (well, sort of) is that the XSLT uses, to align the 
COBOL...  tables and cells.  (Of course, it's much smarter than that 
original setup - I'm actually quite happy with the new stuff.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PROBLEM SOLVED - State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T07:14:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031218081541.362$Ny@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<brporn$n45$1@panix1.panix.com> <vu1p1sslpbqc7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
> Isn't it strange how you can find an answer you weren't even looking for
> by researching something else?  I had that happen to me today as well.  :)

It is common enough that there is a word for it: serendipity. :-)

I love those 'niche' (see!) words that mean precisely what you want to
say in very specific situations. The French have been particularly good
at coining such words English has borrowed lots of them (like 'niche').
So many that, considering French is a relatively small language (~300k
words) compared to English (~750k words), it makes me wonder what
else the French talk about? Besides sex, of course, which must account
for a substantial portion of those ~300k words. ;-)

Have you ever stopped to wonder at how many slang terms have been
used for the (ahem!) more 'interesting' parts of human anatomy, and
related activities? The mind boggles at the number that must have been
used throughout history. There are words and expressions in common use
today that would make many people blush who now use them casually in
public, to know their origins. :-)
```

```
How right you are. :-)  You're far more knowledgeable in this than I (as
you were studying Cicero, I was studying Newton :), but only a few days
ago I made a post about how some of the English names for meats, while
still 'on the hoof' so to speak, as the Anglos would have dealt with them,
have Anglo names (hog, cow), but when prepared for eating, as the ruling
Normans would have dealt with them, have French names (pork, beef).

But all this aside, I suspect you would agree that the French have some
useful highly specific words that have been grafted into English, such
as carte blanche, savoir-faire, niche, verve, etc. I seem to be constantly
running across these English  'Frenchisms', but I'm at a loss to think of
more at the moment. :-)
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> ><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> too far along... hyuk hyuk!


The expression "Massive Overkill" comes to mind here. :-)  It has been
said that Principia, above all other creations of the human mind,
demonstrates the heights which human intellect can achieve. There have
been lots of incredibly brilliant minds through history, but if there was
ever anyone with greater pure intellectual ability then Isaac Newton, I
don't know who it was. Newton wrote Principia in less than three years.
Though Newton's motivation seemed to decline after Principia, if his
intellect dwindled as he got older, it was apparent only to him. Newton
was solving, often within a day, incredibly difficult problems that were
submitted to him almost until his death at age 85. He solved Leibniz's
1716 challenge in five hours, at age 74. Someone once said (paraphrase)
"Geometry is a powerful tool indeed, but only Newton can wield it well."
```

```
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Some might nominate Euler... but what do *they* know, anyhow?

Euler would certainly be in the running, if anybody. When you consider the
sheer volume of Euler's output, and its quality, and considering he did it
with a quill pen, often by candle or lamp light at a table with kids and cats
in his lap and on his back, it is astonishing.

> >Someone once said (paraphrase)
> >"Geometry is a powerful tool indeed, but only Newton can wield it well."
>
> My favorite was Bernoulli's (no slouch he!) 'tanquam ex ungue leonem'...

My high school Latin teacher promised to give me a 'D' if I promised not
to take Latin again, But IIRC, you refer to January, 1697 when Newton,
who hated notoriety, solved in one day two difficult problems proposed by
Bernoulli (with a suggested six months to solve), and submitted the
solutions anonymously to the Royal Society. Bernoulli, recognizing Newton's
work, made that comment "The lion is known by his claw" (or about so :-).
```

```
Was it Bernoulli who said that? ("I recognise the lion by his claw"?) I
never realised.

Poor old Bernoulli is one of the most under acknowledged Scientists in
history, in my opinion.

Every time I go sailing, or flying, or tune a carburettor (not often these
days, since fuel injection), I think of him.

Most people think that yachts sail because the wind blows the sail along;
Bernoulli knew better.

Few of the general public understand what causes the force that lifts their
200 ton jumbo jet off the ground. Bernoulli did, long before jumbo jets were
even dreamed of.

And if you do happen to mention his name they think he was Italian...<G>

Pete.

> DD
>
```

#### ↳ Re: PROBLEM SOLVED - State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T13:27:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QWhEb.2$s71.1@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<brporn$n45$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,

Here's a snippet of REXX code that I've used in a "pre-compiler" step to ward
off such iniquities:

/*
  Loop through the program to find the items that need to be validated
*/
          intECode = 0
          Do i = 1 to InFile.0
             /* Bypass all comments */
             If Substr(InFile.i,7,1) = '*' Then
                Iterate
             Else Do
                /* Standardize the input line */
                Call CleanUp_Line
                /* We came for the PROGRAM-ID field                   */
                If Pos('PROGRAM-ID',InFile.i) > 0 Then
                   Leave
                /* PROCESS/CBL must appear before Identification Div  */
                If Pos(' CBL ',InFile.i) > 0 Then Do
                   intECode = 12
                   Leave
                End
                If Pos(' PROCESS ',InFile.i) > 0 Then Do
                   intECode = 12
                   Leave
                End
             End
          End
          /* No need to check for any other errors at this point... */
          If intECode > 0 Then Do
             Say ' A PROCESS/CBL statement was detected in program',
                  SRC_Name,
                 'on line' i'.'
             Say ' PROCESS/CBL statements are not permitted in source',
                 'code!'
             Say ' All compile options must be entered on the compile',
                 'options panel.'
             Say ' '
             Exit intECode
          End

Perhaps you can use something similar in your CLIST.

Larry Kahm
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PROBLEM SOLVED - State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

```
Y'see... it isn't 'my' CLIST, actually, but the CLIST of the guy in the 
corner cubicle who gives me work assignments... and he's *very* proud of 
his CLIST, too...

... and I'm the New Consultant On Site here, been on the job about a month 
or so... and from what I've seen the level of technical expertise is such 
that my bringing up 'arcana' such as compiler options and overrides in

right now and I *really* don't understand what you're saying... so let's 
just leave it as it is, shall we?'

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PROBLEM SOLVED - State of WORKING-STORAGE in Statically-Called Subroutines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-19T04:16:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bYuEb.10105$Pg1.3810@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<brporn$n45$1@panix1.panix.com> <QWhEb.2$s71.1@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`

```
If you do NOT want to allow PROCESS / CBL cards, there seems to me to be a MUCH
easier way to prohibit them.

Use the "installation" customization feature of
   ALOWCBL=NO

See:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3C110/2.8

Now, as I read Doc's thread, I thought there cite WANTED all the source code to
have CBL cards - and that might be a "harder" standard to enforce (and a whole
lot sillier - IMHO - than simply making DYNAM a "fixed shop compiler option") -
See:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3c110/1.2.1

NOTE:
  DYNAM should not be fixed for CICS or DB2 compiles, but you can use a
"separate" options module for those compiles by adding a steplib for those
compiles.
```

```
news:2A%Db.11500$lh6.4055@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
> Does anyone know of an activeX form resizer that will work with Fujitsu
> PowerCobol?  I've tried some of them that are designed for VB, and they
```

#### ↳ Re: form resizer

- **From:** pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira)
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T03:09:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5b8d7c7.0312180309.6fbfa472@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2A%Db.11500$lh6.4055@bignews4.bellsouth.net>`

```
"db" <db@db.com> wrote in message news:<2A%Db.11500$lh6.4055@bignews4.bellsouth.net>...
> Does anyone know of an activeX form resizer that will work with Fujitsu
> PowerCobol?  I've tried some of them that are designed for VB, and they
> don't seem to work, unless I'm doing something wrong.
> 
> Thanks.

What version are you using?
Since version 5 (the current one is 7) there is built-in support for
form and control dynamic resizing, where you can control, inside your
code, the behaviour of each control when the form is resized by the
user. Support for the resizing of the form itself is even older.

best regards

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft
```

- **Date:** 2003-12-18T14:54:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rsb3uv414ck62r0dmkhk7vgvp1q3l3qvtp@4ax.com>`

```
Here is a small piece of code :

testrim is calling trim. The name says what it does :

testtrim
      $set sourceformat"free"    
       1 b pic x(2000).
       procedure division.
           move "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ" to b
           call "trim" using 
               by reference address of b
               by content length of b
           end-call
           exit program
           stop run.

Can someone explain why this version works :
trim.cbl
      $set sourceformat"free" 
       working-storage section.
       01 i pic 9(4) comp.
       linkage section.
       01 ls-pStr usage pointer.
       01 ls-len   pic s9(9) usage binary.
       01 lnk-texte   pic x.
       procedure division using ls-pStr, ls-len.
       paragraphe.
           *>version 1
           set address of lnk-texte to ls-pStr
           perform varying i from ls-len by -1
           until ((i < 1) or (lnk-texte(i:1) <> " "))
               move x'00' to lnk-texte(i:1)
           end-perform
           exit program
           .
while this version hangs if b has a picture higher than 108 ? (but is
ok if you define b pic x(108) !!).

      $set sourceformat"free" 
       working-storage section.
       01 tally-count pic 9(7).
       linkage section.
       01 ls-pStr usage pointer.
       01 ls-len   pic s9(9) usage binary.
       01 lnk-texte   pic x.
       procedure division using ls-pStr, ls-len.
       paragraphe.
           *> version 2  
           set address of lnk-texte to ls-pStr
           move zero to tally-count
           inspect function reverse (lnk-texte(1:ls-len))
             tallying tally-count
             for leading spaces
           if tally-count > 0 then
             move low-values to lnk-texte(ls-len - tally-count + 1 :
tally-count)
           end-if
           exit program
           .

I am working with Micro Focus v4.1 under Linux.

This is a small routine to cut spaces at the end of a string. This is
useful in combination with the string instruction :

string 
name delimited by low-values
" " deimited by size
last-name delimited by low-values
into a-string...

Regards.

Alain
```

```
Alain Reymond wrote:
> Here is a small piece of code :
>
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
> into a-string...

The answer is: Luck?

Where does the subprogram get access to "lnk-texte?" It's certainly never
passed by the calling program.

As an aside, isn't this process a bit more complicated than it needs to be,
what with "pointer" and "by content?"

Just ruminating, mind you:

01  LengthOfString    PIC 9(9).
01  MyString  PIC X(2000).

Move "ABC" to MyString.
Compute LengthOfString = Function Length (MyString)

CALL 'TRIM' Using LengthOfString MyString

--- Called program

Linkage Section.
01  Lnk-Len    PIC 9(9).
01  Lnk-StringX.
   02  Filler occurs 1 to 32000 Depending On Lnk-Len PIC X.

Procedure Division Using Lnk-Len Lnk-StringX.

Inspect Function Reverse Lnk-StringX Tallying TALLY for Leading Space.
Move Low-Values to Lnk-StringX(Lnk-Len - Tally + 1:)
Exit Program
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: trim

- **From:** Alain Reymond <>

```
>> Here is a small piece of code :
>>
…[98 more quoted lines elided]…
>Exit Program


Your solution works. And with 
1 lnk-len   pic s9(9) usage binary.
you can use length of directly in the call which spares a move or
compute.

lnk-texte is not passed by the calling program. It is a string that
point to the string passed by the program. ( set address of lnk-texte
to ls-pStr). I can work on this string because I know the length of
it.

But there is no luck in computing! There must be a logical explanation
to the fact that a solution with a pointer does not work when the size
of the passed string is too long...

Regards.

Alain
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: trim

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T17:39:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auidndhBv9JVp3-iRVn-hQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<rsb3uv414ck62r0dmkhk7vgvp1q3l3qvtp@4ax.com> <-MadnXfnQZ6NTHyi4p2dnA@giganews.com> <db94uvgp1ragqjrcvgk0j4c11bl640be0n@4ax.com>`

```
Alain Reymond wrote:
>>> Here is a small piece of code :
>>>
…[105 more quoted lines elided]…
> compute.

If you say so. I've never used it. I've never had to use a type declaration
of pointer, either. Nor have I ever found a need to use BY CONTENT when
calling a COBOL subprogram.

One reason is that using "pointer" is like using dynamite -- ask any C
programmer. When it goes bad it goes spectacularily bad.

>
> lnk-texte is not passed by the calling program. It is a string that
> point to the string passed by the program. ( set address of lnk-texte
> to ls-pStr). I can work on this string because I know the length of
> it.

Sure, but it still seems to be the hard way.

>
> But there is no luck in computing! There must be a logical explanation
> to the fact that a solution with a pointer does not work when the size
> of the passed string is too long...

Now if you were using the Fujitsu compiler, you could:

Compute Len = Function Stored-Char-Length(B).
Move Low-values to B(Len + 1:)

thereby avoiding all these complications.

In our universe, we squeeze out multiple blanks in a text field. After that
it's pretty easy to do what I figure is your next operation:

STRING TEXT-FIELD DELIMITED "  " {that's double-blank}.....
```

<a id="t-5edd33d66f"></a>
## Thread: CoBOL moved to OO — 145 this month / 259 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### CoBOL moved to OO

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T14:47:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca>`

```
We have a lot of PeopleSoft software in our shop.   I'm told that their move to
OO is by encapsulating much of their existing CoBOL code and using tags and such
to call it in an OO setup.

How common is this with big mainframe applications?
```

#### ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2003-12-18T15:32:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.00000369.019ccad7@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca> <brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,

> How common is this with big mainframe applications?

It's commonly proposed as a solution, but this is the first time I've 
heard that it can be made to work.

Since the latest major upgrade, Cobol has OO constructs which I've 
looked at and seem OK, but I've had no personal involvement.

Non-I/O routines can be objectified fairly easily, but since Cobol is 
all about I/O, I can't imagine genuine legacy code written for serial 
files and converted to simple databases can easily be objectified.

And anyway, the managers who want to do this want shiny new systems.

I've been involved with a team investigating automatic objectifying, 
but I can certainly offer one observation: my business users were 
nonplussed - they have a working system, and we wanted to spend 
millions of pounds with /no/ change in behaviour! Where's the business 
benefit in that?

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

```

> 've been involved with a team investigating automatic objectifying,
> but I can certainly offer one observation: my business users were
> nonplussed - they have a working system, and we wanted to spend
> millions of pounds with /no/ change in behaviour! Where's the business
> benefit in that?

The business advantage to PeopleSoft may be primarily in marketing.   They sell
a "state-of-the-art OO system", but still keep their valuable business code
developed over years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-22T17:02:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<22b3dcd54cff07a52f959b3cd483727d@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca> <brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VA.00000369.019ccad7@ieee.org> <bs75hv$hm1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org> wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> developed over years.

In other words, their customers get to pay through the nose for no benefit
to the customer. Typical OO 'benefit', I see this every day. Our industry
seems to have forgotten the concept of ROI.

I view the procedural vs. OO issue as closely analogous to the issue of
iterative vs. recursive logic. Iterative logic (procedural) is better for the
vast number of applications, in clarity, simplicity and efficiency. But there
is a small class of tasks where recursive (OO) logic is simpler and clearer.
But recursive (OO) is never more efficient than iterative (procedural),
except in programmer time in those cases where it is simpler and clearer.
IMHO, going exclusively OO is closely comparable to using recursive
logic for every loop situation.  I.e. It's nuts.  It costs a fortune to implement,
and makes programming, debugging and maintenance far more complex,
costly and time consuming, and the result is less reliable. Pretty much
what we see today in OO, with a 45%+ failure rate in implementing new
systems. :-)

Don't worry, I have my asbestos pants on. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>

```
I think OO vs procedural is something like packaging vs wrapping.

With packaging, using standard class pattern, it is possible to know the
content with opening it.

With wrapping, you can use anything to wrap up the same stuff in any way you
like, but it is not easy to know the content without opening it/breaking it.
And another problem is to put your wrap into another container.

For standard packaging, there are special designed trailers, car... They
need investment at the beginning for long time saving.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T19:16:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bscom7$gut$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca> <brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VA.00000369.019ccad7@ieee.org> <bs75hv$hm1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <22b3dcd54cff07a52f959b3cd483727d@news.teranews.com> <QNGFb.3949$d%1.866120@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On 22-Dec-2003, Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> I think you are right that there may be little benefit to attempting
> retrofit, but in terms of architecture OOP (Cobol OOP that is) is a much
> more logical structure for maintenance.

It could be.   But an interdependent structure requires a more logical
structure.   The more independence a program has, the less likely a mistake or
change will effect other applications.

For some uses, maintenance likes it if you make one simple change that is reused
everywhere.

But for other uses, rigorous testing and "don't touch my program" makes
maintenance harder.   Every time you stick in a change with exceptions (keep
program A running the old way), this interdependence does not make maintenance
easier.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T11:08:43+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fe76bd1_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca> <brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VA.00000369.019ccad7@ieee.org> <bs75hv$hm1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <22b3dcd54cff07a52f959b3cd483727d@news.teranews.com> <VA.00000375.02bf5c97@ieee.org> <VPIFb.4318$d%1.931294@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:VPIFb.4318$d%1.931294@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Doug Scott wrote:
> > Judson,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes Don, it is.

But you are arguing with people who have no idea what they're talking about,
have never been involved in successful OO implementation, do not understand
the basic fundamentals, (witness Judson's post equating "iterative" to
"procedural"), and are resisting to the last ditch anything that looks like
it might threaten their comfortable little world, predicated on "What we
know", "What we have always done", and "Best practice (as we perceive
it)...".

Those of us who have taken the trouble to understand OO, realise what a
powerful tool it is. Those of us who have gone further and realised that OO
components can be wrapped and re-used in a way that called modules never
could, are unlikely to ever return to the "Procedural COBOL" fold (except,
maybe, for Batch Processing...).

For my own part, I'm tired of arguing it. I don't really care whether people
use OO or not.

I care about young people losing their COBOL jobs to Java programmers, and
that is one reason I strongly advocate them learning OO (even Java...). But
for the dyed-in-the-wool Fortress COBOLlers, I reckon they'll get what they
deserve. It is just a matter of time...

I remember similar resistance to Djykstra's paper when it was first
published..."What?! The whole of computer programming can be resolved down
to THREE operations?!!! Rubbish!!".

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T17:24:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b15f52de7cc255400373dfa35846ecab@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca> <brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VA.00000369.019ccad7@ieee.org> <bs75hv$hm1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <22b3dcd54cff07a52f959b3cd483727d@news.teranews.com> <VA.00000375.02bf5c97@ieee.org> <VPIFb.4318$d%1.931294@news20.bellglobal.com> <3fe76bd1_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> But you are arguing with people who have no idea what they're talking about,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> it)...".

You cannot be referring to me, because I have a completely different
set of reasons for objecting to an "OO and nothing else" mentality. I
have said before, and I reiterate, simply show me some statistical data
that show an *across the board* improvement in development time,
development costs, maintenance costs and training costs, and I will be
happy to endorse an *across the board* move to OO. Without that, you
argue simply from personal opinion, as you accuse others of doing.
Within 10 years after structured techniques were in use, I could have
buried you under tons of such statistics in support of structured
techniques. Structured techniques were clearly and decisively superior
to previous techniques in every measurable way, except possibly in
total lines of code. In fact, contrary to the trend we discuss, significant
such data was available BEFORE the push toward structured. Now,
after much longer trials, I have yet to see any such objective data to
confirm a wholesale move toward OO. Perhaps I've been overlooking
it, so kindly point me to it. :-)

Please note that none of the above involves "understanding the basic
concepts", "resisting to the last ditch", threatening any "comfortable
little world", or any "supposed knowledge". If OO is as good as you
say, then fine, show me the statistical data to back it up, based on
data obtained from real world projects. Considering the truly vast
expenditures in money and manpower that have been invested in a
wholesale move toward OO, everyone should be constantly tripping
over such data, because investing that kind of resources in anything
less than clearly demonstrably superior is, as I have been saying,
nuts. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T12:14:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc32a74c7d0fea9dc6ad89517ef5bd5d@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca> <brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VA.00000369.019ccad7@ieee.org> <bs75hv$hm1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <22b3dcd54cff07a52f959b3cd483727d@news.teranews.com> <VA.00000375.02bf5c97@ieee.org> <VPIFb.4318$d%1.931294@news20.bellglobal.com> <3fe76bd1_6@news.athenanews.com> <b15f52de7cc255400373dfa35846ecab@news.teranews.com> <%x4Gb.463547$275.1354316@attbi_s53>`

```
"Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> though when it's so judgmental.

Thank you, Gary. I apologize if I am being 'judgmental', I don't mean to be.
I agree I am being blunt but, for better or worse, it seems to be a part of
my personality. I do have very strong opinions and a high emotional stake
in these issues, for what I believe to be very good reasons, that I will try
to explain below. I hope this helps those of you who strongly disagree
with me to at lease understand where I'm coming from. :-)

It may seem that I am "resisting new technology" because of personal
reasons, such as not wanting to learn it, or being threatened by it. Though
I do not learn or enjoy learning as much as I did 30 years ago, that has
nothing to do with my position. I like new technology, and enjoy using new
things. I love the WSIWYG interface in my word processor, where it is
vastly superior to any character based interface. Windows GUI interface
is nice in many ways, but clunky in others, requiring a console interface
for things such as creating a text file listing all files named "???0716*.*"
on partition "D:". GUI simply is not always better. PC based client/server
systems are fine, one I wrote has been running since 1987. But PC based
C/S is not the best way to approach every situation. I don't dislike OO, I
can see certain advantages to that approach. I don't dislike the C/C++ type
languages, they are okay for writing system level software, for which C
was originally created, but crummy for writing business apps, for which
they are ill-suited. Sure, you can do it, but why? No fixed point scaled
data type, cryptic syntax and dangerous constructs like =/==, or pointers
and indirection, which business applications rarely or never require. I
won't argue the issue here, except to point out that my reasons are very
objective.

The bottom line of why I am so adamant on these issues, and why I feel
so strongly about them is simple and clear. There is an attitude in our
industry today that I view as almost a mass insanity, because it is not
logical, it goes against long established business practices, and it has
been highly unproductive to the point of disaster in every single situation
with which I am personally familiar. This insane attitude can be stated
clearly as "Mainframes are always bad, dump them all for PC based
client/server. Character interfaces are always bad, dump them all for
GUI. Procedural code is bad, dump it all for OO. And do all of this
IMMEDIATELY! No thinking required, and especially don't ask what it
costs, or what the specific benefits are." I have seen this attitude grow
and grow in management, and watched in horror as shop after shop has
been devastated by it. Millions and millions of dollars spent as if it
were water, and for all the benefit the users derived, it might as well
have been piled up and set on fire. I see this happening FOR REAL
every single day, and it makes me sick to my stomach. It's not the
technology per se that bothers me, it is this blind adherence to a position
that is stupid and indefensible, and clearly demonstrably so, in every
case where I have seen it happen. No exceptions, not one. No 'partial
successes'. Just pure unadulterated failures, one after another. One of
my clients has spent in excess of $20 million and four years trying to
do exactly what I describe above. The users have yet to see even one
benefit, and none is remotely close. Not one application has been taken
from the mainframe, and no significant (non trivial) application has yet
to be brought up on client/server. None. Nada. Zip. It is virtually certain
that this will be the situation a year from now. Some shops can benefit
from dumping the mainframe and going PC based C/S. Some apps can
benefit from GUI interfaces. Some tasks are better handled with OO.
But not ALL of them!

Now, am I unique in that the experiences I have seen are only the rare
exceptions? I don't know, but I would be immensely relieved if I saw
proof that they were. Several local disasters are better than industry
wide disasters. But the reasons for the failures I have seen are logical
and obvious, at least to me. And, from what I can tell, it seems this
same attitude that has caused these disasters is being propounded and
defended in this forum. I have seen with my own eyes lots of serious,
undeniable, objective proof of why this attitude can bring disaster. If
I am asked to believe my completely consistent experiences are the
exception, then I need to see equally believable, serious, undeniable,
and objective proof of this. If that proof exists, fine, give it to me and
I'll listen. But do not expect me to except an opinion without concrete
evidence that what I clearly see happening around me is not also
happening industry wide. From my perspective, this seems a very
reasonable position to take, under the circumstances. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CoBOL moved to OO

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-25T12:10:23+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fea1d45_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca> <brseln$6t5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VA.00000369.019ccad7@ieee.org> <bs75hv$hm1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <22b3dcd54cff07a52f959b3cd483727d@news.teranews.com> <VA.00000375.02bf5c97@ieee.org> <VPIFb.4318$d%1.931294@news20.bellglobal.com> <3fe76bd1_6@news.athenanews.com> <b15f52de7cc255400373dfa35846ecab@news.teranews.com> <%x4Gb.463547$275.1354316@attbi_s53> <bc32a74c7d0fea9dc6ad89517ef5bd5d@news.teranews.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:bc32a74c7d0fea9dc6ad89517ef5bd5d@news.teranews.com...
> "Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK, bring it on...<G>

> It may seem that I am "resisting new technology" because of personal
> reasons, such as not wanting to learn it, or being threatened by it.
Though
> I do not learn or enjoy learning as much as I did 30 years ago, that has
> nothing to do with my position. I like new technology, and enjoy using new
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> for things such as creating a text file listing all files named
"???0716*.*"
> on partition "D:".

This can be done with a GUI interface; it is just that the way you choose to
do it is more familiar to you.

>GUI simply is not always better. PC based client/server
> systems are fine, one I wrote has been running since 1987. But PC based
> C/S is not the best way to approach every situation. I don't dislike OO, I
> can see certain advantages to that approach.

I contend that you are not qualified to judge.

It would be much more honest if you simply said: "I don't like watching
installations rushing to embrace OO simply because of fashion...", rather
than getting into a discussion on the merits of OO, or otherwise, without
having used it. Theoretical knowledge is one thing; practical experience is
quite another.

> I don't dislike the C/C++ type
> languages, they are okay for writing system level software, for which C
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A series of platitudes, that "everybody knows", without much substance...

You start by saying you "don't dislike"... then proceed to give reasons why
you DO dislike...<G>

The fact is that you prefer COBOL. It's OK. Just say that.

> The bottom line of why I am so adamant on these issues, and why I feel
> so strongly about them is simple and clear. There is an attitude in our
> industry today that I view as almost a mass insanity, because it is not
> logical, it goes against long established business practices, and it has
> been highly unproductive to the point of disaster in every single
situation
> with which I am personally familiar. This insane attitude can be stated
> clearly as "Mainframes are always bad, dump them all for PC based
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> costs, or what the specific benefits are."

Yes, there are some places where that would be true, and I agree with you,
it is stupid.

It staggers me that this has been the UNIVERSAL experience, for you.

>I have seen this attitude grow
> and grow in management, and watched in horror as shop after shop has
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> technology per se that bothers me, it is this blind adherence to a
position
> that is stupid and indefensible, and clearly demonstrably so, in every
> case where I have seen it happen. No exceptions, not one. No 'partial
> successes'. Just pure unadulterated failures, one after another.

Frankly, unbelievable...

Given this is true, I can understand your horror.

So, what would you do about it? Avoid new technology? Not a very good
solution, is it? (Like throwing the baby out with the bathwater...)

Obviously, the sites where this has occurred are suffering from much more
than just failure to cope with new technology. I am immediately tempted to
wonder how they managed their existing COBOL projects, and whether these
were invariably successful...


>One of
> my clients has spent in excess of $20 million and four years trying to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to be brought up on client/server. None. Nada. Zip. It is virtually
certain
> that this will be the situation a year from now. Some shops can benefit
> from dumping the mainframe and going PC based C/S. Some apps can
> benefit from GUI interfaces. Some tasks are better handled with OO.
> But not ALL of them!

The conclusion you reach is debatable. It is certainly true that THIS shop
needs some severe overhauling before it even attempts to implement new
technology. It looks to me like some "culture change" could be in order.
There isn't much point in sowing good seed on stony ground, never watering
it, then deciding it was crap seed because it didn't grow...

Some tilling, cultivation, and removal of rocks, would provide a more level
playing field...

>
> Now, am I unique in that the experiences I have seen are only the rare
> exceptions? I don't know, but I would be immensely relieved if I saw
> proof that they were.

No, there are many failures when COBOL shops attempt to implement this
technology, especially if they do so using the existing practices and
procedures that haven't changed for 40 years. (Sometimes existing Local
Standards are not conducive to new ways of doing things, and this in itself,
can provide a source of conflict, before you even START getting into doing
what needs to be done...)

However, it certainly isn't a case of UNIVERSAL failure. As I mentioned
elsewhere, I have seen COBOL programmers successfully implement these new
technologies, when properly managed, supported,  and motivated. The
resistance seems to be more political than practical. I have been trying for
some years now to get my head round WHY it is so... It still isn't clear.

The results of it are pretty devastating though. Every day we see more COBOL
jobs disappearing. COBOL is simply being overtaken by events. The total
inability to adapt and change to the new IT wave is placing it under
sentence of death. It's like watching the light in the sky that is the
approaching comet, getting bigger every day, and nobody is prepared to build
a spaceship...

> Several local disasters are better than industry
> wide disasters. But the reasons for the failures I have seen are logical
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> reasonable position to take, under the circumstances. :-)

So, you believe the failures are because of an attitude?

I believe they are because of a lack of understanding of what is involved,
and the need for change. I have no doubt whatsoever that I could implement a
successful OO project on these sites, given the support of Senior
Management, and some time for "education" that was not assessed as being
part of the "project schedule".

Even leaving that aside, I believe they could improve their existing
productivity using the technology they know and love, just by managing
change better within their IT organisation.

Programmers are generally bright people and they like doing stuff that is
different (especially if it enhances their CV...<G>).

And it wouldn't cost $20 million.

I am genuinely sorry that your first hand exposure to new technology has
been so negative.

It is not the fault of the technology. (It is used with great success by the
REST of the World...who are unfettered by considerations about how things
have always worked.)

Thanks for your explanation as to why you feel the way you do.

Pete.
```
