```
On 29-Dec-2003, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> > Six invokes for a command used once in a program is nothing.   Decisions
> > such as
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the first call and before the last call? Remember Murphy's Law. :-)

But you will notice that the code said:
  DISPLAY 'SIPR702 BEGAN ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (5:2)
                       '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (7:2)
                       '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:4)
                       ' ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (09:2)                 hour
                       ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:2)
                       ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (13:2).

Saying when a program starts doesn't happen 1000 times a second.

I was under the impression that this gets converted into one call though.
```

```
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Saying when a program starts doesn't happen 1000 times a second.

True. But saying when a transaction began processing might. ;-)

> I was under the impression that this gets converted into one call though.

I would think this should be the case, even if COBOL standards do not
dictate it. A compiler would be pretty inept not to detect and optimize
such a pattern. :-)
```

```
On Mon, 29 Dec 2003 20:39:56 GMT "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
wrote:

:>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

:>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

:>> > If that code is in a transaction routine that is called 1000 times a second,
:>> > the overhead isn't 'nothing'. And what about turning over midnight after
:>> > the first call and before the last call? Remember Murphy's Law. :-)

:>> But you will notice that the code said:
:>>   DISPLAY 'SIPR702 BEGAN ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (5:2)
:>>                        '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (7:2)
:>>                        '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:4)
:>>                        ' ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (09:2)                 hour
:>>                        ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:2)
:>>                        ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (13:2).

:>> Saying when a program starts doesn't happen 1000 times a second.

:>True. But saying when a transaction began processing might. ;-)

:>> I was under the impression that this gets converted into one call though.

:>I would think this should be the case, even if COBOL standards do not
:>dictate it. A compiler would be pretty inept not to detect and optimize
:>such a pattern. :-)

REXX has a requirement/definition that all TIME/DATE based functions in one
statement will all use the same clock value, even if the statement is complex
enough to require much time between the separate function calls.

Does COBOL?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date manipulation

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-30T16:15:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bss8bi$k0l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<DiNEb.3912$XF6.86150@typhoon.sonic.net> <8579uv03o7bt44rtm3vk6diftvj2014bn6@4ax.com> <h7ecnYuqdOohJnmiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <d14aee019cf2a7fd54fe17a24339da7a@news.teranews.com> <bs9m75$j0t$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bfdfc3e8.0312231640.595d6b41@posting.google.com> <bspd3s$mmc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <8c4cee85a6472bf6db0fb07534a2af2a@news.teranews.com> <bspuov$cq1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0312291438.63031a9f@posting.google.com> <bss1fh$rkl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ue73vvo0bpqsdlevi4of7h9i217s2uhr53@4ax.com>`

```
On 30-Dec-2003, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> :>> > I was under the impression that this gets converted into one call
> though.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Perhaps a static variable or random number affects the result?

It assumes that a display statement is meant to act as though it was
instantaneous.

It's easy enough to do - the question is whether or not that is the way it's
supposed to work.   I was under the impression that that IS the way it is
supposed to work, and that is the way it does work in my environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date manipulation

_(thread depth: 11)_

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-30T20:36:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bssnkl$s9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<DiNEb.3912$XF6.86150@typhoon.sonic.net> <8579uv03o7bt44rtm3vk6diftvj2014bn6@4ax.com> <h7ecnYuqdOohJnmiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <d14aee019cf2a7fd54fe17a24339da7a@news.teranews.com> <bs9m75$j0t$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bfdfc3e8.0312231640.595d6b41@posting.google.com> <bspd3s$mmc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <8c4cee85a6472bf6db0fb07534a2af2a@news.teranews.com> <bspuov$cq1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0312291438.63031a9f@posting.google.com> <bss1fh$rkl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0312301051.2a015cf4@posting.google.com>`

```

On 30-Dec-2003, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> > > > I was under the impression that this gets converted into one call
> > > > though.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> How about FUNCTION READ_NEXT_RECORD ?

It depends on how the compiler was written and what the standards were.

You're gradually changing the questions from how a compiler could do something
to whether it is a good idea for it to do something similar.    The compiler can
be written however the standards say it should be written.   I didn't make the
decisions, but I thought I read that this particular function was only called
once per statement.   I did say "I was under the impression", which seemed when
I wrote it to be enough waffling.
```

```
Richard, that brings up a thought, relative to the OO threads now ongoing.
What about those Universal Object References Thane was mentioning, in
this context? I can see where you could potentially get a very different
result from early and late binding in this case. In one case the compiler
knows, in the other it doesn't. Implementation might be a bit tricky.

No criticism of OO here, it is the ramifications of the concept that I'm
curious about. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Date manipulation

- **From:** "Francis ANDRE" <francis.andre@easynet.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-12-20T21:15:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fe4adfd$0$397$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<DiNEb.3912$XF6.86150@typhoon.sonic.net>`

```
Why don't you use MOVE CORRESPONDING???  (fast.... just 3 moves of
2 bytes instead of a full decimal multiplication)?? and easy to do in one
sentence

01  DT-MMDDYY     PIC 9(06)  VALUE 0.
01  DT-MMDDYY-R REDEFINES DT-MMDDYY.
      02  MM                PIC 9(2).
      02  DD                  PIC 9(2).
      02  YY                  PIC 9(2).


01  DT-YYMMDD     PIC 9(06)  VALUE 0.
01  DT-YYMMDD-R REDEFINES DT-YYMMDD.
      02  YY                  PIC 9(2).
      02  MM                PIC 9(2).
      02  DD                  PIC 9(2).

MOVE CORRESPONDING DT-YYMMDD-R TO DT-DDMMYY-R

Regards

FA


"Kindrick Ownby" <kownby@sonic.net> a �crit dans le message de news:
DiNEb.3912$XF6.86150@typhoon.sonic.net...
>
> I have a vague recollection from Y2K discussions
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-e6413d5517"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Standards Issue — 47 this month / 54 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-22T14:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com>`

```
I suspect Bill will want to comment on this one. :-)  Yes, I know it's
way too late (by about 20 years), but I had to get it off my chest. :-)

IIRC, in COBOL 74 and earlier, any explicit REDEFINES other than
at the 01 RECORD level was required to be the same size as the item
being redefined, but COBOL 85 relaxed that and permitted an explicit
REDEFINES item to be smaller (not larger) than the item it redefines.
According to the latest working document I have (Dec 2001), the 2002
standard continues this.

I submit that this is not the way it should have been handled, for a very
good reason. Consider this pattern that is one of the most frequent uses
of REFEFINES:

            03    PRIME-VALUES.
                    05    FILLER        PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0002.
                    05    FILLER        PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0003.
                    05    FILLER        PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0005.
                    05    FILLER        PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0007.
                            ...
                    05    FILLER        PIC 9(04)    VALUE  9967.
                    05    FILLER        PIC 9(04)    VALUE  9973.
            03    PRIMES                   REDEFINES PRIME-VALUES
                                                     PIC  9(04)    OCCURS 1229 TIMES.

In this particular example, there are exactly 1229 primes below 10,000.
If the number of values is correct, the compiler will flag any OCCURS
greater than 1229, but not a lesser value. Why should a compiler permit
such an obvious error? I believe there should be syntax for REDEFINES
that would require the sizes to be equal. Actually, it would have been
better had COBOL 85 made this the default and provided a syntax to
relax the constraint.

Yes, it's beating a dead horse, but what do you gurus think?

P.S.- If you would like a simple prime number list generator, you can
download PRIMES.ZIP from the URL below.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-22T18:29:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AKGFb.121211$ea%.39266@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com>`

```
Your example reminds me of the following piece of code from my customer:

  03    array-values.
                    05    a    PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0002.
                    05    a    PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0003.
                    05    a    PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0005.
   03    array1 REDEFINES array-values PIC  9(04)    OCCURS 3 TIMES.

Does the three "a" variables has the same memory? if not, how to distinguish
one from the other? Ambiguous? error allowed?

What this one means?
  03    array-values.
                    05    a    PIC 9(02)    VALUE  0002.
                    05    a    PIC 9(06)    VALUE  0003.
                    05    a    PIC 9(04)    VALUE  0005.
   03    array1 REDEFINES array-values PIC  9(04)    OCCURS 3 TIMES.

It seems Cobol lacks in defining and initializing an array like this:
   03 array1[3] PIC 9(04) {2, 3, 5}.

Thomas

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com...
> I suspect Bill will want to comment on this one. :-)  Yes, I know it's
> way too late (by about 20 years), but I had to get it off my chest. :-)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                                      PIC  9(04)    OCCURS
1229 TIMES.
>
> In this particular example, there are exactly 1229 primes below 10,000.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-22T14:53:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vuemfe90200v01@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<AKGFb.121211$ea%.39266@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <AKGFb.121211$ea%.39266@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Thomas A. Li wrote:
> Your example reminds me of the following piece of code from my customer:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    03    array1 REDEFINES array-values PIC  9(04)    OCCURS 3 TIMES.

I'm not sure that this would compile, and if it would, I'm not sure how 
you would uniquely identify "a" as a variable.  (It might compile if you 
never reference "a" anywhere in the code...)

> Does the three "a" variables has the same memory? if not, how to distinguish
> one from the other? Ambiguous? error allowed?

No, each of those variables takes up a different amount of memory (12 
bytes in all).

> What this one means?
>   03    array-values.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    03 array1[3] PIC 9(04) {2, 3, 5}.

You're right - it does lack that construct.  However, there is another 
way to do it...

03  array-values   Pic X(12) Value "000200030005".
03  array1 REDEFINES array-values PIC 9(04) OCCURS 3 TIMES.

This is a little less verbose, and works with strings up to 160 
characters.  After that, you're better off either using the above 
construct (which, if I were doing them, I'd leave off the names 
entirely, which is the same as coding FILLER), or writing procedure code 
to fill the table when the program starts.

One of the more common constructs you'll see is something like this...

01  Month-Area.
     03  Pic X(36) Value "JanFebMarAprMayJunJulAugSepOctNovDec".
01  Month-Table  Redefines Month-Area.
     03  Months  Occurs 12 Times  Pic X(03).

Then, Months (1) is "Jan", Months (2) is "Feb", etc.  It's virtually the 
same thing as saying

01  Months[12] Pic X(03) Values 
{"Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun","Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"}.

As to the "why", that's before my time - but, this is "the way it is"...  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-22T23:27:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p5LFb.15458$Pg1.215@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <AKGFb.121211$ea%.39266@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <vuemfe90200v01@corp.supernews.com>`

```
COBOL 2002 has the ability to "initialize" individual elements of a table, e.g.

  03  EachElem pic 99 Occurs 3 times Value 1,3,27.

It also lets you initialze all "trailing" elements to a specific value - or a
range of elements to a value.

Check with your "vendor of choice" of (planned) availability of this 2002
Standard feature.

P.S.  The '85 Standard *does* explicitly allow for non-unique, impossible to be
made unique data-names AS LONG AS you never reference them.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T10:51:24+13:00

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote in message
news:tjPFb.153481$%TO.3166@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com...
> Neat.
> When I got the time, I have to read COBOL 2002.
>

Try reading "BEOWULF"  and "CANTERBURY TALES" in the original Middle
English, then Edward Gibbon's "RISE AND FALL OF THE ROMAN EMPIRE" first.
That should get you used to long, boring, tedious use of English. Then
progress to "CASE LAW" and "THE LAW OF TORT" to get a feel for some of the
explanations in the 2002 standard.

Finally, limit yourself to no more than 3 pages a day. (If you can manage to
read 3 pages and stay awake...).

Then realise, that nobody really cares anyway and this document has lost
nearly all credibility it might have once had, by being 15 years late.

I suggest that your time would be better spent reading the COBOL manual
provided by your vendor, which at least is pertinent to what you need, and
immediately applicable, because that is the implementatiuon you are dealing
with.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T11:00:10+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fe8bb50_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <AKGFb.121211$ea%.39266@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <vuemfe90200v01@corp.supernews.com> <p5LFb.15458$Pg1.215@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <umPFb.153509$%TO.487@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```

"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote in message
news:umPFb.153509$%TO.487@twister01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com...
> What's the base classes of all COBOL 2002 classes?
> Is it possible to reflect type info at runtime?
>
> Thanks in advance and merry Chritmas.

Please don't try and interpret COBOL in terms of OO. The OO parts of the
2002 Standard are there simply to make it look as if COBOL is "keeping up".
Nobody has any intention of actually using them.

You might as well request information in Swahili, as request OO data from
Fortress COBOLlers.

It's like asking a Hottentot about Cosmology. (If you need to know where the
nearest waterhole is, useful information is immediately forthcoming; if you
want to know about Dark Matter, look elsewhere...)

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T10:39:55+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com...
<snip>

> In this particular example, there are exactly 1229 primes below 10,000.
> If the number of values is correct, the compiler will flag any OCCURS
> greater than 1229, but not a lesser value. Why should a compiler permit
> such an obvious error?

It isn't an error...obvious or otherwise. The compiler has no knowledge of
what you are defining. (Neither should it have...).

What if you had arbitrarily decided to define a table of primes less than
11,000?  The compiler is flagging storage allocation errors, not incomplete
data.

I'm sure you understand that, Judson, and am amazed at your comment.

Forcing REDEFINES to match exactly the field being REDEFINED would be a
retrograde step, in my opinion.

Allowing fields larger than the original to REDEFINE, is not logical because
the extra storage is NOT REDEFINEing anything.

As for the Standard, it will be 15 years before we see anything if past
experience is anything to go by, so don't hold your breath...

>I believe there should be syntax for REDEFINES
> that would require the sizes to be equal.

I disagree.

Why FORCE this when there is no need to, and no good case for doing so?  It
comes back to the Authoritarian approach again <sigh>...

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-22T17:36:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
> news:63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> what you are defining. (Neither should it have...).

But it knows if the two are equal, because it computes the length to 
decide whether it's too big.  :)  (Does standard COBOL produce a REMARK 
when the REDEFINES is smaller than the original area?  Maybe a 
post-compiler listing scanner could flag that.)

>>I believe there should be syntax for REDEFINES
>>that would require the sizes to be equal.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why FORCE this when there is no need to, and no good case for doing so?

I think what he's wanting is an compile option to force it, like the 
ones that do bounds checking on tables and reference modification.  I 
think that would be the best implementation...  I could see where it 
would be handy to let the compiler check your work, especially if you're 
using some strange picture clauses.  (Of course, I'm the guy that works 
on the 9-bit machine every day...  ;> )
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T16:01:23+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fe7b065_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com> <vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > It isn't an error...obvious or otherwise. The compiler has no knowledge
of
> > what you are defining. (Neither should it have...).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> post-compiler listing scanner could flag that.)

The LENGTH of the data item has nothing to do with the MEANING of the data
item.

I agree a compiler option to flag discrepancy in REDEFINES object and
subject LENGTH , might be useful to those people who need it. That is NOT
the same thing as changing the Standard to FORCE compliance.

>
> >>I believe there should be syntax for REDEFINES
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> on the 9-bit machine every day...  ;> )

Well, that isn't what he said, Daniel. I operate on what I read.

I dislike intensely words like "require" and "mandatory" and will resist in
the strongest terms efforts to enforce a Standard that contains these words,
unless it can be shown to be useless or meaningless without them. (Usually
it can't...)

As you share the same environment as Judson, maybe there is something about
Unisys that encourages this approach <G>; could it be a result of striving
to make 9 bits work in an 8 bit World <G>?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T07:58:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vugigneaaiub9a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3fe7b065_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com> <vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com> <3fe7b065_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> The LENGTH of the data item has nothing to do with the MEANING of the data
> item.

True.  :)  (Of course, I remember in some of our security training that 
source code should be classified at the highest classification of 
information that could be derived from it.  For instance, if you had a 
variable called POUNDS-OF-FUEL, and it was 5 digits, someone could 
deduce that the aircraft this system supports can only carry 99,999 
pounds of fuel, and be able to make other deductions such as how far it 
can fly before refueling, etc.)

> I agree a compiler option to flag discrepancy in REDEFINES object and
> subject LENGTH , might be useful to those people who need it. That is NOT
> the same thing as changing the Standard to FORCE compliance.

And then he said...

>>>>I believe there should be syntax for REDEFINES
>>>>that would require the sizes to be equal.

He didn't say "I believe we should eliminate the unequal REDEFINES and 
only do it my way"...  He said he thought there should be syntax to do 
it the old-school way.  (Maybe the "EXACTLY" keyword...  "My-Redef 
Redefines My-Variable Exactly."  :>  )

>>>Why FORCE this when there is no need to, and no good case for doing so?
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well, that isn't what he said, Daniel. I operate on what I read.

I don't remember his message advocating -requiring- anyone to use this 
additional syntax...  I guess what I was saying what that you were 
reading a little more into that than I thought was there.

> I dislike intensely words like "require" and "mandatory" and will resist in
> the strongest terms efforts to enforce a Standard that contains these words,
> unless it can be shown to be useless or meaningless without them. (Usually
> it can't...)

heh...  The Picture clause is now optional for elementary items!  ;)  (I 
know what you're saying - that last part was a joke...)

> As you share the same environment as Judson, maybe there is something about
> Unisys that encourages this approach <G>; could it be a result of striving
> to make 9 bits work in an 8 bit World <G>?

heh - I don't know.  I wasn't aware that the Redefines didn't have to be 
equal, as I cut my teeth on COBOL 74, and got many compile errors. 
(This was also before I really understood the 9-bit byte...)

Funny war story about that...  There was this program that ran on some 
old tapes and came up with historical reports in a 3-D table (day, type 
code, hours & percentages) in memory, then dumped these totals to a 
report.  Horrendous code - a spaghetti-code mix of go to's and perform 
thru's, numbered paragraph (1000-Do-This) that were not in sequence 
(!!!)...  Anyway, there was this open deficiency that had been open 
about six months, where the totals came out completely off (1,413,234% 
uptime isn't really reasonable, although it would be nice... :> )

My boss spoke up in a meeting and said "Daniel can fix that in 2 weeks." 
  He informed me of this on a Friday afternoon, and that weekend I went 
to work.  The first week was spent pretty much figuring out how the code 
was flowing, and once I did, everything looked as it should.  I put a 
trace in the program to display the variables as they were computed, and 
narrowed it down to this one computation.  The moves above it were fine 
- "Move Up-Hours to In-Up-Hours" or something like that.

Then, I asked for help from a senior guy in another office, because I 
didn't understand how this "right" code was giving "wrong" results.  He 
told me to look at the picture clauses, and sure enough - Up-Hours was 
Pic 9(03) Comp and In-Up-Hours was Pic X(03).  What this did was move a 
binary representation as-is to the alphanumeric field.  Changing that X 
to a 9 fixed the program!  (It converted computational numeric to 
display numeric in the move if they were both 9's.)

This was also one of the more memorable times in my education of "the 
code always does what you tell it to do, even if you didn't realize you 
were telling it to do THAT!"  :)  Looking back, that experience was a 
learning experience on several fronts - practice in desk checking what 
the flow -actually- is, not what the code implies; know thy data (always 
check picture clauses for compatibility); and "I never want to own THAT 
program!"  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vugigneaaiub9a@corp.supernews.com...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
> > The LENGTH of the data item has nothing to do with the MEANING of the
data
> > item.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That's interesting... so would it make sense to define all these fields as
floating point <G>?

> > I agree a compiler option to flag discrepancy in REDEFINES object and
> > subject LENGTH , might be useful to those people who need it. That is
NOT
> > the same thing as changing the Standard to FORCE compliance.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Restating it with my emphasis:

"I believe there should be syntax for REDEFINES
that would *REQUIRE* the sizes to be equal."

This is what I took exception to.


> He didn't say "I believe we should eliminate the unequal REDEFINES and
> only do it my way"...  He said he thought there should be syntax to do
> it the old-school way.  (Maybe the "EXACTLY" keyword...  "My-Redef
> Redefines My-Variable Exactly."  :>  )

Yes, an EXACTLY keyword would be useful here, but it needs to be the
default.
>
> >>>Why FORCE this when there is no need to, and no good case for doing so?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> reading a little more into that than I thought was there.

Read his statement and my emphasis on it again. If you still feel the same,
we can agree to differ.
>
> > I dislike intensely words like "require" and "mandatory" and will resist
in
> > the strongest terms efforts to enforce a Standard that contains these
words,
> > unless it can be shown to be useless or meaningless without them.
(Usually
> > it can't...)
>
> heh...  The Picture clause is now optional for elementary items!  ;)  (I
> know what you're saying - that last part was a joke...)
>
Hahaha!

Unfortunately for you young whippersnappers, some of us old-timers remember
COBOL BEFORE there was a PICTURE clause...

COBOL 59 used the SIZE and USAGE clauses to define data. When PICTURE was
first introduced, there was a mixed reaction to it.

COBOL 59:     01   mydata   Size is 6 usage is Alphanumeric.
TODAY:          01   mydata   pic x(6).

I really must try defining data without pictures in modern Fujitsu COBOL,
just to see if it "remembers" the "oldene dayes"...<G>.

I know IBM "remembered" and accepted "OTHERWISE" as a synonym for "ELSE"
right into the 1980s.

BOTTOM LINE: COBOL is a rich enough language that there are many ways to do
things. This makes the need for "required" or "mandatory" less necessary...

> > As you share the same environment as Judson, maybe there is something
about
> > Unisys that encourages this approach <G>; could it be a result of
striving
> > to make 9 bits work in an 8 bit World <G>?
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, most of us have been there <G>.

Pete.
```

```
>>additional syntax...  I guess what I was saying what that you were
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> we can agree to differ.

I still feel the same.  Even with your emphasis, he says (my emphasis) 
"THERE SHOULD BE syntax that forces..."  Not "THE SYNTAX SHOULD 
force..."  I guess we each chose to focus on a different part of what he 
said.  :)

>>heh...  The Picture clause is now optional for elementary items!  ;)  (I
>>know what you're saying - that last part was a joke...)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> just to see if it "remembers" the "oldene dayes"...<G>.

Heh - I'll try in with UCOB and see what I get...

> I know IBM "remembered" and accepted "OTHERWISE" as a synonym for "ELSE"
> right into the 1980s.
> 
> BOTTOM LINE: COBOL is a rich enough language that there are many ways to do
> things. This makes the need for "required" or "mandatory" less necessary...

That's great.  :)

> Yes, most of us have been there <G>.

Does that mean I'm part of the club now (as of whenever that was that 
happened)?  ;)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T10:32:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bscbhp$f3k$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <3fe7b065_3@news.athenanews.com> <vugigneaaiub9a@corp.supernews.com> <bscan7$9ma$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bscan7$9ma$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On 23-Dec-2003, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>fit the next airplane/budget/count that comes along.

... and it is even more common practise to publish specs on most aircraft 
in Jane's Defense Weekly or a similar reference-work.

(In Basic Training we were told not to use toothpaste for two reasons.  
The first reason was that it was easier to keep a non-toothpasted 
toothbrush in 'inspection order' so that we wouldn't have points taken off 
during inspections... and the second 'reason' was that toothpaste 'didn't 
work'; the sergeant said 'You think toothpaste works?  Tell you what, put 
a big ol' dab of that stuff under your tongue before you go to sleep and 
see how much cleaner your teeth are when you wake up.'  (chorus of 
trainees: 'Eeeeeewwwwwwwwwww!')  After this bit of instruction I went up 
to the sergeant and said 'Sir, toothpaste is not intended as a 
contact-cleaner; it is a dentifricial agent and is supposed to be used as 
an abrasive compound during the act of brushing.'  The sergeant replied 
'Airman, when I want your opinion I'll give it to you.')

(Later on, that same day, we were given the demonstration of why it Makes 
Perfect Sense to have the toilet-paper come off the top of the roll; the 
sergeant took a roll in one hand, a ruler in the other and showed that if 
the paper came off the top you cut break it off cleanly with a simple 
twist of the ruler but when it came off the bottom the ruler would get 
hopelessly entangled.  I knew enough, at that point, *not* to ask 
'Sergeant, do the regulations state we can't use the latrine without 
carrying a ruler?')

DD
```

```
> (Later on, that same day, we were given the demonstration of why it Makes
> Perfect Sense to have the toilet-paper come off the top of the roll; the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

On a tangent...

I remember going to a meeting where the speaker had us raise our hands to see
which of us have our toilet paper going over the top and which under the bottom.
  He seemed to think he made his point - but I didn't raise my hand.   When I
replace a roll the end of the roll is sort of stuck together so it's not obvious
until I start using it which way I place it.

Cleaning maids put it over the top so that they can put a funny little fold in
the end of the roll to show that they were there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T14:57:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aed37e1e9a3113da140551f60132bb89@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com> <vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com> <3fe7b065_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> I agree a compiler option to flag discrepancy in REDEFINES object and
> subject LENGTH , might be useful to those people who need it.

Then we are in agreement. :-)

> That is NOT the same thing as changing the Standard to FORCE compliance.

Agreed. However, I never suggested that. :-) What I said was two things:
First, that I would like a syntax in the individual REDEFINES clause that
would cause the compiler to generate an error if the REDEFINES size does
not match the item it is redefining. And second, I opined that it would have
been better had COBOL-1985 kept the COBOL-1974 convention that then
required them to be equal, but added additional syntax to relax the constraint.
In no case did I suggest changing the standard to require them to be equal.
Wow! This would break countless programs, and I would be lynched by
hordes of irate COBOL programmers! :-)

> I dislike intensely words like "require" and "mandatory" and will resist in
> the strongest terms efforts to enforce a Standard that contains these words,
> unless it can be shown to be useless or meaningless without them. (Usually
> it can't...)

Wow, Pete, you really have a visceral reaction to authority! ;-)  Compilers
must 'require' your program to be submitted in acceptable syntax, no? You
could even say it is 'required' that compilers 'mandate' correct syntax. ;-)
But how could the situation be different? Even the language we are using as
we communicate now 'mandates', within very broad parameters, how the
syntax and semantics are to be used and understood. The very need for
consistency in order to be understood is a kind of 'mandate'. :-)

Perhaps it is just unfortunate phraseology on my part, but you really do
have a wrong impression in thinking I am inclined toward an 'authoritarian'
attitude in general. I believe in maximum personal freedom, as long as it
does not infringe on the freedoms of others. For example, I believe that
government should be only as powerful as absolutely necessary, and not
a bit more so (government at every level in the U.S. is currently far too
powerful, IMO). Of course, even people who agree on that will probably
disagree on exactly what 'absolutely necessary' means in this context. :-)
```

```

news:aed37e1e9a3113da140551f60132bb89@news.teranews.com...

Yes. Good <G>.

>
> Agreed. However, I never suggested that. :-)

Here's your quote:

" I believe there should be syntax for REDEFINES
that would *REQUIRE* the sizes to be equal. "

(My emphasis...maybe you should read what you write instead of saying you
never wrote it <G>?)


>What I said was two things:

No, what you SAID is reproduced above; you are now progressing to what you
WISH you'd said...

> First, that I would like a syntax in the individual REDEFINES clause that
> would cause the compiler to generate an error if the REDEFINES size does
> not match the item it is redefining. And second, I opined that it would
have
> been better had COBOL-1985 kept the COBOL-1974 convention that then
> required them to be equal, but added additional syntax to relax the
constraint.
> In no case did I suggest changing the standard to require them to be
equal.
> Wow! This would break countless programs, and I would be lynched by
> hordes of irate COBOL programmers! :-)
>
> > I dislike intensely words like "require" and "mandatory" and will resist
in
> > the strongest terms efforts to enforce a Standard that contains these
words,
> > unless it can be shown to be useless or meaningless without them.
(Usually
> > it can't...)
>
> Wow, Pete, you really have a visceral reaction to authority! ;-)

Yes, that is true and fair comment. I recognise the need for Authority, but
having been on both sides of the fence, I am extremely sensitive to it being
abused. I also don't like it just for the sake of "control" or because
someone thinks they "knows better".  My experience has been that Authority
imposed on groups of bright people simply stifles their creativity and is
counter-productive. For me, a better way is to motivate them towards a
desired goal, provide every possible support to see they attain that goal,
and there is then no need to impose Authority. A full discussion of this is
probably outside the scope of this forum. It comes down to "Management
Style", just like a lot of things in COBOL come down to "Coding Style".


> Compilers
> must 'require' your program to be submitted in acceptable syntax, no? You
> could even say it is 'required' that compilers 'mandate' correct syntax.
;-)
> But how could the situation be different? Even the language we are using
as
> we communicate now 'mandates', within very broad parameters, how the
> syntax and semantics are to be used and understood. The very need for
> consistency in order to be understood is a kind of 'mandate'. :-)
>
Depends on your viewpoint. There is enough richness in the English Language
for each of us to have many options as to how we formulate our arguments and
approach debate. I don't feel constrained or "required" or "mandated" to
adopt certain approaches when posting to this forum. (I love this place
because it is one fo the last bastions of true "Free Speech" on the Planet.)
Emphasis on *FREE*. Unmoderated, uncensored, unmandated, unconstrained.

> Perhaps it is just unfortunate phraseology on my part, but you really do
> have a wrong impression in thinking I am inclined toward an
'authoritarian'
> attitude in general.

Don't worry about it. You can survive fine without my approval <G>.

>I believe in maximum personal freedom, as long as it
> does not infringe on the freedoms of others. For example, I believe that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> disagree on exactly what 'absolutely necessary' means in this context. :-)

Good. Glad to hear it <G>...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T12:39:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2b7410d4e07516325ad2b79b4833dce@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com> <vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com> <3fe7b065_3@news.athenanews.com> <aed37e1e9a3113da140551f60132bb89@news.teranews.com> <3fe8c355_9@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> never wrote it <G>?)

My statement is very clear. If you use the syntax for ADD, then you
want the compiler to generate code to ADD. Same with every syntax
element in COBOL, though some are required or documentary. Why
would you interpret my comment differently, as you do? Simply put,
if you use the proposed syntax, it would instruct the compiler to
enforce the REDEFINES to be the same size. If you do not use the
syntax, then the compiler behaves as it does now. That is the only
reason to suggest a new syntax. If the idea were to force the equal
size all the time, there would be no need for new syntax! :-)

> > Compilers
> > must 'require' your program to be submitted in acceptable syntax, no? You
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Emphasis on *FREE*. Unmoderated, uncensored, unmandated, unconstrained.

Yes, I agree. But, if I were to write "hksdfl" to mean "consistent", then
no one would understand me. This is what I mean by 'mandate'. I am free
to write what I want, but if I am to be understood, I am constrained to
confine my writing to what would be understandable under the (very
broad) rules of the language.

Pete, I don't think we're nearly as far apart here as you think. From reading
your responses to my posts, I have sometimes been quite surprised by how
you interpreted my remarks. Not surprised at your position, but that you
understood my comments in the way you did. Most of the time, when you
appear to disagree with me, I read what you wrote and think "That's what
I meant." It makes me wonder if I have somehow caused you to think that I
am coming from a very different perspective than you, which leads you to
interpret what I say with that expectation? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T17:49:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bs9v7r$3a5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <3fe7650d_8@news.athenanews.com> <vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com> <3fe7b065_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```

On 22-Dec-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> I dislike intensely words like "require" and "mandatory" and will resist in
> the strongest terms efforts to enforce a Standard that contains these words,
> unless it can be shown to be useless or meaningless without them. (Usually
> it can't...)

I'm not quite so stringent.   It is mandatory for a compliant version of CoBOL
to allow one to code using the COMPUTE verb, and I wouldn't have it any other
way.

I also require that the compiler produces decimal arithmetic (however the
machine does it).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-12-27T17:17:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031227121711.29482.00001119@mb-m02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com>`

```

LX-i lxi0007@netscape.net 
Date: 12/22/03 6:36 PM EST
Message-id: <vuf005pb7t02d9@corp.supernews.com>

Commented in part,  about a suggestion from 
Judson McClendon concerning REDEFINES lengths ...
<<
...
I think what he's wanting is an compile option to force it, like the 
ones that do bounds checking on tables and reference modification.  I 
think that would be the best implementation...  I could see where it 
would be handy to let the compiler check your work, especially if you're 
using some strange picture clauses.  (Of course, I'm the guy that works 
on the 9-bit machine every day...  ;> )

...
>>

Judson's  (the original poster's) idea has merit, IMHO.  We really do intend in
some coding situations that the redefine be exactly the same length.  The
leniency of the newer compilers may or may not be the proper default.

It is an established default.  

Perhaps inaddition to a massively powerful compiler switch that would be
coercive for the whole compilation unit, we could consider a new syntax element

that selectively controls requirement of exact redefine length match
As in  a phrase like
   REDEFINES EXACTLY or
   REDEFINES BY LENGTH or
   REDEFINES SAME LENGTH
or something like that to engage the machine in always checking that we have
it right in those cases we intend it to be exactly the same length.  Checking
the DMAP listing for lengths is tedious and error prone ... something certainly
worth considering automating.

This would not merely help us be a little more efficient in test compiler
during development, but really puts a gate at the production install ... you
could not get
the production install to work if copybooks were not right, etc.


This stuff is always fun when considered in the context of ODOs (occurs
depending on).  But there is another issue, perhaps not to far around the bend
that we may be dealing with, and that is the potential for the new decimal 
to be implemented as infinite precision.  

It seems to me that those data items will flex in length dynamically, and if we
include them in an area redefined, that the meaning of length has got to 
be well defined.

This occurs to me because of the humerous aspect that the poster to whom I am
responding works with a reasonable 9-bit machinery, and the majority of us work
with odd-ball 8-bit.  The Chen-Ho elements that are at the foundation of the
new decimal are ten-bit chunks.  If we ever do anything like inifinite
precision, we get a varying number of these Chen-Hos.


Live From Alexander City, Alabama
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```
> This stuff is always fun when considered in the context of ODOs (occurs

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

I wouldn't even think of putting a redefines on an ODO...  (I'm not

> This occurs to me because of the humerous aspect that the poster to whom I am
> responding works with a reasonable 9-bit machinery, and the majority of us work
> with odd-ball 8-bit.  The Chen-Ho elements that are at the foundation of the

heh - 9-bit is reasonable and 8-bit is odd?  :)  We're winning! 
(Actually, this 9-bit thing really threw me for a loop when I first 
started having to deal with bits...  I didn't understand how everything 
that was in my training (8 bits in a byte, 2 bytes in a word) wasn't 
right - the Unisys 2200 has 9 bits in a byte, and 4 bytes in a word.)

> Live From Alexander City, Alabama

That's not far away at all - you watch the same local stations I do!  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T15:06:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bs9llt$gii$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com>`

```

On 22-Dec-2003, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> In this particular example, there are exactly 1229 primes below 10,000.
> If the number of values is correct, the compiler will flag any OCCURS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> relax the constraint.

Does your compiler have a setting to flag this as a warning?   Mine does.

With records defined in IDDs, I have programs with code like this in their
initialization routines:

COMPUTE DISPLAY-LEN-IABRCNS = FUNCTION LENGTH (IABRCNS)
COMPUTE DISPLAY-LEN-OLD-BNS = FUNCTION LENGTH (OLD-BNS-REC)

IF DISPLAY-LEN-IABRCNS = DISPLAY-LEN-BNS
     CONTINUE
ELSE
     do an early abort...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T12:47:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46623d9b91162e8455b20228c97147ed@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F5401F.16345123122003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>
> Any compiler worth its price will give you a warning in the event that
> you redefine a smaller area.

Not sure I understand what you mean, Joe. When you say "give you a
warning in the event that you redefine a smaller area" do you mean that
the compiler would flag "A REDEFINES B" when A is smaller than B,
or when B is smaller than A? Any compiler COBOL 85 or later must
flag A smaller than B as an error to be compliant to the standard. What
I would like is the ability to request the compiler also flag as an error
when B is less than A. As someone else suggested, perhaps the syntax
A REDEFINES B EXACTLY, or something similar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T17:17:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VSjGb.4340$lo3.3657@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<63507cfe5ec04c4b9442580f5dffb293@news.teranews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F5401F.16345123122003@corp.supernews.com> <46623d9b91162e8455b20228c97147ed@news.teranews.com>`

```
Just so it is clear, having a LARGER item redefine a smaller item is a
"perfectly valid" extension to the '85 Standard - and it does exist in several
compilers that I  know.  (They evaluate all the "redefinitions" and allocate the
LARGEST amount of any of the REDEFINES).  They *must* (to be Standard compliant)
provide a flagging mechanism (that most certainly need not be the default) to
flag such statements as "non-Standard" - but they are not in "violation" of the
Standard if they support such syntax - as a documented extension.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-12-27T18:07:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031227130705.29482.00001123@mb-m02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8545dc5cd4c4c4c250ae794390d78c@news.teranews.com>`

```
I would take Judson's idea here and backfill even more intelligence into a
compiler as optional features.

In general a compiler, in a modern world, ought to easily be able to see that a
MOVE SPACES to a group level which has subordinates that are not PIC X (or the
like) is an initializing action (and could just silently note that). And
obviously, it ought to be able to see that an INITIALIZE of a group level is an
initializing action.

A good compiler could then do a review.  If the referant initialized is a
secondary redefines of something that has alternative lengths, the programmer
might benefit from an assist in these cases. Flip a switch on, get all the
noise you care to read about. Leave the switch in the default NOREDEFINESNOISE
mode, and live as blissfully as ever.

Judson's original post dealt with the limited (but important) case of the
tabular redefine. Redefines have many variations.

Length is the focal issue of this thread. Alignment of subfields is another
(which did  come up as a tangent in some of the posted examples).  Even more
arcane is the redefines that accomplish no-sign instead of unsigned data layout
(usually all intentional). But sometimes you would like feedback from the
compiler on some of it. And the length disagreement would be the easiest for
the compiler to figure, and would be a meaningful easy target for compiler
writers.

The simples redefines noise feedback would then be
  a) REDEFINES lengths are not in agreement
This could be augmented with a syntax that        takes it from informational
to error diagnostic, the EXACTLY syntax. 
   b) possible feedback (optional) about initializing actions, group level MOVE
SPACES, INITIALIZE, and
   c) LENGTH OF operations
   d) It would be nice to have a highlighted list of every place a
length-variant redefined was passed explictly to a submodule in a USING clause.
Not so much if it was a subordinate of the group passed as the data item in the
USING list, but if we elevated the visibility of the thing to the compiler.Yet
there are issues if a group which has redefining subordinates is passed to an
external piece of code. Especially if it was the last item in a group item ...
here I am thinking the   BY CONTENT phrase actually copies things in order to
protect the caller's working storage and the largest redefines will
determine the length (isn't that a spark of a short-circuit of the protection,
the submodule could see more than you want it too ....albeit a copy ...
external code could _see_ the fuel capacity recorded in later data fields not
really intended to be passed, ... especially if an initializing action does not
clear the full length).


Keep in mind that we do intend that length match in some cases, and intend that
it is really okay that they do not in others.

So we would want the noise turned off by default, but could benefit by feedback
from the compiler that reviews this REDEFINES issue comprehensively.

Live from near Martin Lake, Alabama
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-27T19:46:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vusdf9770dqrd1@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<20031227130705.29482.00001123@mb-m02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8545dc5cd4c4c4c250ae794390d78c@news.teranews.com> <20031227130705.29482.00001123@mb-m02.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote:

> In general a compiler, in a modern world, ought to easily be able to see that a
> MOVE SPACES to a group level which has subordinates that are not PIC X (or the
> like) is an initializing action (and could just silently note that). And
> obviously, it ought to be able to see that an INITIALIZE of a group level is an
> initializing action.

I wouldn't want this as the default behavior.  Although I agree that a 
syntax that forces redefines to be equal would be a nice addition, I 
don't want the compiler taking liberties in interpreting my code. 
That's one thing that's very annoying with people, and very refreshing 
in a compiler.  :)  Move means Move, and Initialize means Initialize.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-12-28T23:09:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031228180935.19959.00001136@mb-m01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<vusdf9770dqrd1@corp.supernews.com>`

```

LX-i lxi0007@netscape.net 
Date: 12/27/03 8:46 PM EST
Message-id: <vusdf9770dqrd1@corp.supernews.com>

chimed in

<<

I wouldn't want this as the default behavior.  Although I agree that a 
syntax that forces redefines to be equal would be a nice addition, I 
don't want the compiler taking liberties in interpreting my code. 
That's one thing that's very annoying with people, and very refreshing 
in a compiler.  :)  Move means Move, and Initialize means Initialize.


>>

I am not suggesting that a MOVE SPACES change in any way at all. Just suggeting
that a compiler can note it for later use in a small report that shows up at
the end (optionally, of course). The report would say, do you know that you did
an initialization (with a MOVE SPACES or an INITIALIZE verb) refering to one
length and you passed the same, REDEFINED area in a USING clause by means of
another length ... here are the relevant line numbers.

This relates to the supposed protection you get from the ordinary (COBOL 85)
USING ... BY VALUE, and the extraordinary (COBOL 2002) USING ... BY CONTENT;
both of which do copies, the length of which could be ambiguous when influenced
by REDEFINES.

It is not an error to init a different length than you ultimately use.  It
could however be a powerful hint when debugging.

I am driving home a point, I think implied by the original poster. That is,
beyond just the tabular redefinitions, there are many kinds of redefinitions.
New problems are possible when redefines are not exactly the same length, ...
including initialization imperfections.  The compiler could detect those as
'possible' issues
and report them.

Like most posting here, I am stongly enthusiastic about the now well
established capability to redefine with alternate lengths.  But the total
silence of the compilers now is worth reviewing.

There are two ideas in tow here: a massive switch and/or a new syntax element. 
I am only saying that internally if you do enough work for that you could also
easily see when the programmer _might_ be stepping on their own foot by having
_possible_ contradictions in the way the alternate lengths are used. And if the
compiler had that much info it could just list it under the control of an
option.

But I am not saying that it should intervene and revise anything like a MOVE
SPACES.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-29T01:01:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92LHb.9716$lo3.9690@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vusdf9770dqrd1@corp.supernews.com> <20031228180935.19959.00001136@mb-m01.aol.com>`

```
"RKRayhawk" <rkrayhawk@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20031228180935.19959.00001136@mb-m01.aol.com...
<SNIP>
>
> This relates to the supposed protection you get from the ordinary (COBOL 85)
> USING ... BY VALUE, and the extraordinary (COBOL 2002) USING ... BY CONTENT;
> both of which do copies, the length of which could be ambiguous when
influenced
> by REDEFINES.
>

Before anyone else "chimes" in - "BY CONTENT" was introduced in the '85 Standard
(and - usually? - passes a COPY of the parameter).  BY VALUE was added in the
2002 Standard (and as an extension in many earlier compilers).  It (usually?)
passes the actual "paratemeter " - but "on the stack".

In both cases, the called (or invoked) subroutine (or method or function) gets
access ONLY to a storage of the "size" of the passed parameter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-12-29T14:14:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bb8545dc5cd4c4c4c250ae794390d78c@news.teranews.com> <20031227130705.29482.00001123@mb-m02.aol.com> <vusdf9770dqrd1@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On 27-Dec-2003, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> I wouldn't want this as the default behavior.  Although I agree that a
> syntax that forces redefines to be equal would be a nice addition, I
> don't want the compiler taking liberties in interpreting my code.
> That's one thing that's very annoying with people, and very refreshing
> in a compiler.  :)  Move means Move, and Initialize means Initialize.

Speaking of interpreting - I was in a shop where the standard was only one 01
level in working storage and no 77 levels.   It seems that they didn't like the
way CoBOL moved data to word boundaries.

In most shops lately people cluster similar working storage fields under an 01,
and few use 77 levels.   I don't know if the efficiency issues have been
overcome, or whether they just don't matter anymore.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-29T18:12:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9732ebbe850931ee13efeb5da7f2dc8@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8545dc5cd4c4c4c250ae794390d78c@news.teranews.com> <20031227130705.29482.00001123@mb-m02.aol.com> <vusdf9770dqrd1@corp.supernews.com> <bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> In most shops lately people cluster similar working storage fields under an 01,
> and few use 77 levels.   I don't know if the efficiency issues have been
> overcome, or whether they just don't matter anymore.

At one time on the Burroughs/Unisys B5xxx/A series machines 77 level
entries were stack variables and faster to access. Don't see how they
could speed the others up, but if 77's are no longer stack variables, there
would be no difference anymore. :-)
```

```
> At one time on the Burroughs/Unisys B5xxx/A series machines 77 level

Machines that were more limited in how many registers they had might have had
more troubles being so efficient.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T10:17:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1072174576@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Reading corresponding data from 2 files — 5 msgs

### Reading corresponding data from 2 files

- **From:** ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie)
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T10:39:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bee6ba6.0312231039.70498319@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi

I am using Fujitsu v3 compiler.

I am having some trouble with reading information from the file into the program.
I have two files.
File 1 holds account info.
File 2 holds transaction info for each account on 'File 1'
The problem is:
I first read File 1 , for the account.  
I then read File 2 for the transactions for that account, but there may be more 
than one transaction for each account.
When I read File 2 for each transaction, I can only get the first transaction and 
not all the transactions.
What I ideally want to do is read first account and then first transaction and then
prompt the user for Y or N to display the next transaction for this account.

Any ideas what i'm doing wrong here??

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
	FILE-CONTROL.
		SELECT account-file ASSIGN TO "File 1"
			ORGANIZATION IS Indexed
		        ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
			RECORD KEY IS ac-no


		SELECT transaction-file ASSIGN TO "File 2"
			ORGANIZATION IS Indexed
			ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
			RECORD KEY IS transaction-ac-no.
----------------------------------------------------------

PERFORM UNTIL account-check = "Y"

PERFORM get-account-key
		
READ account-file          
	INVALID KEY 
	DISPLAY "Account not found. Try again!"
	MOVE "N" TO account-check
		
	NOT INVALID KEY 
	DISPLAY "Account found"
	DISPLAY "Customer name: " credit-ac-last-name ", " credit-ac-first-name
		DISPLAY "Credit Limit: " credit-ac-limit 
		DISPLAY "Balance: credit-ac-balance " credit-ac-balance
		MOVE "Y" TO account-check
                MOVE ws-ac-in TO credit-ac-no
     	END-READ
     	
*###################ABOVE WORKS FINE - gets specific account from File 1.
*##########################Problem is in this loop....?

*READ TRANSACTIONS for THIS account
		MOVE ac-in TO transaction-ac-no
		START transaction-file KEY = transaction-ac-no
			INVALID KEY 
				DISPLAY "INVALID KEY IN START"
				SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE 	
			NOT INVALID KEY 
				DISPLAY "TEST: NOT INVALID KEY IN START"
				SET not-end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
         		        READ transaction-file  NEXT RECORD 
					AT END SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
				END-READ
		END-START

	SET not-end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
	MOVE "Y" TO user-answer
	PERFORM UNTIL user-answer NOT = "Y" OR end-of-file-flag 
            OR ac-in NOT = transaction-ac-no
	READ transaction-file NEXT RECORD AT END SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
		NOT AT END 
		DISPLAY "Description:            " credit-trans-description
		DISPLAY "Transaction amount:     " credit-trans-amt
	END-READ
			
  	DISPLAY "user answer here..... Y/N? > " WITH NO ADVANCING
			ACCEPT user-answer from console
	
	IF user-answer = "Y" OR "y" THEN 
		DISPLAY "Enter new transaction amount: " WITH NO ADVANCING
			ACCEPT ws-trans-amt-in from console
                        ...

		END-IF

END-PERFORM

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Any help aprechiated!
Thanks in advance,
Ritchie
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading corresponding data from 2 files

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T10:39:05+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fe8b65d_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312231039.70498319@posting.google.com>`

```

"ritchie" <ritchie_s01@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:3bee6ba6.0312231039.70498319@posting.google.com...
> Hi
>
> I am using Fujitsu v3 compiler.
>
> I am having some trouble with reading information from the file into the
program.
> I have two files.
> File 1 holds account info.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I then read File 2 for the transactions for that account, but there may be
more
> than one transaction for each account.
> When I read File 2 for each transaction, I can only get the first
transaction and
> not all the transactions.
> What I ideally want to do is read first account and then first transaction
and then
> prompt the user for Y or N to display the next transaction for this
account.
>
> Any ideas what i'm doing wrong here??
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> PERFORM UNTIL account-check = "Y"

The scope of this perform includes ALL of the code below. Before obtaining
transactions this condition is set TRUE (see the NOT INVALID KEY code...).
On completion of the first transaction the END-PERFORM is encountered and so
no further action can occur until you get another Account. It will only
process the first transaction. After that it reaches the end of this perform
scope and the condition is TRUE, so it "stops"...

Review the scope of this perform, and decide exactly when it should be set
to TRUE.

Try indenting the source so you can easily see what ranges are involved.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading corresponding data from 2 files

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T22:13:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kmehuvkndlt472q2gf7ku67g3at97tjo5g@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312231039.70498319@posting.google.com>`

```
On 23 Dec 2003 10:39:52 -0800, ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie) wrote:

>Hi
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The code below should be  changed so it does one start, and then only
ONE read. (two reads are also possible but use then on other
constructs).
>*READ TRANSACTIONS for THIS account
>		MOVE ac-in TO transaction-ac-no
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
At this point you have already done a read, and you may already have
an end of file flag set, so the following SET is incorrect.
It should be set only before either the start, or before the first
read.
>	SET not-end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
>	MOVE "Y" TO user-answer
>	PERFORM UNTIL user-answer NOT = "Y" OR end-of-file-flag 
>            OR ac-in NOT = transaction-ac-no
As you have read the first record of this "transaction acc-no" this
following is basically making you loose your first record.
>	READ transaction-file NEXT RECORD AT END SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
>		NOT AT END 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-PERFORM

The following is not the code but may help you to see what you have
wrong, and correct it.

Option 1.

Read file-1
valid record
   start file-2
   not end of file
      loop until eof or invalid-record or answer=n
       read file-2 next
       if valid record
          process record
       end-if
      end-loop
end.
   
Option 2.

Read file-1
valid record
   start file-2
   not end of file
      read file-2 next

      loop until eof or invalid-record or answer=n
       process record
       read file-2 next
      end-loop
end.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading corresponding data from 2 files

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T14:18:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0312231418.1be34d73@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312231039.70498319@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie) wrote in message 

> I have two files.
> File 1 holds account info.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> not all the transactions.

Are you sure that 'all the transactions' are actually in the file ?

> 		SELECT transaction-file ASSIGN TO "File 2"
> 			ORGANIZATION IS Indexed
> 			ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
> 			RECORD KEY IS transaction-ac-no.

You seem to have the unique key as just the account number, you can
have only one record for each different account number.  If you have
been writing records to the file they have probably failed to write
and gave a file status error code of '22' = Duplicate record key.

First thing wrong is that if you want multiple records per account you
need to have a key that is more than just the account, it might
include a sequence number, or a type and reference, for example a
unique invoice number or journal number or payment reference.

2nd thing is add a file status to every file and _check_it_.  Without
this you may never know what is happening.

> *READ TRANSACTIONS for THIS account
>  MOVE ac-in TO transaction-ac-no
>  START transaction-file KEY = transaction-ac-no

This will only start with an exact match.  You would change this to >=
or NOT < when you add additional fields to the transaction key.

> ...
>    READ transaction-file  NEXT RECORD 

This reads the first matching record

>     PERFORM UNTIL user-answer NOT = "Y" OR end-of-file-flag 
>            OR ac-in NOT = transaction-ac-no
>           READ transaction-file NEXT RECORD AT END SET end-of-file-flag TO RUE

This reads the _SECOND_ matching record, but there isn't one.

I would suggest using something like:

        SELECT Transaction-File
             ...           
            RECORD KEY Transaction-Key
        ...     

        FD  Transaction-File.
        01  Transaction-Record.
            03  Transaction-Key.
             05 Transaction-Ac-no         PIC X(6).
             05 Transaction-Ref           PIC X(6).
             05 Transaction-Seq           PIC 999.
            ....

        MOVE SPACES    TO Transaction-Key
        MOVE Ac-In     TO Transaction-Ac-No
        START Transaction-File
            KEY NOT < Transaction-Key
            INVALID KEY
                 MOVE HIGH-VALUES  TO Transaction-Key
        END-START
        MOVE "Y"      TO User-Answer
        PERFORM 
            UNTIL Transaction-Ac-No NOT = Ac-In
               OR User-Answer NOT = "Y"

            READ Transaction-File
                AT END
                    MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO Transaction-Key
            END-READ
            IF ( Transaction-Ac-No = Ac-In )
                process record

                DISPLAY ...
                ACCEPT ...
            END-IF
        END-PERFORM
```

- **From:** ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie)
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T11:14:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bee6ba6.0312241114.75a0a98c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0312231039.70498319@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0312231418.1be34d73@posting.google.com>`


<a id="t-c3f0e2bfd5"></a>
## Thread: The *fluxion* guy — 2 msgs

### The *fluxion* guy

- **From:** Habitant <berlutte@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-12-23T22:51:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jq2iuvoqg9mr2g347fsdb4v2mvjgefl5gr@4ax.com>`

```
maths.ucd.ie/courses/mst3022/history5.pdf

Newton concluded by expressing regret about controversy arising from
his earlier research publications, with the implication that he
intended to publish little in future. Leibniz replied on June 21,
1677. He described his method of drawing tangents, which proceeded
not by fluxions of lines but by differences of numbers. He also
introduced his notation of dx and dy for infinitesimal differences or
differentials of coordinates. 

While Leibnizï¿½s differential calculus seems quite familiar to modern
day students of mathematics, the same cannot be said of Newtonï¿½s
fluxional calculus, which appears unnecessarily complicated. We will
briefly attempt to describe how Newtonï¿½s calculus proceeded. He
assumed that we may conceive of all geometric magnitudes as generated
by continous motion for example, a line is generated by the motion of
a point, etc. The quantity thus generated is called the fluent or
flowing quantity. The velocity of the moving magnitude is the fluxion
of the fluent. There then arise two problems. The first is to find
the fluxion of a given quantity, or more generally the relation of the
fluents being given, to find the relation of their fluxions. This is
akin to implicit differentiation. The second method is the inverse
method of fluxions: from the fluxion, or some relation involving
it, to find the fluent. This amounts to what we call integration,
possibly of a differential equation. Newton referred to these
procedures as the method of quadrature and the inverse
method of tangents. The infinitely small part by which a fluent such
as x increased in a small interval of time o was called the moment of
the fluent and its value was shown to 10 be o . x. Given x, Newton
denoted the fluent whose fluxion was x by x0 or [x]. This is the
same as the integral of x (but not with respect to x). Subsequently, 

Newtonï¿½s methods were taught at Cambridge for more than 100 years, and
the word fluxion was retained. The explanations given for the
procedures were usually vague and unconvincing. It was
not until almost 1820 that Leibnizï¿½s notation of dx and the integral
sign started to appear in British mathematical textbooks and papers.


Between 1689 and 1693, Newton had fallen under the influence of a
little-known Swiss mathematician named Nicholas Fatio de Duillier
(1664-1753), who had moved to England in 1687. Later in 1693, Newton
suffered what appears to have been a nervous breakdown. After his
recovery, Newton decided to abandon his career in Cambridge,
devoted to research and scientific discovery, and sought more wordly
fame in London as Warden of the Mint in 1696. Nonetheless, Newton was
alarmed when informed by John Wallis in 1695 that many continental
mathematicians considered the calculus to be the invention of Leibniz
alone, perhaps on the basis of his expositions of the subject in Acta
Eruditorum. In support of Newton, Duillier published a paper through
the Royal Society of London in 1699 in which he implied that Leibniz
had plagiarized the ideas of the calculus from Newton. In 1704,
Leibniz replied in the Acta Eruditorum that he had priority of
publication, and he protested to the Royal Society about the
unfairness of the accusation of plagiarism. The Royal Society
eventually responded by establishing a committee to investigate the
dispute. In 1712, the committee published their conclusions in a
report entitled Commercium epistolicum. The report affirmed that 

Newton had invented the calculus, a point not seriously disputed even
by Leibniz. The report also noted that Leibniz had had access in the
1670ï¿½s to manuscripts and letters describing Newtonï¿½s preliminary
version of the calculus (for example, De analysi ), so that suspicions
of plagiarism were not totally dismissed. It has become apparent that
the Commercium epistolicum was essentially Newtonï¿½s own work. he
dictated the conclusions that the committee reported.

The priority dispute was dominated by nationalistic concerns, British
mathematicians being especially keen to defend Newtonï¿½s honour and
proclaim his genius as a re-flection of British superiority. As a
consequence, the advantages of Leibnizï¿½s notation, subsequently
developed by the Bernoullis and Euler, were ignored in Britain, and by
paying too much deference to Newton, mathematics stagnated there until
the early 19th century. The dispute was led in Britain, albeit
secretly, by Newton himself, and he seems to have wanted to remove all
record of Leibnizï¿½s achievements.
=========================================================

' As a consequence, the advantages of Leibnizï¿½s notation, subsequently
developed by the Bernoullis and Euler, were ignored in Britain, and by
paying too much deference to Newton, mathematics stagnated there until
the early 19th century.'

LOL
```

#### ↳ Re: The *fluxion* guy

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-12-24T13:38:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<55f7a6640388563dc204c22d350750de@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<jq2iuvoqg9mr2g347fsdb4v2mvjgefl5gr@4ax.com>`

```
Thanks for that informative post, Habitat.

Newton was so brilliant, complexities of expression apparently were
inconsequential to him. It has been said (approximately) "Geometry is
indeed a powerful tool, but only Newton could wield it well." Newton
was apparently as comfortable attacking, and usually solved, problems
geometrically that others would only attempt analytically, if at all. I
have read that Newton's own work for Principia was done primarily
analytically, but he transposed much of it into geometric form because
he didn't want controversy over his new fluxion calculus to cloud the
issue. There were a number of times when others claimed to have made
original discoveries and Newton responded that he had done that years
before, and produced his notes to back it up.

Anyone with substantially above average intelligence sometimes gets
the impression that dealing with 'average' people can be like dealing
with children. One can only imagine what life must have been like for
Newton. There was no one with whom he could discuss things as an
equal. Only a few, a very few, people in the entire world were even
remotely close to him intellectually. But he had no real peer, certainly
no one he could truly look up to intellectually, though he apparently
did admire a number of intellects. I ask myself, what would I be like
if virtually the entire population of the planet were as children to me
intellectually? I know that I have struggled with pride over my modest
abilities. I get impatient when I have to explain something more than
once. What would I be like if I had no intellectual peer? My guess is
that I would not be particularly humble, and do a far poorer job than
Newton on that score. It is a good bet that few of us would have shown
the kind of modesty that Newton often did. In many ways Newton was
truly humble. I find that incredible and admirable.
```
